In 1915 the city council finally voted to move to Eastern Time, pushed by boosters who wanted lighter evenings for golf and street life. Then in 1967, Michigan was slow to start daylight saving, not flipping the clocks until June instead of late April, briefly marooning it behind the rest of the Eastern zone for about six weeks. For software, the lesson is that even a single American city can carry multiple offset transitions across its history, and assuming a region was always on its current zone will silently corrupt historical timestamps.

title: Germany and Austria-Hungary introduce the world's first national daylight saving time
body: German Empire and Austro-Hungarian Empire (wartime governments)
whatChanged: On 30 April 1916 at 23:00 local time, Germany and Austria-Hungary advanced clocks one hour (Sommerzeit) to UTC+2, reverting on 1 October 1916 at 01:00. This was the first nationwide compulsory DST anywhere, adopted to conserve coal during World War I. The tz database encodes this as the earliest C-Eur rule (Rule C-Eur 1916 only Apr 30 23:00 1:00 S), which flows into Europe/Berlin, Europe/Vienna, Europe/Budapest, and many other Central European zones.
technicalImpact: Establishes the earliest DST rule in the C-Eur rule set, affecting Europe/Berlin, Europe/Vienna, Europe/Budapest, Europe/Warsaw, and related zones. Any software or tz data handling pre-1970 timestamps for Central Europe must carry this rule. The spring transition fires at 23:00 (not the modern 02:00 convention), so code that assumes a standard spring-forward hour can misplace timestamps from this night.
headline: The first clocks to spring forward
blurb: In 1916, Germany and Austria-Hungary became the first countries in the world to make daylight saving time a national law, shifting clocks to save coal for the war effort.
story: As World War I dragged on, Germany and Austria-Hungary were running short of coal. Officials calculated that shifting the clock forward by one hour in summer would mean less artificial lighting in the evenings, saving fuel. At 23:00 on 30 April 1916, both empires simultaneously moved their clocks forward to midnight, starting the world's first nationwide daylight saving time. Other countries quickly followed, and the practice never fully went away. Every DST system in use today descends directly from this wartime experiment, and the tz database traces the roots of Central European time zones back to this single night.

title: Dublin Mean Time abolished: Ireland forced onto GMT after 1916 Rising
body: British Parliament (via Time (Ireland) Act 1916)
whatChanged: Dublin Mean Time (DMT), based on Dunsink Observatory at UTC-00:25:21, was abolished on 1916-10-01 at 02:00 when the Time (Ireland) Act 1916 took effect. Ireland adopted GMT, matching Britain; clocks were put back 35 minutes that night. DMT had been Dublin's legal time since 1880-08-02, when it was codified alongside Britain's own Greenwich standardisation.
technicalImpact: Pre-1916 Dublin timestamps require a -00:25:21 offset, not UTC+0. Any code assuming Irish historical times were GMT is wrong by 25 minutes. tzdb Zone Europe/Dublin encodes this transition explicitly.
headline: Ireland's lost 25 minutes
blurb: Dublin kept its own local time, 25 minutes behind London, until British law erased it months after the 1916 Rising.
story: Dublin set its clocks by Dunsink Observatory, 8 km northwest of the city centre, the same way London used Greenwich. That put Irish time 25 minutes and 21 seconds behind UTC. In October 1916, months after the Easter Rising, the Time (Ireland) Act abolished Dublin Mean Time and forced Ireland onto GMT; on the night of 1 October, clocks went back only 35 minutes instead of the usual full hour. Many in Ireland saw the move as political, one more English standard imposed from London. Software working with pre-1916 Irish records must apply the -00:25:21 offset or every timestamp is quietly wrong.

title: UK Summer Time Act 1916 creates British Summer Time (GMT+1)
body: Parliament of the United Kingdom
whatChanged: The Summer Time Act 1916, passed in mid-May, moved clocks forward one hour to GMT+1 on 21 May 1916 at 02:00, creating British Summer Time (BST). Clocks reverted on 1 October 1916. The Act applied to Great Britain and Ireland, but in Ireland it advanced clocks relative to Dublin Mean Time (about 25 minutes behind Greenwich) rather than GMT; a companion statute, the Time (Ireland) Act 1916, abolished Dublin Mean Time effective 1 October 1916, aligning Ireland with GMT. The Act established the legislative framework for all subsequent UK daylight saving law.
technicalImpact: The Europe/London zone in tzdb encodes the GB-Eire rule set originating from this Act. Any software handling UK historical timestamps from 21 May 1916 onward must account for the GMT/BST seasonal split. The Act is the legislative root of the annual spring-forward, fall-back cycle that Europe/London still carries today.
headline: Britain's first summer clock
blurb: The UK Parliament passed a law in 1916 to shift clocks forward one hour each summer, creating British Summer Time and the seasonal GMT/BST split still in use today.
story: During World War One, the British government wanted to save coal by making better use of daylight. Parliament passed the Summer Time Act in May 1916, moving clocks forward one hour from late May to early October. Ireland was included, though its clocks were advanced relative to Dublin Mean Time, which was about 25 minutes behind Greenwich; a separate Time (Ireland) Act 1916 abolished Dublin Mean Time on 1 October 1916, finally aligning Ireland with GMT. That wartime decision created the GMT/BST split that every computer must handle when working with UK times, and it is still encoded in the time zone database that software relies on.

title: Russia introduces summer time for the first time (Provisional Government decree)
body: Russian Provisional Government
whatChanged: The Russian Provisional Government decreed clocks advance one hour from 1 July 1917, introducing summer time (Moscow Summer Time) for the first time in Russia. The measure was originally set to end 31 August 1917 but was extended, first to 1 October then indefinitely. The Soviet government reversed it with Decree No. 142 (dated 22 December 1917, Old Style), moving clocks back one hour at the end of December 1917, returning to Moscow Mean Time. The IANA tz database encodes this transition at 28 December 1917, 00:00. (All dates are Old Style/Julian, as Russia did not adopt the Gregorian calendar until February 1918.) This began a long and irregular history of Russian DST transitions encoded in the tz database.
technicalImpact: Russia's 1917 to 1921 transitions are encoded in the tz database Europe file under the Russia rule set, requiring careful handling of Julian vs Gregorian calendar dates and a series of one-off transitions that cannot be captured by simple recurring rules. Any software parsing historic Russian timestamps around this period must account for these irregular shifts.
headline: Russia's first summer clock
blurb: The Russian Provisional Government advanced clocks by one hour in summer 1917, introducing daylight saving time to Russia for the first time.
story: In the middle of World War One, Russia's Provisional Government pushed clocks forward one hour starting July 1917, following the trend set by Germany and Britain. The measure was extended past its original end date while the country descended into revolution. When the Bolsheviks took power, one of their early acts was to roll the clocks back in late December 1917. The episode kicked off more than a century of erratic Russian clock changes, each one a separate entry the tz database must track individually, complicating any software that handles historic Russian timestamps.

title: Alaska timezone abbreviation conflicts: AST, NST, BST, YST overloads
body: United States Congress / IANA tzdb maintainers
whatChanged: From 1918, Alaska Standard Time used the abbreviation AST (offset around -10). The Uniform Time Act of 1966, effective April 1, 1967, renamed it Alaska-Hawaii Standard Time (AHST). Nome Standard Time (NST, around -11) was later renamed Bering Standard Time (BST). The trouble is that these short labels were already claimed elsewhere: AST also means Atlantic Standard Time, NST also means Newfoundland Standard Time, and BST also means British Summer Time. The tzdb northamerica file tracks Alaska's offsets and abbreviation history in its commentary.
technicalImpact: The abbreviations AST, NST, and BST are each used by more than one IANA tzdb zone. Any code that resolves a timezone by abbreviation rather than by IANA zone name (for example America/Anchorage) risks silently picking the wrong offset, a well-known design limitation of timezone abbreviations.
headline: When Alaska's clocks shared names with everyone else's
blurb: Alaska used timezone abbreviations that were already claimed by other regions, creating silent lookup errors for software relying on abbreviations.
story: For decades, Alaska's standard time zones shared abbreviations with places half a world away. AST meant Alaska as well as Atlantic; BST meant Bering as well as British Summer Time; NST covered both Nome and Newfoundland. Congress renamed Alaska's zones in 1967 to reduce confusion, but the old abbreviations stuck in legacy code and databases. Software that looks up a timezone by its short label rather than its IANA name can still silently land in the wrong country.

title: Standard Time Act of 1918 (Calder Act) - first US federal time law
body: US Congress (65th); enforcement delegated to the Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC)
whatChanged: First US federal law establishing standard time zones and a nationwide daylight saving time, adopting the railroad time-zone system into law. DST ran from the last Sunday in March to the last Sunday in October. The national DST mandate was repealed in 1919 over a presidential veto, leaving only the standard-time-zone framework.
technicalImpact: Established the legal definition of US time zones that every timekeeping system ultimately encodes. Root of the US standard-time offsets (EST -05, CST -06, MST -07, PST -08) the IANA tz database carries; America/* zones reference the 1918 Act.

validation.supportingQuote: The Standard Time Act of 1918, also known as the Calder Act, was the first United States federal law implementing Standard time and Daylight saving time. ... Signed into law by President Woodrow Wilson on March 19, 1918 ... authorized the Interstate Commerce Commission to define the limits of each time zone ... [DST] repealed ... over President Woodrow Wilson's veto [in 1919].

headline: America's first national time law
blurb: Congress put the railroad time zones into law and added a nationwide summer clock change.
story: In 1918 the United States passed its first federal law setting standard time zones across the country. It adopted the zone system the railroads had been using. The law also added a national daylight saving period for part of the year. The summer clock change was repealed the next year, but the time zones stayed.

title: US Standard Time Act of 1918: first nationwide time law
body: United States Congress (65th), signed into law by President Woodrow Wilson
whatChanged: Public Law 65-106 (signed 1918-03-19) established the first federal standard for both time zones and daylight saving time across the US. DST ran from the last Sunday in March to the last Sunday in October. The law was repealed for DST purposes by Public Law 66-40 in August 1919, making DST national for only 1918 and 1919. (See Rule US 1918-1919 in tzdb northamerica.)
technicalImpact: tzdb Rule US covers 1918-1919 only. Historical queries for US local time between 1919 and 1942 must not assume national DST applied; observance reverted to local option until wartime resumption in 1942. Software or libraries doing pre-1970 US time conversion must use the correct rule set for this gap.
headline: America's first time law
blurb: The US passed its first nationwide law setting both standard time zones and daylight saving time, then repealed the DST part just one year later.
story: Before 1918 the US had no federal rule for clocks. Congress passed the Standard Time Act to fix that, creating official time zones and introducing daylight saving for the first time nationally. Public opposition was strong, and Congress repealed the daylight saving portion the very next year. That left a two-year window, 1918 and 1919, as the only years with nationally mandated DST until World War II. Software doing historical US time calculations must know DST did not apply nationally between 1920 and 1941.

title: Creston, BC has observed permanent UTC-7 since June 1918
body: Town of Creston, British Columbia (municipal decision, no formal ordinance on record)
whatChanged: In June 1918 Creston switched from PST (UTC-8) to PDT (UTC-7) and never switched back. When daylight saving ended elsewhere in Canada on 27 October 1918, Creston kept its clocks still. It has remained on UTC-7 (Mountain Standard Time) ever since, ignoring wartime federal DST mandates and all subsequent Canadian DST rules. During WWII the paper classification was changed to PST with PDT applied, which left the wall clock at the same UTC-7, so no actual time change occurred.
technicalImpact: The IANA tz database added America/Creston as a dedicated UTC-7 zone (no DST rules) in tzdata 2012a, then in 2021b moved its data to 'backzone' with a backward-compatibility link to America/Phoenix; the zone name still resolves to UTC-7 for all post-1970 timestamps. Any system that assumes all of British Columbia follows Pacific time (UTC-8 standard, UTC-7 DST) will compute Creston timestamps incorrectly for every moment after mid-1918.
headline: The town that stopped its clocks in 1918
blurb: Creston, BC switched its clocks forward in June 1918 and simply never changed them again, leaving the town permanently on UTC-7 while the rest of British Columbia follows daylight saving.
story: In June 1918 Creston moved its clocks forward to what was then Pacific Daylight Time. When autumn came and the rest of Canada set clocks back, Creston did not. The town stayed put, quietly sitting at UTC-7 through wartime federal mandates, provincial rules, and every clock-change debate since. Local museum research traced the full history, confirming no change has happened in over a century. Because of this, the IANA time zone database gives Creston its own zone, America/Creston, and software that lumps all of BC into Pacific time will get Creston's local time wrong.

title: Ghana (Gold Coast) uses 20-minute and 30-minute DST offsets
body: British colonial administration of the Gold Coast (Ghana)
whatChanged: Africa/Accra applied DST of +0:20 (not the usual +1:00) from 1919 through 1941. From 1942 Feb 8 the territory moved to a fixed +0:30 with no DST, holding it until 1946 Jan 6. DST then returned for 1950 through 1955, but at +0:30. Both +0:20 and +0:30 are highly non-standard DST magnitudes. After 1956 the zone settled permanently at GMT.
technicalImpact: Zone arithmetic assuming DST is always +1:00 or +0:30 produces wrong results for pre-1957 Africa/Accra. The tzdb SAVE field for Rule Ghana is 0:20 and 0:30, not the usual 1:00. Systems reconstructing historical UTC times in Accra before 1957 must read the Ghana rules in the backzone file.
headline: The 20-minute summer clock
blurb: Colonial Ghana shifted its clocks by just 20 minutes for summer, one of the smallest DST offsets ever recorded.
story: Most countries move their clocks forward by a full hour for summer. Colonial Ghana moved them by only 20 minutes, an almost negligible nudge. Later rules pushed the base offset to 30 minutes, and DST stayed at non-standard fractional sizes until Ghana settled on plain GMT from 1956. Software that reconstructs historical local times in Accra before 1957 must account for these odd fractions or it will be minutes off.

title: Moscow abandons Local Mean Time for Soviet UTC-based zones (1919)
body: Soviet Council of People's Commissars (USSR)
whatChanged: Moscow's pre-1916 LMT was 2:30:17, recorded as MMT (Moscow Mean Time) until 1916-07-03 when it shifted to 2:31:19 after Pulkovo Observatory synchronization. Soviet decrees in 1919 established UTC-based time zones across Soviet territory, placing Moscow at UTC+3 from 1919-07-01. Moscow ran MSK/MSD around UTC+3 through the 1920s, then dropped to UTC+2 (EET) on 1922-10-01, before the Soviet Decree Time policy returned it to UTC+3 on 1930-06-21. Later deviations: 1991-03-31 to 1992-01-19 (UTC+2), 2011-03-27 to 2014-10-26 (UTC+4), then back to UTC+3.
technicalImpact: Code assuming MSK always equals UTC+3 will produce wrong results for 1922-1930 (UTC+2 standard) and for 2011-2014 (UTC+4). The transition from fractional LMT seconds (2:30:17) to whole-minute offsets is a common edge case in historical timestamp conversion.
headline: Moscow's clock, by decree
blurb: The Soviet government scrapped Moscow's astronomically-derived local time in 1919 and assigned it a clean UTC+3 offset by decree.
story: Before 1916, Moscow's official time was set by the Moscow Observatory and ran at 2 hours 30 minutes and 17 seconds ahead of UTC, an odd fractional offset inherited from the stars. After a Pulkovo Observatory resync it ticked up slightly to 2 hours 31 minutes and 19 seconds. The Soviet government ended all of that in 1919, establishing clean UTC-based time zones and landing Moscow at UTC+3 from 1 July 1919. The offset has shifted several times since, dropping to UTC+2 in 1922 then returning to UTC+3 in 1930 via the Soviet Decree Time policy, and swinging up to UTC+4 between 2011 and 2014, which means software treating MSK as a fixed UTC+3 will silently produce wrong timestamps for stretches of the 20th and 21st centuries.

title: Soviet Russia adopts the international time-zone system, placing Moscow at UTC+3
body: Council of People's Commissars of Soviet Russia
whatChanged: A decree of 8 February 1919 ordered Russia to adopt the international belt time-zone system, effective 1 July 1919. Moscow shifted from its local mean time (UTC+2:30) to the zone-2 belt offset recorded as UTC+2:31:19; its UTC+3 base offset came only in October 1921. Other cities followed on the same or nearby dates: Samara moved from LMT UTC+3:20 to UTC+3, Yekaterinburg from LMT UTC+4:02 to Perm provincial time UTC+3:45:05 (on 15 July 1919), reaching UTC+4 only in 1930. This established the zone structure that underpins Europe/Moscow, Europe/Samara, Asia/Yekaterinburg, and all subsequent Russian tz entries.
technicalImpact: The 1919 transition is the earliest named entry in Europe/Moscow and several other Russian zones in the IANA tz database. Software reconstructing historical timestamps before 1919-07-01 for Russian locations must handle fractional-minute LMT offsets (e.g. UTC+2:30:17 for Moscow, UTC+3:20:20 for Samara) that predate the decree.
headline: Russia's first time zones
blurb: Soviet Russia replaced each city's own local sun time with a single decree that sliced the country into standard international time-zone belts.
story: Before 1919, Russian cities each kept their own mean solar time: Moscow ran at roughly UTC+2:31, Samara at UTC+3:20, and so on. A decree signed in February 1919 ordered the whole country to abandon those fractional offsets and align to the international zone-belt system, taking effect on 1 July 1919. Moscow moved to the zone-2 belt offset (UTC+2 base, recorded in the tz database as 2:31:19), and only adopted a clean UTC+3 base, the offset it still uses as Moscow Standard Time, from October 1921. For software, those pre-1919 entries mean any timestamp reconstruction for Russian locations before that date must handle odd fractional-minute offsets that no modern clock would ever display.

title: Burma standardises on UTC+06:30 (Myanmar Standard Time)
body: The British colonial administration of Burma rounded Rangoon's local mean time to a standard half-hour offset around 1920. The offset survived Japanese wartime occupation and independence, and remains in use today as Myanmar Standard Time under the zone name Asia/Yangon.
whatChanged: Burma transitioned from Rangoon Mean Time (about UTC+6:24:47) to a rounded standard offset of UTC+06:30 in 1920. During Japanese occupation the zone shifted to UTC+09:00 (from 1942 May until 1945 May 2), then reverted to UTC+06:30. No DST has ever been observed. The zone is now named Asia/Yangon in the IANA database.
technicalImpact: UTC+06:30 is one of only a handful of active 30-minute offsets worldwide. Code that assumes whole-hour offsets, or uses integer-hour arithmetic on UTC offsets, will miscompute times in Myanmar. Libraries must carry explicit half-hour handling for this zone.
headline: Burma's half-hour clock
blurb: Burma standardised its clocks on a 30-minute offset in 1920, a quirk that has outlasted colonialism and occupation.
story: Before 1920, clocks in Rangoon ran on local mean time tied to the city's own meridian, about 6 hours 24 minutes and 47 seconds ahead of Greenwich. British administrators rounded this to the neater UTC+06:30, and that offset stuck. Japan forced the country onto Tokyo time during the war, but Burma reverted to +06:30 once the occupation ended in 1945. The half-hour offset still trips up software that treats UTC offsets as whole numbers.

title: World's fleets adopt the nautical standard time zone system
body: Anglo-French Conference on Time-keeping at Sea (1917); adopted by naval and merchant fleets 1920-1925
whatChanged: The Anglo-French Conference on Time-keeping at Sea (1917) recommended 24 hourly nautical time zones of exactly 15 degrees longitude with no political deviation, plus a nautical date line on the 180th meridian. All major naval fleets adopted the system between 1920 and 1925, replacing continuously adjusted local solar time kept aboard ships. Around 1950 letter designators were added, assigning Z (Zulu) to the Greenwich zone and A through Y to the remaining zones.
technicalImpact: The nautical system is the origin of 'Zulu time' (Z = UTC) used in ISO 8601, RFC 3339, and virtually every timestamp format developers encounter. The letter Z suffix in datetime strings (e.g. 2024-01-01T00:00:00Z) descends directly from this zone designator. The system also defines the 'pure' mathematical zone model (offset = round(longitude / 15)) that contrasts with messy civil zones, and explains why UTC is the universal anchor in computing.
headline: The origin of the Z in your timestamps
blurb: The world's navies agreed on a clean 24-zone time system at sea, and its Zulu designator for UTC lives in every ISO timestamp developers write today.
story: Before the 1920s, ships kept their own local solar time and adjusted it continuously as they sailed. A 1917 Anglo-French conference proposed dividing the world's oceans into 24 neat zones, each exactly 15 degrees of longitude wide, with no political exceptions. All major navies had adopted it by 1925. Around 1950, letter codes were added, with Z assigned to the zero (Greenwich) zone, later vocalized as 'Zulu' under the ICAO/NATO phonetic alphabet (standardized 1956). That Zulu designation is why timestamps in ISO 8601, RFC 3339, and countless APIs end in a literal Z to mean UTC.

title: El Paso defied federal law and observed Mountain Time for nearly 50 years
body: City of El Paso, Texas (de facto); U.S. Congress (statutory resolution 1970)
whatChanged: On 1921-03-04 federal law placed all of Texas into the Central time zone. El Paso ignored this for nearly 50 years, continuing to observe Mountain Time. On 1970-04-10 Congress amended the law to formally recognise Mountain Time for El Paso, matching what the city had been doing all along.
technicalImpact: The tzdb encodes observed practice rather than statutory text, so America/Denver covers El Paso without a separate zone entry. Illustrates that legal time designations and actual clock practice can diverge for decades, and that tz databases must track real-world usage, not just legislation.
headline: The city that ignored federal time law for 50 years
blurb: El Paso kept its clocks on Mountain Time for nearly five decades after a federal law said it had to follow Central Time.
story: In 1921 the U.S. government put all of Texas into the Central time zone, but El Paso simply refused to comply, arguing it had always used Mountain Time and saw no reason to change. The city ran its clocks an hour behind what the law required for close to 50 years. Congress eventually gave up and rewrote the law in 1970 to match what El Paso was already doing. The episode shows that legal time zones and real observed time can diverge for generations, and is why tz databases record what clocks actually showed rather than what statutes said.

title: UK summer time tied to Easter, a rule the tz database cannot express
body: Parliament of the United Kingdom (Summer Time Act 1922)
whatChanged: From 1922 until World War II, UK law set summer time to begin the day after the third Saturday in April, except when that day was Easter, in which case clocks moved forward the previous Sunday instead. The legal rule was stable across those years, but its Easter dependency cannot be encoded in the tz database rule syntax, so the years are stored as individual or small-range Rule lines in the GB-Eire zone data rather than a single perpetual rule.
technicalImpact: The tz database has no syntax for conditional Easter-based offsets. Each exceptional year carries its own explicit Rule line in the europe file rather than a single repeating rule. Cited in the tz theory document as a canonical example of the gap between a legally stable rule and what a computable rule format can represent, directly relevant to any DST generator or historical time calculation for the UK.
headline: The Easter clause no tz rule can hold
blurb: For nearly two decades, UK law said clocks spring forward in April unless Easter gets in the way, a condition the tz database format simply cannot encode.
story: In 1922 Britain set summer time to start the day after the third Saturday in April. There was one catch: if that day was Easter, the clocks moved a week earlier instead. The rule itself never changed, but the tz database has no way to write 'unless Easter' in its rule syntax. So each exceptional year needs its own hand-crafted entry, even though the law was the same. It is one of the clearest examples of why a legally stable time rule and a machine-readable time rule are not the same thing.

title: Moscow Time reduced from UTC+03:00 to UTC+02:00
body: Soviet government of the Russian SFSR
whatChanged: In October 1922, Moscow's civil time was set back from UTC+03:00 to UTC+02:00 (Eastern European Time). This followed the 1919 Soviet decree that first carved the country into administrative time zones; the offset had fluctuated with summer and winter adjustments between 1919 and 1922. Moscow held UTC+02:00 until 21 June 1930, when the 'Decree Time' order permanently restored UTC+03:00.
technicalImpact: Europe/Moscow in the tz database carries this October 1922 transition. Any software computing durations or rendering historical Moscow timestamps across this boundary must account for the offset change; treating Moscow as a fixed UTC+03:00 (its post-1930 value) silently misplaces any 1922-1930 timestamp by one hour.
headline: Moscow's missing hour
blurb: After years of post-revolutionary timekeeping changes, Moscow clocks were set back one hour in October 1922, where they stayed until 1930.
story: When the Soviet government reorganised the country into administrative time zones in 1919, Moscow's offset shifted several times over the following years as summer and winter adjustments were added and removed. In October 1922 the clocks were wound back to UTC+02:00, two hours ahead of Greenwich. Moscow held that position for almost eight years. In 1930 a sweeping 'Decree Time' order moved the whole country forward by one hour and Moscow returned to UTC+03:00, where it has largely remained since. Software handling historical Russian timestamps must carry this transition or it will misplace any time between 1922 and 1930 by a full hour.

title: Uruguay uses a 30-minute DST offset (1923-1942)
body: Government of Uruguay (Ley No. 7594, 1923-06-28)
whatChanged: Ley No. 7594 of 1923-06-28 set Uruguay's standard time at UTC-3:30 and its summer time at UTC-3, a shift of only 30 minutes. This half-hour DST ran from 1923 until 31 March 1926, when Uruguay suspended seasonal changes and fixed the clock year-round at UTC-3:30. The half-hour summer time was reinstated on 28 October 1933 and continued through 1942. Decreto 1866 (effective 1942-12-14) shifted the base offset from UTC-3:30 to UTC-3:00; the 30-minute summer increment continued, so summer time ran at UTC-2:30 until the season ended on 1943-03-14.
technicalImpact: Software assuming DST is always a 1-hour shift will misplace Uruguayan summer timestamps in 1923-1926 and 1933-1942 by 30 minutes. One of only a handful of countries ever to use a half-hour DST increment; handled correctly in the IANA tz database (southamerica, Uruguay Rule lines for 1923-1926 and 1933-1942).
headline: Uruguay's half-hour summer
blurb: For nearly two decades, Uruguay advanced its clocks by only 30 minutes each summer instead of the standard one hour.
story: When Uruguay codified its timekeeping in 1923, it set standard time at UTC-3:30 and summer time at UTC-3, a gap of just half an hour. After a few seasons the country abandoned the experiment: on 31 March 1926 it fixed its clocks year-round at UTC-3:30 with no seasonal change, and only reinstated the half-hour summer time in October 1933, keeping it until a wartime fuel-saving decree in late 1942 shifted the base offset (summer then running at UTC-2:30). Any software that hard-codes a one-hour DST assumption will silently produce wrong timestamps for Uruguayan summer periods in this era.

title: Uruguay's century of non-standard DST: 30-minute shifts, a 90-minute oil-crisis jump, and drought-triggered clock changes
body: Government of Uruguay (various decrees of the Ministry of National Defense and the executive branch)
whatChanged: Uruguay applied DST in 30-minute increments from 1923-1926 and 1933-1942 (not the standard 1 hour). On 1974-01-13, Decreto 29/974 advanced clocks 90 minutes (UTC-3 to UTC-1:30) in response to the international oil crisis; Decreto 163/974 on 1974-03-10 returned 60 minutes (to UTC-2:30); Decreto 679/974 on 1974-09-01 removed the remaining 30 minutes. Drought and energy emergencies triggered additional mid-year DST advances in 1941 (August), 1968 (May), and 1970 (April). Many transitions occurred at 24:00 rather than the conventional 02:00. Uruguay abandoned DST entirely from 2015 after the Chamber of Tourism (Camara Uruguaya de Turismo) argued the seasonal time change harmed the tourism sector.
technicalImpact: Generic 1-hour DST assumptions produce wrong results across Uruguayan history. The 90-minute advance of January 1974 and the recurring 30-minute increments (used as late as 1959 and 1968, not only before 1942) require per-year rule entries in the tz database. Transitions at 24:00 (midnight end-of-day) rather than 02:00 can break parsers that assume standard POSIX transition times. The tzdb southamerica file encodes dozens of individual Rule entries to capture Uruguay's history correctly.
headline: The country that could not pick a DST offset
blurb: Uruguay used 30-minute, 60-minute, and once 90-minute clock shifts across a century of daylight saving, with drought and oil shocks triggering changes at random points in the calendar year.
story: Most countries that observe daylight saving simply move clocks one hour forward in spring and one hour back in autumn. Uruguay ignored that convention for most of its history. In the 1920s and 1930s it used 30-minute increments instead of a full hour. In January 1974, facing an oil crisis, the government pushed clocks forward by 90 minutes in one go, then unwound the change in two separate steps over the following eight months. Droughts also prompted sudden mid-year shifts in 1941, 1968, and 1970, with no seasonal pattern. The whole arrangement finally ended in 2015 after the Chamber of Tourism (Camara Uruguaya de Turismo) argued that the seasonal clock change was hurting the country's tourism sector. For software, Uruguay is a stress test: its history requires dozens of individual rules in the tz database and breaks any code that assumes DST always means one hour at a predictable time of year.

title: BBC begins broadcasting the Greenwich Time Signal ('the pips')
body: Royal Observatory Greenwich and the BBC
whatChanged: On 5 February 1924, the Royal Observatory Greenwich and the BBC began broadcasting the six-pip Greenwich Time Signal. A Dent astronomical regulator clock ('Dent 2016'), fitted with electrical contacts and run under the control of a mean-time pendulum, fed a one-second pulse to the BBC transmitters. Five short pips preceded a longer sixth pip that marked the exact second. For the first time, an authoritative national time standard reached the public by radio, replacing reliance on time balls and the railway telegraph network.
technicalImpact: Established the model of a single authoritative clock broadcasting a precise reference signal that downstream receivers synchronize to, the same pattern later used by radio time codes (MSF, WWV, DCF77) and ultimately NTP. Introduced the one-second-tick mark that persists in all modern time synchronization protocols. Timezone databases carry no release for this event, as it predates digital timekeeping, but it anchors the conceptual origin of machine time distribution.
headline: The six pips heard round Britain
blurb: The BBC started broadcasting six short radio beeps every hour so anyone with a wireless set could set their clock to the exact second.
story: Before 1924, most people in Britain set their clocks by the one-o'clock time ball at Greenwich or by the railway telegraph, neither of which reached into private homes. On 5 February 1924 the BBC and the Royal Observatory solved that with six short radio pips, the last one marking the precise second. The signal was driven by two extremely accurate pendulum clocks at Greenwich and went out live across the country. The idea proved so robust it still plays on BBC Radio 4 over a century later. That pattern, one authoritative clock broadcasting a reference tick that everything else synchronizes to, is exactly what NTP and modern time servers do today.

title: GMT astronomical day restarted at midnight, ending the noon-epoch convention
body: adopted by the national almanac offices for astronomical use; terminology later standardised by the International Astronomical Union (Universal Time, 1928)
whatChanged: Effective 1 January 1925, astronomers switched from counting Greenwich Mean Time from noon (the classical astronomical day, inherited from Ptolemy) to counting from midnight, matching civil timekeeping. The instant recorded as 'December 31.5 GMT' in 1924 almanacs became 'January 1.0 GMT' in 1925 almanacs, a deliberate 12-hour discontinuity. The old noon-epoch convention came to be called Greenwich Mean Astronomical Time (GMAT) to distinguish it from the new midnight-based convention.
technicalImpact: Any code or dataset parsing pre-1925 astronomical timestamps must treat 'GMT' as a noon-epoch scale offset by 12 hours from civil midnight. Mixing pre-1925 almanac dates with post-1925 civil dates without the GMAT correction silently misplaces events by half a day, a classic epoch-convention trap for historical astronomy data pipelines.
headline: The day GMT moved its midnight
blurb: For centuries astronomers started each day at noon; in 1925 they switched to midnight, creating a hidden 12-hour trap in any historical time data.
story: Astronomers once began each new day at noon, so that a whole night of observations would share one date in their logbooks. Civil life had always started the day at midnight, and by the early twentieth century the mismatch was a growing nuisance. On 1 January 1925 the astronomical almanacs switched to midnight, bringing their calendars in line with the rest of the world. To avoid confusion, the old noon-based reckoning came to be called Greenwich Mean Astronomical Time, while the IAU settled the naming of the new scale as Universal Time in 1928. Any software reading old almanac timestamps without knowing about this switch will misplace historical events by exactly twelve hours.

title: The pre-1970 Local Mean Time trap: 'strange' dates in Shanghai 1927 and wartime India
body: IANA tz database (historical Local Mean Time entries); surfaced by developers
whatChanged: Before standard zones, places kept Local Mean Time (LMT), an offset set by longitude down to the odd second, and the tz database does not promise accuracy before 1970. The most famous example: a Stack Overflow question showed that subtracting two 1927 Shanghai timestamps returned a result about 5 minutes 52 seconds off, from the LMT-to-standard step that tzdata then encoded at the end of 1927. tzdb has since revised Asia/Shanghai to use LMT +8:05:43 only until 1901, citing unreliable historical data, so current versions no longer reproduce that exact figure. India's Asia/Kolkata adopted IST (+5:30) in 1906 and shifted to wartime +6:30 from 1 September 1942 until 15 October 1945.
technicalImpact: Date math across these transitions returns results that look broken. Java, pytz and others faithfully apply the historical LMT offsets, so code that assumes whole-minute historical offsets, or that pre-1970 dates are reliable at all, gets surprised. That tzdb itself later changed the Shanghai data underlines its own warning: do not trust civil time before 1970.

reverify.note: newly added from Yasin's links; famous, well-documented, editorial prose new

sidenotes.label: The Java case: India showing +06:30 for wartime years

headline: The dates that look broken but aren't
blurb: Subtract two 1927 Shanghai timestamps and you get an answer nearly six minutes off, because old clocks ran on local mean time.
story: Long ago, towns set their clocks by the sun, so each had a slightly odd time measured to the second. The time database keeps these old settings but warns they are not reliable before 1970. Shanghai's old clock was about six minutes off a round number and snapped back at the end of 1927. Programs that do math across that moment return strange answers, which has puzzled developers for years.
reviewNote: Corrected per current IANA asia file: Shanghai LMT-to-+08:00 is 1901 in current tzdb (the 1927 figure is older Shanks and Pottenger data the famous SO question relied on); India IST adopted 1906 and the wartime +6:30 ended 15 Oct 1945.

academicRefs.authors: Lear, E. and Eggert, P.
academicRefs.title: Procedures for Maintaining the Time Zone Database (RFC 6557)
academicRefs.venue: IETF / RFC Editor (Internet Engineering Task Force, Informational RFC)
academicRefs.keyFinding: The RFC, co-authored by the tz database maintainer Paul Eggert, states verbatim: 'In order to correct historical inaccuracies, a new TZ name MAY be added when it is necessary to indicate what was the consensus view at a given time and location. Such TZ names are usually not added when the inaccuracy was prior to 1970.' It also instructs the coordinator to 'use judgment and whatever available sources exist to assess what the average person on [the] street would think the time actually is' and to reflect 'the consensus on the ground,' rather than to set authoritative policy.

academicRefs.authors: Ishibashi, Y.
academicRefs.title: Constructing the 'automatic' Greenwich time system: George Biddell Airy and the telegraphic distribution of time, c.1852-1880
academicRefs.venue: The British Journal for the History of Science (Cambridge University Press), vol. 53, no. 1, pp. 25-46
academicRefs.keyFinding: Using Royal Observatory archival material, the paper shows the early standardized Greenwich time system was 'unstable,' with 'clear limits in terms of its scale of automation and degree of accuracy' and persistent dependence on manual intervention, even as Airy worked to make Greenwich the single authoritative time reference for Britain's railways and telegraph network.

academicRefs.authors: Pilkington, J. D. H.
academicRefs.title: Developments of Time Systems Since 1884
academicRefs.venue: The Journal of Navigation (Cambridge University Press), vol. 38, no. 2, pp. 207-208
academicRefs.keyFinding: Surveys a century of time-system change, noting that despite shifts in timekeeping science most nations aligned to within one second of the Greenwich/Washington-Conference framework, and that longitude-dependent time zones spread via railway expansion with legislation typically following established local practice rather than preceding it.

title: IAU adopts 'Universal Time' to disambiguate Greenwich Mean Time
body: International Astronomical Union (IAU)
whatChanged: GMT was ambiguous because the astronomical day historically began at noon while civil GMT began at midnight. In 1925 astronomers shifted the astronomical day to a midnight start, and in 1928 the IAU introduced 'Universal Time (UT)' as the unambiguous term for mean solar time at Greenwich with the day starting at midnight.
technicalImpact: Removed a 12-hour ambiguity baked into 'GMT', which could denote either a noon-starting astronomical day or a midnight-starting civil day. The 1928 name fixed this in astronomical publications. The separate, later move to UTC (1972) is why standards, RFCs, and tz documentation now prefer 'UTC' and treat 'GMT' as a legacy alias rather than a precise specification.

validation.supportingQuote: In 1928 the International Astronomical Union changed the designation of the standard time of the Greenwich meridian to Universal Time. ... In 1925 GMT was adopted by astronomers so that the astronomical day began at midnight, the same time as the civil day.

headline: A clearer name for Greenwich time
blurb: Astronomers introduced Universal Time to settle confusion about when the day begins.
story: Greenwich Mean Time was confusing because astronomers and everyday people once started the day at different hours. Astronomers had already moved their day to begin at midnight. In 1928 they introduced the name Universal Time to mean Greenwich time with a clear midnight start. Software today still favors this clearer naming over the older term.

academicRefs.authors: Nelson, R. A.; McCarthy, D. D.; Malys, S.; Levine, J.; Guinot, B.; Fliegel, H. F.; Beard, R. L.; Bartholomew, T. R.
academicRefs.title: The leap second: its history and possible future
academicRefs.venue: Metrologia
academicRefs.keyFinding: Reviews the historical evolution of time measurement and defines Universal Time (UT1) as 'the measure of astronomical time defined by the rotation of the Earth on its axis with respect to the Sun... nominally equivalent to mean solar time referred to the meridian of Greenwich and reckoned from midnight.' Establishes that UT is the direct successor of Greenwich Mean Time and that the modern conventions (UTC kept within 0.9 s of UT1) inherit from this Greenwich mean-solar-time lineage; over the past 1000 years Earth's rotation has slowed ~1.4 ms/century, the physical reason astronomical UT and atomic scales diverge.

academicRefs.authors: Seago, J. H.; Seidelmann, P. K.
academicRefs.title: The Mean-Solar-Time Origin of Universal Time and UTC
academicRefs.venue: Advances in the Astronautical Sciences (AAS/AIAA Astrodynamics Specialist Conference), paper AAS 13-486
academicRefs.keyFinding: States that 'Universal Time is the measure of Earth rotation that serves as the astronomical basis of civil timekeeping. Since the end of the 19th century, Universal Time has been maintained to preserve continuity with Newcomb's mean solar time at Greenwich.' Quotes the 1884 International Meridian Conference resolution that the universal day 'is to be a mean solar day... to begin for all the world at the moment of midnight of the initial meridian' and gives Newcomb's mean-sun right-ascension formula (epoch '1900 Jan 0, Greenwich Mean noon', i.e. 31 Dec 1899 12h UT). Simulations affirm UT has separated from Newcomb's mean sun by ~(1/365)ΔT, about 0.2 s presently, well within the +/-0.9 s UT1-UTC tolerance.

academicRefs.authors: Seago, J. H.; Seidelmann, P. K.
academicRefs.title: Mean Solar Time and Its Connection to Universal Time
academicRefs.venue: The Science of Time 2016 (Astrophysics and Space Science Proceedings, vol. 50), Springer
academicRefs.keyFinding: Per the published abstract, Universal Time, 'as the successor to Greenwich Mean Time (GMT), was intended to maintain uniform time as the angle between zero longitude and the mean sun.' Variability in Earth's rotation has caused UT to diverge from the original geometric mean-solar-time concept by ~(1/365.2422)ΔT; after accounting for reference-system origin changes since the end of the 19th century, simulated transits confirm the Sun on average now crosses zero longitude at 12h 00m 00.2s Universal Time.

title: New Zealand observes 30-minute daylight saving (1928 to 1940)
body: Government of New Zealand
whatChanged: New Zealand advanced its clocks by 30 minutes (SAVE 0:30) each summer from 1928 through 1940, against a base offset of UTC+11:30 (New Zealand Mean Time), reaching UTC+12:00 in summer. From 1941 wartime regulations held this shift year-round, and the standard offset itself was formalised at UTC+12:00 on 1 January 1946 by the Standard Time Act 1945. Seasonal one-hour DST only returned in the 1970s.
technicalImpact: Code that assumes DST is always exactly one hour will compute wrong offsets for New Zealand timestamps between 1928 and 1940. Combined with the 1946 base-offset change from UTC+11:30 to UTC+12:00, New Zealand has had several distinct UTC offsets historically, making pre-war NZ date arithmetic a common edge-case failure.
headline: New Zealand's half-hour summer
blurb: For over a decade, New Zealand advanced its clocks by only 30 minutes each summer instead of the full hour used today.
story: Before World War II, New Zealand ran on UTC+11:30 and chose to save energy in summer by shifting clocks just half an hour forward, reaching UTC+12:00 each summer. This ran each year from 1928 to 1940, spanning two separate rule periods in the tz database. From 1941, wartime emergency regulations held the half-hour shift year-round, freezing the country at UTC+12:00, and the Standard Time Act 1945 then codified UTC+12 as the permanent standard offset from 1 January 1946. Because summers had already reached UTC+12 since 1928, the 1946 change left summer timestamps unchanged and only affected winter. Seasonal one-hour DST did not arrive until the 1970s. Software that assumes DST is always a round hour will quietly miscalculate any New Zealand timestamp from this era.

title: Soviet Union introduces permanent 'decree time': all clocks advanced +1h, never rolled back for 61 years
body: Council of People's Commissars (Sovnarkom) of the USSR
whatChanged: A Sovnarkom decree dated 16 June 1930 shifted all clocks forward one hour at 00:00 on 21 June 1930 throughout the USSR. The intended autumn reversion never occurred, so every Soviet standard time zone ran one hour ahead of its geographic base offset (decree time, dekretnoye vremya) continuously until 31 March 1991. When DST was introduced in 1981, summer clocks ran two hours ahead. The change affected all Soviet republics spanning UTC+2 through UTC+12.
technicalImpact: Historical Soviet timestamps between 1930-06-21 and 1991-03-31 are offset by +1h relative to pre-decree zone baselines. The IANA tz database encodes these shifts per zone (e.g. Europe/Moscow reflects the Moscow offset moving from UTC+2 to UTC+3 in 1930 and back in 1991). Any application doing local-time arithmetic over Soviet historical records must load tz data that includes decree time, or it will be off by one hour for the entire 61-year period.
headline: The USSR's clock that never fell back
blurb: The Soviet Union pushed its clocks forward one hour in 1930 and simply never pushed them back, leaving the entire country on permanent daylight saving time for six decades.
story: On 21 June 1930, the Soviet government advanced every clock in the country by one hour. The plan was to restore them in autumn, but autumn came and the clocks stayed put. The stated reason was saving electricity during dark winter evenings. The arrangement, known as decree time (dekretnoye vremya), held for 61 years across 11 time zones. It was only unwound in March 1991 as the Soviet Union collapsed. Software and historians both have to account for this gap: any system computing historical Soviet local times between 1930 and 1991 must apply the extra hour, or every local timestamp is wrong by 60 minutes.

title: Bolivia's one-and-only DST season (1931-1932)
body: Government of Bolivia
whatChanged: Bolivia used Calamarca Mean Time (CMT, UTC-4:32:36) until 1931-10-15, then entered its only ever DST period (+1h applied to CMT) until 1932-03-21, after which it settled permanently at UTC-4. No DST has been observed since.
technicalImpact: Any Bolivia timestamp between 1931-10-15 and 1932-03-21 requires a +1h adjustment on top of the base CMT offset. Software using only the modern UTC-4 rule will misplace those historical times by one hour. The tzdb entry in southamerica (America/La_Paz) encodes all three phases.
headline: Bolivia's one summer that never came back
blurb: Bolivia tried daylight saving time exactly once, for a single season in 1931-1932, and never touched its clocks again.
story: Before 1931 Bolivia ran its clocks at a quirky offset based on the local mean time of Calamarca, a small town near La Paz, formally adopted as Calamarca Mean Time in 1890. In October 1931 the country pushed clocks forward by an hour for summer. By March 1932 the experiment was over and Bolivia landed on the clean UTC-4 it uses today. That lone DST window is a trap for any software that assumes Bolivia has always been a simple, unchanging UTC-4 zone.

title: British North Borneo observes 20-minute seasonal DST (1935-1941)
body: British North Borneo Chartered Company (administering present-day Sabah) and the Raj of Sarawak under the Brooke dynasty, now part of Malaysia
whatChanged: British North Borneo (the territories now forming Sabah and Sarawak, Malaysia) applied a seasonal DST offset of exactly 20 minutes each year from 14 September to 14 December, 1935 through 1941. Standard time was UTC+08:00; during the DST window clocks advanced to UTC+08:20. The NBorneo rule in the IANA tz database encodes this as two transitions per year: SAVE 0:20 on 14 Sep at 00:00, and SAVE 0 on 14 Dec at 00:00.
technicalImpact: A DST offset of 20 minutes is essentially unique in recorded timekeeping history. Any code that assumes DST shifts are multiples of 30 or 60 minutes will silently compute wrong UTC equivalents for local timestamps in this window. The Asia/Kuching zone in the IANA tz database carries the NBorneo rule, so compliant tz libraries handle it correctly, but hand-rolled offset arithmetic or incomplete zone data will not.
headline: The 20-minute DST nobody expected
blurb: For seven years, clocks in British North Borneo sprang forward by just 20 minutes each September and fell back each December.
story: Most countries that use daylight saving time shift their clocks by a full hour. The territories of northern Borneo now forming Sabah and Sarawak, Malaysia, did something almost no one else ever has: they added only 20 minutes. Every year from 1935 to 1941, on 14 September, clocks ticked forward those 20 minutes. On 14 December they fell back again. The practice ended when Japan occupied the region in early 1942 and imposed its own time. Software that assumes DST offsets come in neat 30- or 60-minute increments will silently produce wrong timestamps for any date inside those seasonal windows.

title: Singapore and North Borneo use a 20-minute DST offset (UTC+7:20)
body: British colonial administration (Straits Settlements and British North Borneo)
whatChanged: From 1933 Jan 1, Singapore advanced its clocks 20 minutes to an effective offset of UTC+7:20, introduced under the Daylight Saving Ordinance 1932; on 1936 Jan 1 this was made permanent standard time, dropping the DST designation, and it held until 1941 Sep 1 when the offset moved to UTC+7:30. North Borneo applied a separate seasonal +0:20 each year from Sep 14 to Dec 14, 1935 through 1941. Both are recorded in the IANA tzdb Asia/Singapore zone and the NBorneo rule.
technicalImpact: A 20-minute DST offset is extremely rare. Any code that assumes DST adjustments are always 30 or 60 minutes will silently miscalculate timestamps for Singapore between 1936 and 1941 and for North Borneo between 1935 and 1941.
headline: The 20-minute summer
blurb: Colonial Singapore and North Borneo once observed daylight saving time in 20-minute increments, one of the strangest DST offsets ever recorded.
story: Under British colonial rule, Singapore advanced its clocks by just 20 minutes on 1 January 1933 rather than the usual 30 or 60, giving an effective offset of UTC+7:20, a number that fits no tidy pattern. Introduced under the Daylight Saving Ordinance 1932, the shift was permanent and year-round, and from 1936 it was formally adopted as standard time, dropping the daylight-saving label entirely. North Borneo ran a genuinely seasonal version of the same 20-minute experiment each year from 1935 to 1941, advancing clocks from 14 September to 14 December. Both practices ended with the Japanese occupation in 1942. The episode is a reminder that DST offsets have never been as tidy as programmers tend to assume.

title: Newfoundland keeps a UTC-3:30 standard offset (only half-hour zone in the Americas)
body: Newfoundland (Commission of Government); not yet part of Canada
whatChanged: Newfoundland uses a standard offset of UTC-3:30 (Newfoundland Standard Time), based on the mean solar time of 52°30' W, a legacy of its status as a separate Dominion. IANA America/St_Johns shows the offset moving to a clean -3:30 in 1935 (earlier entries carry the LMT-derived -3:30:52). Summer time is UTC-2:30.
technicalImpact: Defeats any assumption that North American offsets are whole hours. The DST transition lands at a :30 boundary, so naive 'spring forward at 2am to 3am' logic misfires. A long-standing test case in datetime libraries.

validation.supportingQuote: Zone America/St_Johns	-3:30:52 -	LMT	1884 ... -3:30:52 StJohns N%sT	1935 Mar 30 / -3:30	StJohns	N%sT	1942 May 11

headline: Newfoundland keeps its half-hour clock
blurb: Newfoundland uses a time that sits on a half-hour, unlike the rest of the Americas.
story: Newfoundland was once a separate dominion and set its own clock to match its location. The result is a time offset that falls on the half-hour rather than a whole hour. In 1935 it settled on this tidy half-hour setting. Because its clock changes land on the half-hour, it often breaks software that assumes clean whole-hour jumps.

title: Chicago runs on Eastern Standard Time for eight months in 1936
body: City of Chicago (ordinance)
whatChanged: America/Chicago temporarily shifted from UTC-6 (Central) to UTC-5 (Eastern Standard Time) from 1936-03-01 02:00 to 1936-11-15 02:00, then reverted to Central time. No DST adjustment applied during this window; the zone ran on fixed EST.
technicalImpact: Any code assuming America/Chicago is always UTC-6 standard / UTC-5 daylight breaks for this eight-month window. Historical timestamp arithmetic, scheduling lookups, or calendar conversions touching 1936 Chicago dates must rely on tz database data rather than hard-coded offsets.
headline: Chicago's eight-month Eastern detour
blurb: Chicago quietly ran on Eastern Standard Time for most of 1936, a politically driven anomaly baked into the tz database.
story: In late 1935, Chicago's city council voted to shift the city's clocks one hour east, onto Eastern Standard Time effective March 1, 1936, intended as a permanent move to align radio broadcast scheduling with the East. The switch lasted from March 1 to November 15, after which the city abandoned the experiment and returned to Central time. It left no lasting policy, but it left a permanent mark in the tz database. Software treating America/Chicago as a fixed Central offset will silently produce wrong timestamps for any 1936 date in that eight-month range.

title: Kenya's brief UTC+2:45 offset (1936-1942)
body: Colony and Protectorate of Kenya (British colonial administration)
whatChanged: The Alteration of Time Ordinance 1936 moved Kenya from UTC+2:30 to UTC+2:45 at midnight on 31 December 1936, based on the 41 deg 15 min E meridian. The Defence (Amendment of Laws No. 120) Regulations then moved it to UTC+3 at midnight on 31 July 1942 to align with wartime military operations. UTC+3 was made permanent by an amendment to the Interpretation and General Clauses Ordinance in 1950.
technicalImpact: UTC+2:45 is a rare 45-minute offset; any code that normalises offsets to 30-minute increments will silently corrupt pre-1942 Nairobi timestamps. The offset appears in Africa/Nairobi in tzdb for the 1936 to 1942 window.
headline: Nairobi's forgotten 45-minute offset
blurb: Kenya ran on an unusual 45-minute-past-the-hour time zone for about five and a half years during the colonial era before wartime pressure pushed it to a round UTC+3.
story: In 1936 the British colonial government set Kenya's clocks to UTC+2:45, pegged to a reference meridian of 41 deg 15 min E (the solar meridian corresponding to UTC+2:45, not Nairobi's actual longitude of ~36.8 deg E). The offset lasted only until 1942, when wartime coordination with Allied forces required a shift to the cleaner UTC+3. Kenya has stayed at UTC+3 ever since, but those five-plus years linger in the tzdb as a 45-minute offset window that can trip up software expecting neat half-hour boundaries.

title: Netherlands replaces Amsterdam Mean Time with a clean UTC+00:20 offset (Dutch Time)
body: Government of the Netherlands
whatChanged: On 1 July 1937 the Netherlands replaced Amsterdam Mean Time (UTC+0:19:32.13, a law-codified LMT-derived offset in use since 1909) with a rounded offset of exactly UTC+00:20, known as Dutch Time (Nederlandse Tijd). The IANA Europe/Amsterdam zone encodes the transition as: '0:19:32 Neth %s 1937 Jul 1' followed by '0:20 Neth +0020/+0120 1940 May 16'. The Netherlands remained on this unusual 20-minute offset until German occupation pushed it to CET (UTC+1) in May 1940.
technicalImpact: A non-hour, non-half-hour offset (20 minutes) that lived in production tz data; software that assumes whole- or half-hour zone boundaries mishandles historical timestamps in this window. The adjacent 1940 transition is a documented source of tz regression bugs (e.g. the tz 2022c regression in Amazon Corretto).
headline: The 20-minute nation
blurb: For three years the Netherlands kept national time at exactly 20 minutes ahead of Greenwich, one of the oddest standard offsets ever codified into law.
story: The Netherlands legally adopted uniform Amsterdam Mean Time in 1909, a quirky offset of 19 minutes and 32 seconds derived from the longitude of Amsterdam. In 1937 the government rounded it up to a clean 20 minutes, calling it Dutch Time. The offset vanished in 1940 when Germany occupied the country and imposed Central European Time. Those three years of 20-minute time still sit in the tz database and can trip up any software that assumes time zones fall on neat hour or half-hour boundaries.

title: Taiwan forced onto Tokyo time (JST) under Japanese wartime unification
body: The Imperial Japanese government abolished its separate Western Standard Time zone in 1937 and moved Taiwan, along with the rest of the empire's western territories, onto Japan Standard Time. Taiwan stayed an hour ahead of its prewar offset for the duration of the Pacific War, reverting only after Japan's surrender handed the island to the Republic of China.
whatChanged: Taiwan had used UTC+08 (Western Standard Time) since 1896. On 1937-10-01 Japan abolished its western zone and shifted Taiwan to JST (UTC+09). After the war, Taiwan reverted to UTC+08 on 1945-09-21 at 01:00 local time, with clocks set back one hour, so 01:00 JST became 00:00 CST.
technicalImpact: Historical Taiwanese records from 1937-10-01 to 1945-09-21 are in JST (UTC+09), not CST. The reversion at 01:00 rather than midnight is an unusual transition time that can trip up naive date-time parsing. The Asia/Taipei zone encodes both transitions; code assuming Taiwan was always UTC+08 will misread wartime timestamps by one hour.
headline: Taiwan's wartime hour
blurb: Japan forced Taiwan onto Tokyo time in 1937, shifting the island one hour forward for eight years.
story: When Japan unified its empire's clocks in 1937, Taiwan lost its own standard time and was moved to JST, one hour ahead of where it had been. The island stayed on Japanese time through the entire Pacific War. When Japan surrendered and Taiwan passed to the Republic of China in September 1945, clocks were wound back an hour at the unusual time of 01:00 rather than midnight. Software handling old Taiwanese records has to account for this wartime offset or risk misreading historical timestamps by a full hour.

title: Republic of China adopts five standard time zones at the 1939 Chongqing Standard Time Conference
body: Ministry of the Interior, Republic of China
whatChanged: On 9 March 1939 the Ministry of the Interior convened a Standard Time Conference in Chongqing and formally adopted the five-zone scheme (Kunlun UTC+5:30, Sinkiang-Tibet UTC+6, Kansu-Szechwan UTC+7, Chungyuan UTC+8, Changpai UTC+8:30) with revised borders, effective 1 June 1939. At the same time, the entire country was directed to use Kansu-Szechwan Time (UTC+7) for the duration of the Second Sino-Japanese War, immediately overriding the multi-zone plan.
technicalImpact: The patchwork of local times observed before 1939, the short-lived five-zone decree, and the wartime UTC+7 override all feed into the pre-1949 history encoded in IANA zones for China (Asia/Shanghai and others). Software handling historical Chinese timestamps must account for these overlapping regimes.
headline: Five zones, one war, one clock
blurb: China formally adopted five time zones in 1939, then immediately set the whole country to a single wartime offset.
story: By the 1930s China's inner regions had never properly taken up the five-zone plan first proposed in 1918, leaving local times in disarray. In March 1939 the government held a conference in Chongqing to fix this, agreeing on five zones with updated borders starting June 1939. The Second Sino-Japanese War was already raging, so the same meeting also ordered every region onto a single wartime clock (UTC+7), making the five-zone decision almost immediately moot. The resulting tangle of overlapping regimes is baked into IANA time zone data for China, and any software handling pre-1949 Chinese timestamps has to navigate it.

title: Shanghai wartime DST chaos under three competing authorities (1940-1945)
body: Shanghai International Settlement, Shanghai French Concession, Wang Jingwei puppet regime, and (from 1942) the Japanese occupation government
whatChanged: Between 1940 and 1945, three separate authorities in Shanghai each set their own DST rules, sometimes refusing to follow each other. In 1940 the spring-forward was Jun 1 and fall-back Oct 12. In 1941 the fall-back was delayed from Sep 30 to Nov 1 after the International Settlement and French Concession authorities disagreed over the date. From Jan 31, 1942, after Japan took full control, DST was applied year-round until Sep 1, 1945. The tzdb Rule Shang lines (1940-1949) encode each transition separately.
technicalImpact: Historical Shanghai timestamps from 1940-1945 are ambiguous by up to one hour depending on which authority a record followed. The tzdb picks one interpretation (the dominant one for each transition), so any surviving records from the French Concession, the Settlement, or unofficial business practice may disagree with computed local times.
headline: Three clocks in one city
blurb: For five years during World War II, Shanghai ran under three competing authorities, each setting its own clocks.
story: During the Japanese occupation of Shanghai, the city was split between the International Settlement, the French Concession, and a Japanese-backed puppet government, each with its own idea of what time it was. In 1941 the two settlement authorities could not agree on the autumn fall-back, pushing it from September 30 to November 1. By 1942 Japan had absorbed the settlements and kept daylight saving time running year-round until 1945. Any software reading Shanghai records from these years may be off by up to an hour depending on which authority issued the original timestamp.

title: Spain shifts from GMT to CET (UTC+0 to UTC+1) under Franco
body: Government of Spain (Francisco Franco)
whatChanged: On 1940-03-16 at 23:00 local time, Spain advanced clocks one hour from GMT (UTC+0) to Central European Time (UTC+1) to align with Nazi Germany. The change was framed as temporary but was never reversed. Mainland Spain (Europe/Madrid) has remained on UTC+1 standard time ever since, while the Canary Islands (Atlantic/Canary) settled on UTC+0.
technicalImpact: Europe/Madrid carries a permanent base-offset transition at 1940-03-16 23:00 from +00:00 to +01:00 in the tz database. Spain sits geographically near the GMT meridian (same longitude as Lisbon and London), so its UTC+1 standard offset means solar noon falls around 13:30 to 14:00, the largest solar-clock mismatch in Western Europe. Code that derives local time from geographic longitude will produce wrong results for Spain.
headline: Franco's permanent one-hour gift to Berlin
blurb: Spain moved its clocks one hour forward in 1940 to match Nazi Germany, and never moved them back.
story: In March 1940, Franco's government advanced Spain's clocks by one hour to synchronise with Germany and occupied France. The change was sold as a wartime convenience, but it outlasted the war by decades and was quietly made permanent. Today Spain shares a time zone with Germany and Poland despite sitting on the same longitude as Britain and Portugal. Software that estimates local time from geographic coordinates will be off by a full hour for the Iberian Peninsula.

title: Netherlands abandons Amsterdam Time (UTC+0:19:32) for Berlin Time under German occupation
body: German occupying authorities (Reich)
whatChanged: The Netherlands had used Amsterdam Mean Time (UTC+0:19:32.13), simplified to Dutch Time (UTC+0:20) on 1937-07-01. In May 1940, German occupation authorities imposed Berlin Time (UTC+1:00), a jump of roughly 40 minutes. The Netherlands has remained on UTC+1 (CET) ever since. In tzdb, Europe/Amsterdam is a backward-compatibility Link to Europe/Brussels; the full historical Zone Europe/Amsterdam (the 0:19:32 LMT origin, the 1937 Dutch Time offset, and the 1940 May 16 jump to UTC+1) lives in the tzdb backzone file.
technicalImpact: Europe/Amsterdam's historical sub-minute offset (0:19:32.13) and abrupt 40-minute 1940 jump break any assumption that pre-1970 offsets are whole or half hours. Code parsing old Dutch timestamps against this zone can silently produce wrong results.
headline: The occupation that reset Dutch clocks
blurb: Germany's 1940 invasion forced the Netherlands to abandon its quirky 20-minutes-past-the-hour local time and adopt Central European Time, where it has stayed ever since.
story: The Netherlands kept its own local solar time long after most of Europe standardized, running 19 minutes and 32 seconds ahead of UTC. In 1937 the government rounded this to a tidy 20 minutes past the hour, creating Dutch Time. When Germany invaded on 10 May 1940, the occupation authorities imposed Berlin Time days later, jumping Dutch clocks forward by about 40 minutes in one go. That UTC+1 setting never changed after the war, and the old odd offset survives only as a historical curiosity inside the tzdb Europe/Amsterdam zone, where it can still trip up software parsing pre-1940 Dutch timestamps.

title: Britain runs UTC+2 summers during World War II (Double British Summer Time)
body: UK Government (Home Office), via Statutory Rules and Orders 1941 No. 476, 1942 No. 506, 1944 No. 932, 1945 No. 312
whatChanged: Britain added a second hour of daylight saving each summer from 1941 to 1945, putting clocks at UTC+2 (BDST) in summer and UTC+1 (BST) in winter rather than returning to GMT. The name 'Double British Summer Time' was settled informally in April 1941 after the BBC asked the Home Office. A further peacetime BDST occurred in 1947 (Summer Time Act 1947, April 13 to August 10), making UTC+2 briefly post-war standard again.
technicalImpact: UK timestamps during WW II summers (1941-1945) are UTC+2, not the usual BST UTC+1. Code or queries that assume BST always equals +1 will be wrong by one hour for those summers. The Rule GB-Eire entries in the IANA tzdb europe file encode each statutory transition date precisely.
headline: Britain's wartime double-summer clock
blurb: To save fuel and maximise daylight during World War II, Britain moved its clocks two hours ahead of GMT each summer from 1941 to 1945.
story: Wartime Britain needed every hour of usable daylight to support factory output and reduce blackout risk. Parliament passed a series of statutory orders pushing summer clocks to UTC+2, two full hours past Greenwich, while keeping UTC+1 through the winter instead of reverting to GMT. The nickname 'Double British Summer Time' was coined informally in 1941 after the BBC pressed the Home Office for an official term. Even after the war, 1947 saw a brief peacetime repeat under the Summer Time Act. Historical software queries covering those summers must use UTC+2, not the UTC+1 most code assumes for BST.

title: Britain operates British Double Summer Time (UTC+2) during World War II, 1941-1945
body: Government of the United Kingdom (wartime statutory instruments S.R.&O. 1941 No. 476, 1942 No. 506, 1944 No. 932, 1945 Nos. 312 and 1208)
whatChanged: From 25 February 1940, Britain skipped the autumn clock-back and stayed on BST (UTC+1) through winter. From 4 May 1941, clocks went forward a further hour each spring to British Double Summer Time (BDST, UTC+2), then dropped back to BST (UTC+1) each autumn, so Britain was never on GMT during this period. BDST ran each year from around early May to mid-August (exact dates set by statutory instrument). In 1945 the final BDST ended 15 July (back to BST), and BST ended 7 October (back to GMT). A one-off recurrence of BDST also ran from 13 April to 10 August 1947.
technicalImpact: Europe/London in the IANA tzdb encodes stacked UTC+2 offsets for 1941-1945 and a 1947 repeat via the GB-Eire rule set. Code that assumes the UK is always GMT or GMT+1, or that DST adds at most one hour, will miscompute historical UK timestamps in this window. The 1945 two-step fall-back (BDST to BST in July, then BST to GMT in October) is a rare double-unwind edge case for naive offset conversion.
headline: Britain's wartime double clocks
blurb: To save fuel and extend daylight working hours during World War II, Britain pushed its clocks two hours ahead of GMT in summer, a stacking of offsets it used every year from 1941 to 1945.
story: During World War II the UK government decided that more daylight in the evenings would help the war effort, so it stopped putting clocks back at the end of summer 1940 and kept Britain on BST all winter. Then each spring from 1941 to 1945 it pushed clocks forward another hour, creating British Double Summer Time at UTC+2. At the end of each summer clocks fell back one hour to BST, and at the end of the war they fell back a second time to GMT. The arrangement briefly returned in 1947 during a fuel crisis. Any software handling historical UK timestamps from this era needs to account for the two-hour offset and the unusual pair of autumn clock-changes in 1945.

title: Bahrain standardizes competing offsets to UTC+3:30 (1941), then shifts to UTC+4 (1944)
body: Reginald George Alban, British Political Agent, Manama; BOAC (British Overseas Airways Corporation)
whatChanged: Before 1941-07-20, Bahrain had no standard time: British India steamers, Petroleum Concessions, and Cable & Wireless used +0330, while Eastern Bank, BOAC, and Bapco used +0323, and California Arabian Standard Oil (Casoc) observed DST from 1941-05-24. Alban brokered a consensus and the government adopted +0330 on 1941-07-20. BOAC then lobbied for later sunset, and Bahrain switched to +0400 on 1944-01-01.
technicalImpact: Pre-1941 Bahrain timestamps are essentially unresolvable: two competing offsets (+0323 and +0330) coexisted in the same city depending on employer. The Asia/Bahrain zone entry in the tzdb backzone file starts with LMT (3:22:20) before the 1941 standardization.
headline: Two clocks, one city
blurb: Before 1941, which offset you used in Bahrain depended on who you worked for.
story: In the early 1940s, Bahrain had no single official time. Your employer decided your clock: oil workers at Bapco ran seven minutes behind the steamship crews of the British India Company, and an American oil outfit observed daylight saving on top of that. A British political agent finally brokered a deal in July 1941, and everyone moved to UTC+3:30. Three years later, an airline complained about losing evening daylight and the whole territory jumped to UTC+4. Any software trying to timestamp events in pre-1941 Bahrain faces two plausible offsets with no way to choose between them.

title: Hong Kong's wartime half-hour fall-back creates UTC+8:30 'Winter Time'
body: Hong Kong colonial government (British crown colony)
whatChanged: On June 15, 1941, Hong Kong advanced clocks one hour to HKST (UTC+9) as a wartime measure. On October 1, 1941, clocks fell back only 30 minutes to 'Hong Kong Winter Time' (HKWT, UTC+8:30) rather than the full hour, keeping daylight saving partly in effect. Japan invaded on December 25, 1941, and imposed JST (UTC+9). After liberation, Hong Kong returned to HKT (UTC+8) on November 18, 1945. The abbreviation HKWT is tzdb-invented and has no official documentary source.
technicalImpact: The HKWT abbreviation is not attested in any official source, only in tzdb. Software parsing Hong Kong timestamps from October 1 to December 25, 1941 will encounter this invented abbreviation and a non-standard half-hour offset (UTC+8:30). The unusual 30-minute DST fall-back step breaks assumptions of whole-hour DST transitions.
headline: The half-hour fall-back that never quite ended summer
blurb: In 1941, wartime Hong Kong wound its clocks back only half an hour in autumn, leaving the colony in a permanent semi-summer time until Japan invaded on Christmas Day.
story: When war loomed in 1941, Hong Kong pushed its clocks forward one hour in June to save daylight. When autumn came, instead of undoing the full change, authorities took only half of it back, landing on UTC+8:30 and keeping some daylight saving in place. That arrangement lasted just 12 weeks before Japan occupied the city on Christmas Day and reset everything to Tokyo time. Software dealing with old Hong Kong timestamps will hit a half-hour offset and an abbreviation, HKWT, that the tz database authors coined themselves because no official name was ever recorded.

title: India's only DST: wartime clocks advanced to UTC+6:30 (1942-1945)
body: British India (Government of India under the British Crown)
whatChanged: India advanced clocks one hour from IST (UTC+5:30) to UTC+6:30 for two wartime windows: October 1941 to 15 May 1942, then again from September 1942 until 15 October 1945 (with a brief return to IST in between). It is the only well-documented period in which India observed daylight saving time.
technicalImpact: Software that assumes Asia/Kolkata is always UTC+5:30 will compute wrong timestamps for dates in these two windows. The tzdb Asia/Kolkata zone carries the correct entries; any system using a historical offset shortcut rather than the full zone data will be off by one hour for the affected periods.
headline: India's only wartime clock shift
blurb: India advanced its clocks by one hour during World War II, the only well-documented period in its history in which it has observed daylight saving time.
story: As a British territory during World War II, India pushed clocks forward one hour to save fuel and align with the war effort. The advance ran in two windows: October 1941 to 15 May 1942, and again from September 1942 until 15 October 1945, with a brief reversion to standard IST in between. No DST has been observed in India since 1945. Any software that hardcodes UTC+5:30 for all of Indian history will silently mis-time events from those wartime months.

title: Vietnam's seven offset changes across war and decolonization (1942-1975)
body: Power in Vietnam changed hands repeatedly through the war and decolonization, and each authority reset the clock. The Japanese military command pushed the offset to UTC+9 in 1945; Ho Chi Minh's Democratic Republic of Vietnam pulled it back to UTC+7; French Indochina set UTC+8 in 1947; South Vietnam under Ngo Dinh Diem shifted it twice more; and reunification settled the country on UTC+7 in 1975. The IANA Asia/Ho_Chi_Minh zone encodes the full sequence.
whatChanged: Indochina and South Vietnam cycled through seven offset changes between three offsets (+07, +08, +09): to +08 on 1942-12-31 at 23:00; to +09 on 1945-03-14 at 23:00 (Japanese decree); back to +07 on 1945-09-02 (Vietnam); to +08 on 1947-04-01 (French-controlled Indochina); back to +07 on 1955-07-01 (South Vietnam); to +08 on 1959-12-31 at 23:00 (South Vietnam); and to +07 on 1975-06-13 (unification). Several transitions occurred at 23:00 wall-clock time rather than midnight.
technicalImpact: Seven offset changes in 33 years, with several transitions landing at 23:00 rather than midnight, make historical Vietnamese timestamps ambiguous. The Asia/Ho_Chi_Minh zone encodes all of them, but code that uses local time for records in this period without knowing which authority was in control can silently land one to two hours off.
headline: Vietnam's seven clocks in thirty-three years
blurb: Vietnam changed its UTC offset seven times between 1942 and 1975 as colonial rulers, Japanese occupiers, and successive governments each reset the clock.
story: During the war and its aftermath, whoever held power in Vietnam also set the time. Japan pushed clocks to UTC+9 in 1945; Ho Chi Minh moved them back after independence was declared; the French, then South Vietnamese president Diem, each shifted them again. By the 1975 reunification, the country had been wound forward and back seven times across three offsets. Software handling historical Vietnamese timestamps has to know which government was in control on a given date, or risk errors of one to two hours.

title: WWII year-round 'War Time' daylight saving
body: US federal government under President Franklin D. Roosevelt; enacted by Congress (Public Law 77-403)
whatChanged: Instituted year-round daylight saving time nationwide as a wartime energy-conservation measure, in effect continuously from 1942-02-09 to 1945-09-30. Zones were renamed Eastern/Central/Mountain/Pacific War Time (EWT/CWT/MWT/PWT), each one hour ahead of standard time. After Japan's surrender the abbreviations changed to Peace Time (EPT/CPT/MPT/PPT) on 1945-08-14 while the +1 offset held, before clocks returned to standard time on 1945-09-30.
