This was encoded in IANA tz as Europe/London holding a fixed 1:00 standard offset with no DST rule across those years. On 31 October 1971 at 02:00 UTC, the UK reverted to the standard GMT/BST cycle, switching back to GMT for winter.
technicalImpact: Any historical date computation for UK timestamps between October 1968 and October 1971 must account for the fixed UTC+1 offset with no autumn fallback. The IANA tzdb europe file encodes this as a distinct zone segment (1:00 BST 1971 Oct 31 2:00u) before resuming the GB-Eire rule set. Libraries or applications that backfill DST rules naively for Europe/London will produce wrong local times for this three-year window.
headline: Three winters without a clock change
blurb: For three years the UK skipped its autumn clock-back and stayed one hour ahead of GMT all year round.
story: In 1968 the UK government decided to try keeping the country on summer time permanently. The idea was to give people more daylight in the evenings and help business align with Europe. For three years, from October 1968 to October 1971, British clocks never fell back in autumn. Scotland bore the brunt: winter sunrises crept past 9am in Edinburgh and past 10am further north. Public opposition grew and Parliament voted by 366 to 81 to end the trial. Software must handle this gap carefully, because any historical UK timestamp in those three years sits at a fixed UTC+1 with no DST transition, unlike every surrounding year.

title: Argentina's informal double summer time era (1969-1974)
body: Government of Argentina
whatChanged: On 1969-10-05 Argentina shifted its base offset from UTC-4 to UTC-3, making what had been its summer offset the new standard (this UTC-3 base held in tzdb until 1999). Historical sources and the tzdb southamerica file label the 1969-1974 era 'double summer time'. A brief additional 1-hour DST was applied in 1974 (Jan 23 to May 1), pushing clocks to UTC-2 during that window, after which the base returned to UTC-3.
technicalImpact: Systems computing Argentina local time between 1969 and 1974 must use UTC-3 as the base, not UTC-4, with an extra +1h applied only in the 1974 Jan 23 to May 1 window. The tzdb southamerica file encodes this as the Zone line switching STDOFF from -4:00 to -3:00 on 1969-10-05, with the Arg rules covering the short 1974 DST. A common error is applying only a 1-hour DST on top of -4:00, yielding the wrong offset.
headline: Argentina's permanent summer
blurb: Argentina locked its clocks at summer time in 1969 and kept them there for years, leaving software that assumes a simple one-hour DST with the wrong answer.
story: In October 1969, Argentina stopped switching clocks back each autumn and simply stayed two hours ahead of its old standard time, at UTC-3. Contemporary sources called this 'double summer time' because it was twice the usual one-hour offset. The arrangement settled the country onto UTC-3, with only one brief extra hour added in early 1974. Any code that applies a flat one-hour DST rule to Argentina in this era will silently produce wrong timestamps.

title: POSIX-style perpetual zones (EST, MST, HST, etc.) codified as ahistorical in tzdb backzone
body: IANA Time Zone Database maintainers (Paul Eggert et al.)
whatChanged: The backzone file in the IANA tzdb defines standalone Zone entries for HST (-10:00), PST8PDT, MST (-7:00), MST7MDT, CST6CDT, EST (-5:00), EST5EDT, WET, CET, MET, and EET. These entries pretend that standard time extends backward indefinitely, which is factually wrong for most zones. In current tzdb the same names are implemented as links to geographic zones, but backzone provides the ahistorical standalone Zone form and explicitly labels them as such.
technicalImpact: Any program that sets TZ=EST or TZ=MST gets a perpetual fixed-offset zone with no DST transitions, silently producing wrong local times whenever DST applies. This is one of the most common timezone bugs in software. The backzone comment makes the ahistorical nature explicit, but the dangerous names remain in the database for backward compatibility with POSIX-era systems.
headline: The zones that pretend history never happened
blurb: Short timezone abbreviations like EST and MST in tzdb are permanently fixed offsets with no daylight saving history, quietly giving wrong times to any software that uses them.
story: When POSIX standardized its TZ string format, it allowed short fixed-offset specifications with no daylight-saving rules. The IANA timezone database adopted matching short names like EST and MST as standalone zones for POSIX compatibility, but they describe a fictional world where clocks never changed. A developer who sets TZ=EST instead of America/New_York gets a zone frozen at UTC-5 all year, including in summer when New York is actually at UTC-4. The tzdb backzone file calls these zones out by name as ahistorical, but they remain available. The result is one of the most widespread and silent timezone bugs in production software.

title: The POSIX Epoch as the tzdb accuracy boundary
body: IANA tz database maintainers (currently Paul Eggert and the tz community)
whatChanged: The tz database defines 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC (the POSIX Epoch) as its primary accuracy boundary. Timezones are grouped so that all clocks within a zone agree on every timestamp from that moment forward. Pre-1970 transitions are recorded where known, but the database explicitly treats that data as incomplete and unreliable, drawn largely from astrology books and uncited sources that frequently contradict each other.
technicalImpact: Any application computing historical timestamps before 1970 must treat tzdb data as best-effort only. LMT (Local Mean Time) offsets used as pre-standard-time fallbacks are especially unreliable. Software that consumes tzdb through its language or platform libraries inherits this limitation without warning, meaning silent wrong results for pre-1970 date arithmetic.
headline: Where the clock history runs out
blurb: The world's main timezone database treats 1970 as the dividing line between reliable and unreliable time data.
story: When engineers built the tz database, they drew a line between time data they could organize reliably and time data they could not. They settled on midnight on 1 January 1970, the same zero point Unix computers use, a cutoff the maintainers themselves describe as somewhat arbitrary. From that moment forward, all clocks within a timezone are made to agree; before it, local timekeeping around the world was messy and often unrecorded, so the database collects what it can but flags it as guesswork. Most of the pre-1970 entries come from astrology books whose authors invented data when real records were missing. Any software that silently trusts tz data for dates before 1970 may be working with figures that are simply wrong.

title: tzdb draws the line at 1970: calendrical chaos kept out of scope
body: IANA tz database maintainers (Paul Eggert and contributors)
whatChanged: The tzdb Theory file describes 1970 (the POSIX Epoch, 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC) as a 'somewhat-arbitrary cutoff,' noting significant challenges to moving it earlier due to the wide variety of local practices before computer timekeeping became prevalent. The companion calendars file catalogs that messiness (Julian-to-Gregorian switches at differing dates, the French Revolutionary calendar, Soviet 5- and 6-day work weeks) as evidence for why extending the database further into the past is impractical. Pre-1970 transitions are included selectively for continuity, not comprehensively.
technicalImpact: Software using tzdb for pre-1970 historical timestamps gets best-effort data only. Calendar reform differences (Julian-to-Gregorian switches, revolutionary calendars, Soviet work-week variants) are not modeled, making sub-day accuracy impossible for many historical locales before 1970.
headline: Where tzdb stops trusting itself
blurb: The tz database officially treats everything before 1970 as best-effort, because calendar history is too messy to model reliably.
story: The Unix epoch (January 1, 1970) is not just a technical convenience. It marks the edge of what tzdb's maintainers trust. The tzdb Theory file calls 1970 a 'somewhat-arbitrary cutoff' and warns there are significant challenges to moving it earlier, even by a decade or two, because of the wide variety of local practices before computer timekeeping became prevalent. The companion calendars file makes that messiness concrete: before 1970, countries switched between Julian and Gregorian calendars at wildly different times, France briefly used a revolutionary calendar, and the Soviet Union experimented with five- and six-day work weeks. Although calendrical issues are formally out of scope for tzdb, they show exactly why pushing the database further into the past is impractical. Developers relying on tzdb for pre-1970 timestamps should treat the results as approximate, not authoritative.

title: tzdb pre-1970 data sourced largely from uncited astrology books
body: IANA tz database maintainers (tz community)
whatChanged: The theory.html file of the IANA tz database documents that most pre-1970 timezone entries come from unreliable sources, often astrology books that lack citations, whose compilers evidently invented entries when the true facts were unknown without reporting which entries were known versus invented. These books often contradict each other and are typically found incorrect when checked against primary sources.
technicalImpact: Pre-1970 tzdb offsets cannot be trusted for legal, genealogical, or compliance work. Applications doing historical timestamp arithmetic before 1970 must consult national archives rather than relying on tzdb data, which may silently contain invented values.
headline: The invented timezone entries
blurb: The timezone database's pre-1970 historical data comes largely from astrology books whose authors quietly made up entries when the real facts were unknown.
story: When the tz database was assembled, reliable historical records of local time before 1970 were scarce. Compilers turned to astrology books, which were among the few sources that listed historical offsets in bulk. Those books contained invented entries mixed with real ones, with no indication of which was which. Software that performs calculations on timestamps before 1970, such as genealogy tools or legal date reckoning, can produce silently wrong results because the underlying data was fabricated.

title: Unix epoch defined: 00:00:00 UTC, 1 January 1970
body: Bell Labs Unix engineers (Ken Thompson, Dennis Ritchie and colleagues)
whatChanged: Bell Labs engineers chose 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC as the origin instant for Unix time (time_t): a signed 32-bit integer counting elapsed seconds from that point. The choice was informal and pragmatic, picking a recent convenient date. It was later codified in POSIX and adopted by C's time(), virtually every operating system, filesystem, and relational database.
technicalImpact: The epoch anchors timestamps across POSIX, C, Java, Python, JavaScript (as milliseconds), SQL, and most filesystems. A signed 32-bit time_t overflows at 2038-01-19 03:14:07 UTC (the Year 2038 problem), a direct consequence of this epoch choice. Systems still on 32-bit time_t require migration to 64-bit representations to survive past that overflow.
headline: The second everything counts from
blurb: Unix engineers picked midnight on 1 January 1970 as time zero, and nearly every computer clock still counts from that moment.
story: When Unix was being built at Bell Labs in the late 1960s, engineers needed a fixed instant to count seconds from. Early versions actually used 1971 and then 1972 as epochs and counted in 1/60-second ticks, which overflowed a 32-bit counter in about two and a half years; the team rewound the origin to midnight at the start of 1970 and switched to counting whole seconds, a recent round date that fit neatly into a 32-bit integer. That choice spread through POSIX, C, Java, Python, JavaScript, and nearly every database and filesystem in use today. The catch is that a 32-bit counter starting there runs out in January 2038, a deadline that still drives migration work in embedded and legacy systems.

title: International Atomic Time (TAI) formally established by the CGPM
body: 14th General Conference on Weights and Measures (CGPM) / BIH, later BIPM
whatChanged: The CGPM formally recognized International Atomic Time (TAI) and requested the CIPM to provide its definition (the technical definition had been given by the CCDS in 1970), a continuous atomic timescale built from cooperating cesium clocks, with origin set so the atomic scale agreed with UT2 at 1 January 1958 00:00:00 (UT2-TAI approximately 0 at that epoch by convention). TAI ticks the SI second uniformly with no leap-second adjustments.
technicalImpact: TAI is the monotonic, gap-free timescale that leap-second-aware systems use internally (tz 'right'/zoneinfo-leaps counts every TAI second). The fixed TAI-UTC integer offset is the quantity that changes on every leap second; GNSS and distributed databases effectively track TAI rather than UTC.

validation.supportingQuote: Ultimate consecration came from the official recognition by the 14th CGPM in 1971, which introduced the designation 'International Atomic Time' and the universal acronym TAI.

headline: A steady atomic clock for the world
blurb: Officials formally created a continuous timescale built from atomic clocks that never skips.
story: In 1971 the world formally recognized International Atomic Time. It is built from many atomic clocks working together and ticks at a perfectly steady rate. Unlike everyday time, it never pauses or adds a second to stay in step with the Earth. Many precise systems rely on this smooth, gap-free timescale behind the scenes.

academicRefs.authors: Guinot, B. and Arias, E. F.
academicRefs.title: Atomic time-keeping from 1955 to the present
academicRefs.venue: Metrologia (IOP Publishing / BIPM)
academicRefs.keyFinding: States plainly: 'In 1971 the TA(BIH) was renamed TAI'; the BIH had set the origin of its scale by coincidence with UT2 on 1 January 1958, 0h UT2; and the 14th CGPM in 1971 gave official recognition introducing the designation International Atomic Time and the acronym TAI for the BIH atomic time since 1955. Also records that the relative frequency of TAI, found ~1e-12 too high, was corrected by exactly -10.0e-13 on 1 January 1977, after which TAI is steered by primary frequency standards.

academicRefs.authors: Bureau International des Poids et Mesures (BIPM Time Department)
academicRefs.title: Establishment of International Atomic Time and Coordinated Universal Time (BIPM Annual Report on Time Activities, section)
academicRefs.venue: BIPM (technical report)
academicRefs.keyFinding: Describes the operational realization: TAI/UTC are computed monthly from ~450 atomic clocks in 80+ centres; an iterative ALGOS algorithm forms the free scale EAL (weighted average, optimized for long-term stability, NOT constrained to the SI second); TAI is then derived from EAL by adding a linear time function ('steering of TAI') against primary/secondary caesium and optical frequency standards so the TAI scale interval matches the SI second.

title: Ireland models winter (GMT) as negative DST atop a UTC+1 'standard time'
body: Oireachtas (Standard Time Act 1968 / Standard Time (Amendment) Act 1971), encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: Irish law defines standard time as UTC+1 year-round; the 1971 amendment keeps UTC+1 as the legal standard and designates GMT as 'winter time'. IANA Europe/Dublin is modeled with a standard offset of +1:00 (abbrev IST) and a negative SAVE of -1:00 in winter (abbrev GMT), the opposite sign convention from every other DST-observing zone.
technicalImpact: The only mainstream zone with negative DST. Code assuming the DST flag means 'clocks are ahead' or that SAVE >= 0 reports Ireland's seasons backwards. When tzdb encoded this with negative SAVE in 2018 (releases 2018a/2018b), it broke ICU and OpenJDK, which did not support negative SAVE; the project reverted in 2018c and from 2018d shipped parallel 'vanguard' and 'rearguard' data formats.

validation.supportingQuote: "Ireland's Standard Time (Amendment) Act, 1971 states that standard time in Ireland remains at UT +01." ... "In January 2018 we discovered that the negative SAVE values in the Eire rules cause problems with tests for ICU" ... IST = Irish Standard Time after 1968, with standard time in summer and negative daylight saving time in winter.

headline: Ireland flips daylight saving backward
blurb: Ireland treats winter as the time it pulls clocks back from, the reverse of how others do it.
story: Most places treat summer as the season they push clocks forward. Irish law instead defines its main time as the summer setting and treats winter as the time pulled back from it. The result is the same clock readings as its neighbors, but described in reverse. This backwards way of recording it has caused real problems in time software.

title: First Edition Unix counts time in 1/60s from 1971; 2.26-year overflow forces redesign to seconds-since-1970
body: Ken Thompson and Dennis Ritchie / Bell Labs
whatChanged: The Unix Programmer's Manual (First Edition, 3 Nov 1971) defined system time as the number of sixtieths of a second since 00:00:00 on 1 Jan 1971, stored in a 32-bit counter. The BUGS section of the time(II) manual page noted that 2^32 sixtieths of a second is only about 2.26 years, guaranteeing an overflow by late 1973. The epoch was patched more than once (the Third Edition (February 1973) shifted the origin to 1 Jan 1972 but kept 1/60s units). The design was ultimately replaced by counting whole seconds from 00:00:00 on 1 Jan 1970, producing the modern Unix epoch that all POSIX systems use today.
technicalImpact: The redesign established the Unix epoch (1970-01-01T00:00:00Z) that every C library, POSIX system, and language runtime still uses. The 32-bit signed second counter it left behind later became the source of the Year 2038 problem. Systems that had already hard-coded 1971 or 1972 origins required data migration when the epoch changed.
headline: The clock that ran out in two years
blurb: The original Unix timekeeping design would have overflowed in about two years, so engineers swapped in a new epoch and unit that became the foundation every computer clock still uses.
story: When Unix was first documented in November 1971, time was kept as a count of 1/60-second ticks since the start of that year. The manual's own bug list warned that a 32-bit counter at that rate would wrap around in roughly 2.26 years. Engineers patched the epoch at least twice before settling on a cleaner design: count whole seconds from the first moment of 1970. That choice, made to fix an embarrassingly short overflow window, became the Unix epoch that every operating system, programming language, and database timestamp inherits today. The original flaw is gone, but the 32-bit counter it left behind is still ticking toward 2038.

title: UTC's final fractional step: end of the rubber-second era
body: CCIR Study Group 7 (International Radio Consultative Committee), implementing Recommendation 460
whatChanged: On 1971-12-31, UTC took a final irregular fractional step of exactly 0.107758 TAI seconds. At the same time, UTC's tick rate was changed to match the SI second exactly (ending the pre-1972 practice of variable-rate 'rubber seconds'), and UTC began tracking UT1 instead of UT2. The cumulative effect of all time steps and frequency offsets since 1958 was made to total exactly 10 seconds, so that 1972-01-01 00:00:00 UTC equaled 1972-01-01 00:00:10 TAI exactly. From that point on, only whole-second leap seconds would be used to keep UTC within 0.9 s of UT1.
technicalImpact: Any software that tries to compute precise UTC-TAI offsets or sub-second timestamps before 1972-01-01 faces an irregular, non-integer discontinuity at this boundary. The POSIX/tz 'right' zoneinfo files record leap seconds only from 1972 onward; the 1971 fractional step is not encoded, making UTC arithmetic before that epoch unreliable without specialist libraries. Languages and runtimes that expose the TAI-UTC offset (via the leapseconds file) silently treat pre-1972 UTC as if the 10-second offset already applied, which can introduce sub-second errors for historical timestamps.
headline: The last rubber second
blurb: On the last day of 1971, UTC took a tiny fractional step to clean-slate its history and launch the modern leap-second system the next morning.
story: Before 1972, the keepers of UTC kept clocks accurate by adjusting the length of individual seconds, stretching or compressing them like rubber. By the end of 1971, all those small adjustments had added up to a messy total. To clean the slate, UTC took one last fractional step of just over a tenth of a second on 1971-12-31, making the gap between UTC and atomic time an exact, tidy 10 seconds. From 1972-01-01 onward, seconds became fixed-length and only whole leap seconds would be used. Software that handles timestamps before 1972 cannot rely on the leap-seconds file because this final fractional step is not recorded there, making precise UTC-to-TAI conversion in that era a hidden trap.

title: Leap seconds: March and September never used in 50+ years of operation
body: International Earth Rotation and Reference Systems Service (IERS), Earth Orientation Center, Paris Observatory
whatChanged: IERS policy formally allows leap second insertions at the end of December or June (first preference), and March or September (second preference). In practice, from the first insertion in June 1972 through the most recent in January 2017, all 27 insertions fell on June 30 or December 31. March and September have never been used.
technicalImpact: Systems and monitoring scripts that only check for leap second announcements in January and July (after IERS Bulletin C releases in those months) are correct by historical precedent but technically incomplete. A leap second scheduled for end of March or September would be legal under IERS rules and would silently break any monitor or parser that assumes only June and December endpoints. Note: CGPM Resolution 4 (November 2022), reinforced at WRC-23 (2023), set a path to increase the UT1-UTC tolerance by or before 2035, effectively suspending leap seconds for at least a century; none has been inserted since January 2017, so the March/September scenario is now largely moot in practice.
headline: The months nobody ever used for a leap second
blurb: Leap seconds can legally be added at the end of March or September, but none ever has been.
story: The rules say leap seconds may be inserted at the end of any of four months, December and June first, March and September as backup. In over 50 years, every single one landed in June or December. That track record has quietly trained software to check for announcements only twice a year. If IERS ever picks March or September, monitors that hardcode those two windows will miss it entirely.

title: All 27 leap seconds are positive: a negative one has never happened
body: International Earth Rotation and Reference Systems Service (IERS)
whatChanged: Between 1972 and 2016, 27 leap seconds were inserted into UTC, all positive (adding 23:59:60). The IERS leapseconds file documents the template for a negative leap second (removing 23:59:59) but notes it has never been used. Earth's rotation has been slowing, so only extra seconds have ever been required; a negative leap second would need Earth to speed up instead.
technicalImpact: Virtually all leap-second handling in operating systems, NTP stacks, and time libraries assumes a positive leap second. A negative leap second (where 23:59:58 jumps straight to 00:00:00, skipping 23:59:59) is essentially untested in production code and likely to cause errors or infinite loops in affected systems.
headline: The leap second that never went backwards
blurb: Every leap second ever added to world time has been positive, adding an extra tick, and none have ever been removed.
story: Since 1972 the world has added 27 extra seconds to the clock to keep atomic time in step with the spinning Earth. Each one added a second at 23:59:60, a moment that does not normally exist. The rules also allow a negative leap second, which would skip 23:59:59 entirely, but Earth's rotation never sped up enough for one to be needed. Almost no production software has ever handled the skip case, making a future negative leap second a known, untested hazard.

title: leap-seconds.list uses NTP epoch (1900), not POSIX epoch (1970)
body: IANA / IERS (International Earth Rotation and Reference Systems Service)
whatChanged: The leap-seconds.list file distributed with the tz database encodes all timestamps as seconds since 1900-01-01 (the NTP epoch), not since 1970-01-01 (the POSIX/Unix epoch). Column 1 is the NTP timestamp of each leap second insertion; column 2 is DTAI, the cumulative difference TAI minus UTC in seconds. The tz database also ships a separately formatted leapseconds file that uses zic Leap records in the form `Leap YEAR MON DAY HH:MM:SS +/- S` (for example `Leap 1972 Jun 30 23:59:60 + S`) instead.
technicalImpact: Parsing leap-seconds.list with POSIX epoch assumptions produces dates 70 years in the past. Correct parsing requires adding 2208988800 seconds to each NTP timestamp to convert to Unix time, or computing the date by counting seconds from 1900-01-01. This off-by-epoch mistake has appeared in NTP libraries, time-sync daemons, and custom leap-second parsers. Applications that independently parse the file rather than using a well-tested library are most at risk.
headline: The file that starts in 1900
blurb: The official leap-seconds file counts time from 1900, not 1970, so any code that reads it with Unix-time assumptions gets dates that are 70 years wrong.
story: Every time a leap second is added to UTC, the fact gets recorded in a file called leap-seconds.list, which is distributed as part of the tz database. The timestamps in that file are not Unix timestamps. They count seconds from January 1, 1900, following the NTP standard, not from the Unix epoch of January 1, 1970. The gap between those two starting points is exactly 2208988800 seconds. Code that forgets to add that constant will misplace every leap second by 70 years, silently producing wrong dates with no obvious error. The tz database ships a second file called leapseconds that avoids the problem by using zic Leap records dated by calendar fields (Leap YEAR MON DAY HH:MM:SS +/- S), but the machine-readable leap-seconds.list remains the more widely consumed format and the more common source of the bug.

title: POSIX cannot represent the leap second: the smearing divergence problem
body: POSIX standard (IEEE Std 1003.1) / ITU-R TF.460-6
whatChanged: POSIX defines time_t as a linear count of seconds since 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC with exactly 86400 seconds per day. The UTC time 23:59:60 introduced by every positive leap second since 1972 is simply not a valid POSIX second. The tzdb leapseconds file records each insertion as 'Leap YEAR MON DAY 23:59:60 + S', a real UTC moment POSIX cannot express. Vendors resolve the gap differently: Google has smeared the extra second since 2008, evolving from a 20-hour smear to today's 24-hour linear smear; AWS Time Sync Service does the same; Linux CLOCK_TAI and GPS time avoid the problem by counting continuously without adjustment.
technicalImpact: When two systems disagree on leap-second strategy (one smears, one steps, one ignores), their clocks diverge by up to one second during and after the leap. Financial systems, distributed databases (Cassandra, Kafka, Spanner), and log correlation across hosts have experienced real incidents. Code that subtracts two POSIX timestamps across a leap boundary silently under-counts elapsed time by one second. Prefer CLOCK_TAI or GPS-derived time for monotonic sub-second ordering in high-precision systems.
headline: The second POSIX forgot
blurb: POSIX time cannot represent the extra second that UTC inserts to track Earth's slowing rotation, so every system quietly handles it differently.
story: UTC occasionally adds a leap second at midnight, creating the clock reading 23:59:60 before rolling to midnight. POSIX, which counts exactly 86400 seconds every day, has no slot for that extra second, so the timestamp simply does not exist in the standard. Different vendors fill the gap in different ways: Google and AWS smear the extra second slowly across a full day; other systems freeze the clock or step it back. When two services in the same transaction use different strategies, their clocks drift apart by up to a second, which is enough to reorder events, break distributed locks, and corrupt time-ordered logs. The fix requires either using a monotonic atomic clock (TAI) or agreeing on a single smearing standard across the whole stack.

title: Modern UTC begins: TAI-UTC set to exactly 10s and the 1-second leap-second regime
body: CCIR (now ITU-R) Recommendation 460; implemented by BIH
whatChanged: At 1 January 1972 00:00:00, UTC was set to exactly TAI minus 10 seconds, abandoning the rubber-second/frequency-offset scheme. From then the UTC second equals the SI/TAI second exactly, and sync with UT1 is maintained only by whole-second leap steps keeping |UT1-UTC| within 0.9s, inserted preferentially 30 June or 31 December as the special second 23:59:60.
technicalImpact: Created the discontinuity that breaks naive software: a UTC minute can contain 61 seconds, and POSIX time_t cannot represent 23:59:60. Root cause of leap-second outages and the POSIX '86400 seconds/day' fiction. The IANA tz 'posix' config excludes leap seconds while 'right' counts them; the two diverge at the first leap second.

validation.supportingQuote: "A positive or negative leap-second should be the last second of a UTC month, but first preference should be given to the end of December and June... A positive leap-second begins at 23h 59m 60s and ends at 0h 0m 0s of the first day of the following month." (§2.1-2.2). "The departure of UTC from UT1 should not exceed ±0.9 s" (§1.2). "[UTC] corresponds exactly in rate with TAI but differs from it by an integer number of seconds. The UTC scale is adjusted by the insertion or deletion of seconds (positive or negative leap-seconds) to ensure approximate agreement with UT1" (Annex 1, C).

headline: Modern UTC and the leap second begin
blurb: In 1972 the world clock got a new rulebook, with occasional one-second steps to stay in sync.
story: On January 1, 1972, the world adopted a new way of keeping official time. Each second now matches a steady atomic count. To stay lined up with the spinning Earth, clocks add a single extra second once in a while. That extra second confuses software that assumes every minute has exactly sixty seconds.

academicRefs.authors: Nelson, R. A., McCarthy, D. D., Malys, S., Levine, J., Guinot, B., Fliegel, H. F., Beard, R. L., Bartholomew, T. R.
academicRefs.title: The leap second: its history and possible future
academicRefs.venue: Metrologia (BIPM/IOP Publishing)
academicRefs.keyFinding: The defining epoch of 1 January 1972, 0h 0m 0s UTC was set exactly 10 s behind TAI, the approximate accumulated TAI-UT1 difference since TAI's inception in 1958, with a unique fractional-second adjustment so UTC would differ from TAI by an integral number of seconds; from then the UTC second equals the atomic (caesium) SI second, step adjustments are exactly 1 s to maintain approximate agreement with UT, and the term 'leap second' was coined for the stepped second. This replaced the earlier frequency-offset / 100-200 ms step scheme, which had become intolerable partly because a proposed early-1970s aircraft collision-avoidance system needed stable frequency.

academicRefs.authors: Consultative Committee for Time and Frequency (CCTF), BIPM (working document CCTF/09-32)
academicRefs.title: Coordinated Universal Time (UTC) ,  History
academicRefs.venue: Bureau International des Poids et Mesures (BIPM), CCTF working document
academicRefs.keyFinding: Documents that CCIR decided the new UTC system would begin 1 January 1972 under Recommendation 460, with (a) the UTC second corresponding to the caesium-atom SI second, (b) step adjustments of exactly 1 s to keep approximate agreement with UT, and (c) standard time signals carrying the UTC-UT difference (DUT1, coded in 0.1 s multiples). Detailed implementation instructions were drafted by CCIR Study Group 7 in February 1971 (Report 517). Confirms the 1 Jan 1972 epoch was set 10 s behind TAI.

academicRefs.authors: Finkleman, D., Allen, S., Seago, J. H., Seaman, R., Seidelmann, P. K.
academicRefs.title: The Future of Time: UTC and the Leap Second
academicRefs.venue: American Scientist (also arXiv:1106.3141)
academicRefs.keyFinding: Explains that starting in 1972 radio signals began broadcasting atomic (SI caesium-133) seconds, and since then leap seconds have occasionally been added to that stream to keep the broadcast time synchronized with Earth's actual (slightly slower, irregular) rotation; UTC is maintained by a continuous count of SI seconds, a physical phenomenon distinct from Earth's rotation, making leap seconds the reconciliation mechanism between atomic and rotational timekeeping.

title: UTC formally defined relative to TAI at DTAI=10 seconds
body: International Telecommunication Union (ITU-R) and Bureau International des Poids et Mesures (BIPM)
whatChanged: On 1972-01-01, the modern UTC scale was anchored to TAI with an initial difference of exactly 10 seconds (TAI minus UTC = 10). This is the first row in the IERS leap-seconds.list file (NTP timestamp 2272060800). It is not a leap second insertion; it is the starting point from which all subsequent leap-second counts are measured.
technicalImpact: Software converting between TAI and UTC must seed the offset at 10 seconds at the 1972 epoch, not at zero. Treating the first row of leap-seconds.list as a leap-second event causes an off-by-10-second error in any TAI/UTC conversion before the first true leap second (1972-07-01). The tzdb leapseconds file comments this explicitly.
headline: The 10-second anchor
blurb: In 1972 the world set atomic time (TAI) exactly 10 seconds ahead of the new coordinated time scale (UTC), locking in the starting point for every leap-second count since.
story: Before 1972, there was no clean relationship between atomic clocks and the civil time everyone used. When the modern UTC scale launched on 1 January 1972, the difference between atomic time (TAI) and UTC was fixed at exactly 10 seconds to account for the drift that had accumulated since atomic clocks started running. That 10-second figure is the first entry in the global leap-seconds table, but it is not itself a leap second; it is the baseline. Every leap second added since then is counted on top of it. Software that misreads this first row as a leap-second event will miscalculate all TAI-to-UTC conversions by 10 seconds.

title: Liberia abandons Monrovia Mean Time, the last non-standard UTC offset in the world
body: Government of Liberia (President William R. Tolbert, Jr.)
whatChanged: Liberia dropped Monrovia Mean Time (MMT, UTC-0:44:30) on 7 January 1972 and adopted UTC+0 (GMT). This made Liberia the last country in the world to abandon a UTC offset that was not a multiple of 15 or 20 minutes. The tzdb uses the abbreviation MMT rather than the numeric form -004430 because the numeric form would be one byte over the POSIX limit for timezone abbreviations.
technicalImpact: Pre-1972 Liberia timestamps carry a -44.5-minute offset, breaking any code that assumes 15- or 30-minute offset multiples. The tzdb abbreviation MMT is itself a workaround for a POSIX constraint, and the Africa/Monrovia zone records three distinct offsets (-0:43:08 LMT, -0:44:30 MMT, then 0:00 GMT) that can surprise systems parsing historical African timestamps.
headline: The last odd-minute clock
blurb: In 1972, Liberia became the final country to drop a clock offset that was not a multiple of 15 or 20 minutes, the standard the rest of the world had settled on.
story: For decades, clocks in Monrovia ran 44 minutes and 30 seconds behind Greenwich, the last trace of an era when each city simply set its noon by the sun. Most countries had long since snapped to a clean hour or half-hour offset, but Liberia held on. On 7 January 1972, the government of President Tolbert changed the country to Greenwich Mean Time, closing the last gap. Software that processes historical Liberian timestamps still has to handle that odd -44.5-minute offset, and the timezone database uses the abbreviation MMT rather than the numerically accurate form because the precise string would be one byte too long for POSIX rules.

title: Public Law 92-267 allows partial-state DST exemptions in the US
body: United States Congress (signed into law by President Nixon)
whatChanged: PL 92-267, signed 30 March 1972, amended the Uniform Time Act of 1966 to permit a state that spans more than one time zone to exempt only the portion lying in a given zone from observing daylight saving time, rather than requiring an all-or-nothing statewide choice. This created the legal basis for split-state DST observance (notably Indiana counties, the Florida panhandle, and parts of other divided states).
technicalImpact: Forced the tz database to model sub-state regions as separate zones (e.g. America/Indiana/Indianapolis vs America/Indiana/Knox). Any software that assumed one DST rule per state broke; only zone-level lookups remain correct.
headline: The law that split Indiana's clocks
blurb: A 1972 US law let states observe daylight saving time in only part of their territory, creating a patchwork that still complicates timezone databases today.
story: Before 1972, US federal law required a state to either observe daylight saving time everywhere or nowhere. Congress changed that with PL 92-267, allowing states that straddle two time zones to exempt just one portion. Indiana took full advantage, leaving different counties on different schedules for decades. The tz database had to create separate entries for individual Indiana counties, and any code that mapped state to DST rule silently produced wrong results for affected residents.

title: Leap second #1 (first ever) inserted at 1972-06-30 23:59:60 UTC
body: Bureau International de l'Heure (BIH); now announced by IERS Bulletin C
whatChanged: The first-ever leap second was inserted following 1972-06-30 23:59:59 UTC, creating second 23:59:60 and moving TAI-UTC from 10 to 11 seconds. The first real-world application of the 1972 regime and the first deliberate 61-second minute.
technicalImpact: The exact instant where IANA tz 'right' (leap-aware) and default 'posix' time_t first diverge: time_t 78,796,800 maps to 1972-06-30 23:59:60 UTC under 'right' but is the following second under POSIX. The canonical test case for any leap-second-aware time library.
headline: The first leap second is added
blurb: In mid 1972 the world clock gained its first ever extra second.
story: At the end of June 30, 1972, timekeepers added one extra second to the official clock. It was the first leap second ever used. The minute ran from second 59 to a rare second 60 before rolling over. Many programs cannot handle that unusual sixty-first second.

title: Leap second #2 inserted at 1972-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: BIH / CCIR; IERS in later years
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 11s -> 12s. The only calendar year with two leap seconds.
technicalImpact: Same 86400-second-day assumption breakage as all positive leap seconds; second of two insertions in 1972, the only year with two.
headline: A second leap second in one year
blurb: Late 1972 added another extra second, the second one that year.
story: At the end of December 31, 1972, the world clock gained one more extra second. This was the second leap second of the year. So far 1972 is the only year that needed two. Each added second can trip up software that expects a fixed-length minute.

title: Emergency Daylight Saving Time Energy Conservation Act (1973 oil-crisis year-round DST)
body: US Congress (93rd); signed by President Richard Nixon (Public Law 93-182), administered by US DOT
whatChanged: In response to the 1973 oil crisis, Congress mandated year-round DST. Signed 1973-12-15; year-round DST began 1974-01-06. After backlash over dark winter mornings, Congress amended the Act 1974-10-05; standard time resumed 1974-10-27 and DST restarted 1975-02-23, producing a second one-off exception that year.
technicalImpact: Produced irregular one-off DST transitions in 1974 (DST started January 6) and 1975 that diverge from the regular 1967-1986 rule. IANA tz encodes these as explicit single-year exceptions; a library naively applying the steady last-Sunday-April rule misconverts 1974/1975 US timestamps.

validation.supportingQuote: Signed into law by President Richard Nixon on December 15, 1973 ... Pub. L. 93-182 ... This trial period began on January 6, 1974, and was intended to end on April 7, 1975. Lawmakers ended the experiment early on October 27, 1974.

headline: Year-round daylight time during the oil crisis
blurb: To save energy in 1974, the US kept clocks on daylight time all winter.
story: After the 1973 oil crisis, the US decided to keep daylight saving time on all year. The change began in January 1974 and meant darker winter mornings. People pushed back, and standard time resumed in late 1974, but DST restarted unusually early on February 23, 1975 and ran through October 26, 1975. These one-off dates make both 1974 and 1975 tricky for software that expects the regular schedule.

title: Leap second #3 inserted at 1973-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: BIH / CCIR; IERS in later years
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 12s -> 13s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.

validation.supportingQuote: 2335219200	13	# 1 Jan 1974 (offset becomes 13s, immediately following 2303683200	12	# 1 Jan 1973), confirming a leap second inserted at end of 1973 changing TAI-UTC 12s -> 13s.

headline: Another leap second is added
blurb: The end of 1973 brought one more extra second to the clock.
story: At the end of December 31, 1973, timekeepers added one extra second to the official clock. It was the third leap second so far. The step kept the clock in line with the slowly changing Earth. These added seconds need careful handling in time software.

title: US Energy Crisis DST: clocks forward in January 1974 and February 1975
body: United States Congress (93rd)
whatChanged: Public Law 93-182 (1973-12-15) pushed the 1974 spring-forward to January 6, the earliest DST start in US peacetime history. Public Law 93-434 (1974-10-05) set the 1975 spring-forward to the last Sunday of February. Both laws were emergency responses to the 1973 Arab oil embargo. Louisville and some Indiana counties did not observe the 1974 change, adding local exceptions.
technicalImpact: Any application hard-coding April as the DST start, or using a single rule for 1970s US time, computes wrong UTC offsets for winter/early-spring 1974 and 1975. Indiana county exceptions add further edge cases. tzdb northamerica encodes these as one-off Rule US entries for 1974 (Jan 6) and 1975 (Feb lastSun).
headline: DST in January
blurb: To save energy during the oil embargo, the US moved its clocks forward in January 1974 instead of the usual April.
story: The 1973 Arab oil embargo sent the US scrambling for ways to cut fuel use. Congress passed a law in December 1973 pushing the 1974 spring-forward all the way to January 6, giving the country extra evening daylight in the dead of winter. A follow-up law in October 1974 set the 1975 start to late February. Both years broke the April assumption that US time-zone rules had followed for decades, and software that baked in a fixed spring start month still gets the offsets wrong for those two winters.

title: Western Australia repeatedly trials and rejects daylight saving time
body: Government of Western Australia
whatChanged: Western Australia observed DST four separate times: 1974-75, 1983-84, 1991-92, and a multi-year trial from December 2006 to March 2009. Each period ended without permanent adoption. WA has remained on UTC+8 year-round since 2009.
technicalImpact: Historical date and time calculations for WA between 1974 and 2009 require the correct tzdb AW rules. Applications using Australia/Perth must carry all four short-lived DST windows or produce wrong offsets for those periods.
headline: The state that kept saying no to summer time
blurb: Western Australia ran four separate daylight saving trials over 35 years and rejected DST every time.
story: Western Australia tried daylight saving four times, but each trial ended with the question put to a public referendum, and voters said no every time. Four trials between 1974 and 2009 each ended with clocks going back for the last time. Software handling historical Australian times must include all four trial windows or calculations for Perth around those years will be off by an hour.

title: US Emergency Daylight Saving Time Energy Conservation Act (1974 year-round DST trial)
body: United States Congress (Pub. L. 93-182, signed by President Nixon 1973-12-15)
whatChanged: Under Pub. L. 93-182, the US sprang forward on 1974-01-06 at 02:00 local time instead of the usual late-April date, then held DST year-round through the winter. The tzdb US rule encodes this as a one-off: 'Rule US 1974 only - Jan 6 2:00 1:00 D'.
technicalImpact: The January spring-forward breaks any code that assumes the US enters DST in April. The tzdb US rule set has a special one-off entry for 1974 to capture this; software that hardcodes seasonal DST ranges or skips tzdata for historical dates will compute wrong offsets for early 1974.
headline: America's winter clocks, pushed forward
blurb: The US sprung its clocks forward in January 1974, months early, as an emergency energy-saving measure during the oil crisis.
story: After the 1973 Arab oil embargo sent energy prices soaring, Congress passed a law moving the 1974 spring-forward from late April to January 6, keeping the whole country on daylight saving time through the winter. The idea was that an extra hour of evening light would cut electricity demand. The experiment was unpopular: dark winter mornings meant children walking to school before sunrise, and the policy was explicitly rolled back by Congress in October 1974, roughly ten months in and well before its planned April 1975 end. For software, the episode left a one-off rule in tzdb that breaks any system assuming US DST always starts in spring.

title: Uruguay advances clocks 90 minutes in response to the 1973 oil crisis
body: Government of Uruguay (Poder Ejecutivo)
whatChanged: On 13 January 1974 Uruguay advanced its clocks by 90 minutes, shifting from UTC-3 to the highly unusual UTC-1:30. On 10 March 1974 it pulled back 60 minutes to UTC-2:30, and on 1 September 1974 it restored the final 30 minutes, returning to UTC-3. Then on 22 December 1974 it advanced clocks 60 minutes again, to UTC-2. Four separate offset changes occurred across 1974, the first three driven by decrees citing the international rise in oil prices.
technicalImpact: UTC-1:30 is an extremely rare half-hour offset in the western hemisphere. Any application with hard-coded South American offset bounds assuming no zone west of UTC-2 would produce wrong results for Uruguay throughout early 1974. Four distinct offset transitions in a single calendar year stress-test any tz database that assumed a stable annual DST pattern for the region. The changes are recorded in the IANA tzdb southamerica file under the America/Montevideo zone and Uruguay rules.
headline: Ninety minutes forward, oil prices to blame
blurb: Facing the 1973 oil crisis, Uruguay jumped its clocks forward by 90 minutes at once, briefly landing on an offset almost no country has ever used.
story: When the 1973 oil embargo sent energy prices soaring, Uruguay's government reached for an unusual lever: add daylight by moving the clocks forward a full 90 minutes in a single step. The change took effect on 13 January 1974, pushing the country to UTC-1:30, an offset that barely appears anywhere in the historical record. Two further changes unwound it in stages, one in March and one in September, each restoring part of the lost hour. By autumn Uruguay was briefly back to UTC-3, but the year was not done: on 22 December 1974 clocks advanced another hour to UTC-2, so four separate government orders shifted the offset within a single year. Software that assumed South American clocks moved in predictable one-hour DST steps, or that hard-coded a lower bound of UTC-2 for the region, would have calculated times incorrectly for every Uruguayan timestamp in that window.

title: US ends year-round DST experiment early, falling back on 1974-10-27
body: United States Congress (93rd); signed by President Gerald Ford as Public Law 93-434
whatChanged: Public Law 93-434 (enacted 1974-10-05) amended the Emergency Daylight Saving Time Energy Conservation Act of 1973 to restore standard time for the period from the last Sunday in October 1974 through the last Sunday in February 1975. The US fell back from DST to standard time on 1974-10-27, interrupting the year-round DST trial that had begun on 1974-01-06. DST did not resume until the last Sunday of February 1975.
technicalImpact: tzdata northamerica encodes this with two irregular US rules: 'Rule US 1974 only Jan 6 2:00 1:00 D' and 'Rule US 1975 only Feb lastSun 2:00 1:00 D', surrounding a winter 1974-75 with no DST. Systems relying on the normal US DST schedule for that period compute offsets incorrectly without these one-off rules.
headline: The DST trial that winter darkness killed
blurb: The US tried year-round daylight saving time in 1974 to save energy, then cut it short when Americans found themselves sending children to school in pitch-black winter mornings.
story: Congress passed year-round DST in late 1973 as an emergency response to the oil crisis, pushing the US spring-forward to January 6, 1974. Public support collapsed within months as winter mornings stayed dark until after 8 a.m. in many cities. Congress amended the law, and on October 27, 1974 the US fell back to standard time for the winter, ending the experiment early. The episode left an irregular DST window (Jan 6 to Oct 27, 1974) that tzdata captures with dedicated one-off rules, and any software using hardcoded yearly DST assumptions gets that year wrong.

title: Leap second #4 inserted at 1974-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: BIH / CCIR; IERS in later years
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 13s -> 14s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.
headline: Another leap second is added
blurb: The end of 1974 added one more extra second to the clock.
story: At the end of December 31, 1974, the world clock gained one extra second. It was the fourth such adjustment. The step kept official time matched to the turning Earth. Software that ignores these seconds can drift out of step.

title: Leap second #5 inserted at 1975-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: BIH / CCIR; IERS in later years
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 14s -> 15s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.
headline: Another leap second is added
blurb: The end of 1975 brought one more extra second to the clock.
story: At the end of December 31, 1975, timekeepers added one extra second to official time. It was the fifth leap second. The small step kept clocks aligned with the Earth's rotation. These rare seconds remain a common source of bugs.

title: Leap second #6 inserted at 1976-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: BIH / CCIR; IERS in later years
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 15s -> 16s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.
headline: Another leap second is added
blurb: The end of 1976 added one more extra second to the clock.
story: At the end of December 31, 1976, the world clock gained one extra second. It was the sixth such step. The adjustment kept official time in sync with the spinning Earth. Time software has to account for each added second.

title: Iran's DST followed the Persian solar calendar, not the Gregorian calendar
body: Islamic Republic of Iran (and predecessors)
whatChanged: Iran defined its DST transitions by the Iranian solar calendar: clocks advanced one hour at midnight on the first of Farvardin (Iranian New Year, roughly 21 March) and fell back at midnight on the 30th of Shahrivar (roughly 21-22 September). Because the Persian solar calendar is anchored to the vernal equinox, the equivalent Gregorian date shifts by one or two days each year, and no simple Gregorian formula captures it. In 1977-1978 Iran raised its standard time base from UTC+03:30 to UTC+04:00, so with the usual one-hour DST applied the summer offset peaked at UTC+05:00 before the base settled back to UTC+03:30. Iran abolished DST permanently after the 2022 cycle, remaining on UTC+03:30 year-round from March 2023 onward. The tzdb Rule Iran table carries individual year-by-year entries because no compact recurrence rule can express the solar calendar alignment.
technicalImpact: Any code hardcoding Gregorian DST dates for Iran breaks in some years. Correct handling requires astronomical Persian calendar conversion, not arithmetic. The 2022 abolition (tzdb 2022b) invalidates cached assumptions that Asia/Tehran observes DST; systems must update to avoid adding a phantom +1 hour offset from March 2023 onward.
headline: Iran's clock ran on the solar calendar
blurb: Iran tied its clocks to the Persian solar calendar rather than fixed Gregorian dates, meaning the exact day clocks changed varied every year and could not be expressed as a simple rule.
story: Most countries pick a fixed calendar formula for DST, such as the last Sunday of March. Iran used the Persian solar calendar instead, starting summer time on the first day of Farvardin and ending it on the 30th of Shahrivar. Because that calendar is tied to the astronomical vernal equinox, the matching Gregorian date slips by one or two days from year to year. The tzdb database had to record a separate rule for nearly every year from 1977 to 2022 because no compact formula could capture it. Iran abolished DST entirely after the 2022 cycle, fixing its clocks permanently at UTC+03:30, which means any software that assumed Iran still shifts its clocks will now be wrong.

title: European Community coordinates summer time for the first time, starting 1977
body: European Communities (predecessor to the European Union)
whatChanged: The IANA tz database encodes the EU rule set starting in 1977: clocks advance one hour on the first Sunday on or after April 1 at 01:00 UTC, and revert in autumn (the last Sunday in September in 1977, with October 1 used in 1978). Multiple EC member states (Belgium, France, the Netherlands, Luxembourg and others) abandoned their individual national DST rules and aligned on this shared transition date in 1977. The first formal EC directive on summer time came in 1980, but the de-facto coordinated practice began in 1977 and is encoded in the tzdb 'EU' rule block starting that year.
technicalImpact: The 'EU' rule block in the IANA tz database (tzdb europe file) is the historical anchor for all Europe/* zones that follow EU transitions. Software that processes European timestamps before 1977 must use zone-specific national rules; from 1977 onward it can rely on the shared EU rule. Errors in the boundary year affect any timestamp in spring or autumn 1977 for Belgium, France, the Netherlands, Luxembourg, and several other zones.
headline: Europe's first common summer-time switch
blurb: In 1977, European Community member states began changing their clocks on the same day for the first time, replacing a patchwork of national schedules with a single shared rule.
story: Before 1977, each European country set its own dates for moving clocks forward and back, so neighbors could be out of step by days or even weeks each spring and autumn. Starting in 1977, key EC members agreed to spring forward on the first Sunday of April at the same moment, which the IANA tz database records as the origin of the EU rule block. A formal EC directive followed in 1980, hardening the practice into law. For software, 1977 is the cut-off year: timestamps from European zones before that date need country-specific rules, while timestamps from 1977 onward can use the unified EU rule.

title: Leap second #7 inserted at 1977-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: BIH / CCIR; IERS in later years
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 16s -> 17s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.
headline: Another leap second is added
blurb: The end of 1977 brought one more extra second to the clock.
story: At the end of December 31, 1977, timekeepers added one extra second to the official clock. It was the seventh leap second. The step kept clocks lined up with the Earth's slow changes. Each extra second can confuse programs that expect steady minutes.

title: Leap second #8 inserted at 1978-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: BIH / CCIR; IERS in later years
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 17s -> 18s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.

validation.supportingQuote: 2492985600 18 # 1 Jan 1979 (preceded by: 2461449600 17 # 1 Jan 1978), confirming TAI-UTC went 17s -> 18s at the 1978-12-31/1979-01-01 boundary, i.e. the leap second 1978-12-31 23:59:60 UTC.

headline: Another leap second is added
blurb: The end of 1978 added one more extra second to the clock.
story: At the end of December 31, 1978, the world clock gained one extra second. It was the eighth such adjustment. The small step kept official time matched to the turning Earth. These seconds still need careful handling in software.

title: Leap second #9 inserted at 1979-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: BIH / CCIR; IERS in later years
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 18s -> 19s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.
headline: Another leap second is added
blurb: The end of 1979 brought one more extra second to the clock.
story: At the end of December 31, 1979, timekeepers added one extra second to official time. It was the ninth leap second. The step kept clocks aligned with the Earth's rotation. Software that tracks precise time must include each added second.

title: GPS time epoch fixed at 00:00:00 UTC 6 January 1980, with a 10-bit week counter
body: US Department of Defense (GPS Joint Program Office)
whatChanged: GPS system time zero was set to 00:00:00 UTC on Saturday 6 January 1980. GPS time counts seconds continuously and never applies leap seconds, so it runs ahead of UTC by the accumulated leap-second count (18 seconds as of 2017). The transmitted week number is stored in only 10 bits (values 0 to 1023), causing it to roll over to zero every 1024 weeks (roughly 19.6 years). The first rollover occurred on 21 August 1999; the second on 6 April 2019.
technicalImpact: Receivers and software that did not account for the 10-bit week rollover displayed dates 19.6 years in the past after each rollover. Any system converting GPS timestamps to UTC must also add the current GPS-UTC leap-second offset (18 s since 2017), which changes whenever IERS inserts a leap second. Navigation, aviation, telecoms timing, and any software ingesting raw GNSS data are all affected.
headline: The GPS clock that forgets every 19 years
blurb: GPS time started on 6 January 1980 and uses a counter so small it wraps back to zero roughly every 19.6 years, resetting receivers that are not prepared for it.
story: When engineers designed the GPS signal in 1980, they gave the week-number field only 10 bits of storage, enough for 1024 weeks before it rolls back to zero. The clock also runs completely independent of leap seconds, so it steadily pulls ahead of ordinary UTC time. In 1999 and again in 2019 the counter hit its limit and rolled over, and receivers that were not updated displayed dates nearly two decades in the past. Any software that reads raw GPS timestamps must both handle the rollover and add the current leap-second offset to get the right UTC time.

title: Kashgar (Xinjiang) forced from UTC+5 to UTC+8 by PRC standardisation
body: People's Republic of China (State Council)
whatChanged: Kashgar used local mean time until 1928, then UTC+5:30 until 1940, then UTC+5 until May 1980, when the IANA tz database records it switching to China Standard Time (UTC+8); China had decreed nationwide UTC+8 in 1949, and the tz maintainers note the 1980 date for the far west is uncertain. The jump from UTC+5 to UTC+8 is a 3-hour shift, the largest single-step change among Chinese cities recorded in the tz database backzone (versus 2 hours for Urumqi and 1 hour for Chongqing).
technicalImpact: Asia/Kashgar in the IANA tz database backzone records the full pre-1980 history. After the merge to UTC+8, the zone became an alias tied to China Standard Time. Software using only the canonical zone misses the 3-hour historical discontinuity. The resulting roughly 3-hour gap between wall-clock time and solar noon in Kashgar (solar noon falls near 15:00 Beijing Time) is a known source of confusion for scheduling and calendar software targeting Xinjiang.
headline: China's 3-hour time leap
blurb: China decreed a single national time zone in 1949, but Kashgar's clocks did not formally jump to it until 1980, leaping forward 3 hours, the biggest such shift among Chinese cities in the tz database.
story: China decreed a single national time zone in 1949, but the far-western region of Xinjiang long ran on its own time, roughly 3 hours behind Beijing and close to the local sun. The IANA tz database records Kashgar finally moving to mandatory UTC+8 in May 1980, though historians and the tz maintainers note this date is uncertain and may reflect the delayed documentation of the 1949 decree rather than a fresh 1980 change. Either way, Kashgar's clocks ended up 3 hours ahead of solar time, so the sun now rises and sets hours later by the clock than it does naturally. That gap, up to 3 hours off solar time, still confuses scheduling software today and is one reason many Xinjiang residents quietly keep a second, informal clock about 2 hours behind Beijing.

title: First EEC summer-time directive (80/737/EEC) harmonizes DST start dates from 1981
body: Council of the European Communities
whatChanged: Council Directive 80/737/EEC, adopted 22 July 1980, required EEC member states to begin summer time on the same date starting in 1981 (29 March 1981, 01:00 GMT; 28 March 1982, 01:00 GMT). It harmonized only the start of the summer-time period. End dates remained uncoordinated: continental members ended in late September while the UK and Ireland ended in late October. The directive was an interim measure covering 1981 and 1982, succeeded by further directives. Full harmonization of both start and end dates did not arrive until the seventh directive (94/21/EC, adopted 1994), effective from 1996.
technicalImpact: Established the legal foundation for synchronized European DST transitions that tz database European zone rules depend on. Before this directive, EEC member-state DST start dates diverged, so software had to track per-country rules independently. From 1981 a shared start rule applied, but the split end-date regime (continent versus UK and Ireland) remained a source of distinct zone offsets in Europe databases until 1996.
headline: Europe's first shared clock-change rule
blurb: The EEC's first summer-time directive made member states flip their clocks on the same spring date for the first time, though autumn clock-back dates stayed out of sync for another 15 years.
story: Before 1981, each EEC country decided its own date to start daylight saving time, creating a patchwork of offsets that tangled train timetables and phone networks. In July 1980 the Community issued its first binding directive, requiring members to begin summer time on the same Sunday in spring, at 1 a.m. GMT. The autumn end date was left to each country, so the UK and Ireland stayed an hour apart from the continent for part of the autumn for another decade and a half. Full alignment on both dates came only with the seventh directive (94/21/EC, adopted 1994), effective from 1996. For software, this directive marks the point where a single rule began to replace separate national ones for European spring transitions.

title: EU DST harmonization: last Sunday in March and October, at 01:00 UTC
body: European Parliament and Council of the European Union
whatChanged: European DST was standardized in stages. In the tzdb EU rule set, the 1977-1980 model has clocks spring forward on the first Sunday in April and fall back on the last Sunday in September; in practice pre-1981 national start dates varied across Europe with no common rule. In 1981 the spring transition was harmonized to the last Sunday in March (Directive 80/737/EEC). In 1996 the autumn transition moved to the last Sunday in October. Directive 2000/84/EC (adopted 19 January 2001) codified the current rule: last Sunday in March and last Sunday in October, both at 01:00 UTC, effective from 2002 onward.
technicalImpact: Software written before 1996 that hardcoded September as the DST end month will compute timestamps from 1977-1995 correctly but produce 1-hour errors for post-1995 autumn dates. The tzdb EU, W-Eur, and C-Eur rule sets encode each transition shift; implementations relying on older rule snapshots may mismatch on historical and future European timestamps.
headline: Europe finds its DST rhythm
blurb: The EU spent two decades negotiating a single DST rule, settling on the last Sunday in March and October at 01:00 UTC.
story: Before 1981, European countries started summer time on different dates in April, creating short windows of chaos at borders. A series of EU directives slowly pulled the dates together, first fixing the spring switch in 1981, then the autumn switch in 1996. Directive 2000/84/EC locked in the final rule from 2002 onward. Any software that hardcoded the old September end date still mishandles historical European timestamps from the 1977-1995 period.

title: Leap second #10 inserted at 1981-06-30 23:59:60 UTC
body: BIH (Bureau International de l'Heure)
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 19s -> 20s. First June leap second since 1972 (after eight December-31 insertions, Dec 1972 through Dec 1979, with no leap second in 1980).
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; first to occur at end of June rather than December, exercising the June-30 insertion branch of leap-aware code.

validation.supportingQuote: 2571782400	20	# 1 Jul 1981, "The decision to introduce a leap second in UTC ... is the responsibility of the Earth Orientation Center of the International Earth Rotation and reference System Service (IERS)."
validation.corrections: Replace 'First June (mid-year) leap second' with 'First June leap second since 1972 (ending a run of nine consecutive December-31 insertions; the very first leap second, 1972-06-30, was also a mid-year/June-30 insertion)'. Correspondingly amend technicalImpact: it was NOT the first to occur at end of June, that was 1972-06-30, though it was the first June insertion in nine years. Date 1981-06-30, TAI-UTC 19s->20s, and leap-second #10 are all correct. Note body attribution: in 1981 the responsible body was BIH; IERS did not exist until 1988.

headline: A leap second arrives in June
blurb: Mid 1981 added an extra second, the first June step in nine years.
story: At the end of June 30, 1981, the world clock gained one extra second. It was the tenth leap second, and the first one set in June since 1972. Eight of the nine before it had landed at the end of December; only the very first, in June 1972, had not. Handling a mid-year step tests parts of software that rarely run.

title: Leap second #11 inserted at 1982-06-30 23:59:60 UTC
body: BIH / IERS
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 20s -> 21s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.
headline: Another leap second is added
blurb: The middle of 1982 brought one more extra second to the clock.
story: At the end of June 30, 1982, timekeepers added one extra second to official time. It was the eleventh leap second. The step kept clocks lined up with the Earth's rotation. Each added second remains a careful detail for time software.

title: Leap second #12 inserted at 1983-06-30 23:59:60 UTC
body: BIH / IERS
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 21s -> 22s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.
headline: Another leap second is added
blurb: The middle of 1983 added one more extra second to the clock.
story: At the end of June 30, 1983, the world clock gained one extra second. It was the twelfth leap second. The small step kept official time matched to the turning Earth. Programs that track precise time have to account for it.

title: Alaska collapses four time zone offsets into two
body: US Department of Transportation (Secretary Elizabeth Dole), under authority delegated by the Uniform Time Act
whatChanged: On 1983-10-30 at 02:00 local time, Alaska's four offsets (-8 PST for the Panhandle, -9 YST for Yakutat, -10 AHST for Anchorage and most of the state, -11 BST for Nome and the Bering coast) were consolidated. Nearly all of Alaska moved to -9 and adopted the unified Alaska Standard Time (AKST) abbreviation. Nome's clocks moved 1 hour forward at the transition (from UTC-10 BDT, since DST was active, to UTC-9 AKST); its standard-time baseline shifted 2 hours (-11 to -9). The Aleutian Islands (America/Adak) shifted from -11 to -10 and remained a separate zone.
technicalImpact: America/Nome carries a -11 to -9 discontinuity on 1983-10-30 with a brief intermediate stop at -9 under YST before switching to AK%sT on 1983-11-30. Historic queries for Alaskan timestamps must account for non-trivial offset jumps and abbreviation reuse: AST was used for -10 Alaska time before 1967 but also means Atlantic Standard Time (-4), creating label collisions in older data.
headline: Alaska's four clocks become two
blurb: In 1983 Alaska went from four different UTC offsets spanning three hours to just two, erasing decades of fragmented timekeeping in a single night.
story: Before 1983, flying across Alaska meant resetting your watch up to three times: the Panhandle ran on Pacific time, most of the state on Alaska-Hawaii time, and Nome on a zone shared with the Bering Sea. The US Department of Transportation standardized this in 1983 (Secretary Elizabeth Dole signed the decision; Congress had delegated time-zone authority via the Uniform Time Act), pulling nearly all of Alaska into one new offset (-9, Alaska Standard Time). Nome's clocks moved forward one hour overnight. Software querying historical Alaska timestamps has to navigate the resulting jumps, merged zone abbreviations, and a label collision where the old -10 abbreviation (AST) also means Atlantic Standard Time.

title: Leap second #13 inserted at 1985-06-30 23:59:60 UTC
body: BIH / IERS
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 22s -> 23s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.
headline: A leap second was added in 1985
blurb: Clocks gained one extra second at the end of June 30, 1985.
story: Time keepers added a single extra second to the official clock on June 30, 1985. This kept clocks lined up with the slow, slightly uneven spin of the Earth. It was the thirteenth such second added since these adjustments began.

title: Lord Howe Island adopts a 30-minute DST shift (UTC+10:30 <-> UTC+11:00)
body: Government of New South Wales (Governor's order under the Standard Time Act 1971)
whatChanged: From summer 1985, Lord Howe Island's DST advances the clock by only 30 minutes (LH SAVE = 0:30), going from UTC+10:30 to UTC+11:00 rather than UTC+11:30. Before 1985 it used a full one-hour shift. IANA Australia/Lord_Howe encodes this with a dedicated 'LH' rule whose SAVE is 0:30.
technicalImpact: The canonical real-world case proving DST offsets are not always whole hours. Any library assuming DST always adds exactly 3600 seconds (or stores DST as a boolean) computes the wrong wall-clock time here.

validation.supportingQuote: Rule	LH	1985	only	-	Oct	lastSun	2:00	0:30	- ; Zone Australia/Lord_Howe ... 10:30	LH	%z	1985 Jul

headline: An island shifts clocks by half an hour
blurb: Lord Howe Island started moving its clocks just thirty minutes for summer instead of a full hour.
story: Most places that change clocks for summer move them by a full hour. Starting in 1985, Lord Howe Island chose to move its clocks by only thirty minutes. Software that always assumes a full hour gets the time wrong on this island.

title: Nepal shifts from UTC+5:30 to UTC+5:45 (Nepal Standard Time)
body: Government of Nepal
whatChanged: Nepal advanced its clocks 15 minutes from Indian Standard Time (UTC+5:30, used since 1920) to UTC+5:45, establishing an independent Nepal Standard Time effective 1986-01-01, nominally aligned to the meridian of Gauri Shankar. IANA Asia/Kathmandu encodes 5:30 until 1986, then 5:45.
technicalImpact: One of several quarter-hour (45-minute) offsets in use, alongside Chatham (UTC+12:45) and Eucla (UTC+8:45). Quarter-hour offsets break code storing offsets in whole/half hours, and any system deriving zone offset from longitude/15 mis-places Nepal. The 15-minute India difference causes cross-border scheduling bugs.

validation.supportingQuote: Nepal Standard Time has existed since January 1, 1986, when Nepal adopted the new time zone after they advanced their clocks by 15 minutes ahead of their previous time zone Indian Standard Time.
validation.corrections: technicalImpact: change "One of only two 45-minute offsets in use (with Chatham)" to "One of several quarter-hour (45-minute) offsets in use, alongside Chatham (UTC+12:45) and the unofficial Australian Central Western Time (UTC+8:45)." All other fields (date 1986-01-01, body, offset change, IANA encoding) are correct as written.

headline: Nepal set its own clock
blurb: Nepal moved its clocks fifteen minutes ahead to create its own national time.
story: On January 1, 1986, Nepal pushed its clocks fifteen minutes ahead of the time it had shared with India. This gave Nepal its own national time, tied to a peak in the country. The unusual fifteen minute step trips up programs that expect time differences in whole or half hours.

title: IANA tz database uses city names, not country names, to survive political changes
body: IANA tz database maintainers (Arthur David Olson, Paul Eggert, and contributors)
whatChanged: The tzdb naming convention uses continent/city identifiers (e.g. Europe/Prague, Asia/Kolkata) rather than country names, so that country renames (Swaziland to Eswatini) or sovereignty transfers do not invalidate zone IDs. Backward-compatibility links in the 'backward' file preserve old names when a canonical ID is renamed, as happened when Asia/Calcutta became Asia/Kolkata in 2008.
technicalImpact: Applications should store IANA zone IDs, never country names or abbreviations. When a canonical name changes (e.g. Asia/Calcutta to Asia/Kolkata), the old name keeps working as a backward link, but code should migrate to the current canonical name to avoid relying on compatibility shims.
headline: Why timezone IDs use cities, not countries
blurb: The tz database ties zone names to cities, not countries, so political renames never break existing software.
story: When countries rename themselves or change borders, any system that stored a country name as a timezone key suddenly holds a stale reference. The tzdb designers solved this by anchoring every zone to a city name instead. A city rarely changes its name, and when one does, the tzdb keeps the old ID as a backward-compatibility link rather than breaking it: when the city of Calcutta was officially renamed Kolkata in 2001, the tz database adopted Asia/Kolkata as the canonical ID in 2008 and retained Asia/Calcutta as a link. So software that stored 'Asia/Calcutta' in a database from 1995 still works today, though developers should migrate to the current canonical name to stay on solid ground.

title: China adopts and then abolishes nationwide daylight saving time (1986-1991)
body: State Council of the People's Republic of China
whatChanged: China introduced nationwide DST starting May 4, 1986, advancing all clocks from UTC+8 to UTC+9 at 02:00. Spring transitions moved forward on the first Sunday on or after April 11 (May 4 in 1986 only); fall transitions moved back on the first Sunday on or after September 11, each at 02:00. The practice ran every year from 1986 through 1991, then was abolished. China returned to permanent UTC+8 with no DST.
technicalImpact: The tz database encodes six years of DST rules for Asia/Shanghai (Rule PRC, 1986 to 1991) that apply nowhere else in the post-1991 world. Software handling historical Chinese timestamps must account for the UTC+9 summer offset during those years. Any system that assumes China has always been permanently UTC+8 will miscompute times between spring and autumn for 1986 to 1991.
headline: China's six summers of daylight saving
blurb: China tried daylight saving time for six years starting in 1986, then dropped it and never went back.
story: China spans the width of four natural time zones but uses a single clock set to Beijing time. In 1986 the government added a summer twist: clocks would spring forward one hour and fall back each September. The experiment ran for six years, affecting more than a billion people under one unified summer offset. After 1991 China stopped observing DST and has used permanent UTC+8 ever since. Software handling Chinese timestamps from 1986 to 1991 must account for this brief UTC+9 window, or calculations from those summers will be wrong by an hour.

title: U.S. moves DST start from last Sunday to first Sunday in April (P.L. 99-359)
body: United States Congress (99th Congress, signed into law 1986-07-08)
whatChanged: Public Law 99-359, enacted 1986-07-08, amended the Uniform Time Act (15 U.S.C. 260a) to move the U.S. DST start from the last Sunday in April to the first Sunday in April, effective 1987 through 2006. The end date (last Sunday in October) remained unchanged. This shifted clocks forward roughly two to three weeks earlier than before.
technicalImpact: Every tz database consumer had to update Rule US for 1987-2006. The tzdb northamerica file replaced 'Apr lastSun' with 'Apr Sun>=1' for the start rule. Any hardcoded DST date logic targeting the last Sunday of April was wrong for the entire 20-year span until the Energy Policy Act of 2005 changed the dates again.
headline: America's earlier spring clock
blurb: A 1986 U.S. law moved the start of daylight saving time roughly three weeks earlier, from the last Sunday to the first Sunday in April.
story: For most of U.S. history under the Uniform Time Act, clocks sprang forward on the last Sunday of April. In 1986, Congress passed Public Law 99-359 to shift that date to the first Sunday of April, giving Americans more evening daylight from early spring. The change took effect in 1987 and applied through 2006; the Energy Policy Act of 2005 then pushed the start even earlier, to the second Sunday of March beginning in 2007.
