For software, two decades of DST tables had to be rewritten, and any application that hardcoded the old April date silently gave wrong times for 20 years.

title: Creation of the Olson/zoneinfo (tz) database by Arthur David Olson
body: Arthur David Olson (US National Institutes of Health) and volunteer contributors
whatChanged: Olson created a structured, version-controlled compilation of the world's civil time-zone and daylight-saving rules in human-editable source form, compiled to binary zoneinfo. Earliest documented public reference is Olson's 1986-11-24 post. Zones are named Area/Location (e.g. America/New_York) under a convention designed by Paul Eggert, and all agree about timestamps after the 1970 POSIX epoch.
technicalImpact: Became the de-facto time-zone reference for Unix, Linux, macOS, the JVM, glibc, Python, Ruby, ICU/CLDR, Oracle and countless applications. Any program resolving local time from UTC depends on it; an out-of-date copy silently produces wrong wall-clock times. Released to the public domain.

validation.supportingQuote: "occasionally as the Olson database, referring to the founding contributor, Arthur David Olson." ... "Olson, Arthur David (24 November 1986). 'seismo!elsie!tz ; new versions of time zone stuff'" ... "The database, as well as some reference source code, is in the public domain."

headline: The world's time zone database is born
blurb: An engineer started a shared, editable record of every country's clock rules.
story: In 1986 Arthur David Olson began collecting the world's clock and daylight saving rules into one organized, editable record. Each place got a clear name, like a region paired with a city. This collection became the trusted reference that phones, computers, and countless programs use to turn a moment into the right local time.

academicRefs.authors: Tiwari, S., Chen, S., Joukov, A., Vandervelde, P., Li, A. and Padhye, R.
academicRefs.title: It's About Time: An Empirical Study of Date and Time Bugs in Open-Source Python Software
academicRefs.venue: 2025 IEEE/ACM 22nd International Conference on Mining Software Repositories (MSR)
academicRefs.keyFinding: Across 151 manually analyzed date/time bugs (≈520 person-hours) from open-source Python projects, time zones are the single largest conceptual category: ≈53.6% of bugs are time-zone-related, with a further 19.2% specifically arising during time-zone manipulation (converting timestamps between zones). The authors' Insight #1 states time zones represent the most significant source of these defects; the standard library (datetime/zoneinfo/pytz/dateutil) is repeatedly misused, e.g. dropping or attaching the wrong zone.

academicRefs.authors: Hayashi, J., Higo, Y., Matsumoto, S. and Kusumoto, S.
academicRefs.title: Impacts of Daylight Saving Time on Software Development
academicRefs.venue: 2019 IEEE/ACM 16th International Conference on Mining Software Repositories (MSR)
academicRefs.keyFinding: Mining GitHub repositories, the authors show DST is frequently overlooked at project inception, especially for software originating in regions that do not observe it, and that DST-handling code is later retrofitted in response to users in DST regions; they classify the types of DST-adaptation changes and correlate them with developer geography.

title: Excel treats 1900 as a leap year, a bug kept for Lotus 1-2-3 compatibility
body: Microsoft (Excel; bug inherited from Lotus 1-2-3)
whatChanged: Excel's 1900 date system counts 29 February 1900 as a real day, but 1900 was not a leap year. The error was inherited deliberately from Lotus 1-2-3 so existing spreadsheets stayed compatible, and Microsoft has kept it ever since.
technicalImpact: Every Excel serial date on or after 1 March 1900 is offset by one day relative to a correct calendar, so date math that crosses into spreadsheets (or uses the 1900 epoch) silently drifts. Libraries that read or write .xlsx (Apache POI, openpyxl) must replicate the phantom leap day to stay compatible.

reverify.source: Microsoft Learn KB + Wikipedia (Leap year bug)
reverify.note: newly added this pass; classic well-documented bug, editorial prose new

headline: The spreadsheet day that never existed
blurb: Excel still counts a fake February 29, 1900, a quirk it copied from Lotus 1-2-3 and never removed.
story: Excel stores dates as a running count of days from the year 1900. By mistake it includes February 29, 1900, a day that never happened because 1900 was not a leap year. The mistake was copied on purpose from an older program so old files would still work. It is still there today, so spreadsheet dates can be a day off from real calendars.

title: Leap second #14 inserted at 1987-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: BIH (Bureau International de l'Heure)
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 23s -> 24s. Administered by the BIH; IERS took over coordination on 1988-01-01.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second, administered by the BIH; the IERS Bulletin C process applied to later insertions from 1989 onward.

validation.supportingQuote: 2776982400 24 # 1 Jan 1988, and: "The decision to introduce a leap second in UTC is the responsibility of the Earth Orientation Center of the International Earth Rotation and reference System Service (IERS)."
validation.corrections: Responsible body: BIH (Bureau International de l'Heure), not IERS. The body/whatChanged/technicalImpact text should drop the IERS attribution. Corrected body: "Positive leap second administered by the BIH (Bureau International de l'Heure); the last leap second under BIH coordination before the IERS took over scheduling on 1 January 1988." Date (1987-12-31), number (#14), positive sign, and TAI-UTC 23s -> 24s are all correct and unchanged. Note: the first IERS-coordinated leap second was 1989-12-31.

headline: A leap second was added in 1987
blurb: Clocks gained one extra second at the end of December 31, 1987.
story: An extra second was added to the official clock on December 31, 1987. This small adjustment kept clock time matched to the Earth's rotation. It was the last such second handled by the older timing office before a new group took over coordination.

title: IERS Bulletin C: the leap-second announcement mechanism
body: IERS (International Earth Rotation and Reference Systems Service), Earth Orientation Centre, Paris Observatory
whatChanged: Twice a year IERS publishes Bulletin C, which either announces a 1-second step in UTC at the next opportunity (after 23:59:59 UTC on June 30 or December 31) or confirms no step. Under ITU-R TF.460, a leap second can in principle be inserted at the end of any month (end of December and June are first-preference dates, end of March and September second-preference), but in practice only June and December have ever been used.
technicalImpact: Bulletin C is the single authoritative trigger downstream consumers (NTP servers, IANA tzdb leap-seconds.list, OS vendors, GNSS operators) watch to update timekeeping. Its ~6-month lead time is why leap-second handling can be staged in advance; leap-seconds.list carries an expiration date tied to the latest Bulletin C.

validation.supportingQuote: "Contains announcements of the leap seconds in UTC" (IERS Bulletin C page); corroborating: "Bulletin C is mailed every six months, either to announce a time step in UTC, or to confirm that there will be no time step at the next possible date. The Earth Orientation Center is located at the Observatoire de Paris."

headline: A regular notice for leap seconds
blurb: A timing body began publishing a twice a year notice that says whether a leap second is coming.
story: Starting in 1988, a timing organization began issuing a notice twice a year called Bulletin C. Each notice either announces that one extra second will be added at the end of June or December, or confirms that none is needed. Everyone who keeps clocks accurate, from servers to satellite systems, watches this notice to know when to adjust.

academicRefs.authors: Nelson, R. A.; McCarthy, D. D.; Malys, S.; Levine, J.; Guinot, B.; Fliegel, H. F.; Beard, R. L.; Bartholomew, T. R.
academicRefs.title: The leap second: its history and possible future
academicRefs.venue: Metrologia (BIPM / IOP Publishing)
academicRefs.keyFinding: UTC corresponds exactly in rate with TAI but differs from it by an integral number of seconds and is kept within 0.9 s of UT1 by occasional insertion of a 1 s step; the decision to insert a leap second is made by the IERS. Since UTC's 1972 introduction (through 2001) there had been twenty-two leap seconds, all positive.

academicRefs.authors: Panfilo, G.; Arias, F.
academicRefs.title: The Coordinated Universal Time (UTC)
academicRefs.venue: Metrologia (IOP Publishing), vol. 56, 042001
academicRefs.keyFinding: States the defining constraint |UT1-UTC| < 0.9 s and that since 1972 UTC differs from TAI by an integral number of seconds changed at the insertion of a leap second 'which occurs as predicted and announced by the International Earth Rotation and Reference Systems Service (IERS).' Notes that since 1988 the BIPM is responsible for the computation of TAI and UTC (as of 2017, TAI-UTC = 37 s).

academicRefs.authors: Luzum, B.; Bizouard, C. (IERS Rapid Service / USNO and Earth Orientation Centre, Paris Observatory)
academicRefs.title: IERS Earth Rotation (UT1) Dissemination ,  Role of the IERS in the leap second
academicRefs.venue: Document for the 20th Meeting of the CCTF, BIPM
academicRefs.keyFinding: Authoritative IERS/BIPM statement of the announcement logistics: leap seconds are added or subtracted from the last second of a UTC month; to date they have only occurred in June and December; UTC is adjusted so that |UT1-UTC| < 0.9 s; and 'IERS will provide notification of next leap second roughly 6 months in advance,' while ITU only requires 8 weeks notification.

title: Newfoundland's one-year double daylight saving time experiment
body: Government of Newfoundland and Labrador
whatChanged: In 1988 only, Newfoundland applied a 2-hour DST offset (SAVE=2:00, LETTER=DD in the StJohns rules), springing clocks forward from UTC-3:30 to UTC-1:30 on the first Sunday in April. The province reverted to the standard 1-hour offset in 1989.
technicalImpact: Any code assuming DST offset is always 1 hour will compute UTC-1:30 as UTC-2:30 for America/St_Johns in summer 1988, producing a 1-hour error. The tzdb northamerica file captures this as a single 'only' rule for 1988.
headline: The province that sprung forward twice
blurb: For one summer, Newfoundland moved its clocks ahead two full hours instead of the usual one.
story: In 1988, the Newfoundland government tried an experiment: instead of the normal one-hour spring forward, it pushed clocks ahead two hours, branding it Double Daylight Saving Time. The promise was more usable evening daylight and energy savings. It was unpopular, putting the province an hour and a half out of step with the rest of Atlantic Canada, and the government dropped it after a single year. Software that assumes DST always shifts a clock by exactly one hour will calculate Newfoundland summer times in 1988 an hour off.

title: POSIX.1-1988 standardizes time_t and the Unix epoch as a portable contract
body: IEEE (Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers)
whatChanged: IEEE Std 1003.1-1988, the first official POSIX standard, codified time_t and the Unix time interfaces as a portable contract across Unix variants: seconds elapsed since 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC, with every day assumed to be exactly 86,400 seconds. This deliberately excluded leap seconds from the model, cementing the leap-second-blind epoch that almost all software still depends on.
technicalImpact: Any system implementing POSIX time must assume 86,400 seconds per day, which means a UTC leap second either causes a clock jump or is silently smeared. This design choice is the root of recurring leap-second bugs (e.g., Linux kernel crashes in 2012) and directly shapes the Year 2038 problem, since time_t was typically a 32-bit signed integer counting from this epoch.
headline: The standard that froze the clock at 86,400
blurb: The first official POSIX standard locked in a timekeeping model where every day has exactly 86,400 seconds, ignoring leap seconds entirely.
story: Before 1988, Unix timekeeping conventions varied across vendors. The IEEE POSIX committee needed a single portable definition and chose the simplest possible rule: count seconds from midnight on 1 January 1970, with every single day treated as exactly 86,400 seconds. Leap seconds, which keep atomic clocks aligned with Earth's rotation, were left out of the model on purpose. That decision made cross-platform code easy to write but created a gap between POSIX time and true UTC that grows by one second each time a leap second is inserted. Software that assumes the POSIX model can misbehave or crash when a leap second arrives, a problem that has bitten Linux, Java, and several large web services decades after the standard was written.

title: POSIX time_t defined to exclude leap seconds
body: IEEE / The Open Group (POSIX standards body)
whatChanged: POSIX.1-1988 defined time_t as a count of seconds since 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC ignoring leap seconds. This requirement has carried forward through every POSIX edition. During an inserted leap second, the POSIX clock officially stops, though in practice implementations either progress glacially, smear the second over a wider window, or jump backward.
technicalImpact: POSIX timestamps and true UTC diverge by the total count of inserted leap seconds (27 as of 2026) accumulated over the leap-second era (1972-2035). Any application requiring sub-second or sub-minute accuracy across that era must explicitly account for this gap. NTP smearing approaches used by Google and AWS silently alter the meaning of timestamps near leap-second boundaries, creating hidden disagreements between systems.
headline: The clock that pretends leap seconds never happened
blurb: POSIX, the standard that governs Unix-like systems, requires that the time_t counter skip over leap seconds entirely, meaning POSIX time and true UTC silently drift apart.
story: When POSIX was first standardized in 1988, it defined time_t as a simple count of seconds from midnight on 1 January 1970, explicitly ignoring leap seconds. The problem is that real UTC does have leap seconds, inserted to keep clocks aligned with Earth's rotation. This means a POSIX clock and a true UTC clock diverge by one second every time a leap second is inserted, currently 27 seconds apart. During an actual leap second, POSIX systems handle it inconsistently: some freeze briefly, some smear the second across minutes or hours, some even jump backward. Software that assumes time_t equals UTC will quietly compute wrong time differences for anything spanning a leap-second insertion.

title: POSIX TZ variable is process-global: a thread-safety hazard
body: POSIX standards committee / tzdb maintainers (NetBSD-inspired extensions)
whatChanged: POSIX defines TZ as a process-global environment variable, which makes it hard to safely use different timezones in different threads of the same process. The tzdb codebase added tzalloc, tzfree, localtime_rz, and mktime_z as extensions to enable thread-safe multi-timezone operations without touching the global TZ variable.
technicalImpact: Calling putenv/setenv on TZ in a multi-threaded process is a race condition. Code should use tzalloc/localtime_rz (where available) or language-level abstractions such as Java's ZoneId or Python's zoneinfo module, which do not depend on the global TZ variable.
headline: The timezone that belongs to the whole process
blurb: POSIX gives every thread in a process one shared timezone setting, making it a trap for multi-threaded code.
story: When POSIX standardized time handling, it tied the active timezone to a single environment variable, TZ, shared across the entire process. Any thread that changes TZ affects every other thread at the same instant, a classic race condition. The tzdb project later added a family of thread-safe functions (around 2014), tzalloc, tzfree, localtime_rz, and mktime_z, inspired by NetBSD, so callers can hold an explicit timezone object instead of mutating global state. Modern runtimes like Java and Python sidestep the problem with their own timezone types, but C and C++ code that reaches for setenv is still vulnerable.

title: Brazil limits DST to southern and central regions, excluding the Amazon north
body: Government of Brazil (Federal Decree 96,676, 1988-09-12)
whatChanged: Decree 96,676 (signed 1988-09-12) introduced DST from 16 October 1988 covering 19 states plus the Federal District but not the northern states of Acre (AC), Amazonas (AM), Para (PA), Roraima (RR), Rondonia (RO), and Amapa (then a territory). This ended nationwide uniform DST and created a persistent north/south split that the tz database encodes as separate America/Manaus, America/Belem, and America/Sao_Paulo zones.
technicalImpact: Applications that treated all of Brazil as one DST region began returning wrong offsets for northern cities. The tz database needed multiple Brazil zones to capture the divergence; code relying on a single BRT offset broke for Manaus and Belem from this point on.
headline: Brazil's DST divide
blurb: Brazil stopped applying daylight saving time to the whole country, carving out the northern Amazon states that would never observe DST again.
story: For most of its history Brazil switched clocks nationwide. In 1988 the federal government signed a decree that kept DST for the south and centre but dropped it entirely for the Amazon north, where seasonal daylight barely shifts anyway. The split hardened over later years as more states were confirmed in or out. Software that assumed one Brazilian time zone suddenly returned wrong offsets for cities like Manaus and Belem every summer.

title: Leap second #15 inserted at 1989-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: IERS
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 24s -> 25s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.

validation.supportingQuote: 2840140800	25	# 1 Jan 1990, and header: "The decision to introduce a leap second in UTC is the responsibility of the Earth Orientation Center of the International Earth Rotation and reference System Service (IERS)."

headline: A leap second was added in 1989
blurb: Clocks gained one extra second at the end of December 31, 1989.
story: One extra second was added to the official clock on December 31, 1989. The adjustment kept clock time in step with the Earth's slightly uneven spin. These occasional extra seconds let precise systems stay aligned with the planet.

title: Sub-Saharan Africa timezone abbreviations: invented, contested, and ambiguous
body: Mark R V Murray (community contributor) and tz database maintainer Paul Eggert
whatChanged: In 1997, Mark R V Murray reported that SAST (+02) is South Africa's official abbreviation, CAT (+02) is commonly used north of South Africa, and WAT (+01) best fits West Africa. The tz database maintainer separately invented WAST (+02 DST), CAST (+03 DST), SAST for +03 (DST), and EAT (+03) in the 1990s. WAT may also have been used historically for -01, though the database notes it cannot find solid citations. EAT gained traction in real timestamps; the DST variants remain rare and only appear in past data.
technicalImpact: Parsing or displaying timezone abbreviations for African zones is unreliable. The same abbreviation (e.g. SAST) can mean +02 or +03 depending on context, and WAT may map to both +01 and historically -01. Code must never use abbreviations as timezone identifiers; use IANA zone names (e.g. Africa/Johannesburg) or numeric UTC offsets instead.
headline: The African abbreviations nobody agreed on
blurb: Several timezone abbreviations used across sub-Saharan Africa were invented by the tz database maintainer in the 1990s, with no official body ever agreeing on them.
story: When the IANA tz database needed abbreviations for African timezones, there was no official standard to draw from. A contributor reported in 1997 what was locally used (SAST, CAT, WAT), and the maintainer invented the rest (EAT, WAST, CAST) to fill gaps. The result is a patchwork where SAST can mean two different offsets, and WAT may once have been used for a negative offset (-01), though the database notes it cannot find solid citations. Software that parses these abbreviations as identifiers will silently produce wrong times.

title: Falkland Islands DST: Stanley observed it, rural farms decided for themselves
body: Falkland Islands community (no formal governing rule existed)
whatChanged: During the 1990s through the 2000s, DST in the Falkland Islands was consistently observed in Stanley but not enforced in rural areas (West Falkland and parts of the Camp). No law defined the boundary. Individual farms chose independently whether to follow 'Stanley Time', and the list of which farms observed DST changed every year. The tzdb maintainers recorded only Stanley's time, explicitly noting they had no reliable data for rural areas.
technicalImpact: Any software using a single IANA zone for the Falklands (Atlantic/Stanley) silently assumes all residents followed Stanley's DST schedule. For rural farms during this era, that assumption is wrong. tzdb itself documents the gap, making this a rare case where the database acknowledges it cannot represent real local practice.
headline: The shopkeeper's DST list
blurb: In the Falkland Islands, whether your clock changed for summer time depended on which farm you lived on, not any law.
story: Stanley, the capital, observed daylight saving time each year. But across the Camp (the rural interior), farms made up their own minds. West Falkland and parts of East Falkland were mostly sheep farmers who had no use for the change. One Stanley shopkeeper kept a handwritten list of which farms were on DST each season so she could catch customers at home for meals. The tzdb database records only Stanley's schedule and says plainly it has no better data for the rest of the islands, meaning any software claiming to know a rural Falklands timestamp from this era is guessing.

title: Leap second #16 inserted at 1990-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: IERS
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 25s -> 26s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.

validation.supportingQuote: 2871676800 26 # 1 Jan 1991 (and header: "The decision to introduce a leap second in UTC is the responsibility of the Earth Orientation Center of the International Earth Rotation and reference System Service (IERS).")

headline: A leap second was added in 1990
blurb: Clocks gained one extra second at the end of December 31, 1990.
story: Time keepers added a single extra second to the official clock on December 31, 1990. This kept clock time matched to the slow drift of the Earth's rotation. It was one of many such small corrections made over the years.

title: Queensland's Whitsunday resort islands kept DST after the rest of Queensland stopped
body: Resort operators of Hayman, Lindeman, and Hamilton Islands (Whitsunday Islands, Queensland, Australia)
whatChanged: When Queensland ended its DST experiment after the 1991-1992 summer, the Whitsunday resort islands (Hayman, Lindeman, Hamilton) continued observing DST for two more summers (1992-1993 and 1993-1994) under the 'Holiday' rule set, confirmed by IATA flight schedules. The islands were effectively UTC+11 in summer while the rest of Queensland stayed at UTC+10.
technicalImpact: Code applying Queensland time uniformly was wrong for these islands during 1992 to 1994. tzdb carries a dedicated Australia/Lindeman zone with Holiday rules to capture this split.
headline: Islands out of step with their own state
blurb: After Queensland scrapped daylight saving, three Whitsunday resort islands quietly kept observing it for two more summers.
story: Queensland ran a short DST experiment and abandoned it in 1992, but the Whitsunday resort islands, Hayman, Lindeman, and Hamilton, carried on for two more summers, confirmed by IATA flight schedules. For those two years, guests on the islands were an hour ahead of travelers on the mainland just across the water. tzdb had to add a separate Australia/Lindeman zone to model the split, a reminder that political boundaries and timekeeping boundaries do not always match.

title: South Australia extends DST end date for Adelaide Festival (1990s)
body: Government of South Australia
whatChanged: South Australia delayed the end of daylight saving time by several weeks in even-numbered years from 1991 onward to cover the Adelaide Festival. DST end dates varied: 1992-03-22, 1993-03-07, 1994-03-20, before stabilising to last Sunday in March from 1995. The tzdb Rule AS has individual single-year entries for each erratic end date.
technicalImpact: No single formula captured the pattern, so the IANA tzdb encodes separate Rule AS entries for each affected year. Any system relying on a simple recurring DST rule for South Australia would compute wrong offsets for the early 1990s transition dates.
headline: The festival that scrambled the clocks
blurb: South Australia pushed its clocks-back date into late March on even years so daylight saving would last through the Adelaide Festival.
story: Every two years the Adelaide Festival draws international performers and crowds, and the South Australian government chose to keep summer hours running a few extra weeks so festival-goers would have more evening light. The end dates were decided close to the time and were not always publicly announced far ahead, prompting one tzdb contributor in 1992 to quip the rule was 'the Sunday we pick for this year'. Because no tidy formula fit the irregular pattern, the IANA timezone database records each year's end date individually, making the South Australian rules a rare and awkward exception in what is normally a region of clean recurring DST schedules.

title: Soviet successor states fragment into independent time rules after the USSR dissolution
body: Governments of Azerbaijan, Georgia, and other former Soviet republics
whatChanged: After the USSR dissolved in December 1991, successor states stopped following a single coordinated schedule. Asia/Baku, which had run on base UTC+3 with RussiaAsia summer rules (DST to UTC+4) since March 1991, moved to a fixed UTC+4 with no DST at the last Sunday of September 1992 (02:00 standard time in the tzdb), tracking its own offsets thereafter. Asia/Tbilisi switched from RussiaAsia to the E-EurAsia ruleset in 1992, then raised its base offset from UTC+3 to UTC+4 (still on E-EurAsia rules) at the last Sunday of September 1994. What had been one Soviet schedule became a patchwork of per-country zones.
technicalImpact: A single coordinated Soviet schedule split into many independent zones with diverging DST rules, so tzdb needed separate entries per country and software could no longer assume one stable offset for the Caspian and Caucasus region. Historical timestamp calculations for the area in the early 1990s are sensitive to which tzdata version is used.
headline: When the Soviet clock shattered
blurb: After the USSR collapsed, each successor state started writing its own time-zone rules, fracturing what had been one coordinated Soviet schedule.
story: The Soviet Union kept its republics on time rules coordinated from Moscow. When the USSR dissolved at the end of 1991, newly independent states like Azerbaijan and Georgia set their own clocks. Through the early 1990s they peeled away from the old shared schedule, with Baku shifting its offset in 1992 and Tbilisi moving to a different DST ruleset by 1994. Each divergence became its own tzdata entry that had to be maintained separately, and any software that assumed a single stable offset for the post-Soviet Caucasus got it wrong.

title: Russia restores decree time, shifting Moscow from UTC+2 to UTC+3
body: Government of the Russian Federation
whatChanged: On 1992-01-19 at 02:00 local time, Russia reintroduced decree time (a permanent one-hour advance above standard time), moving Europe/Moscow and most other Russian zones forward by one hour. Moscow went from UTC+2 back to UTC+3, Kamchatka from UTC+11 to UTC+12, and so on across nearly all Russian zones. This reversed the abolition of decree time that had taken effect across the USSR on 1991-03-31.
technicalImpact: The tzdb encodes a +1h transition across nearly every Russian zone around 1992-01-19. Code doing historical date arithmetic for Russian times in the window between the 1991 abolition and 1992-01-19 must account for this temporary one-hour dip, and libraries that assume Russian offsets were stable before the 2011 permanent-DST change will produce wrong results for that window.
headline: Russia's short detour off decree time
blurb: Russia (and the broader USSR) abolished its permanent one-hour clock advance in March 1991, then Russia put it back in January 1992, leaving a narrow gap that trips up historical time calculations.
story: Since the 1930s the Soviet Union had kept its clocks one hour ahead of where the sun said they should be, a policy called decree time. When the USSR began to fall apart, the advance was abolished across the union from 31 March 1991. The experiment was short-lived: on 19 January 1992 the Russian government reinstated the one-hour advance. Moscow moved from UTC+2 back to UTC+3, and similar jumps happened in time zones across the country. The episode left a narrow historical window where Russian clocks sat one hour lower than before or after, and software that ignores it quietly miscalculates timestamps from that period.

title: Queensland votes to abandon daylight saving after a three-year trial
body: Queensland Government and voters via referendum
whatChanged: Queensland trialled daylight saving from the 1989-90 summer through 1991-92. A public referendum on 22 February 1992 rejected DST, with 54.5% voting no. Queensland has observed AEST (UTC+10) year-round ever since, while New South Wales and Victoria shift to AEDT (UTC+11) and South Australia to ACDT (UTC+10:30) each summer.
technicalImpact: Queensland staying on UTC+10 year-round while its neighbours shift to UTC+11 means the offset between Queensland and adjacent states swings between zero and one hour depending on the time of year. Code that maps an Australian state name to a fixed offset breaks for cross-border scheduling during DST periods.
headline: Queensland's eternal summer time standoff
blurb: Queensland held a referendum in 1992 and voted to stay on standard time year-round, splitting itself from its DST-observing neighbours every summer.
story: After a three-year trial from 1989 to 1992, Queenslanders were asked at the ballot box whether they wanted daylight saving. Rural and northern voters, who disliked brighter evenings disrupting farm schedules, outvoted the city dwellers in the south-east: 54.5% said no. The state locked itself to UTC+10 all year, while New South Wales and Victoria shift an hour forward each spring. That seasonal gap still catches software off guard when scheduling meetings or comparing timestamps across Australian state lines.

title: Russia's 1992 DST dates were wrong in tzdata for decades (corrected in 2016d)
body: IANA tz team (correction sourced by Stepan Golosunov, applied in tzdata2016d)
whatChanged: Russia and nearby ex-Soviet locations transitioned to DST on 1992-03-29 at 02:00 local and back on 1992-09-27 at 03:00 local. For years, tzdata recorded these as 1992-03-28 at 23:00 and 1992-09-26 at 23:00 (a Saturday 23:00 pattern), which was wrong. tzdata2016d corrected both dates by one day and shifted the clock time to the standard Sunday 02:00 pattern used in surrounding years.
technicalImpact: Any system using pre-2016d tzdata computes the wrong instant for Russia's spring and autumn 1992 DST boundaries. Applications doing historical timestamp arithmetic, log correlation, or financial settlement covering March to September 1992 in Russian time zones may be off by one day or a few hours when running on older tz releases.
headline: Russia's 1992 DST dates were wrong for years
blurb: Tzdata recorded Russia's 1992 daylight saving transitions on the wrong day and time for decades before a researcher found the original decree.
story: When the Soviet Union dissolved, Russia issued new rules for clock changes. Tzdata originally encoded the 1992 spring and autumn transitions as Saturday at 23:00, but the real transitions happened Sunday at 02:00 and 03:00, one day later. The error sat undetected until researcher Stepan Golosunov traced the primary source and reported it in 2016. Any software doing historical date math in Russian time zones for 1992 on pre-2016d tzdata computes the wrong instant for those two transitions.

title: Leap second #17 inserted at 1992-06-30 23:59:60 UTC
body: IERS
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 26s -> 27s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.
headline: A leap second was added in 1992
blurb: Clocks gained one extra second at the end of June 30, 1992.
story: An extra second was added to the official clock on June 30, 1992. This adjustment kept clock time lined up with the Earth's rotation. Such corrections happen only at the end of June or December.

title: Ecuador's one-season DST experiment: the 'hora de Sixto'
body: President Sixto Duran Ballen, Republic of Ecuador
whatChanged: Decree No. 285 established a single season of DST for Ecuador, advancing clocks by one hour from 1992-11-28 to 1993-02-05. The experiment was not repeated. Ecuador has been permanently UTC-5 (America/Guayaquil) before and after.
technicalImpact: Historical timestamps for Ecuador between 1992-11-28 00:00 and 1993-02-05 00:00 local time require a +1h adjustment. Any system using a tz release that predates this data may be missing the two transitions entirely, since the rules were reconstructed from press sources well after the fact.
headline: Ecuador's one-season clock experiment
blurb: Ecuador tried daylight saving time exactly once, and the public hated it so much they mocked it by naming it after the president who signed it.
story: In late 1992, President Sixto Duran Ballen signed Decree 285 pushing Ecuadorian clocks forward one hour for the summer. Locals called it 'hora de Sixto' and complained that it was still dark when children went to school. A popular protest song by Jaime Guevara, 'Qué hora es', captured the backlash with a line about dawn arriving in the middle of the night. The clocks went back in February 1993 and DST was never tried again, leaving a short two-month window in history where Ecuador's UTC offset differs from its permanent -5.

title: Asia/Katmandu renamed to Asia/Kathmandu in the tz database
body: IANA tz database maintainers
whatChanged: The canonical IANA zone identifier for Nepal's capital was corrected from Asia/Katmandu (one 'h') to Asia/Kathmandu (two 'h's), matching the standard English spelling of the city. The old name is preserved as a backward-compatibility link in the tzdb backward file: Link Asia/Kathmandu Asia/Katmandu.
technicalImpact: Code or configs hard-coding 'Asia/Katmandu' still work because the backward link is retained, but the canonical name is 'Asia/Kathmandu'. Systems that do not load backward-compat links (some embedded or stripped tzdb builds) will fail to resolve the old spelling.
headline: One 'h' too few in Kathmandu
blurb: The tz database corrected the spelling of Nepal's capital from Katmandu to Kathmandu, keeping the old name alive as a compatibility alias.
story: Early tz database entries spelled Nepal's capital as Katmandu, dropping one 'h'. The maintainers later corrected it to Kathmandu to match standard English usage. The old identifier was not deleted; it lives on in the backward file as a link to the canonical name. Software that strips backward-compat entries or hard-codes the old spelling will silently fail to find Nepal's UTC+5:45 zone.

title: CET and MET both link to Europe/Brussels in the tz database
body: IANA tz database maintainers
whatChanged: The tz database backward-compatibility file defines CET and MET as Link aliases for Europe/Brussels, and EET as a Link alias for Europe/Athens. These links exist so legacy software using abbreviation-style zone identifiers does not break, but the abbreviations are not canonical zone names.
technicalImpact: Code that uses 'CET' or 'MET' as a zone identifier resolves to Europe/Brussels DST rules, which may not match the caller's intent. Abbreviations like CET also appear in formatted datetime strings, so parsing them back as zone identifiers is a separate and ambiguous operation. Prefer canonical names such as Europe/Paris or Europe/Berlin; the backward file links are legacy shims only.
headline: When CET, MET, and EET became aliases
blurb: The tz database maps the abbreviations CET and MET to Europe/Brussels as legacy shims, which can silently resolve to the wrong rules if used as zone identifiers.
story: Early software identified time zones by short abbreviations like CET (Central European Time) and MET (Middle European Time), but those strings are not unambiguous zone names. The tz database keeps them alive in its backward-compatibility file as Link entries pointing at Europe/Brussels so old code does not break outright. The catch is that many countries share the CET abbreviation but follow different historical DST rules, so resolving CET always returns Brussels behavior. Any code that stores or parses these abbreviations as if they were zone identifiers risks silently using the wrong offset history.

title: Etc/GMT zero-offset aliases (GMT+0, GMT-0, GMT0, Greenwich) kept for backward compatibility
body: IANA tz database maintainers
whatChanged: Etc/GMT+0, Etc/GMT-0, Etc/GMT0, Etc/Greenwich, GMT+0, GMT-0, GMT0, and Greenwich are all defined as Link aliases pointing to Etc/GMT in the tzdb backward file. They exist solely to preserve compatibility with older POSIX and operating-system naming conventions.
technicalImpact: The POSIX sign convention for Etc/GMT+N zones is inverted relative to ISO 8601: Etc/GMT+5 means UTC-5, not UTC+5. The many zero-offset aliases amplify the confusion. Code that compares or normalizes timezone strings should treat all GMT-variant spellings as equivalent to UTC before doing any offset arithmetic.
headline: Eight names, one clock
blurb: The tz database keeps eight different names for plain UTC zero, all pointing to the same zone, because old systems used all of them.
story: Different operating systems each used their own spelling for zero-offset GMT: GMT0, GMT+0, GMT-0, Greenwich, and more. Rather than break those systems, the tz database kept every spelling as an alias pointing at Etc/GMT, adding parallel Etc/-prefixed variants (Etc/GMT0, Etc/GMT+0, and so on) in its own namespace for consistency. The catch is that POSIX flips the sign convention, so Etc/GMT+5 is actually five hours behind UTC, the opposite of what most people expect. With so many aliases all pointing to the same zone, that sign confusion is easy to stumble into when code tries to parse or compare timezone strings by name.

title: Etc/GMT zones use POSIX sign convention: Etc/GMT+5 means UTC-5, not UTC+5
body: IANA tz database maintainers (following POSIX convention)
whatChanged: The Etc/GMT+N and Etc/GMT-N zones in the tz database follow the POSIX TZ sign convention, where positive offsets are west of Greenwich and negative offsets are east. This is the inverse of ISO 8601 and everyday usage: Etc/GMT+5 means UTC-5 (five hours behind UTC), and Etc/GMT-5 means UTC+5 (five hours ahead). The etcetera source file documents this explicitly. In tzdb 2016g, these zones also switched to numeric %z abbreviations (for example -05), which surface the actual offset and help catch the confusion.
technicalImpact: A persistent source of bugs: code using Etc/GMT+5 expecting UTC+5 silently gets UTC-5. The 2016g switch to numeric abbreviations (for example -05 instead of invented names) helps surface the inversion at runtime, but the sign trap remains for any developer reading only the zone name.
headline: The timezone that lies about its sign
blurb: Etc/GMT+5 does not mean five hours ahead of UTC; it means five hours behind, because the tz database follows an old POSIX rule where positive numbers go west.
story: When POSIX defined its time zone string format, it made positive offsets mean west of Greenwich, the opposite of how ISO 8601 and most people read a UTC offset. The tz database inherited that convention for its Etc/GMT zones, so Etc/GMT+5 maps to UTC-5 and Etc/GMT-5 maps to UTC+5. A comment in the etcetera source file warns about this directly. In 2016, tzdb release 2016g switched these zones to %z abbreviations, which render as the numeric offset (for example -05) at runtime, making the real offset visible and reducing silent mistakes. The sign trap remains in the zone names themselves, and developers who pick a zone by name alone without checking the abbreviation can get an offset many hours away from what they intended.

title: tzdb switches from country abbreviations to city-based zone names
body: Paul Eggert (tz database contributor)
whatChanged: Before 1993, tzdb identified zones mostly by country or territory abbreviations like Brazil/East, Canada/Atlantic, and US/Pacific. In 1993, Paul Eggert introduced the city-based naming scheme (America/New_York, America/Sao_Paulo, Australia/Sydney) that the database still uses. The old names were kept as compatibility links, now collected in the 'backward' file.
technicalImpact: Code and config written before 1993 may still reference names like Brazil/East, Canada/Atlantic, or US/Pacific. These resolve correctly on any system that ships the backward file but are non-canonical. Builds that strip the backward file (make BACKWARD=) will fail to resolve them entirely.
headline: The day cities replaced countries in every timezone name
blurb: In 1993, the tz database replaced short country abbreviations with city-based names, the convention computers still follow.
story: Before 1993, the timezone database labeled zones by country shorthand: Brazil/East, US/Pacific, Canada/Atlantic. Paul Eggert switched to city-based names like America/New_York and America/Sao_Paulo, which pinpoint exactly which locale's rules apply rather than leaning on a country that might contain several. The old names were kept as aliases so existing systems would not break. Any software or config still using the pre-1993 abbreviations depends silently on the backward compatibility file being present, and breaks on minimal deployments that strip it.

title: Canada/East-Saskatchewan excluded from tzdb due to POSIX 14-character filename limit
body: IANA tz database maintainers
whatChanged: The link 'Canada/East-Saskatchewan' (pointing to America/Regina) was intentionally left commented out in the tzdb backward-compatibility file because its 'East-Saskatchewan' component (17 characters) exceeds the historical 14-character filename limit. The file's comment notes it as a name that 'exceeded the 14-character limit and was an unused misnomer'.
technicalImpact: Older UNIX systems with 14-character filename limits cannot store tzdata files with long names. The omission means code that probes for 'Canada/East-Saskatchewan' by name finds nothing, while 'Canada/Saskatchewan' works fine. A rare but concrete case where filesystem history shapes timezone namespace design.
headline: The timezone name too long for old UNIX
blurb: A Canadian timezone alias was never added to the tz database because one component of its name was too long for early UNIX filesystems.
story: Early UNIX filesystems could only handle filename components up to 14 characters. The tz database ships timezone data as individual files, so every component of a zone name is also a directory or filename. In 'Canada/East-Saskatchewan' the 'East-Saskatchewan' component is 17 characters, over the limit, so the maintainers left it permanently commented out, noting it was also an unused misnomer. The shorter 'Canada/Saskatchewan' covers the same region instead. It is a small reminder that filesystem constraints from the 1970s still leave fingerprints on how time zones are named today.

title: EST, MST, and HST in the tz database link to fixed-offset zones, not DST-aware ones
body: IANA tz database maintainers (originally Arthur David Olson, later Paul Eggert and contributors)
whatChanged: In the tzdb 'backward' compatibility file, EST links to America/Panama (fixed UTC-5, no DST), MST links to America/Phoenix (fixed UTC-7, no DST), and HST links to Pacific/Honolulu (fixed UTC-10, no DST). The DST-aware Eastern zone is America/New_York; the legacy POSIX name EST5EDT links to America/New_York. These links have long been part of the IANA tzdb and remain in current releases.
technicalImpact: Code that passes 'EST' expecting New York Eastern time with DST transitions will silently compute wrong offsets during summer, off by one hour. The correct zone for New York is America/New_York or the legacy POSIX string EST5EDT. Many languages and libraries (Java, Python, PostgreSQL) warn about or reject bare abbreviations like EST precisely because of this ambiguity. Applications that hardcode 'EST' instead of a canonical zone identifier are a persistent source of time calculation bugs.
headline: EST does not mean New York
blurb: In the standard time zone database, the abbreviation EST points to Panama, not New York, so software using EST for Eastern time silently gets the wrong answer in summer.
story: Most people assume EST means the US East Coast, but the tz database routes it to Panama, a country that stays on UTC-5 all year with no daylight saving. New York needs a different name: America/New_York. The confusion arose because abbreviations like EST predate the modern zone database and were kept around for compatibility with old systems. Any code that relies on a bare abbreviation like EST, MST, or HST instead of a full zone identifier risks quietly computing the wrong time whenever daylight saving kicks in elsewhere. This is why every major programming environment now recommends canonical zone names over abbreviations.

title: Leap second #18 inserted at 1993-06-30 23:59:60 UTC
body: IERS
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 27s -> 28s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.

validation.supportingQuote: 2918937600 27 # 1 Jul 1992 / 2950473600 28 # 1 Jul 1993, and: "The decision to introduce a leap second in UTC is the responsibility of the Earth Orientation Center of the International Earth Rotation and reference System Service (IERS)."

headline: A leap second was added in 1993
blurb: Clocks gained one extra second at the end of June 30, 1993.
story: One extra second was added to the official clock on June 30, 1993. The small change kept clock time in step with the Earth's spin. It was another in a long series of these adjustments.

title: Kwajalein skips August 21, 1993 to cross the date line
body: Republic of the Marshall Islands
whatChanged: Kwajalein moved from UTC-12 to UTC+12 at 24:00 on 1993-08-20, skipping August 21 entirely. Friday August 20 was followed directly by Sunday August 22. The tzdb 2018h release corrected an earlier transcription error that had recorded the transition time as 00:00 instead of 24:00, which determines which day goes missing.
technicalImpact: The 24:00 boundary (end-of-day rather than start-of-day) determines which calendar date is absent. zdump was patched in tzdb release 93e to handle the 24-hour jump. The 2018h correction shifted the missing day by one, meaning software that cached the old transition time computed the wrong absent date. Any system doing date arithmetic across this transition must account for a literal 24-hour gap in local wall time.
headline: The day that never was
blurb: Kwajalein jumped from UTC-12 to UTC+12 overnight, wiping August 21, 1993 off the calendar entirely.
story: Kwajalein had long kept U.S. mainland days to serve the Army missile range there, leaving it a full day behind the rest of the Marshall Islands. In August 1993, at the request of the Republic of the Marshall Islands, the atoll crossed the date line to join its neighbors. Clocks went from Friday August 20 straight to Sunday August 22, and August 21 simply never happened. Software doing date arithmetic across that window must account for a genuine 24-hour hole in local time.

title: Rio de Janeiro mayor attempted a city-only DST change, reversed the same day (1993)
body: Mayor Cesar Maia of Rio de Janeiro
whatChanged: The mayor of Rio de Janeiro unilaterally tried to make the city observe DST to extend evening daylight for tourists. The federal government refused to recognize the change, so clocks across the city were split between two times for part of a day, causing business chaos. The mayor backed down that same afternoon.
technicalImpact: The episode never produced a tzdb rule change because the policy collapsed within one day. It survives only as a tzdb comment (southamerica) noting that sub-national, city-level timezone shifts are possible and that even an hours-long split can cascade into serious scheduling and system failures.
headline: The mayor who changed time for a day
blurb: Rio's mayor tried to give the city its own summer time to boost tourism, and the federal government shut it down within hours.
story: Around November 1993, the mayor of Rio de Janeiro decided the city should spring its clocks forward for the tourist season, without waiting for the federal government. The federal government refused to go along, leaving Rio split between two official times for part of a day. The resulting business chaos forced the mayor to back down that same afternoon. No tzdb rule was ever written, but the incident shows that even a same-day timezone split can cause real damage to any system that assumes a city shares one time.

title: Namibia's inverted DST: 23 years of negative summer time
body: Government of Namibia (Namibia Time Act 1994; Namibia Time Act 9 of 2017)
whatChanged: Namibia's 1994 Time Act designated CAT (UTC+2) as 'summer time' (September to April) and WAT (UTC+1) as 'winter time' (April to September). Because the higher offset was the base, tzdb encoded the winter WAT period as negative DST (SAVE = -1:00), meaning isdst=1 correlated with the smaller UTC offset. On 2017-09-03 at 02:00 local time, when DST would normally have begun, the Namibia Time Act 9 of 2017 fixed the country permanently to CAT (UTC+2); abolishing the winter WAT period meant the negative-DST anomaly ended. The Caprivi (Zambezi) Region informally observed CAT year-round throughout this period without official recognition.
technicalImpact: Any code assuming isdst=1 implies a larger UTC offset than standard time broke silently for Namibia during 1994-2017. The tzdb 'rearguard' section provided an alternative positive-DST encoding for older zic versions that could not handle negative SAVE values. Software using the vanguard encoding needed a zic that supported negative DST.
headline: The DST that ran backwards
blurb: For 23 years, Namibia's 'summer time' was actually the higher offset, forcing timezone databases to use negative DST to model it correctly.
story: Most countries set their clocks forward in summer, so DST always means a bigger UTC offset. Namibia flipped this: its 1994 Time Act made UTC+2 the summer standard and UTC+1 the winter period, which tzdb had to encode as negative daylight saving. Code that assumed DST always means a larger offset gave wrong results for Namibia during those 23 years. In 2017 Namibia abolished the whole arrangement, staying permanently on UTC+2 and ending the anomaly.

title: EU Directive 94/21/EC aligns autumn DST end to last Sunday in October from 1996
body: Council of the European Union
whatChanged: Directive 94/21/EC (adopted 30 May 1994) standardized the EU summer-time end date for 1995 through 1997: last Sunday in September for 1995 (with the UK and Ireland keeping a transitional fourth-Sunday-in-October date that year), then last Sunday in October for 1996 and 1997. Full single-date harmonization thus took effect in 1996. Before this, continental member states ended DST in late September while the UK and Ireland ended it on the fourth Sunday in October, leaving clocks out of step for several weeks each autumn. The October end date was retained by every subsequent EU summer-time directive (made open-ended by 2000/84/EC) and remains the rule in tzdata today.
technicalImpact: Tzdata encodes this as 'Rule EU 1996 max - Oct lastSun 1:00u 0 -', replacing the September rule that ran from 1979 through 1995. Any tz-aware system that cached or hard-coded a September end for European zones needed updating before autumn 1996.
headline: Europe's autumn clocks finally lined up
blurb: A 1994 EU directive ended years of member states turning clocks back on different dates by fixing a single last-Sunday-in-October rule from 1996.
story: For years, continental EU countries ended summer time in late September while the UK and Ireland waited until October, leaving European clocks out of sync for several weeks every autumn. Directive 94/21/EC fixed that: from 1996, every member state would fall back on the last Sunday in October. Though 94/21/EC itself covered only 1995 through 1997, later directives (97/44/EC, then 2000/84/EC, which made the arrangement open-ended) re-enacted the same date, and it remains the rule today. Software that hard-coded a September end date for any European timezone had to be patched before autumn 1996.

title: Leap second #19 inserted at 1994-06-30 23:59:60 UTC
body: IERS
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 28s -> 29s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.
headline: A leap second was added in 1994
blurb: Clocks gained one extra second at the end of June 30, 1994.
story: Time keepers added a single extra second to the official clock on June 30, 1994. This kept clock time matched to the Earth's rotation. These occasional seconds keep precise timing aligned with the planet.

title: Kanton Island jumps from UTC-11 to UTC+13 as Kiribati unifies its date
body: Government of Kiribati
whatChanged: Pacific/Kanton moved from UTC-11 to UTC+13 at the end of 1994-12-30, skipping all of 1994-12-31. Kiribati unified all its islands onto the same calendar date, moving the Phoenix Islands across the International Date Line. The offset jump is 24 hours.
technicalImpact: A 24-hour forward jump means any timestamp log or scheduled job anchored to 1994-12-31 in this zone is effectively undefined. UTC+13 also pushes past the +12 boundary that many older timezone libraries assumed was the maximum, breaking offset arithmetic. Pacific/Kanton is the canonical zone for Kiribati's +13 territory.
headline: The island that skipped New Year's Eve
blurb: Kanton Island leaped 24 hours forward on the last day of 1994, erasing New Year's Eve so that Kiribati could share a single calendar date across all its islands.
story: Kiribati is spread across both sides of the International Date Line, which meant some islands were a full day apart from others in the same country. In 1994 the government decided to fix that by moving the Phoenix and Line Islands forward across the line. For Kanton, the only inhabited Phoenix Island, that meant going to bed on 1994-12-30 and waking up on 1995-01-01 with no New Year's Eve in between. The offset went from UTC-11 all the way to UTC+13, a 24-hour jump that broke timezone software assuming offsets never exceed +12.

title: Pacific/Kanton: Kiribati's Phoenix Islands cross the date line from UTC-11 to UTC+13
body: Government of Kiribati
whatChanged: On 31 December 1994, Kiribati moved its Phoenix Islands (represented by Kanton, formerly spelled Canton) from UTC-11 to UTC+13, a 24-hour jump that placed the islands on the west side of the International Date Line alongside the rest of Kiribati. The Line Islands simultaneously moved from UTC-10 to UTC+14.
technicalImpact: A 24-hour offset jump is the largest single-transition shift in the tz database. Any timestamp arithmetic or day-count calculation spanning this transition will be wrong by a full day without current tzdb data. The correct pre-transition offset was UTC-11, not UTC-12 (an earlier era that ended in 1979).
headline: The island that skipped a day
blurb: Kiribati moved its Phoenix Islands across the international date line on New Year's Eve 1994, jumping 24 hours forward overnight.
story: Kiribati is a nation scattered across both sides of the international date line, which meant different islands had different dates. To fix that, the government moved the Phoenix and Line Islands across the date line at the end of 1994, so the whole country shared the same calendar day. Kanton Island went from UTC-11 straight to UTC+13, a 24-hour leap with no equivalent anywhere else in the tz database. For software, any calculation crossing that date risks being off by an entire day if the tzdb transition data is missing or stale.

title: Kiribati moves the Line Islands to UTC+14, creating the world's farthest-forward time zone
body: Government of the Republic of Kiribati
whatChanged: To put the whole country on the same calendar date, Kiribati moved its eastern Line Islands (including inhabited Kiritimati) from UTC-10:00 to UTC+14:00 at the end of 1994-12-31, bending the International Date Line eastward and making Pacific/Kiritimati the highest positive UTC offset in existence.
technicalImpact: UTC+14 breaks the common (wrong) assumption that valid offsets range only -12 to +12. Off-by-one errors appear in 'which day is it' logic, ISO-week calculations, and any UI clamping offsets to ±12h. A single UTC instant can span three different calendar dates worldwide.

validation.supportingQuote: Zone Pacific/Kiritimati ... -10:00 - %z 1994 Dec 31 / 14:00 - %z, with the comment that Kiribati "declared it the same day [throughout] the country as of Jan. 1, 1995".

headline: Kiribati jumps to the world's earliest time
blurb: Kiribati moved its eastern islands across the date line so the whole country no longer straddled it.
story: At the end of 1994, Kiribati moved its eastern islands across the International Date Line so the country no longer straddled it, ending a split where its two halves shared only four weekdays. This gave those islands the earliest local time on Earth. It also broke a common but wrong assumption in software that time can never be more than twelve hours ahead of the global reference.

title: Kiribati Line Islands jump from UTC-10 to UTC+14, skipping 31 December 1994
body: Government of Kiribati
whatChanged: Kiribati moved the Line Islands (including Kiritimati/Christmas Island) from UTC-10 to UTC+14 and the Phoenix Islands from UTC-11 to UTC+13, effective 1 January 1995. The transition skipped 31 December 1994 entirely: Friday 30 December 1994 was followed directly by Sunday 1 January 1995. UTC+14 is the maximum legal offset on Earth.
technicalImpact: Code that bounds offsets at plus or minus 12 or 13 hours breaks for Pacific/Kiritimati. The skipped date (31 December 1994) creates a one-day local-time gap that breaks range-based date arithmetic. Any system computing 'days until year 2000' from this zone produced wrong results until updated. Captured in tzdb release data96b (1996-01-27).
headline: The day Kiribati erased New Year's Eve
blurb: Kiribati skipped 31 December 1994 on its eastern islands, jumping 24 hours overnight to put the whole country on the same calendar day.
story: Before 1995, Kiribati's eastern islands sat on the opposite side of the international date line from its western islands, nearly a full day behind. The government decided to fix this by moving the Line Islands across the date line in one leap, erasing 31 December 1994 in the process. Residents went from Friday 30 December straight to Sunday 1 January. The change also positioned Kiritimati (Christmas Island) to be the first inhabited place to enter the year 2000, a distinction the government actively sought. For software, the jump introduced UTC+14, the largest offset on Earth, which breaks any code that assumes time zones fall within plus or minus 12 or 13 hours.

title: Kiritimati leaps from UTC-10 to UTC+14, crossing the date line
body: Government of Kiribati
whatChanged: Kiritimati (Christmas Island, Line Islands, Kiribati) jumped from UTC-10 to UTC+14 at the end of 1994-12-31. Saturday December 31, 1994 was skipped entirely: the calendar went from Friday December 30, 1994 directly to Sunday January 1, 1995. The shift of 24 hours moved the island from one of the last places to start a day to the first, crossing the International Date Line. Kiribati declared a uniform date across the whole country as of January 1, 1995.
technicalImpact: UTC+14 is the most positive offset in the tz database. Systems with hardcoded maximum-offset assumptions of +12 or +13 reject or corrupt Kiritimati timestamps. The skipped day (1994-12-31) means any software generating or storing date sequences for that zone must handle a gap of 24 hours with no valid local timestamps. tzdb was updated in release tzdata96b (1996-01-27).
headline: The island that skipped Saturday
blurb: Kiritimati jumped 24 hours forward overnight, erasing an entire day and becoming the first place on Earth to start each new day.
story: In late 1994, the tiny Pacific island of Kiritimati sat 10 hours behind Greenwich, making it one of the last places on Earth to greet each new day. The government of Kiribati wanted the whole country on the same calendar, and decided to leap the Line Islands across the international date line in one move. On the night of Friday December 30, 1994, residents went to sleep and woke up on Sunday January 1, 1995. Saturday December 31 simply never existed on the island. The jump made Kiritimati UTC+14, the furthest-ahead timezone on the planet, and broke any software that assumed the maximum possible UTC offset was +12 or +13.

title: TZif gains 64-bit transition times, splitting old 32-bit readers from new
body: IANA tz working group (Arthur David Olson, Paul Eggert, and contributors)
whatChanged: In 1995 the tz library added support for 64-bit time_t, enabling transition timestamps beyond the 2038 32-bit limit. The project later shipped separate 32-bit and 64-bit code tarballs (tz32code and tz64code) around release 2006b. The updated binary TZif format (version byte '2') stores a 32-bit section followed by a 64-bit section so older readers can still parse the file, but they silently see only pre-2038 transitions. RFC 8536 (2019) formally documented the format; RFC 9636 (2024) superseded it.
technicalImpact: Any TZif reader compiled against pre-v2 specs, or using a 32-bit time_t, silently ignores all post-2038 rule transitions. The zic 'fat' vs 'slim' output distinction exists specifically to work around bugs in software that mishandles the 64-bit section. Code that hard-codes 32-bit time_t or reads only the first TZif block will produce wrong results for any timezone transition after 19 January 2038.
headline: The file format that split time in two
blurb: The tz binary format gained a 64-bit section so clocks could handle dates past 2038, but old readers quietly ignored it.
story: Unix timestamps are stored as 32-bit numbers on older systems, which run out of room in January 2038. In 1995 the tz library started building in support for 64-bit timestamps, and it later shipped a revised TZif file format that carries both a 32-bit block and a 64-bit block back to back. Old software reads the first block and stops, believing it has complete data. The silent mismatch means systems with unupdated tz libraries will apply wrong offsets for any rule that only appears in the 64-bit section, a problem that will surface gradually as governments set transitions past 2038.

title: Leap second #20 inserted at 1995-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: IERS
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 29s -> 30s. DTAI reaches 30s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.

validation.supportingQuote: 3029443200	30	# 1 Jan 1996, "The decision to introduce a leap second in UTC is the responsibility of the Earth Orientation Center of the International Earth Rotation and reference System Service (IERS)."

headline: A leap second was added in 1995
blurb: Clocks gained one extra second at the end of December 31, 1995.
story: An extra second was added to the official clock on December 31, 1995. This kept clock time in step with the Earth's rotation. It was one more in the running series of these adjustments.

title: Sri Lanka's decade of fractured time: three offset changes and a civil-war split zone
body: Government of Sri Lanka; Sri Lanka Telecommunications Regulatory Commission
whatChanged: Sri Lanka advanced from UTC+05:30 to UTC+06:30 on 1996-05-25 00:00 to save power during a blackout crisis. On 1996-10-26 at 00:30 (not midnight) it stepped back to UTC+06:00. On 2006-04-15 at 00:30 it reverted to UTC+05:30. Tamil Tiger-controlled areas never accepted the 1996 change and remained on UTC+05:30 throughout, meaning Sri Lanka ran two concurrent effective offsets from 1996 to 2006.
technicalImpact: Three zone transitions in ten years, two of them at 00:30 rather than midnight or 02:00, break assumptions in many tz libraries. The de-facto dual-zone period (government vs. LTTE areas) had no formal tzdb entry, so any software calculating times in Tamil-held territory during 1996-2006 would silently produce wrong results. tzdb Zone Asia/Colombo captures all three government transitions.
headline: Sri Lanka's decade of fractured clocks
blurb: Sri Lanka changed its clocks three times in ten years, twice at the unusual hour of 00:30, while a civil war left part of the country on a different time altogether.
story: In 1996 Sri Lanka was hit by severe electricity shortages and advanced its clocks by a full hour to push evening demand earlier. Five months later it walked the change back by half an hour, then in 2006 dropped the remaining offset to realign with India. Each of the last two transitions happened at 00:30 in the morning, an unusual non-standard handoff time. During the whole period, Tamil Tiger-controlled areas never moved their clocks at all, meaning the island effectively ran on two different offsets at once. Software that assumed a single offset for 'Sri Lanka' during those years would silently produce wrong timestamps for roughly a third of the country.

title: WAT standardized to UTC+1, dropping the old UTC-1 usage
body: tzdb maintainers (Paul Eggert, with report from Mark R V Murray)
whatChanged: In 1997, Mark R V Murray reported that 'WAT' (West Africa Time) was best assigned to UTC+1, covering Nigeria and surrounding countries. Earlier editions of the database had used WAT for UTC+1, but the maintainers also recalled the same abbreviation being used for UTC-1 in the past. The africa file locked WAT to UTC+1 and noted the UTC-1 usage as something it could no longer confirm.
technicalImpact: Code or data that parses 'WAT' from historical records without a year anchor may resolve it to UTC+1 when the original meant UTC-1, producing a two-hour error. The tzdb africa file flags this ambiguity explicitly; any parser relying solely on the abbreviation string is vulnerable.
headline: WAT: one abbreviation, two offsets
blurb: The timezone abbreviation WAT was once used for both UTC-1 and UTC+1, and the tz database chose UTC+1 in 1997 while noting the old meaning could no longer be confirmed.
story: West Africa Time carries a hidden trap: the three letters 'WAT' were once used for UTC-1 in some contexts, but UTC+1 is where Nigeria and its neighbors actually sit. In 1997 a contributor reported the conflict, and the tz database locked WAT to UTC+1 while admitting the old UTC-1 citations could no longer be tracked down. That honest footnote in the africa file is now the surviving record of the confusion. Any software that parses 'WAT' from an old timestamp without checking the year risks a silent two-hour error.

title: Leap second #21 inserted at 1997-06-30 23:59:60 UTC
body: IERS
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 30s -> 31s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.

validation.supportingQuote: 3076704000	31	# 1 Jul 1997, "The decision to introduce a leap second in UTC ... is the responsibility of the ... (IERS)."

headline: A leap second was added in 1997
blurb: Clocks gained one extra second at the end of June 30, 1997.
story: One extra second was added to the official clock on June 30, 1997. The adjustment kept clock time matched to the Earth's slightly uneven spin. These small corrections happen only at the end of June or December.

title: EU Directive 97/44/EC unifies summer-time end date across member states
body: European Parliament and Council of the European Union
whatChanged: Directive 97/44/EC (22 July 1997) fixed a common summer-time period for 1998 to 2001: clocks advance at 01:00 GMT on the last Sunday of March and revert at 01:00 GMT on the last Sunday of October in all EU member states. The UK had previously used a later October end date; it aligned via the Summer Time Order 1997 (S.I. 1997/2982). This replaced the patchwork of national end dates that had survived through earlier directives.
technicalImpact: Triggered tz database rule updates for EU zones (visible in the tzdb europe file, where Europe/London adopts the EU rule set from 1996). Any system using stale tzdata that still carried UK-specific October end-date rules would compute wrong clock-back times for the UK and Ireland from 1996, when the EU common end date took effect.
headline: Europe's clock-change patchwork stitched shut
blurb: A 1997 EU directive forced every member state to end summer time on the same Sunday in October, closing the last gap left by a decade of partial harmonization.
story: For years, most EU countries fell back on one Sunday in October while the UK and Ireland held out a week or two later. The Seventh Directive 94/21/EC (30 May 1994) closed that split, mandating a single end date on the last Sunday in October for all member states from 1996. Directive 97/44/EC (22 July 1997) extended the unified arrangement for the next four-year block, 1998 to 2001. The UK wrote it into national law as S.I. 1997/2982. Software that had hard-coded Britain's old October end date, or used stale tzdata, started computing wrong UTC offsets for UK timestamps once the EU rule took effect in 1996.

title: Brazil moved DST forward to fix satellite TV times for Pope's 1997 visit
body: Government of Brazil (Decree 2,317, 1997-09-04)
whatChanged: Brazil started DST on October 6, 1997, earlier than its usual mid-October schedule. The tzdb records that international television networks ignored Brazil's DST policy and bought the wrong times on satellite for coverage of Pope John Paul II's visit. Decree 2,317 of 1997-09-04 set that year's rule.
technicalImpact: A real-world case of hardcoded UTC offsets causing international broadcast scheduling failures. Systems that assumed Brazil's offset was fixed without DST awareness booked satellite windows an hour off. The tzdb southamerica file documents it as the driver of a one-off DST rule for 1997.
headline: The satellite bookings that moved Brazil's clocks for the Pope
blurb: Brazil started daylight saving earlier than usual in 1997 because international TV networks bought the wrong satellite time for Pope John Paul II's visit.
story: When Pope John Paul II visited Brazil in October 1997, international broadcasters had already booked satellite transmission windows for the coverage. They set those times using a fixed Brazilian offset and ignored that Brazil observed daylight saving time. Rather than leave the bookings an hour off, Brazil started that year's DST early, on October 6. The IANA time zone database preserves this as the stated reason for the unusual start date, a rare documented case of a timezone assumption driving a national DST decision.

title: IANA tz database codifies POSIX vs 'right' (leap-aware) handling of the UTC regime
body: IANA Time Zone Database (tz) maintainers; theory.html clarified to public domain by Arthur David Olson
whatChanged: The tz database ships two mutually exclusive configurations: the default 'posix' data, where every day has exactly 86400 seconds and leap seconds are ignored, and the leap-aware 'right' (zoneinfo-leaps) data, which counts every real second since 1972. The two diverge at the first leap second. This is a long-standing design; the 'right' and 'posix' trees were moved from subdirectories to sibling directories on 25 May 1998, and the underlying choice is older still.
technicalImpact: The concrete developer-facing fork created by the 1972 UTC regime: the same timestamp resolves differently depending on configuration, and mixing 'right' and 'posix' zoneinfo on one system corrupts all timestamps. Almost all general-purpose software runs POSIX (leap-ignoring) time and handles leap seconds out-of-band (NTP smearing).

validation.supportingQuote: "leap second support is rarely used directly because POSIX requires leap seconds to be excluded and many software packages would mishandle leap seconds if they were present" ... leap-aware files are "by default installed in a separate directory zoneinfo-leaps" and "the leap-second choice is system-wide" ... "This web page is in the public domain, so clarified as of 2009-05-17 by Arthur David Olson."

headline: Two ways to count time, kept apart
blurb: The time zone database offers two setups, one that ignores added seconds and one that counts them.
story: The time zone project ships two separate setups that cannot be mixed. The default one treats every day as the same length and leaves out the added seconds. The other one counts every added second since 1972. Because of this split, the same moment can read differently depending on which setup a computer uses, and mixing them corrupts timestamps.
reviewNote: Re-dated from 2009-05-17, which is actually when Arthur David Olson clarified the files as public domain, not when this split happened. Anchored instead to the documented 1998-05-25 sibling-directory move; the design predates even that.

title: Leap second #22 inserted at 1998-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: IERS
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 31s -> 32s. Last leap second of the 20th century; then a 7-year gap until 2005.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; followed by the longest gap to date (none until end of 2005), which lulled some systems into never exercising leap-second code paths.

validation.supportingQuote: 3124137600\t32\t# 1 Jan 1999, "The decision to introduce a leap second in UTC is the responsibility of the Earth Orientation Center of the International Earth Rotation and reference System Service (IERS)."
validation.corrections: date: change "1998-12-31" to "1999-01-01" (the IANA-cited source lists it as "1 Jan 1999"; the inserted second 1998-12-31 23:59:60 UTC equals the UTC instant 1999-01-01 00:00:00). All other fields (IERS body, TAI-UTC 31s->32s, ~7-year gap until end of 2005) are accurate.

headline: Last extra second of the 1900s
blurb: Clocks gained one extra second at the end of 1998, and then none came for seven years.
story: On the last day of 1998, timekeepers added one extra second to the world clock. It was the final added second of the twentieth century. After it, no extra second was added for about seven years. That long quiet gap meant some computer systems never ran the code that handles these extra seconds.

title: Tonga's on-off DST: millennium tourism gambit, then a 14-year gap, then one more season
body: Kingdom of Tonga (Privy Council and Cabinet)
whatChanged: Two separate DST episodes 14 years apart, each lasting only 1-3 seasons. Any code or database that hardcodes 'Pacific/Tongatapu has no DST' is wrong for 1999-2002 and 2016-2017 dates. The 2016 revival required a tzdb update (2016i) mid-season; the 2017 abandonment required another (2017c). After the 2016 revival, the government officially decided not to renew DST (announced 2017-10-26) and it was abandoned again.
technicalImpact: Two separate DST episodes 14 years apart, each lasting only 1-3 seasons. Any code or database that hardcodes 'Pacific/Tongatapu has no DST' is wrong for 1999-2002 and 2016-2017 dates. The 2016 revival required a tzdb update (2016i) mid-season; the 2017 abandonment required another (2017c).
headline: Tonga's two-act DST experiment
blurb: Tonga switched its clocks twice in history, once to join the millennium tourism race and once more 14 years later, then gave up both times.
story: In 1999 Tonga introduced daylight saving time to push its clocks to UTC+14 and join Kiribati in being first into the year 2000, after Kiribati had moved its Line Islands to a permanent UTC+14 in 1995. The law was written for three years and simply lapsed in 2002 when nobody renewed it. Fourteen years later the government quietly revived DST for one season following Fiji's rules, then officially decided not to renew it: an announcement on 2017-10-26 by the Secretary for Information & Communications confirmed DST would not be observed, and Tonga has had none since. The two episodes, separated by a 14-year gap, are a trap for any software that assumes Pacific/Tongatapu is a fixed UTC+13 zone.

title: GPS Week Number Rollover (10-bit WN), 1999 and 2019
body: U.S. Space Force / National Coordination Office for Space-Based PNT (GPS.gov)
whatChanged: The legacy GPS C/A-code navigation message broadcasts the week number in a 10-bit field (max 1,023). Counting from the GPS epoch (1980-01-06), it overflows every 1,024 weeks (~19.7 years), resetting to zero. First rollover 1999-08-21; most recent 2019-04-06 at 23:59:42 UTC.
technicalImpact: Receivers without a pivot/base-week offset compute a date ~19.7 years in the past, corrupting timestamps, almanac/ephemeris validity, and any system deriving wall-clock time from GPS. Modernized CNAV uses a 13-bit week number; legacy C/A devices face the next rollover 2038-11-20.

validation.supportingQuote: The main civil GPS signal (C/A code) broadcasts the GPS week number using a 10-bit code with a maximum value of 1,023 weeks. ... The last GPS Week Number Rollover occurred at 23:59:42 UTC on April 6, 2019.

headline: GPS week counter runs out and resets
blurb: The week counter used by GPS only goes so high, so it resets and can confuse some devices about the date.
story: GPS keeps track of time by counting weeks, but the count uses a small field that can only reach a certain limit. When it fills up, it rolls back to zero, which happens about every twenty years. This happened in 1999 and again in 2019. Older receivers that do not adjust for the reset can report a date roughly twenty years in the past.

academicRefs.authors: Qabalin, M. K., Naser, M., Hawajreh, W. M. and Abu-Zaideh, S.
academicRefs.title: GPS Week Number Rollover Timestamp Complement
academicRefs.venue: Sensors (Basel)
academicRefs.keyFinding: The legacy GPS LNAV message carries the week number in a 10-bit field (max 2^10 = 1024, range 0-1023), so it is modulo 1024 and resets to zero at the end of week 1023; the paper's Table 1 lists the exact cycle boundaries from the GPS epoch of 6 January 1980 (0): cycle 1 ends 21 August 1999 (1024), cycle 2 ends 6 April 2019 (2047), cycle 3 ends 20 November 2038 (3071), each 1024 weeks = 19.7 years = 7168 days apart, with CNAV's 13-bit week field (8192 weeks/157 years) pushing the next event to 6 January 2137.
