Empirically, affected fleet devices reported timestamps exactly 7168 days in the past (e.g. a Bofan PT95 reporting 28 April 1991 instead of 12 December 2010). Of three tested fixes (firmware controller ~0.0013s/$10; transport-layer TCP packet reforming ~0.164s/$3; database triggers, 1.247s/38% delay), the transport-layer timestamp-complement was most effective.

title: Argentina DST yo-yo: law enacted, applied once, then repealed within months (1999-2000)
body: President Fernando de la Rua, Government of Argentina
whatChanged: Argentina enacted Ley 25155 (1999), which moved clocks forward one hour on 1999-10-03. President de la Rua repealed the law on 2000-03-02 because DST increased energy use in winter rather than reducing it, and clocks reverted on 2000-03-03. The law set expiry on March 5 but clocks changed March 3, leaving a 2-day discrepancy between the legal and applied end dates. Argentina has observed UTC-3 without DST since March 2009, when a second DST experiment covering the 2007-08 and 2008-09 austral summers was abandoned.
technicalImpact: The tzdb southamerica file models the actual applied date of 2000-03-03, not the statutory end of 2000-03-05. Software reading Argentine legal texts against tzdb data sees a 2-day gap. Any application that cached the March 5 expiry was off by two days. The episode is documented in tzdb comments and highlights how rapid executive reversals can produce legal-vs-applied mismatches.
headline: Argentina's DST that lasted one summer
blurb: Argentina turned its clocks forward in October 1999 and reversed the decision less than five months later after the energy savings never materialized.
story: Argentina passed a daylight saving law in 1999 and dutifully moved clocks forward on October 3. Within months the government decided DST raised, not lowered, energy bills in winter. President de la Rua scrapped the law on March 2, 2000, and clocks went back a day later. The legal text said March 5, but the actual changeover happened March 3, a two-day gap that lives on as a footnote in the tz database and can catch software that trusts statutory dates over observed ones.

title: Nunavut's failed single-timezone experiment (1999 to 2001)
body: Government of Nunavut
whatChanged: When Nunavut unified much of the territory under Central Time starting 1999-10-31, eastern Baffin communities refused and stayed on Eastern. The territory then switched the unified zone to Eastern for the winter of 2000, prompting the far west to rebel: tzdb records a brief EST window for America/Cambridge_Bay from 2000-10-29 ending 2000-11-05, when it returned to Central, and the far-west communities returned to Mountain time. On 2001-04-01 Nunavut officially abandoned the experiment and restored three separate time zones: Mountain (America/Cambridge_Bay), Central (America/Rankin_Inlet, America/Resolute), and Eastern (America/Iqaluit).
technicalImpact: America/Iqaluit, America/Cambridge_Bay, America/Rankin_Inlet, and America/Resolute all carry multiple zone transitions between 1999 and 2001 reflecting the failed unification and staged rollback. America/Resolute is especially complex, with conflicting reports from non-residents later retracted and additional transitions in 2006-2007. Any system caching pre-2001 Nunavut offsets or assuming stable zone assignments across this period will compute wrong UTC offsets for those communities.
headline: The territory that tried one clock for everyone
blurb: Nunavut tried to run its vast territory on a single clock, Central Time, starting in late 1999, but eastern Baffin communities refused almost immediately and a later switch to Eastern Time angered the west, so within two years the territory reverted to three zones.
story: When Canada carved Nunavut out of the Northwest Territories in 1999, its new government decided to unify much of the territory on a single clock, Central Time, starting 1999-10-31, for administrative simplicity. Eastern Baffin communities, including Iqaluit, Pangnirtung and Sanikiluaq, rejected the change almost immediately and clung to Eastern Time. To appease them the territory switched the unified zone to Eastern for the winter of 2000, which in turn provoked the far-west communities; Cambridge Bay switched back toward Central Time in November 2000, and the western communities ultimately returned to Mountain Time. By April 2001 the government formally gave up and restored three separate time zones. The episode left a tangle of short-lived transitions in the tz database that can trap software relying on simple or cached offset lookups for those communities.

title: Pangnirtung, Nunavut split into two simultaneous time zones
body: Nunavut territorial government and residents of Pangnirtung, Nunavut, Canada
whatChanged: When the rest of Nunavut adopted Central Time (UTC-6) on 1999-10-31, Pangnirtung divided: government-affiliated bodies (RCMP, health centre, First Air, the national parks office) switched to Central, while the hamlet office, both schools, most businesses, and the airport operator stayed on Eastern Time (UTC-5). Two clocks ran simultaneously in one community.
technicalImpact: A live example of geographic zone ambiguity where software must pick one canonical offset for a single location. The tzdb zone America/Pangnirtung records the switch to Central on 1999-10-31 and back to Eastern on 2000-10-29, treating the Eastern-time holdouts as informal non-conformers outside the canonical record. America/Pangnirtung now lives in tzdb's backzone (legacy) file, with America/Iqaluit serving as the canonical zone for the region.
headline: One town, two clocks
blurb: When Nunavut switched to Central Time in 1999, one community simply refused to pick a side.
story: Nunavut became a territory in April 1999 and tried to unify its clocks on Central Time that October. In Pangnirtung, the RCMP, health centre, and airline office switched, but the hamlet hall, schools, and most shops kept Eastern Time, one hour ahead. Residents were unbothered and planned to ring in the New Year twice, one hour apart. For software, the town exists in only one tzdb zone, so any application picking that zone gets the official story while half the community was living an hour differently.

title: Y2K / Year 2000 problem (two-digit year rollover)
body: Global IT industry; coordinated nationally (e.g. US President's Council on Year 2000 Conversion)
whatChanged: Decades of software stored the year as two decimal digits, so the rollover from 99 to 00 was ambiguous between 2000 and 1900. At midnight 1999-12-31 to 2000-01-01 affected programs could compute negative or nonsensical date intervals and mis-order dated records.
technicalImpact: Mainframe COBOL/PL-I billing, banking, insurance, scheduling and embedded systems risked incorrect interest calculations, mis-sorted records, and crashes. After ~$300B+ of worldwide remediation few major failures occurred, but isolated incidents (Greek cash registers printing 1900-dated receipts, POS terminals, erroneous late fees) appeared.

validation.supportingQuote: Until the 1990s many computer programs (especially those written in the early days of computers) were designed to abbreviate four-digit years as two digits... These computers could recognize "98" as "1998" but would be unable to recognize "00" as "2000," perhaps interpreting it to mean 1900. ... After more than a year of international alarm... few major failures occurred in the transition from December 31, 1999, to January 1, 2000.

headline: The year 2000 computer scare
blurb: Many programs stored the year as only two digits, so the jump from 99 to 00 could be read as 1900 instead of 2000.
story: For decades, software saved the year using just two digits to save space. When 1999 turned into 2000, those two digits read as 00, which a program could mistake for 1900. This could make date math come out negative or wrong and put records in the wrong order. After a huge worldwide effort to fix it, very few serious failures happened, though a handful of small ones did.

academicRefs.authors: De Lucia, A., Pompella, E. and Stefanucci, S.
academicRefs.title: Assessing the maintenance processes of a software organization: an empirical analysis of a large industrial project
academicRefs.venue: Journal of Systems and Software
academicRefs.keyFinding: Empirical analysis of a real large-scale Y2K remediation project (run at an EDS Solution Center) in which the application portfolio was decomposed into loosely coupled 'work-packets' that distributed teams modified independently and incrementally. The authors derive cost/effort models from observed correlations between maintenance size, productivity and the effort needed to make work-packets Y2K-compliant, enabling project managers to estimate total conversion effort for a massive-maintenance process.

academicRefs.authors: Anderson, M.C., Banker, R.D., Natarajan, R. and Ravindran, S.
academicRefs.title: Y2K spending by entrepreneurial firms
academicRefs.venue: Journal of Accounting and Public Policy
academicRefs.keyFinding: Using Y2K disclosures from SEC filings and hypotheses derived from Schumpeter's creative-destruction view, the study finds that Y2K spending rose with economic factors characteristic of entrepreneurial firms and with the competitiveness of a firm's industry; the systematic spending patterns also show that accounting disclosures of IT spending are informative.

academicRefs.authors: Kliesen, K.L.
academicRefs.title: Was Y2K behind the business investment boom and bust?
academicRefs.venue: Review, Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis (Vol. 85, No. 1, pp. 31-42)
academicRefs.keyFinding: Tests whether the late-1990s surge and subsequent 2000-2001 collapse in U.S. business purchases of high-tech equipment and software was a Y2K phenomenon, and concludes that 'the magnitude and timing of Y2K-related expenditures make it unlikely that the investment boom and bust was a Y2K event' (Y2K spending on the order of tens of billions per year was too small/mistimed to explain the cycle).

title: Australia brings DST forward two months for the Sydney 2000 Olympics
body: Governments of New South Wales, Victoria, Australian Capital Territory, and Tasmania
whatChanged: NSW, Victoria, ACT, and Tasmania moved their 2000 spring DST start from the usual late October to the last Sunday in August (27 August 2000), giving an extra two months of evening daylight during the Sydney Olympics. South Australia and Queensland declined. The tzdb australasia file encodes this as a one-off 'only 2000' entry in rules AN, AV, and AT (and LH for Lord Howe Island), all triggering on 'Aug lastSun'.
technicalImpact: Any code or device using the standard 'first Sunday in October' heuristic for Australian eastern DST was wrong from 27 August to 28 October 2000. Microsoft shipped a Windows CE hotfix (KB257178) for this exact bug. The tzdb AN, AV, and AT rules each carry a 'only 2000' Aug lastSun row that modern tz-aware software relies on to handle this two-month anomaly correctly.
headline: Two extra months of summer for the Olympics
blurb: Four Australian states turned their clocks forward in August instead of October so evening events at the 2000 Sydney Olympics would have more daylight.
story: Australia normally starts daylight saving on the last Sunday of October. To give the Sydney Olympics better evening light, New South Wales, Victoria, the ACT, and Tasmania all agreed to spring forward on 27 August 2000 instead, two months early. South Australia declined the early start, and Queensland does not observe daylight saving at all (it abandoned it after a 1992 referendum), so Australia's eastern seaboard was briefly split. The change lasted only until the usual end-of-summer rollback in March 2001. Software that hard-coded October as the Australian DST start broke for the entire Olympic period, and Microsoft had to push a hotfix for Windows CE devices.
tzRelease: 1999e/1999f (rules added mid-1999 after state government announcements)

title: Dominican Republic adopts and reverses DST within 35 days
body: Government of the Dominican Republic (presidential decree)
whatChanged: On 2000-10-29 at 02:00, the Dominican Republic switched from AST (UTC-4, fixed) to EST with US-style DST (UTC-5 base, US rules), intending to align with Puerto Rico. On 2000-12-03 at 01:00, a new presidential decree reversed the change, restoring fixed AST (UTC-4). The full round-trip lasted 35 days.
technicalImpact: Any tz database or application that cached the Dominican Republic's zone after October 29 but before December 3 would compute offsets incorrectly. The America/Santo_Domingo zone in tzdb captures both transitions. The episode also illustrates how one territory's policy reversal (Puerto Rico declining DST) can force an immediate cascade in a neighbor.
headline: The 35-day DST experiment
blurb: The Dominican Republic switched to US-style daylight saving time in late 2000, then reversed the decision five weeks later after Puerto Rico changed its mind.
story: In October 2000, the Dominican Republic moved its clocks to match the United States Eastern zone, partly so it would be in sync with Puerto Rico, which was also planning to adopt DST. Puerto Rico then backed out of the plan, removing the whole reason for the change. The Dominican president issued a new decree and the country reverted to its fixed UTC-4 offset on December 3, just 35 days after the first switch. Software that fetched the zone during those five weeks could silently produce wrong times for anyone scheduling across that gap.

title: Chamorro Standard Time 'ChST' forced tzdb to allow mixed-case zone abbreviations
body: US Congress (Public Law 106-564) and the tzdb maintainers (Paul Eggert)
whatChanged: When US Congress enacted Public Law 106-564 on December 23, 2000, formally naming the time zone of Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands 'Chamorro Standard Time', the tzdb maintainers adopted the mixed-case abbreviation 'ChST' (lowercase 'h') at the sponsoring Congressman's request and updated the theory guidelines to allow mixed-case letters in timezone abbreviations. The previous rule required all-uppercase abbreviations. The valid pattern became [-+[:alnum:]]{3,6} instead of [A-Z]{3,6}.
technicalImpact: Any parser, regex, or validator that assumes timezone abbreviations are all-uppercase will fail to match 'ChST'. The correct POSIX-compatible pattern is [-+[:alnum:]]{3,6}. Code using [A-Z]{3,6} silently drops ChST from Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands.
headline: The lowercase that broke the rulebook
blurb: A US Congressman's insistence on 'ChST' instead of 'CHST' forced timezone databases to abandon the all-caps rule for abbreviations.
story: When Congress officially named the time zone of Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands in December 2000, the sponsoring Congressman wanted the abbreviation written as 'ChST', with a lowercase 'h'. That single letter forced the tzdb maintainers to drop their long-standing rule that all timezone abbreviations must be uppercase. Software that validates abbreviations with a simple all-caps pattern will silently fail on any timestamp from Guam.

title: Etc/GMT zone names use inverted signs per POSIX convention
body: POSIX (IEEE Std 1003.1) and the IANA tz database (tzdb)
whatChanged: The Etc/GMT+N and Etc/GMT-N zones follow POSIX sign convention, where the sign is the opposite of geographic expectation: Etc/GMT+5 means UTC-5 (5 hours west of Greenwich), and Etc/GMT-8 means UTC+8 (8 hours east of Greenwich). This positive-west-of-Greenwich rule comes from early POSIX (IEEE Std 1003.1-1988); POSIX 1003.1-2001 later made these zone names unnecessary since equivalent POSIX TZ strings could express the same offsets. The inversion is documented explicitly in the tzdb etcetera file.
technicalImpact: Code that maps Etc/GMT+N to UTC+N produces incorrect timestamps. The inversion affects any library or application that reads IANA zone identifiers without understanding POSIX semantics. The tzdb etcetera file documents the inversion and recommends using geographic zone IDs instead.
headline: The timezone that goes the wrong way
blurb: In the Etc/GMT timezone family, the plus and minus signs mean the opposite of what almost every developer expects.
story: Most people assume Etc/GMT+5 means five hours ahead of Greenwich, but it actually means five hours behind. This follows a POSIX rule where the sign represents hours west of Greenwich, not east. The tzdb etcetera file spells this out plainly and warns developers not to rely on these zone names. It is one of the most common timezone bugs in software: a developer picks Etc/GMT+5 intending UTC+5, ships code that runs on UTC-5, and the error only surfaces when timestamps land in the wrong half of the globe.

title: Antarctica/Vostok: the tz maintainer admits the time there is a guess
body: Paul Eggert, the IANA tz database maintainer, recorded in 2001 that the historical time kept at Vostok Station was simply unknown, calling the situation hopelessly confusing. Antarctic stations sometimes set their clocks so that noon falls on the warmest part of the day rather than following any solar or meridian rule, which makes old records unreliable. The tzdb zone reflects this honestly: it carries an explicit unknown marker for the early decades rather than a confident offset. Only in December 2023 did the station commander give the tz list a firm answer, after which the zone formally moved to UTC+5.
whatChanged: Eggert noted on 2001-05-04 that Vostok Station's historical offset was unknown and hopelessly confusing. The tzdb zone marks the offset as unknown (-00) only before the station's December 1957 founding and again during the February-November 1994 closure; from founding onward it carries a best-guess UTC+7, then a documented switch to UTC+5 on 2023-12-18 at 02:00 once the station commander clarified the actual usage to the tz list.
technicalImpact: Any historical timestamp for Antarctica/Vostok before 2023 carries low confidence: the zone is built on inference and explicit unknowns, not verified records. Systems needing audit-grade accuracy should flag pre-2023 Vostok times as approximate. The 2023 shift to UTC+5 is the first well-documented Vostok offset change.
headline: Nobody knew what time it was at Vostok
blurb: The IANA tz database maintainer openly admitted that Vostok Station's historical time zone was a guess, not a known fact.
story: Vostok Station sits deep in Antarctica, and for decades no authoritative record confirmed what offset the researchers actually kept. In 2001, tzdb maintainer Paul Eggert wrote that the situation was hopelessly confusing and that stations there sometimes set noon to the warmest hour rather than the sun. Instead of inventing an offset, the database marked the early years as unknown. It took until December 2023, when the station commander told the tz list the real offset and the zone moved to UTC+5, for the record to gain any footing. Software relying on old Vostok timestamps is working with acknowledged guesswork.

title: Argentina's Senate kills DST law the Friday before the Sunday it was meant to start
body: Argentine Senate (Senado de la Nacion Argentina)
whatChanged: On Friday 22 June 2001, the last working day before a scheduled Sunday 24 June DST change, Argentina's Senate annulled Ley 25155, the 1999 law that had introduced DST. The law had been used for one summer (1999-2000) then suspended by presidential decree in March 2000; the Senate now repealed it outright, so the June 2001 reactivation never happened. Argentina remained at UTC-3 year-round.
technicalImpact: Any tz database snapshot older than a week would have encoded a DST transition that never happened. Systems caching zone data in advance could have shown clocks shifting on Sunday 24 June when no shift occurred. The episode highlights the need for near-real-time tz data distribution for countries with unstable DST policies.
headline: Cancelled at the last minute
blurb: Argentina's parliament scrapped a scheduled DST change with just two days' notice, on the Friday before the Sunday it was due to take effect.
story: Argentina passed a DST law in 1999 and observed one summer of it (clocks forward 3 Oct 1999, back 3 Mar 2000) before President de la Rua suspended it by decree, finding it raised winter energy use. In June 2001 the government tentatively scheduled a clock change to reactivate it. On the Friday before the planned Sunday, the Senate formally annulled the whole law. Software that had cached the upcoming transition even a week earlier would have shown a clock change that never came.

title: Brazil delays DST start to protect election results from voting-machine clock conflicts
body: Government of Brazil (presidential decrees 4,399 of 2002 and 5,920 of 2006)
whatChanged: In election years 2002 and 2006, Brazil postponed its DST start by roughly two weeks so it would not fall between the first and second rounds of the presidential election. DST began on 3 November 2002 (Decree 4,399) and 5 November 2006 (Decree 5,920) instead of the usual mid-October date. The same postponement applied in the 2004 municipal-election year, when DST began on 2 November 2004. Polling hours are fixed at 08:00-17:00 under the Electoral Code, and the voting machines could not be updated to reflect a mid-campaign clock change.
technicalImpact: Any system using a static October DST start rule for Brazil produced wrong local times in 2002 and 2006. The tz database carries single-year rules for each affected year (Rule Brazil 2002 only Nov 3; Rule Brazil 2006 only Nov 5), so software relying on outdated tzdata releases silently computed incorrect offsets for the weeks between the usual and actual DST transitions.
headline: The election that froze Brazil's clocks
blurb: Brazil pushed back its annual daylight saving clock change to avoid confusing voting machines mid-election.
story: Brazil runs its elections on fully computerized voting machines, and the Electoral Code sets fixed polling hours. In 2002 and 2006, the DST clock change was due to fall between the first and second rounds of the presidential election. The machines could not be updated mid-campaign, so rather than rewrite the electoral rules or overhaul the hardware, the government simply delayed DST by a couple of weeks. It was a fast fix that worked on election day but quietly broke any software relying on a static October start date for Brazilian DST.

title: Pakistan's erratic DST experiments (2002, 2008-2009) with Cabinet reversals
body: Government of Pakistan / Federal Cabinet
whatChanged: Pakistan trialed DST in 2002 only (clocks forward one hour from early April to early October). The Cabinet later reversed it, reportedly citing a rise in electricity consumption. DST was reintroduced in June 2008, then extended to end November 1. In 2009 DST ran April 15 to November 1, with the end date changed from October to November mid-season. The Cabinet then withdrew planned 2010 DST at the last minute. Six Rule Pakistan lines in tzdb cover the period.
technicalImpact: Systems with cached Pakistan DST rules were wrong multiple times across 2002 to 2009. Each last-minute date change required an emergency tzdata update; any software that did not apply the patch in time computed Pakistan offsets incorrectly. Asia/Karachi is the affected zone.
headline: Pakistan's on-again, off-again clocks
blurb: Pakistan toggled daylight saving time on and off repeatedly between 2002 and 2009, often changing the end date mid-season with little notice.
story: Pakistan introduced DST as a one-year trial in 2002, then scrapped it. Six years later DST came back, and the end date was pushed from August to November before the season was over. The same pattern repeated in 2009, and a planned 2010 revival was cancelled at the last minute. Every last-minute change meant any software relying on a previously cached Pakistan timezone rule was silently computing the wrong time until a tzdata patch landed.

title: Shanks and Pottenger's 'International Atlas' flagged as error-ridden source for South American tzdb data
body: Thomas G. Shanks and Rique Pottenger (authors); Paul Eggert / IANA tz maintainers (commentary)
whatChanged: The IANA tzdb southamerica file documents that its primary source for historical data through 1990, 'The International Atlas' (6th edition, 2003) by Shanks and Pottenger, contains many errors and cites no sources. Specific unverified claims include: Formosa switching to -03:00 on 1991-01-07, Misiones on 1990-12-29, Chaco on 1991-01-04, and Santiago del Estero switching to -04:00 on 1991-04-01, then to -03:00 on 1991-04-26.
technicalImpact: South American timezone data in tzdb through 1990 (and into the 1990-1991 transition era) is derived from a source the maintainers themselves flag as unreliable. Applications doing historical timestamp arithmetic on Argentine provinces or other South American regions in that era should treat tzdb offsets and transition dates as approximate, not authoritative.
headline: The atlas that tzdb says not to trust
blurb: The main historical source for South American time zone data before 1990 is a book that the tzdb maintainers openly acknowledge is full of errors and has no citations.
story: Most of what the world's computers know about South American clocks before 1990 comes from a single reference atlas by Thomas Shanks and Rique Pottenger. The tzdb file itself warns that the book contains many errors and cites no sources. Specific transition dates for Argentine provinces like Formosa, Misiones, Chaco, and Santiago del Estero are listed as unverified claims from that book. Any software doing historical date math on South American timestamps from that era is working from data the database's own authors do not fully trust.

title: tzdb documents repeated errors in Shanks and Pottenger's International Atlas
body: IANA tz database maintainers (Paul Eggert and contributors)
whatChanged: The tzdb europe file documents multiple factual errors in Thomas G. Shanks and Rique Pottenger's 'The International Atlas' (6th edition, 2003), which was a primary source for European time zone data. Confirmed wrong entries include: Wales DST start date (the atlas said 1921, but Wales matched Great Britain from 1880); the Eire 1916 transition count (the atlas listed two, there was only one); Northern Ireland's WW2 DST rules; the GB-Eire 1968 standard time change date; the Jersey, Guernsey, and Isle of Man 1921 date; Austrian 1918 and 1945 DST dates; and various French transition dates. In each case tzdb overrides Shanks with primary government or observatory sources.
technicalImpact: Any tool, astrological software, or time zone website that ingests Shanks and Pottenger directly without applying tzdb corrections inherits these errors. Historical timestamp calculations for affected European locations may be off by hours or days. The tzdb europe file flags each discrepancy inline so maintainers can trace the correction.
headline: The atlas that got Europe's clocks wrong
blurb: The book that underpinned decades of European time zone data contained errors that tzdb had to quietly correct entry by entry.
story: For years, a go-to reference for historical European time zones was a single atlas by Shanks and Pottenger, used by astrologers and database builders alike. When tzdb maintainers cross-checked it against government records and observatory archives, they found a string of wrong dates covering Britain, Ireland, Austria, France, and more. Each error was documented in the source file with a note explaining what the real primary source said. Software that skipped tzdb and read Shanks directly still carries those mistakes today.

title: Argentina splinters into 12 IANA time zones as provinces set clocks independently
body: Individual Argentine provincial governments, acting independently under national decrees and their own authority
whatChanged: Between 2004 and 2009, Argentine provinces made independent offset decisions driven by energy emergencies. In May-June 2004, several provinces shifted to UTC-4 on different dates (Mendoza on 2004-05-23, San Juan and San Luis on 2004-05-31, Catamarca/La Rioja/Rio Gallegos/Ushuaia on 2004-06-01, Tucuman on 2004-06-01 then back on 2004-06-13) while Buenos Aires and Cordoba stayed at UTC-3. In 2008, national Decree 1693/2008 implemented DST from 2008-10-19, but Decree 1705/2008 exempted 12 provinces; Jujuy also opted out independently. San Luis adopted its own DST rule (SanLuis) running until 2009-10-11. After 2009 the entire country settled at UTC-3 with no DST. The result is 12 permanently distinct IANA zones under the America/Argentina/ prefix: Buenos_Aires, Cordoba, Salta, Tucuman, La_Rioja, San_Juan, Jujuy, Catamarca (which also covers Chubut), Mendoza, San_Luis, Rio_Gallegos, and Ushuaia.
technicalImpact: Any application that stores or displays a single 'Argentina time' zone is wrong for timestamps during 2004-2009. The 12-zone split is permanent in tzdb; developers must use the correct province-level zone (e.g. America/Argentina/Mendoza, not America/Argentina/Buenos_Aires) for users in affected provinces. tzdb releases 2004a through 2009p track the incremental changes. Hardcoded ART (UTC-3) offsets produce silent errors for historical data from these provinces.
headline: Argentina's fractured clocks
blurb: Between 2004 and 2009, Argentine provinces set their clocks independently for energy reasons, forcing 12 separate time zone entries in the global tz database.
story: Argentina experienced an energy crisis starting in 2004, and provinces began shifting their clocks back an hour on their own schedules, sometimes reverting within days. A 2008 national DST law added more confusion when a second decree immediately exempted twelve provinces from following it. The province of San Luis went furthest, adopting its own unique DST rule that ran until late 2009. The chaos left a permanent mark: the tz database now requires 12 separate zone entries just for Argentina, one per province group. Software that treats Argentina as a single time zone will silently compute wrong times for any historical timestamp from this period.

title: Cuba skips fall-back for three years due to energy crisis (2004-2006)
body: Government of Cuba (Consejo de Estado)
whatChanged: Cuba did not revert to standard time in October 2004, citing a power shortage at the Antonio Guiteras thermoelectric plant. The fall-back was also skipped in 2005. Cuba remained on DST (UTC-4) year-round until October 29, 2006, when clocks were finally set back one hour. The tzdb Rule Cuba table has a spring-forward for 2004 but no fall-back rule until 2006.
technicalImpact: Any Cuba-aware scheduling system must account for a three-year window (late 2004 through October 2006) where the standard 'last Sunday in October' fall-back did not occur. Code relying on fixed DST patterns for America/Havana will produce wrong offsets for timestamps in this range.
headline: Cuba's three winters without a time change
blurb: Cuba held its clocks on summer time for three consecutive years because a key power plant couldn't handle the load of returning to standard time.
story: In late 2004, Cuba's Antonio Guiteras thermoelectric plant was struggling, and the government decided that keeping clocks on DST (UTC-4) year-round would reduce peak electricity demand. The same logic held through 2005. It wasn't until October 29, 2006 that Cubans finally set their clocks back. For software, this means the America/Havana timezone had no fall-back transition for three straight years, breaking any system that assumed the usual last-Sunday-in-October pattern.

title: Paul Eggert becomes editor/maintainer of the tz database
body: tz project community; Paul Eggert (UCLA)
whatChanged: Paul Eggert took over as editor and maintainer of the tz code and data on 2005-01-17. Eggert had earlier (1993-10-20) proposed the Area/Location naming convention now used throughout the database. He remains the long-running TZ Coordinator.
technicalImpact: Centralized editorial control under a single coordinator established the cadence of multiple releases per year (2021a, 2022g, 2024b) that OS and language vendors track. Vendor tzdata packages and the JVM tzupdater pull directly from Eggert's releases. Eggert designed the database's Area/Location naming convention (America/New_York, Europe/Paris) and serves as the IANA Designated Expert (the TZ Coordinator) under RFC 6557, with Tim Parenti as secondary, making him the single person most responsible for the data nearly all software trusts for civil time.

validation.supportingQuote: "Paul Eggert has been its editor and maintainer since 2005" and "Its uniform naming convention for entries in the database, such as America/New_York and Europe/Paris, was designed by Paul Eggert" (with a citation to "Eggert, Paul (20 October 1993). 'proposal for time zone names'").
validation.corrections: date: "2005" (year only; the day-precise 2005-01-17 is unsupported, Eggert became maintainer gradually during 2005, not via a discrete Jan-17 handover). All other fields (responsible body = tz community / Paul Eggert UCLA; 1993-10-20 Area/Location proposal; technical impact) are accurate as written.

headline: New keeper of the time zone records
blurb: Paul Eggert took charge of the shared database that tracks the world's time zone rules.
story: In 2005, Paul Eggert became the editor and maintainer of the widely used time zone database. Years earlier he had proposed the naming style still used for zones today. Under his lead, the database settled into a steady rhythm of several updates each year. Operating systems and programming tools follow these releases to stay current. The press later dubbed him the 'Time Zone King': one computer scientist at UCLA, treating the world's clock rules as a hobby, whom nearly every device quietly depends on.

sidenotes.label: 'Time Zone King': how one UCLA computer scientist keeps clocks ticking (UCLA)
'The largely untold story of how one guy keeps the world on the right time zone' (OneZero)
r/todayilearned: the guy responsible for the world's time zones

title: Antarctica/Troll: UTC+00 in winter, UTC+02 in summer with no UTC+01 middle step
body: Norwegian Polar Institute (operator of Troll Station, Queen Maud Land, Antarctica)
whatChanged: When Troll became a year-round station on 2005-02-12, its tz zone was defined with a 2-hour DST offset: UTC+00 standard (Antarctic winter) and UTC+02 in summer (last Sunday in March to last Sunday in October). UTC+01 is skipped entirely. This is the only IANA zone with a 2-hour DST save.
technicalImpact: Any code that assumes DST shifts are always 1 hour (a common hardcoded assumption) will be wrong by a full hour for Troll during both spring-forward and fall-back transitions. The zone appears in zone1970.tab and zonenow.tab labels it explicitly 'Troll DST'.
headline: The station that skips an hour of DST
blurb: Troll Station in Antarctica jumps two hours forward in summer and two hours back in winter, skipping the usual one-hour DST step entirely.
story: Troll, a Norwegian research station in Queen Maud Land, runs on UTC+00 during the dark Antarctic winter. When summer arrives, it jumps straight to UTC+02 (Norwegian summer time) so staff can coordinate with the home office in Norway without a confusing offset gap. No other IANA time zone uses a 2-hour DST save. Software that hardcodes a 1-hour DST assumption will compute clocks at Troll wrong by a full hour on both transition days.

title: Energy Policy Act of 2005 extends US DST starting 2007
body: US Congress (109th); signed by President George W. Bush (Public Law 109-58, H.R.6)
whatChanged: Section 110 amended the Uniform Time Act to extend DST by ~four weeks: start moved from first Sunday in April to second Sunday in March, end moved from last Sunday in October to first Sunday in November. Signed 2005-08-08; effective from 2007 (first observance 2007-03-11 to 2007-11-04).
technicalImpact: Caused the 'Year 2007 problem' (DST07): unpatched devices showed local time off by an hour during the new gaps (~2007-03-11 to 2007-04-01 and 2007-10-28 to 2007-11-04), triggering a global patching scramble across Unix/Linux zoneinfo, Java JRE, Windows registry, and embedded devices. Outlook/Exchange required calendar rebasing.

validation.supportingQuote: "The law amended the Uniform Time Act of 1966 by changing the start and end dates of daylight saving time, beginning in 2007. Clocks were set ahead one hour on the second Sunday of March (March 11, 2007) instead of on the first Sunday of April... Energy Policy Act of 2005 (Pub.L. 109-58) ... signed into law by President George W. Bush on August 8, 2005."

headline: United States makes daylight saving longer
blurb: A 2005 law stretched daylight saving time by about four weeks, starting in 2007.
story: In 2005 the United States passed a law to lengthen daylight saving time by roughly four weeks. The change moved the start earlier in spring and the end later in autumn, beginning in 2007. Devices that were not updated showed the wrong time by an hour during the new weeks. This led to a wide rush to patch computers, phones, and calendar software.

sidenotes.label: Stack Overflow: 'Java time zone is messed up' (2007 DST + TZUpdater)
The 2007 DST extension broke Outlook, Java and embedded calendars

academicRefs.authors: Kotchen, Matthew J. and Grant, Laura E.
academicRefs.title: Does Daylight Saving Time Save Energy? Evidence from a Natural Experiment in Indiana
academicRefs.venue: The Review of Economics and Statistics (MIT Press); also NBER Working Paper No. 14429
academicRefs.keyFinding: Using >7 million monthly household billing observations across southern Indiana over three years (a natural experiment from Indiana's staggered statewide DST adoption), DST INCREASES residential electricity demand by ~1% overall, rising to 2-4% in the fall. The mechanism is a tradeoff: DST reduces lighting demand but raises heating/cooling demand. Estimated cost to Indiana households ~$9 million/year in higher bills, plus $1.7-$5.5 million/year in pollution social costs; authors argue the effect is likely stronger elsewhere in the US.

title: IANA/Olson tz release 2005m encodes the EPAct 2005 DST rule ahead of 2007
body: tz (Olson/IANA) time zone database project; coordinator Arthur David Olson / Paul Eggert
whatChanged: Released 2005-08-29, tz 2005m updated the US Rule lines to the new second-Sunday-March / first-Sunday-November DST window mandated by EPAct 2005, ~18 months before its 2007-03-11 effective date so downstream OSes and runtimes could ship patches in time.
technicalImpact: The upstream data change every other system inherited. Vendors who shipped tzdata >= 2005m computed 2007 US local time correctly; those on older zoneinfo were off by an hour during extended-DST windows. Defined the patch baseline for the entire 2006-2007 DST remediation.

validation.supportingQuote: Update timezone data files to release 2005m of the zic database. Among other changes, this reflects the recently passed change in USA daylight savings rules.

headline: Time zone update prepares for new daylight saving
blurb: A 2005 release of the time zone database added the new United States daylight saving dates well before they took effect.
story: Soon after the United States enacted new daylight saving legislation, the shared time zone database was updated to include the new dates. This update arrived more than a year before the 2007 start, giving everyone time to prepare. Systems that picked up this version handled 2007 correctly. Systems using older data were off by an hour during the new weeks.

title: Leap second #23 inserted at 2005-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: IERS (Bulletin C 30, issued July 2005)
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 32s -> 33s. First leap second after a 7-year hiatus.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; first since the rise of large-scale Linux/NTP server fleets, surfacing dormant leap-second handling bugs and prompting many ops teams to re-validate NTP configs.
headline: First extra second in seven years
blurb: An extra second was added at the end of 2005, the first since 1998.
story: At the close of 2005, timekeepers added one extra second to the world clock. It was the first such second in about seven years. By this time many servers ran software that had never dealt with an added second before. The event uncovered hidden bugs and pushed many teams to recheck their time setup.

title: Indiana counties change time zones, creating a double clock-change ambiguity
body: U.S. Department of Transportation
whatChanged: A DOT final rule effective 2 a.m. EST on 2006-04-02 moved Starke, Pulaski, Knox, Daviess, Martin, Pike, Dubois, and Perry counties from the Eastern Time Zone to the Central Time Zone. Because the shift coincided with the start of DST, the literal text of the rule implied the affected counties would change their clocks twice in the same night. The tzdb acknowledges this explicitly and resolves the intended transition as 01:59:59 EST followed directly by 02:00:00 CDT.
technicalImpact: Software must treat this as a single wall-clock jump, not two sequential transitions. The tzdb resolves legislative intent rather than the literal rule text, meaning any implementation that naively replayed the rule as written would produce a phantom duplicate transition at 2 a.m. Zones affected include America/Indiana/Vincennes and related Indiana subzones, updated in the 2006 tzdb releases.
headline: The night Indiana's clocks almost changed twice
blurb: A 2006 federal rule shifting eight Indiana counties to Central Time landed on the same night clocks sprang forward for DST, creating a legal text that implied two clock changes in one night.
story: In early 2006, the U.S. Department of Transportation moved eight Indiana counties from Eastern to Central Time. The rule took effect at 2 a.m. EST on April 2, which was also the start of daylight saving time. Read literally, the counties would have had to set their clocks back an hour (to join Central Standard Time) and then forward an hour (for DST) in the same instant. The tzdb notes the rule was simply in error and records the intended outcome: 01:59:59 EST jumps straight to 02:00:00 CDT. Software that processes timezone transitions by replaying legal rule text verbatim could produce a phantom duplicate transition here.

title: Indiana adopts DST statewide, ending decades of county-by-county chaos
body: U.S. Department of Transportation; Indiana state legislature
whatChanged: Most of Indiana had observed no DST since around 1961, staying on EST (UTC-5) year-round. In April 2006 a DOT boundary ruling took effect, moving most counties to Eastern Time with DST while simultaneously shifting eight western counties (Starke, Pulaski, Knox, Daviess, Martin, Pike, Dubois, and Perry) from Eastern to Central Time. The tzdb backward file preserves three legacy link names, US/East-Indiana, America/Indianapolis, and America/Fort_Wayne, all pointing to America/Indiana/Indianapolis.
technicalImpact: Code that assumed Indiana was always UTC-5 with no DST (common for pre-2006 systems) returns wrong offsets for any timestamp after April 2, 2006. The America/Indiana/ subdirectory contains multiple distinct zones with independent county-level histories, so treating all Indiana timestamps as a single zone produces silent errors.
headline: Indiana's decades without DST
blurb: Indiana resisted Daylight Saving Time for nearly 50 years, leaving software to cope with a patchwork of county-level rules when it finally joined in 2006.
story: For more than four decades Indiana did not observe Daylight Saving Time, sitting on EST year-round while neighboring states shifted clocks twice a year. Different counties drifted onto different rules over time, giving the state one of the most fragmented timezone histories in North America. In 2006 a federal DOT ruling forced most counties to adopt EDT while reassigning eight western counties to Central Time, a double change that landed on the same clock night. Any system that hard-codes Indiana as a fixed UTC-5 region will calculate wrong local times for every Indiana timestamp from April 2006 onward.

title: Indiana adopts statewide DST and moves counties to Central Time
body: US DOT (time-zone boundary final rule, published in the Federal Register 2006-01-20)
whatChanged: After decades of most counties not observing DST, Indiana law required all 92 counties to observe DST from 2006-04-02. Simultaneously a DOT final rule moved eight counties from Eastern to Central Time; six later reverted to Eastern (Pulaski in March 2007, then five more in November 2007), leaving only Perry and Starke in Central.
technicalImpact: Forced creation/realignment of multiple IANA tz Indiana zones (America/Indiana/Indianapolis, /Vincennes, /Knox, /Petersburg, /Tell_City, /Winamac, /Marengo, etc.). Applications keying off a single 'Indiana' assumption broke; correct handling required per-county zone selection.

validation.supportingQuote: DOT is relocating the time zone boundary to move eight Indiana counties (Starke, Pulaski, Knox, Daviess, Martin, Pike, Dubois, and Perry) from Eastern to Central Time Zone... effective April 2, 2006. (Federal Register, "Standard Time Zone Boundary in the State of Indiana," published Jan 20, 2006, 71 FR 3228, Transportation Department / Office of the Secretary)

headline: Indiana finally adopts daylight saving
blurb: Indiana made all its counties observe daylight saving time and shifted some counties to a different time zone.
story: For many years most counties in Indiana did not change their clocks for daylight saving. A 2005 law (effective 2006) required all of its counties to start observing it. At the same time, several counties were moved into a neighboring time zone, and a few later moved back. Software that treated the whole state as one time zone broke and had to handle counties separately.

title: Canada matches the extended US DST schedule for 2007
body: Government of Canada plus most provincial/territorial governments
whatChanged: To stay aligned with the US and avoid cross-border disruption, most Canadian provinces/territories legislated through 2005-2006 to adopt the second-Sunday-March to first-Sunday-November DST window beginning in 2007. Saskatchewan (year-round standard time) was unaffected.
technicalImpact: Extended the Year-2007 DST patch scope to all Canadian America/* zones (Toronto, Vancouver, Winnipeg, Halifax, St_Johns); a 2006 tzdata release brought the Canadian zones into line with the US 2007 rule already added in the 2005 series, so unpatched Canadian systems showed identical one-hour offset errors during 2007 gaps.

validation.supportingQuote: Starting in 2007, the United States and Canada will start Daylight Saving Time (DST) on the second Sunday of March and end on the first Sunday of November. From 1987 to 2006, DST began on the first Sunday in April and ended on the last Sunday of October.
validation.corrections: tzRelease should be "2006d" (not "2005m"). tzdata 2005m covered only the US 2007 DST change; Canada's adoption of the new schedule was added in 2006d, per Paul Eggert's 2006-06-27 comment in the IANA tz northamerica file ("For now, assume all of DST-observing Canada will fall into line with the new US DST rules"). The technicalImpact sentence "the same tzdata 2005m+ update covered both countries" is inaccurate, US and Canada landed in separate releases (2005m for US, 2006d for Canada).

headline: Canada matches the new US clock schedule
blurb: Most of Canada changed its daylight saving dates to match the United States for 2007.
story: To stay in step with the United States and avoid confusion at the border, most parts of Canada updated their daylight saving dates for 2007. The new schedule started earlier in spring and ended later in autumn. One province that does not change its clocks was not affected. The same time zone update covered both countries, so unpatched Canadian systems showed the same one hour errors.

title: Western Australia reintroduces daylight saving time for a 3-year trial
body: Government of Western Australia (Carpenter Labor Government)
whatChanged: Western Australia enacted the Daylight Saving Act 2006, starting a 3-year DST trial on 3 December 2006. Clocks advanced one hour at 2:00 AM, shifting Australia/Perth from UTC+8 to UTC+9 each summer. The trial ran three consecutive summers (2006-07, 2007-08, 2008-09). A referendum on 16 May 2009 rejected permanent adoption with 54.56% voting no, and Perth reverted to UTC+8 year-round. tzdb rule AW 2006 (australasia file) encodes the 3 December start; tzdata 2006p (released 2006-11-27) shipped the fix.
technicalImpact: Australia/Perth gained a DST rule for the first time since 1992, requiring tzdata 2006p and OS/library patches on any system that cached Perth as a fixed UTC+8 zone. Applications that assumed Perth never observed DST produced wrong timestamps for the three trial summers. After the 2009 rejection, tzdb added a final back-to-standard rule (lastSun March 2009), requiring a further update.
headline: Perth's forgotten summer clocks
blurb: Western Australia turned its clocks forward for the first time in 14 years, then voted to stop after three summers.
story: Western Australia had rejected daylight saving in referendums in 1975, 1984, and 1992, but in 2006 its parliament passed a law forcing one more try. Starting 3 December 2006, Perth clocks jumped forward an hour each summer, putting the city at UTC+9 instead of its usual UTC+8. After three years, voters said no again in May 2009, this time by the widest margin yet. Software that treated Perth as a permanently fixed UTC+8 zone produced wrong timestamps across all three trial summers. The episode is a reminder that even zones with long stable histories can suddenly acquire DST rules.

title: Wisconsin fixes DST bar-closing conflict with 2007 Act 3
body: Wisconsin State Legislature
whatChanged: Before 2007, Wisconsin statute 175 set the spring DST clock-change at 01:00 standard time, while bar closing was defined as 02:00 wall clock. Each spring the clock jumped from 01:00 to 02:00 instantly, skipping that wall-clock hour entirely and generating annual citations for alcohol sales during the missing hour. Wisconsin 2007 Act 3 amended statutes 125.32, 125.68, and 175.095 to extend bar closing to 03:30 on the Sunday DST begins, resolving the conflict.
technicalImpact: The DST transition time (01:00 vs 02:00 wall clock) carries concrete legal consequences, not just scheduling ones. Systems that enforce business-hours rules around DST transitions must use the precise wall-clock transition moment from the tzdb, not just the date.
headline: The bar that closed before it opened
blurb: A quirk in Wisconsin law made bars legally open during an hour that did not exist on the clock, producing real citations every spring.
story: Wisconsin bars had to close at 02:00, but the DST clock-jump happened at 01:00, meaning the clock skipped straight from 01:00 to 02:00 before closing time ever arrived. Authorities still issued citations for alcohol sales during that missing hour each year. In 2007 the state legislature passed Act 3 to extend bar hours to 03:30 on DST Sunday, finally closing the loop. The case is a reminder that software enforcing time-based legal rules must use the exact wall-clock transition moment, not just a calendar date.

title: US and Canada extend DST by four weeks under the Energy Policy Act of 2005
body: United States Congress (Public Law 109-58, Energy Policy Act of 2005)
whatChanged: Public Law 109-58, signed 2005-08-08, moved the US spring-forward from the first Sunday of April to the second Sunday of March, and the fall-back from the last Sunday of October to the first Sunday of November. The new rules first applied on 2007-03-11 (spring) and 2007-11-04 (fall), adding three weeks of DST in spring and one in autumn. Systems still running pre-2007 rules computed wrong local time during the newly shifted gap weeks (March 11 through April 1 and October 28 through November 4). Microsoft Outlook and Exchange were the most visible casualties: recurring and all-day appointments in those windows displayed an hour off, and all-day events could span two days or lose their all-day formatting. The event was widely called 'Y2K7' or 'DST07'.
technicalImpact: Any unpatched system (OS, JRE, RDBMS, calendaring server, PDA, embedded clock) returned wrong UTC-to-local conversions during the four newly shifted weeks. Microsoft shipped OS patches and an Exchange calendar-rebasing tool; running the rebase tool out of order silently double-shifted appointments. IANA tzdb northamerica Rule US was updated to reflect 'Mar Sun>=8' and 'Nov Sun>=1' effective 2007 onward.
headline: Four extra weeks of daylight saving
blurb: A US law stretched daylight saving time by four weeks, and millions of computers and calendar apps showed the wrong time because their time zone rules had not been updated.
story: Congress passed a law in 2005 that pushed clocks forward three weeks earlier in spring and kept them there one week longer in autumn, starting in 2007. The goal was to save energy by giving people more evening daylight. Any device or software that had not received an update still followed the old rules and showed the wrong local time during those four new weeks. Calendar programs were hit hardest: meetings and all-day events in the newly shifted period appeared an hour late or spread across the wrong day. IT teams had to patch operating systems, update Java runtimes, and run special tools to repair existing calendar entries, sometimes more than once.

title: Energy Policy Act of 2005 extends US daylight saving time (effective 2007)
body: United States Congress (Public Law 109-58, signed by President George W. Bush on 2005-08-08)
whatChanged: Public Law 109-58 amended the Uniform Time Act of 1966 to move the US spring-forward from the first Sunday of April to the second Sunday of March, and the fall-back from the last Sunday of October to the first Sunday of November. The change took effect in spring 2007. Canada adopted matching rules. Congress retained the right to revert. The tzdb Rule US entry covers 2007-max with Mar Sun>=8 and Nov Sun>=1.
technicalImpact: Any system or library with hard-coded pre-2007 US DST rules (April/October boundaries) silently computes wrong local times for three to four weeks each spring and fall. This is the single most common source of tzdb update breakage in enterprise Java, .NET, and embedded systems that ship bundled tz data and skip updates. The tzdb release that first carried the new Rule US rows (2005m, released 2005-08-29) was needed to fix it; systems that did not apply it remained broken through every affected transition.
headline: The DST extension that broke enterprise calendars
blurb: A 2005 US law pushed daylight saving time three weeks earlier in spring and one week later in fall, starting in 2007, catching millions of systems with outdated timezone data off guard.
story: For decades, US clocks sprang forward on the first Sunday of April and fell back on the last Sunday of October. In 2005, Congress passed the Energy Policy Act, shifting the spring change to the second Sunday of March and the fall change to the first Sunday of November, hoping to save energy by adding more evening daylight. The new dates took effect in 2007, and Canada matched them. Any software, device, or server that had the old April and October boundaries baked in suddenly showed the wrong time for three to four weeks every year. Enterprise systems, meeting schedulers, trading platforms, and embedded devices that skipped timezone database updates were all affected. The episode became the most-cited example of why keeping timezone data current is not optional.

title: US/Canada extended DST takes effect, triggering the Outlook/Exchange calendar 'mini Y2K'
body: U.S. Congress (via the Energy Policy Act of 2005); Microsoft Corporation (patch and tooling response)
whatChanged: The Energy Policy Act of 2005 moved the US and Canadian DST start from the first Sunday in April to the second Sunday in March, and the end from the last Sunday in October to the first Sunday in November, adding three weeks at the spring end and one week at the fall end. The new schedule first applied on 2007-03-11. Systems running pre-patch Windows still encoded the old 1986 rules, so calendar items falling in the new windows (March 11 to April 1, and October 28 to November 4) displayed one hour off. Microsoft shipped OS patches and the Time Zone Data Update Tool (TZMOVE.EXE) to rebase affected Outlook/Exchange appointments.
technicalImpact: Unpatched Windows systems applied old DST rules, causing Outlook and Exchange calendar appointments in the two new DST windows to display one hour late. Recurring meetings were worst affected; all-day events could shift to span two days or revert to 1:00 AM appointments. Administrators had to patch Windows, then run TZMOVE.EXE against each mailbox to rebase stored meeting timestamps. BlackBerry sync, Kerberos authentication, and any service relying on system time were also at risk. The tzdata community tracked the change in the 2005m release.
headline: The calendar hour that went missing in March
blurb: A 2005 US law moved daylight saving time three weeks earlier, and millions of calendar appointments in Outlook and Exchange displayed the wrong time until Microsoft shipped a patch and a rebasing tool.
story: In 2005, the US Congress passed an energy law that shifted the start of daylight saving time from April to mid-March. Most people did not notice until 2007, when the rule first applied on March 11. Computers running unpatched Windows still knew only the old schedule, so any meeting booked in the new early-spring or late-fall window appeared one hour wrong to anyone who had not yet updated. Microsoft had to ship both an OS patch and a separate tool, TZMOVE.EXE, that went through every saved calendar item and shifted its stored timestamp. The episode earned the nickname 'mini Y2K' because it forced a coordinated, deadline-driven update campaign across servers, desktops, and mobile devices, and it remains a textbook example of why stored timestamps must carry explicit timezone metadata rather than relying on the local system clock.

title: Venezuela moves to UTC-4:30 under Chavez, then back to UTC-4 under Maduro
body: Government of Venezuela (Hugo Chavez, then Nicolas Maduro)
whatChanged: Venezuela shifted from UTC-4 to UTC-4:30 on 2007-12-09 at 03:00, with Hugo Chavez citing geography (the country sits between standard meridians) and a desire for schoolchildren to travel in daylight. Nicolas Maduro reversed it on 2016-05-01 at 02:30, returning the country to UTC-4, citing power and water shortages.
technicalImpact: UTC-4:30 is a non-standard half-hour offset that breaks integer-hour timezone assumptions. Both transitions occurred at non-midnight times (03:00 and 02:30), requiring explicit tzdb entries. Systems using stale tz data would misplace Venezuela clocks by 30 minutes during the 2007-2016 window and again after the 2016 reversal.
headline: Chavez's half-hour detour
blurb: Venezuela spent nine years on an unusual half-hour offset after Hugo Chavez moved the clocks 30 minutes back in 2007, only for his successor to undo it in 2016.
story: In December 2007, Hugo Chavez shifted Venezuela's clocks by half an hour to UTC-4:30, calling it a more natural fit for the country. The offset was non-standard, sitting between the usual whole-hour zones most software expects. Nine years later, Maduro reversed the change amid an energy crisis, moving the clocks forward 30 minutes back to UTC-4. Every system handling Venezuelan timestamps needed two separate tzdb updates, and any code assuming whole-hour offsets broke both times.

title: Venezuela shifts to a half-hour offset, UTC-4:00 to UTC-4:30
body: Government of Venezuela (President Hugo Chavez)
whatChanged: At 03:00 on 2007-12-09 Venezuela set clocks back 30 minutes, moving America/Caracas from UTC-4:00 to UTC-4:30 (legal meridian moved to 67°30' W), reviving the offset used 1912-1965. Stated as an energy/'physiological' measure. The half-hour offset stayed until 2016.
technicalImpact: A rare 30-minute offset broke software assuming whole-hour UTC offsets, hard-coded -4 constants, integer-hour offset storage, and hourly-only zone pickers. The 03:00 -> 02:30 step produced a 30-minute backward discontinuity. Unpatched systems remained 30 minutes off.

validation.supportingQuote: "Venezuela adjusted its time zone from UTC-4 hours to UTC-4:30 hours by turning clocks back 30 minutes at 3:00 AM on December 9, 2007." (corroborated across timeanddate.com and Wikipedia; President Hugo Chavez decreed the change, centered on meridian 67 degrees 30' West, reviving the offset Venezuela used 1912-1964.)

headline: Venezuela sets clocks back half an hour
blurb: In 2007 Venezuela moved its clocks back by thirty minutes instead of a full hour.
story: On a December morning in 2007, Venezuela set its clocks back by thirty minutes. This put the country on an unusual half hour offset that it had used long ago. The half hour setting stayed in place until 2016. Software that assumed time zones always shift by whole hours showed the wrong time, and unpatched systems stayed thirty minutes off.

title: Venezuela adopts UTC-4:30 under Chavez, then reverts to UTC-4 in 2016
body: Government of Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic)
whatChanged: On 2007-12-09 at 03:00, Venezuela moved clocks back 30 minutes from UTC-4:00 to UTC-4:30, creating Venezuelan Standard Time (VET). On 2016-05-01 at 02:30, clocks advanced 30 minutes back to UTC-4:00, ending the half-hour offset after nearly nine years.
technicalImpact: Any software that assumed Venezuela was on a whole-hour offset (-4 or -5) produced wrong times for the entire 2007 to 2016 window. The 30-minute step also exposed bugs in code that stored offsets as integer hours. Covered by tzdb releases 2007j (initial change) and 2016d (revert). America/Caracas is the affected zone.
headline: Venezuela's half-hour detour
blurb: Venezuela spent nine years on a quirky half-hour offset before quietly reversing it.
story: In December 2007, President Chavez pushed Venezuela's clocks back 30 minutes, arguing the shift gave schoolchildren more morning daylight. The move put the country on UTC-4:30, an unusual half-hour offset used by almost no other nation in the Americas, and a return to the offset Venezuela had kept from 1912 to 1965. Nine years later, in May 2016, the government reversed the change with little fanfare, returning to UTC-4. Any app that hard-coded Venezuela as -4 or -5 was silently wrong for nearly a decade.

title: Argentina reintroduces DST with weeks of notice, forcing emergency tzdata2007k patches
body: Argentine National Congress (Camara de Diputados and Senate)
whatChanged: Argentina legislated nationwide DST effective 2007-12-30 00:00 local time (clocks sprang forward to 01:00, shifting from ART UTC-3 to ARST UTC-2). DST was set to end 2008-03-16. The law passed the lower chamber just days before the clock change. The tz project shipped tzdata2007k on 2007-12-31 to cover the change. San Luis province subsequently refused to follow the national rule and reverted roughly three weeks later, requiring a permanent America/Argentina/San_Luis sub-zone. Argentina abandoned DST entirely after the 2008-2009 summer.
technicalImpact: Late-notice legislation forced out-of-band tzdata2007k patches across operating systems, JREs (Sun shipped a dedicated tzupdater for JDK-6645783), and databases mid-cycle. San Luis's defection added a new America/Argentina sub-zone that downstream systems caching stale tzdb data had to absorb separately. Any system that hard-coded Argentina as a single zone broke when provinces diverged.
headline: Argentina's last-minute DST comeback
blurb: Argentina brought back daylight saving time with only days to spare, forcing an emergency update to the global timezone database used by every computer.
story: Argentina had dropped daylight saving time in 2000. In late December 2007 its congress voted to bring it back, passing the law just days before the scheduled clock change on December 30. The tz project, which maintains the timezone data used by nearly every operating system, had to rush out a new release (2007k) on New Year's Eve to keep clocks correct. Making things worse, San Luis province decided not to follow the national rule roughly three weeks later and kept its own offset, so Argentina's time zones had to be split into multiple entries in the database. The episode is a textbook case of why late-notice policy changes create cascading failures across every piece of software that works with time.

title: Argentina's 2007-2009 DST revival fractures into 13 provincial holdouts
body: Government of Argentina (national decrees) and provincial governments of Catamarca, La Rioja, Mendoza, Salta, San Juan, San Luis, La Pampa, Neuquen, Rio Negro, Chubut, Santa Cruz, Tierra del Fuego, and Jujuy
whatChanged: Argentina restarted DST on 2007-12-30 (clocks forward 1 hour). Decree 1693/2008 applied Law 26.350 for summer 2008-2009, setting DST from 2008-10-19 to 2009-03-15. Decree 1705/2008 then exempted 12 provinces from observing it. Jujuy self-exempted outside the decree by a separate provincial announcement. By October 2009 the national government announced no further DST, ending the experiment.
technicalImpact: During 2008-2009, Argentina split into two effective offsets: UTC-2 for the provinces observing DST (standard UTC-3 plus one hour), and UTC-3 year-round for the 13 holdouts. Any software or tz database using a single Argentina timezone was wrong for most of the country. The America/Argentina/* zones were needed to capture the divergence, and the episode prompted repeated tzdata updates across 2008-2009.
headline: Argentina's DST that most provinces ignored
blurb: Argentina brought back daylight saving in 2007, but 13 of its provinces refused to observe it, leaving the country split across two effective time offsets for over a year.
story: After years without DST, Argentina turned the clocks forward on the last day of 2007. When the government tried to extend the scheme into the 2008-2009 summer, 12 provinces got a legal exemption and a 13th simply opted out on its own. The national government quietly dropped DST entirely in 2009 rather than enforce it. For software, the episode meant Argentina was not one timezone but many, and keeping tzdata correct required a string of rapid releases.

title: Egypt: Ramadan-driven DST suspensions and reinstatements (2008-2016)
body: Government of Egypt (Cabinet of Ministers and President of Egypt)
whatChanged: Egypt repeatedly shifted or suspended DST to avoid overlap with Ramadan. In 2008, DST ended the last Thursday of August instead of September. In 2009 it ended August 20. In 2010 it ended August 10 (Ramadan), restarted September 9, then ended again the last Thursday of September, producing four clock changes in one year. In 2011 the interim cabinet cancelled DST entirely. In 2014 it was reinstated (May 15), suspended for Ramadan (June 26), reinstated again (July 31), then ended normally in September: four transitions again. In 2015 President Sisi cancelled DST permanently. In 2016 DST was announced to start July 7 but cancelled on July 4, before it took effect; the released tzdata update had already propagated the phantom transition to many systems. DST resumed in 2023 under a new law.
technicalImpact: Africa/Cairo became one of the most irregular zones in the IANA database, with up to four DST transitions in a single calendar year (2010, 2014). Any software that caches or precomputes Egyptian wall-clock time for 2008-2016 is likely wrong. The 2010 sequence alone (spring forward Apr, fall back Aug 10, spring forward Sep 9, fall back Sep lastThu) breaks systems that assume at most two DST boundaries per year.
headline: Egypt's Ramadan clock shuffle
blurb: Egypt repeatedly suspended and reinstated daylight saving time around Ramadan, creating years with three or four clock changes.
story: Egypt observes Ramadan on the Islamic lunar calendar, which shifts roughly 11 days earlier each Gregorian year. When Ramadan fell during the hot summer months, the Egyptian government decided to turn clocks back during the fast so workers would not suffer through the hottest part of the day without food or water. This meant some years saw clocks change direction four times instead of the usual two. The practice flip-flopped so often that DST was cancelled outright in 2011, partially reinstated in 2014, cancelled again in 2015, announced but cancelled days before it took effect in 2016, and finally stabilised only in 2023. For software, Africa/Cairo became a minefield: any system that assumed Egypt followed a simple annual spring-forward, fall-back pattern would silently produce wrong local times for large stretches of this era.

title: San Luis province breaks from Argentina's national DST schedule
body: Government of San Luis Province, Argentina
whatChanged: On 2008-01-21, San Luis broke from the rest of Argentina, switching to UTC-4 standard time with its own SanLuis DST rules; because DST was active at that moment its clock read UTC-3, and it ended that DST season on the 2nd Sunday of March (2008-03-08), earlier than the federal 2008-03-16. Its SanLuis rules used the second Sunday of October and March, versus the federal third Sunday. A 2009 provincial law contained an ambiguity that broke any year where March 1 or October 1 fell on a Sunday. San Luis oscillated between UTC-4 (standard) and UTC-3 (DST) until 2009-10-11, when it stopped switching and settled at UTC-3 year-round under the legal fiction of being UTC-4 on permanent DST. The tzdb models this as plain UTC-3 standard time.
technicalImpact: A unique SanLuis rule set in tzdb covers only 2007-2009 and differs from all other Argentine rules. The ambiguous 2009 law text caused disputed transition dates. The legal fiction of permanent DST at UTC-4 required explicit handling in tzdb to silence POSIX TZ warnings post-2038.
headline: Argentina's rogue province clock
blurb: San Luis province split from Argentina's national time rules and ran its own DST schedule for two years before settling on a permanent legal fiction.
story: In January 2008, San Luis broke from the rest of Argentina and ended daylight saving time months early, forcing tzdb to create a separate set of rules just for one province. The province then wrote its own DST law with a wording flaw that produced an ambiguous transition date whenever the first of March or October landed on a Sunday. By 2010, San Luis simply stopped switching clocks but called UTC-3 summer time while pretending to be UTC-4 on permanent DST. Software that assumed all Argentine provinces shared one set of rules would silently produce wrong timestamps for anyone in San Luis.

title: Asia/Calcutta renamed to Asia/Kolkata in the tz database
body: IANA tz database maintainers (Paul Eggert et al.), following India's official city renaming
whatChanged: The canonical tz identifier was changed from Asia/Calcutta to Asia/Kolkata in tzdb release 2008b. A backward-compatibility link (Asia/Calcutta -> Asia/Kolkata) was added to the backward file so legacy identifiers kept working.
technicalImpact: Java and Joda-Time shipped 'Asia/Calcutta' as the default IST zone string for years. Any hardcoded string, database column, or config file using 'Asia/Calcutta' still resolves correctly only if the tzdb backward file is present; environments that strip backward links silently break IST lookups.
headline: Calcutta becomes Kolkata in the tz database
blurb: When India officially renamed Calcutta to Kolkata, the tz database followed seven years later, retiring 'Asia/Calcutta' as the canonical zone name.
story: India officially renamed Calcutta to Kolkata in 2001, but the tz database kept 'Asia/Calcutta' as the canonical identifier until the 2008b release. The maintainers then promoted 'Asia/Kolkata' to canonical and added a backward-compatibility link so that old identifiers would not immediately break. Software that hardcoded 'Asia/Calcutta', including early Java and Joda-Time defaults, relies on that backward link being present. Strip the backward file from a deployment and IST lookups fail silently.

title: Brazil Law 11.662: Acre moves from UTC-5 to UTC-4, eliminating Brazil's westernmost time zone
body: Government of Brazil (Lei 11.662, signed by President Lula, proposed by Senator Tiao Viana of Acre)
whatChanged: Effective 00:00 on 2008-06-24, Brazilian Law 11.662 (enacted 2008-04-24) eliminated UTC-5 from Brazil entirely. Acre state (America/Rio_Branco) and western Amazonas (America/Eirunepe) moved from UTC-5 to UTC-4. All of Para moved to UTC-3 (previously only eastern Para was at UTC-3), and a new zone America/Santarem was created for western Para, which had been UTC-4. Brazil dropped from four distinct UTC offsets to three.
technicalImpact: Any application caching America/Rio_Branco or America/Eirunepe as UTC-5 returned times one hour behind after 2008-06-24. A brand-new IANA zone (America/Santarem) was created, breaking code that enumerated Brazilian zones without it. tzdb release 2008d covered the change.
headline: The time zone merger that wiped out UTC-5
blurb: Brazil killed its westernmost time zone overnight, shifting Acre and western Amazonas an hour forward and inventing a brand-new zone for western Para.
story: Senator Tiao Viana of Acre pushed the change after constituents complained about how their local clocks lined up with the rest of the country. Law 11.662 took effect at midnight on 24 June 2008, shifting Acre and western Amazonas one hour forward and merging all of Para into a single offset. Brazil went from four UTC offsets to three in one night. Software that hard-coded UTC-5 for Rio Branco, or that had no entry for the new Santarem zone, silently produced wrong times from that date forward.

title: Leap second #24 inserted at 2008-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: IERS (Bulletin C 36, issued July 2008)
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 33s -> 34s. First since 2005-12-31.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second. Oracle Cluster Ready Services / Clusterware misbehaved when NTP adjusted time across the leap second, causing cluster node reboots; Oracle recommended running ntpd with slewing (-x). Also the leap second when Google first deployed its own clock smear, a 20-hour cosine smear, across its services.

validation.supportingQuote: Scientists added a second on 31 December 2008, meaning the final countdown required 11 rather than 10 seconds to bring us into the New Year to allow for the time to hit 23:59:60. ... Oracle's Cluster Ready Services software failed to handle this leap second, causing servers ... to reboot automatically after midnight on New Year's Day 2009.

headline: Extra second trips up a database cluster
blurb: An added second at the end of 2008 caused some Oracle cluster servers to restart.
story: At the end of 2008, one extra second was added to the world clock, the first since 2005. When time syncing software adjusted for it, certain Oracle cluster systems misbehaved and rebooted their nodes. Oracle advised changing the time settings to ease the second in gradually. This was also the second that Google used to first deploy its own smoothing approach, a 20-hour cosine smear, across its services.

title: Zune 30 mass freeze from leap-year infinite loop (2008-12-31)
body: Microsoft (Zune firmware); Freescale Semiconductor (MC13783 PMIC clock driver)
whatChanged: At midnight 2008-12-31, every Microsoft Zune 30 (and Toshiba Gigabeat S) froze and became unresponsive. A day-counting loop in the Freescale MC13783 PMIC clock driver correctly subtracted 365 days per year but, on day 366 of a leap year, never decremented the counter to exit the loop. Devices were bricked for roughly 24 hours until the calendar crossed into 2009-01-01, making the loop condition false. The workaround was to drain the battery and recharge after midday GMT on 2009-01-01.
technicalImpact: Classic leap-year off-by-one that turned into a true infinite loop: the outer loop ran while days > 365, but the inner guard only handled days > 366, so on day 366 of a leap year neither branch executed and the counter never moved. No tzdb change needed; the bug lived in firmware. Underscored that date math in embedded firmware must be tested at year boundaries, leap-year day 365, and day 366.
headline: The day every Zune 30 froze
blurb: Every Microsoft Zune 30 media player locked up on New Year's Eve 2008 because a leap-year bug trapped the device in an infinite loop.
story: On December 31, 2008, millions of Zune 30 owners woke up to a frozen screen. The culprit was a date-conversion routine in the clock driver: when counting days since 1980, it handled leap years by checking for day 366, but never moved the counter past that last day, so it looped forever. The same bug hit Toshiba Gigabeat S players, which shared the Freescale driver. Microsoft's only fix was to let the battery drain completely and recharge after midday GMT on January 1, 2009, once the internal clock advanced past day 366 and the loop condition finally cleared. The incident became a textbook warning about testing date arithmetic at year and leap-year boundaries.

title: Davis Station oscillates between UTC+5 and UTC+7 across two Antarctic summers
body: Australian Antarctic Division
whatChanged: Davis Station (Antarctica) shifted from UTC+7 to UTC+5 on 2009-10-18 02:00 local, reverted to UTC+7 on 2010-03-10 20:00 UTC, shifted back to UTC+5 on 2011-10-28 02:00 local, then reverted permanently to UTC+7 on 2012-02-21 20:00 UTC. Two of the four transition instants are expressed in UTC (suffix 'u' in the tzdb definition) rather than wall-clock time.
technicalImpact: The zone definition mixes wall-clock and UTC-anchored UNTIL timestamps in the same block. Libraries that naively apply the stored offset to compute the transition instant produce the wrong moment for the two 'u'-suffixed transitions. Any tz database consumer must handle the universal-time vs wall-clock distinction correctly.
headline: The station that couldn't pick a time
blurb: Australia's Davis Station in Antarctica toggled its clocks two hours backward and then forward again across two consecutive summers before settling back on its original time.
story: Davis Station sits on the Vestfold Hills in East Antarctica and normally runs seven hours ahead of UTC. The Australian Antarctic Division tried aligning it two hours closer to Hobart for the 2009-10 and 2011-12 summers, but reverted both times. What makes it tricky for software is that two of the four clock changes are recorded in the tz database anchored to UTC rather than local wall time, so a naive offset calculation gives the wrong transition instant.

title: 2009 MENA DST cluster: Morocco, Tunisia, Egypt, Syria, Jordan, and Palestine all change rules
body: Governments of Morocco, Tunisia, Egypt, Syria, Jordan, and Palestine (Palestinian Authority)
whatChanged: Six countries across the Middle East and North Africa each adjusted DST in 2009, mostly to keep clock changes from overlapping Ramadan. Morocco observed DST only from 2009-06-01 to 2009-08-21. Tunisia abolished DST permanently (last observed in 2008). Egypt ended DST on 2009-08-21 rather than the last Thursday of September. Syria moved its start to the last Friday in March. Jordan clarified its last-Thursday rule. Palestine observed DST roughly late March to early September.
