technicalImpact: The short-notice changes required several tzdata point releases in quick succession during 2009, and Java, ICU, and OS timezone packages all needed patching. Systems that cached the year's DST schedule early had already computed wrong transition times before the corrections shipped.
headline: Six clocks, one Ramadan
blurb: In 2009, six Middle East and North Africa countries changed their daylight saving rules at short notice, most to avoid the clock shift falling inside Ramadan.
story: Governments across the region decided that pushing clocks forward while millions of people were fasting created practical headaches with prayer times and daily schedules. Each country picked a different fix: some shortened DST, one skipped it entirely, others just moved the transition date. The decisions came weeks or days before the changes were due, forcing tzdata maintainers to ship several releases in a single year. Any system that had not applied all the patches could show appointment times an hour off for users in the region.

title: Tunisia cancels DST for Ramadan, then drops it permanently
body: Government of Tunisia
whatChanged: Tunisia did not observe DST in 2009 or 2010, widely reported to be because the DST window overlapped with Ramadan fasting. The last DST transition was in October 2008; from 2009 onward Tunisia has remained on UTC+1 year-round, with no DST rules active in the tzdb Africa/Tunis zone.
technicalImpact: Tunisia uses CET (UTC+1) without DST, matching EU winter time but not EU summer time. Systems that infer DST rules from the UTC+1 offset or treat Tunisia as EU-equivalent will apply a spurious +1 hour shift each spring. The tzdb Africa/Tunis zone reflects no DST transitions after 2008.
headline: When Ramadan stilled the clock change
blurb: Tunisia scrapped daylight saving time in 2009 as the DST window fell during Ramadan, and never brought it back.
story: In 2009 Tunisia was due to move its clocks forward in late March as usual, but Ramadan that year fell squarely in the summer months when DST would have been in effect. Reports at the time said the government decided the disruption to fasting schedules was not worth it and kept the clocks still. Tunisia did not reinstate DST in 2010 or any subsequent year, leaving the clocks on UTC+1 permanently. For software, the danger is that Tunisia sits at UTC+1 alongside most of Europe in winter, so any system that guesses DST rules from the base offset will wrongly push Tunis clocks forward each spring.

title: tzdb explicitly disclaims being authoritative for civil time
body: IANA tz database maintainers (originally Arthur David Olson; now Paul Eggert and the tz community)
whatChanged: The tzdb README states a project goal of providing data 'useful even if not 100% accurate', and adds that 'The information in the time zone data files is by no means authoritative; fixes and enhancements are welcome.' The accompanying theory file goes further: 'The tz database is not authoritative, and it surely has errors.' These disclaimers are a long-standing, enduring characteristic of the project rather than a single dated event. Governments change timekeeping rules erratically and with little warning, pre-1970 data is incomplete, and errors remain.
technicalImpact: Software that treats tzdb offsets as legally authoritative for financial, legal, or audit timestamps is on shaky ground. Applications requiring audit-grade accuracy must cross-reference official government gazettes or national standards bodies rather than relying solely on tzdb.
headline: The database that admits it might be wrong
blurb: The world's most widely used time zone database openly states it is not authoritative and may contain errors.
story: Almost every computer, phone, and server on the planet uses the IANA tz database to know what time it is in any given place. Yet the project's own README admits it aims to be 'useful even if not 100% accurate', and states that 'The information in the time zone data files is by no means authoritative'. Governments can and do change their clocks with little or no warning, and the database simply does its best to keep up. For most apps this is fine, but for legal contracts, financial settlements, or audit logs, relying on tzdb alone is a risk that the project itself warns you about.

title: Pakistan's last DST season ends in confusion, clocks abandoned permanently in 2010
body: Cabinet of Pakistan
whatChanged: Pakistan observed DST for its final season in 2009 (clocks forward +1h on April 15, 2009; back at midnight November 1, 2009). The end date was reversed once, from October 1 to November 1, days before it took effect. In March 2010 the government announced DST would restart on April 1, then withdrew the decision after parliament withheld support. Pakistan has not observed DST since. Earlier attempts: 2002 (April 7 to October 6) and 2008 (June 1 to November 1).
technicalImpact: The reversed 2009 end date forced a corrective tzdata release: tzdata2009n moved Pakistan's end to October 1, and tzdata2009o restored it to November 1. Systems that had received tzdata2009n but not tzdata2009o would have switched Pakistan back to standard time a month early. Any software or device without tzdata2009o or later showed Pakistan time as UTC+5 starting October 1 instead of correctly holding UTC+6 until November 1, 2009.
headline: Pakistan's clock that couldn't pick an end date
blurb: Pakistan's last attempt at daylight saving time saw its end date reversed at the last minute, from October 1 to November 1, tripping up software around the world.
story: Pakistan tried daylight saving time in 2002, dropped it, then revived it in 2008 and 2009 to deal with severe electricity shortages. In 2009 the government could not settle on when to turn the clocks back: it first set October 1, then reversed itself days before, settling on November 1. The change arrived late and required a new tzdata patch release. In early 2010 the government announced it would try again, then cancelled when parliament refused to support it. Pakistan has not touched its clocks since. The last-minute change left a trail of devices and services showing the wrong Pakistan time for weeks at a time.

title: Bangladesh's one-year DST experiment (2009)
body: Government of Bangladesh
whatChanged: Bangladesh introduced DST on 2009-06-19 at 23:00 local time, advancing clocks one hour to UTC+7 to save energy during a power crisis. No end date was announced at launch; the government later set December 31, 2009 as the close. A plan to repeat DST in 2010 (March 31 to October 31) was briefly announced but cancelled in March 2010. The Rule Dhaka entry in the tzdb asia file applies only to 2009.
technicalImpact: Software that assumed Bangladesh had no DST was wrong for the entire second half of 2009. The non-midnight transition time (23:00) is unusual and tripped parsers expecting midnight or 02:00 rollover. Any system that hard-coded UTC+6 for Asia/Dhaka silently produced wrong timestamps for roughly six months.
headline: Bangladesh's six-month clock experiment
blurb: Bangladesh tried daylight saving time for six months in 2009 to cut electricity use, then quietly dropped it.
story: A power crisis in 2009 pushed Bangladesh to shift clocks forward one hour starting June 19, with clocks moving at 23:00 the night before, not at midnight. The government promised the change would reduce peak demand; it trimmed about 200 megawatts but also meant children had to leave for school before sunrise. By December the experiment was declared over, and 2010 went ahead without DST. The episode shows how quickly a country can add or remove DST, and why software should never cache 'this country has no DST' as a permanent fact.

title: Bangladesh introduces daylight saving time for the first time (UTC+6 to UTC+7, Jun 2009)
body: Government of Bangladesh (Power Division, confirmed by Bangladesh Telecommunication Regulatory Commission)
whatChanged: Bangladesh moved clocks forward by one hour at 23:00 local time on 19 June 2009 (jumping to 00:00 on 20 June), shifting Asia/Dhaka from UTC+6 to UTC+7. This was the country's first-ever DST observance, introduced to conserve electricity during a severe power shortage. Clocks were set back at midnight on 31 December 2009. The cabinet permanently cancelled DST in March 2010, so it lasted only one season.
technicalImpact: tzdata required multiple rushed releases: 2009i (initial rule, which used new-format syntax that broke older glibc), 2009j (glibc workaround and the release that actually landed in major distros before the transition), 2009o (follow-up after the indefinite-extension announcement), and 2009u (the December rollback). Systems running stale tzdata before 2009i showed Asia/Dhaka one hour behind actual local time for the entire DST window. The short notice between announcement and activation (days, not weeks) meant many production systems were caught without updated rules.
headline: Bangladesh's one-season clock
blurb: Facing rolling blackouts, Bangladesh turned clocks forward an hour in 2009, the first and only time it ever observed daylight saving.
story: In 2009 Bangladesh was losing power for several hours a day, with peak demand of around 5,000 megawatts against available generation of roughly 3,800. The government decided the cheapest fix was to shift the clock forward so people used less electricity in the evening. Clocks jumped ahead one hour just before midnight on 19 June. An end date of 30 September was set in the original announcement, but the change was extended open-endedly that October before clocks finally went back on 31 December. The cabinet scrapped the idea entirely in early 2010. The episode forced three separate emergency releases of the timezone database because the decision came with almost no lead time, leaving software around the world showing the wrong time for Bangladesh.

title: Casey Station jumps 3 hours to UTC+11 for one Antarctic summer
body: On 2009-10-18 at 02:00 local time, Casey Station in Antarctica shifted from UTC+8 to UTC+11, a 3-hour jump. On the same date Davis moved from UTC+7 to UTC+5 and Mawson from UTC+6 to UTC+5. Casey reverted to UTC+8 on 2010-03-05. The move was not a daylight-saving rule but a plain offset change to line up station clocks with resupply and communication corridors.
whatChanged: On 2009-10-18 at 02:00 local time, Casey Station (Antarctica) shifted from UTC+8 to UTC+11, a 3-hour jump. Davis moved from UTC+7 to UTC+5 and Mawson from UTC+6 to UTC+5 on the same date. Casey reverted to UTC+8 on 2010-03-05. The change was not a DST rule but a plain offset change to align station clocks with resupply and communication corridors.
technicalImpact: A 3-hour non-DST wall-clock jump with no DST rule attachment. Systems that assume offsets shift by 1 hour, or only on DST schedule boundaries, would compute wrong local times for Antarctica/Casey during the 2009-2010 summer window. tzdb release 2009s captured the Antarctica change; 2010e captured Casey's revert.
headline: Casey's three-hour summer leap
blurb: Australia's Casey Station in Antarctica jumped 3 hours overnight to match the logistics schedule of its supply ships and aircraft.
story: Running a remote Antarctic base means coordinating closely with flights and ships based in Australia. In 2009 the Australian Antarctic Division decided that shifting Casey's clock 3 hours forward would cut confusion during the busy summer resupply season. The jump happened in one go, with no daylight-saving mechanism behind it. When summer ended in March 2010 Casey's clock snapped back to UTC+8, and the pattern eventually became a regular seasonal practice. For software, the episode is a reminder that offsets can leap by 3 hours for purely operational reasons, with no DST rule in sight.

title: tzdata recorded Bangladesh's 2009 DST end as 23:59 instead of 24:00
body: tz database maintainers
whatChanged: Bangladesh ended its one-off 2009 DST experiment at 24:00 on 2009-12-31 (midnight going into 2010-01-01). The tz database originally stored the Asia/Dhaka fallback transition at 23:59 instead of 24:00, a one-minute error. The correction shipped in tzdata release 2014g (2014-08-28).
technicalImpact: Any software or system using pre-2014g tzdata would compute the Asia/Dhaka UTC offset as UTC+6 from 23:59 on 2009-12-31 rather than from midnight, misplacing timestamps in that one-minute window. The bug affected all languages and runtimes that bundle tzdata and had not yet updated to 2014g.
headline: Bangladesh's one-minute tzdata slip
blurb: The tz database recorded Bangladesh's 2009 clock-back as happening one minute too early, placing the transition at 23:59 instead of the correct midnight.
story: Bangladesh ran DST for the first and only time in 2009, advancing clocks by one hour in June to save energy during a power crisis. On the last night of the year, clocks fell back to standard time at midnight. The tz database first wrote that transition as 23:59 rather than 24:00, a one-minute off-by-one error. The mistake was corrected years later, in tzdata 2014g (August 2014). Any software using the unfixed tzdata would mis-stamp times in that single minute, a small window that still shows how a single-digit error in a zone file can silently corrupt timestamps.

title: Fiji changed its daylight saving end date mid-season almost every year (2010 to 2020)
body: Government of Fiji (Cabinet and Ministry of Labour, Industrial Relations and Employment)
whatChanged: Between 2010 and 2020 Fiji repeatedly revised its DST end date after the initial announcement: 2010 ended about a month early; the 2011-12 end moved from late February to 22 January; later seasons ended in mid-to-late January; and the 2020-21 season ran as a single compressed period. Each revision required a new tzdb update.
technicalImpact: Fiji is the canonical tzdb example of a zone whose DST dates cannot be predicted far ahead. Systems that cache timezone data or ship infrequent OS updates will give wrong offsets for Fiji until the tzdb is refreshed after each Cabinet announcement. The Pacific/Fiji rule set accumulated many 'only' entries as a result.
headline: Fiji's moving goalposts
blurb: Fiji changed its daylight saving end date mid-season almost every year for a decade, forcing repeated emergency timezone database updates.
story: Fiji reintroduced daylight saving in 2009 but never settled on a predictable schedule. The Cabinet kept adjusting the end date after the season had already started, sometimes by more than a month. Timezone databases had to be patched again and again each year just to keep Fiji's clocks correct. Software that bundles timezone data and skips updates will quietly show the wrong time for Fiji users.

title: Y2K10 / 'Y2.01k' 2010 date overflow bugs (BCD/hex misparse)
body: Multiple vendors (Symantec, Windows Mobile/CE, Bank of Queensland EFTPOS); reported via RISKS Forum
whatChanged: A class of 2010 rollover failures from mishandling the year value: code reading a BCD/decimal year as hexadecimal (0x10 = decimal 16) jumped from 2009 to 2016, while other systems hit hardcoded-year or two-digit assumptions crossing into '10'.
technicalImpact: Symantec Endpoint Protection flagged 2010-dated definitions as out of date (endpoints stayed protected); Windows Mobile SMS showed messages dated 2016; Bank of Queensland EFTPOS stamped transactions as January 2016 and declined cards; SpamAssassin scored 2010+ mail as spam. Fixes were per-vendor date-parsing patches.

validation.supportingQuote: The main source of problems was confusion between hexadecimal number encoding and binary-coded decimal encodings of numbers. ... BCD encodes the number 10 as 0x10, while hexadecimal encodes the number 10 as 0x0A; 0x10 interpreted as a hexadecimal encoding represents the number 16. [some] mobile phone software incorrectly reported dates of SMSes as 2016 instead of 2010.

headline: The 2010 date glitches
blurb: When 2010 arrived, some systems misread the year and jumped ahead to 2016 or rejected valid dates.
story: As 2010 began, a group of systems mishandled the new year value. Some read the year in the wrong number format and jumped from 2009 to 2016. Security software rejected fresh updates as expired, some phones dated messages as 2016, and certain card machines stamped the wrong year and declined cards. Each affected vendor had to release its own fix for reading dates.

title: PlayStation 3 'ApocalyPS3' BCD leap-year date bug
body: Sony Computer Entertainment (original 'fat' PlayStation 3 RTC firmware)
whatChanged: The original PS3's internal clock firmware incorrectly treated 2010 as a leap year, so at midnight UTC it rolled to a non-existent 29 February 2010 instead of 1 March; per Sony, the system date could then reset to 1 January 2000. The exact clock mechanism (a widely cited but unconfirmed BCD-arithmetic theory) was never officially disclosed by Sony.
technicalImpact: Affected (non-Slim) consoles worldwide showed error 8001050F and could not sign in to PSN, sync trophies, or launch some installed offline games, clock-dependent DRM and date checks failed. The fault self-cleared once internal clocks advanced past the bad date to real 1 March UTC.

validation.supportingQuote: The bug caused the console to treat 2010 as a leap year and so change the date at midnight GMT Sunday to 29 February rather than 1 March. ... Only original PS3 models were affected; slimline versions were unaffected.

headline: PlayStation 3s lost track of the date
blurb: Older PlayStation 3 consoles thought 2010 had a 29 February that does not exist, which knocked them offline.
story: Many older PlayStation 3 consoles wrongly treated 2010 as a leap year. At midnight they tried to roll to 29 February, a day that did not exist that year, and some then reset to the start of 2000. Players around the world could not sign in, sync trophies, or open some games. The problem cleared on its own once the clocks moved past the bad date into real 1 March.

title: Bangladesh permanently cancels its 2009 DST experiment
body: Cabinet of Bangladesh
whatChanged: Bangladesh ran a one-off DST trial from June 19, 2009 (clocks forward 1 hour to UTC+07:00) through December 31, 2009 (clocks back to UTC+06:00). On March 22, 2010, the cabinet permanently cancelled the planned 2010 DST, keeping Asia/Dhaka on UTC+06:00 year-round. A string of short-notice tzdb releases tracked the churning Dhaka rules, beginning with 2009i (which first introduced Bangladesh DST), followed by 2009j and 2009k, then 2009u (codifying the December 31, 2009 reversal) and further 2010 releases through 2010h.
technicalImpact: Six tzdb releases in under a year updated Asia/Dhaka rules, creating a stale-data hazard for any system that had not kept its tz database current. Systems that cached the UTC+07:00 offset or pre-computed future timestamps for Dhaka incorrectly reflected DST that was never repeated.
headline: The one-summer clock that never came back
blurb: Bangladesh tried daylight saving time for half a year in 2009 to fight an electricity crisis, then permanently scrapped it.
story: In mid-2009, Bangladesh moved its clocks forward one hour to stretch daylight and cut electricity demand during a severe power shortage. The experiment did not reduce load-shedding, and citizens complained the heat only drove up energy use further. The reversal, originally planned for September 30, 2009, was pushed back, and the clocks finally went back on December 31, 2009; on March 22, 2010, the cabinet formally cancelled any future DST. The rapid string of tzdb releases needed to track these shifting dates is a reminder that last-minute policy reversals can leave software running on wrong offsets for months.

title: Russia trims from 11 to 9 time zones under Medvedev
body: Government of the Russian Federation (reform initiated by President Dmitry Medvedev, implementing decrees signed by Prime Minister Vladimir Putin)
whatChanged: On 2010-03-28, Russia eliminated two of its eleven time zones. Kamchatka and Chukotka (Asia/Kamchatka, Asia/Anadyr) moved from UTC+12 to UTC+11. Samara Oblast and Udmurtia (Europe/Samara) moved from UTC+4 to UTC+3 (Moscow time). Kemerovo Oblast (Asia/Novokuznetsk) moved from UTC+7 to UTC+6 (Omsk time). Net result: 9 time zones instead of 11. Captured in tzdata2010g.
technicalImpact: Any system with stale tz data for Asia/Kamchatka, Asia/Anadyr, Europe/Samara, or Asia/Novokuznetsk showed times one hour off after the switch. Updating to tzdata2010g or later was required to get correct local times in affected Russian regions.
headline: Russia loses two time zones overnight
blurb: Russia shrank its span of time zones from eleven to nine in a single change, shifting four regions to neighboring offsets.
story: In 2010, President Medvedev announced that Russia's eleven time zones were an inconvenience for business and administration. On March 28 that year, four regions quietly moved their clocks: Kamchatka and Chukotka dropped an hour, while Samara and Kemerovo aligned with neighboring zones. Software using stale timezone data suddenly showed the wrong local time for millions of people in those regions. The fix required updating to the tzdata2010g release, a reminder that political decisions can make correct time data obsolete overnight.

title: Macquarie Island stops switching clocks, stays on UTC+11 permanently
body: Australian Antarctic Division
whatChanged: From April 4, 2010 onward, Macquarie Island (Antarctica/Macquarie) no longer follows the Hobart DST cycle. When Tasmania clocks fell back to UTC+10 for winter, Macquarie Island remained at UTC+11 year-round. The tzdb zone entry was updated to reflect a fixed UTC+11 offset from 2010, with Tasmania rules resuming only from 2011 onward.
technicalImpact: Code that assumed Antarctica/Macquarie tracked Australia/Hobart or Australia/Tasmania gives wrong results from April 2010: Hobart winter is UTC+10 but Macquarie stays at UTC+11. The divergence affects any system that derives Macquarie time from Hobart rather than querying the zone directly. Addressed in tzdb release 2010h.
headline: The island that never fell back
blurb: Macquarie Island, a remote Australian Antarctic outpost, stopped turning its clocks back in autumn 2010 and has been on UTC+11 ever since.
story: Macquarie Island sits deep in the Southern Ocean and is home to a small Australian scientific station. Until 2010, it kept the same time as Hobart, Tasmania, including the seasonal DST switch. In April 2010 the Australian Antarctic Division decided the island would simply stay on summer time (UTC+11) all year, skipping the autumn fallback. Software that linked Macquarie time to Hobart started giving wrong answers every winter, since Hobart drops to UTC+10 while Macquarie does not.

title: Egypt suspends DST mid-year for Ramadan 2010, forcing two emergency tz updates
body: Egyptian Cabinet (Council of Ministers)
whatChanged: Egypt observed DST in 2010 (clocks forward from the last Friday in April) but its cabinet decided to suspend DST during Ramadan. Clocks reverted to EET (UTC+2) on 2010-08-10 at 24:00, sprang forward again to EEST (UTC+3) on 2010-09-09 at 24:00, then fell back for the season at the end of September. With the regular April spring-forward, that made four transitions in one year, and capturing the mid-year reversal required tzdata2010k (July) and tzdata2010l (August) to ship new rules for Africa/Cairo.
technicalImpact: Systems not patched between the July and August releases computed wrong Cairo offsets during and after Ramadan. The mid-year double-update broke any environment that assumed one annual DST rules refresh, and it predated automated tz-update pipelines, so unpatched JVMs, OS installs, and databases silently showed Cairo one hour off for weeks.
headline: Egypt's Ramadan clock reversal
blurb: Egypt clocked back out of daylight saving time for Ramadan then forward again a month later, forcing two mid-year emergency timezone releases.
story: In 2010, Egypt's cabinet ruled that clocks would drop back one hour for the Ramadan fasting month, then spring forward again once it ended. Added to the normal April spring-forward and the seasonal fall-back at the end of September, that gave Africa/Cairo four clock changes in a single year instead of the usual two. The tzdata maintainers had to ship emergency updates in July and August to capture the shifting Ramadan dates. Any system that missed those patches showed Cairo running one hour fast or slow for weeks around Ramadan.

title: Samoa introduces daylight saving time for the first time (Pacific/Apia)
body: Government of Samoa (Daylight Saving Act 2009)
whatChanged: Samoa (Pacific/Apia) observed DST for the first time starting 2010-09-26 at 00:00, advancing clocks from UTC-11 to UTC-10. DST ended 2011-04-02 at 04:00 (clocks fell back to UTC-11). The tzdb WS rule captures this as a 2010-only September lastSun transition. Multiple tzdata releases around 2010n (October 2010) were needed to encode and correct the Samoa DST start and end dates.
technicalImpact: Systems running stale tzdata before 2010n showed Pacific/Apia at UTC-11 year-round during DST, causing one-hour offsets for Samoa timestamps. The WS rule (2010-only for start, 2011-only for end) required two separate rule entries, which exposed edge cases in tz rule parsing. Any application caching Samoa offsets needed a tzdata update to 2010n or later to reflect the correct UTC-10 during the DST window.
headline: Samoa's first daylight saving time
blurb: Samoa moved its clocks forward for the very first time in 2010, shifting from UTC-11 to UTC-10 for the summer months.
story: Samoa had never used daylight saving time before 2010. The government introduced it to save fuel, and the national electric power corporation confirmed it cut energy demand significantly over the first three months. Clocks jumped forward one hour at midnight on September 26, 2010, then fell back in April 2011. For software, this meant Pacific/Apia carried a brand-new DST rule that many systems had never needed to handle, and stale timezone databases silently applied the wrong UTC-11 offset throughout the entire DST window.

title: Brazil's Bahia rejoins DST in 2011, reverses under public pressure in 2012
body: Governor of Bahia (Jaques Wagner); Brazilian Federal Government (Decree 7.584)
whatChanged: Bahia rejoined DST on 2011-10-16 after 8 years without it (Decree 7.584, signed 2011-10-13). The governor reversed the decision in October 2012, canceling the 2012-10-21 transition due to public opposition. Separately, Tocantins (America/Araguaina) joined DST from 2012-10-21 and then left again in September 2013.
technicalImpact: Two Brazil zones (America/Bahia and America/Araguaina) changed DST participation in consecutive years, each announced very close to the transition date. Systems treating Brazilian DST participation as a fixed list of states silently computed wrong offsets during both windows.
headline: Bahia's DST U-turn
blurb: Bahia flipped in and out of Brazil's daylight saving time within two years, each change announced days before it took effect.
story: Bahia had skipped DST since 2003, then its governor announced a return in October 2011, signed into law just days before clocks were due to change. A year later, facing widespread public backlash, he reversed course again and canceled the 2012 transition. Meanwhile the neighboring state of Tocantins took the opposite path, joining DST in 2012 only to drop it again in 2013. The back-to-back reversals, both confirmed at the last minute, exposed every system that assumed Brazilian DST participation was stable year to year.

title: Casey Station's irregular UTC+8/UTC+11 toggling (2011-2023)
body: From 2009 onward, Casey Station (Antarctica/Casey) toggled between UTC+8 in winter and UTC+11 in summer on an irregular, year-by-year schedule with no repeating rule. The tzdb Zone table encodes more than a dozen explicit transition rows across 2009 to 2023, each with a unique date and time. The zone settled back at UTC+8 permanently from 2023-03-09.
whatChanged: From 2009 onward, Casey Station (Antarctica/Casey) toggled between UTC+8 (winter) and UTC+11 (summer) on an irregular, year-by-year schedule with no repeating rule. The tzdb Zone table encodes more than a dozen explicit transition rows spanning 2009-2023, each with a unique date and time. The zone settled back at UTC+8 permanently from 2023-03-09.
technicalImpact: No POSIX Rule block can describe this pattern; tzdb must encode every transition explicitly. Applications caching a fixed Casey offset or running stale tzdata will compute wrong local times. A canonical example of why hardcoding offsets is unsafe even for apparently simple zones.
headline: The station that made up its mind each year
blurb: Casey Station in Antarctica flipped its clocks between two offsets in most years over more than a decade, with no fixed schedule and a multi-year pause in between.
story: Casey Station is an Australian Antarctic base that started experimenting with summer hours in 2009, moving from UTC+8 to UTC+11 to match Hobart daylight time. The Australian Antarctic Division kept deciding each year whether and when to switch, so the dates were never the same twice. Over more than a decade the tzdb file accumulated a long string of hand-coded transition rows because no repeating rule could describe the pattern. Any app that assumed Casey followed a predictable schedule silently produced wrong timestamps every time the schedule changed.

title: Russia abolishes seasonal clock changes, locking onto permanent summer time (Moscow year-round UTC+4)
body: Russian Federation (President Dmitry Medvedev / Federal Law No. 107-FZ), encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: After the final spring-forward on 2011-03-27, Medvedev cancelled biannual DST transitions and kept Russia permanently on summer time. Moscow became year-round UTC+4 (no fall-back October 2011), and every Russian zone shifted to permanent DST-offset standard time.
technicalImpact: Software hard-coding Europe/Moscow as UTC+3 with summer DST produced times an hour off for ~8 months a year. DST rules had to be deleted, not just shifted, so libraries carrying old transition rules (older JRE tzdata, un-patched Windows, stale moment-timezone) showed phantom DST changes across 9 zones until updated.

validation.supportingQuote: Search snippet for the canonical article: "the bill entitled 'On the Calculation of Time' was approved by the Duma on April 19, 2011, after Russian president Dmitry Medvedev had already proclaimed the end of 'winter time' in February 2011." Russia "turned its clocks one hour forward for the last time on March 27, 2011, making daylight saving time Russia's new standard time."
validation.linkNote: valid page, blocks automated fetch (HTTP 403 to bots)

headline: Russia stopped changing its clocks
blurb: Russia ended its twice-a-year clock changes and stayed on summer time all year.
story: In 2011 Russia made its final spring clock change and then dropped the seasonal switches for good. The country stayed on summer time year-round, so Moscow kept the same time through every season. Software that still expected the old clock changes showed the wrong time for months until it was updated.

title: Falkland Islands drops DST cycling, stays on UTC-3 permanently
body: Falkland Islands Government (confirmed via Cable & Wireless Falkland Islands letter to IANA, 2012-02-22)
whatChanged: The Falkland Islands did not revert clocks in April 2011 as usual, staying on UTC-3 (summer time) year-round as a trial. In 2012, Cable & Wireless Falkland Islands confirmed to IANA the policy was repeated. The tzdb zone Atlantic/Stanley was modeled as permanent UTC-3 from 2010-09-05 02:00 onward, with no further DST transitions.
technicalImpact: Atlantic/Stanley changed from a DST-cycling zone (UTC-4 standard, UTC-3 summer) to a permanent UTC-3 zone. Any tz database snapshot predating tzdb 2012 may show a reversion to UTC-4 in April 2011 or later that never happened. Applications caching old zone data for Atlantic/Stanley would compute times one hour off.
headline: The Falklands stopped turning the clocks back
blurb: The Falkland Islands ran a one-year trial of year-round summer time in 2011 and never went back.
story: Each April the Falklands had always wound clocks back one hour from UTC-3 to UTC-4. In 2011 the government quietly skipped the reversal as a trial. The trial was considered a success, and in 2012 a letter from Cable & Wireless Falkland Islands to IANA confirmed the policy was continuing. Software relying on old zone data for Atlantic/Stanley could show times an hour off from that April onward.

title: Egypt abolishes daylight saving time (2011)
body: Egyptian transitional cabinet (post-revolution government)
whatChanged: On 2011-04-20 Egypt's transitional cabinet decreed the cancellation of DST after a government poll showed majority support for abolition (widely reported as around 79%). The scheduled late-April spring-forward never happened and Egypt stayed on permanent EET (UTC+2). The policy later flip-flopped: DST was reinstated in 2014, cancelled again in 2015, suspended in 2016, and restored in 2023.
technicalImpact: Triggered an off-cycle tz database update (tzdata2011g) and vendor patches, including a Microsoft Windows hotfix for Egypt. Each later reversal required further tzdata releases and OS/runtime updates, leaving software on stale zone data returning wrong offsets for Africa/Cairo.
headline: Egypt's revolving clock
blurb: Egypt's post-revolution government scrapped daylight saving time in April 2011, then changed its mind several times over the following decade.
story: After the 2011 revolution, Egypt's transitional cabinet polled the public and found most Egyptians wanted to drop the clock change. On April 20 it issued a decree scrapping DST, keeping the country on UTC+2 year-round. But the policy reversed in 2014, flipped again in 2015, and kept changing through 2023. Every reversal forced a new tzdata release, and any system running stale zone data returned the wrong local time for Cairo.

title: Google announces the leap smear: spreading the leap second over a window
body: Google (Public NTP)
whatChanged: Google publicly described its 'leap smear': instead of inserting a literal 23:59:60, Google modified its NTP servers to gradually slew time across a window around the leap second, so no clock shows :60 or steps backward and every day has 86400 seconds. Google had first applied a (cosine) smear for the 2008 leap second.
technicalImpact: Removes the entire class of leap-second software faults (no :60 timestamp, no backward step, no kernel STA_INS handling) for clients reading smeared NTP. Trade-off: smeared time is off from true UTC by up to ~0.5s during the window, so smeared and non-smeared sources must not be mixed. Became the de facto industry pattern.

validation.supportingQuote: "Instead of applying leap seconds to our servers using clock steps, we have 'smeared' the extra second across the hours before and after each leap." ... "Google's 2008 smear was a 20-hour cosine smear before the leap (04:00:00 to 00:00:00)."

headline: Google spread an extra second over a day
blurb: Instead of adding a single odd second, Google nudged its clocks slowly so the change went unnoticed.
story: When an extra second is added to world time, it can confuse computers that do not expect it. Google spread that one second across a long window so its clocks never showed an odd time or jumped backward, and every day still had the usual number of seconds. The trade-off is that during the window Google's time is slightly off from official time, so smeared and unsmeared sources should not be mixed. The approach became a common industry practice.

title: Astrolabe, Inc. v. Olson et al. copyright lawsuit filed
body: Astrolabe, Inc. (astrology software vendor) vs. Arthur David Olson and Paul Eggert; US District Court, District of Massachusetts
whatChanged: Astrolabe sued tz maintainers Olson and Eggert on 2011-09-30, claiming copyright over historical time-zone data the database derived in part from the ACS atlases ('ACS American Atlas' and 'ACS International Atlas', compiled by Thomas G. Shanks and others). The claim targeted pre-1970 historical zone information.
technicalImpact: Direct legal threat to maintainers of infrastructure used by virtually all computing time handling. It forced the takedown of the project's mailing list and FTP distribution days later, briefly cutting off the canonical update channel vendors depend on.

validation.supportingQuote: In September of 2011, an astrology software company called Astrolabe filed suit [against Arthur David Olson and Paul Eggert] ... its copyrights were infringed, because the database relies in part on information in an atlas in which Astrolabe claimed to own copyright.

headline: A company sued the time-zone keepers
blurb: A software company sued the people who maintain the world's shared time-zone data over historical records.
story: In 2011 a company called Astrolabe sued the volunteers who maintain the widely used time-zone database. It claimed it owned copyright over some historical time-zone information the database had drawn from old reference books. Because almost all computers rely on this data, the lawsuit was a serious threat to a key piece of shared infrastructure.

title: tz mailing list and FTP distribution site shut down
body: tz project / Arthur David Olson (then at US NIH)
whatChanged: In response to the Astrolabe lawsuit, the database's mailing list and FTP site (hosted at elsie.nci.nih.gov) were taken offline on 2011-10-06, halting official distribution and discussion.
technicalImpact: The authoritative source of tzdata updates went offline for roughly a day, creating a brief supply-chain risk: any government DST/offset change during the outage could not be published through the usual channel. The mailing list resumed on an ICANN server on 2011-10-07, and ICANN assumed formal maintenance on 2011-10-14.

validation.supportingQuote: As a result, on 6 October 2011, the database's mailing list and FTP site were shut down.

headline: The time-zone update channel went dark
blurb: After the lawsuit, the official place to get time-zone updates was taken offline.
story: Soon after the lawsuit, the project's mailing list and download site were shut down. That was the official place where the world got time-zone updates, and it briefly went dark. The mailing list resurfaced the next day on an ICANN server, and within about a week ICANN formally took over hosting and maintenance, restoring the official channel.

title: ICANN/IANA assumes custodianship of the Time Zone Database
body: ICANN (at the request of the IETF)
whatChanged: On 2011-10-14 ICANN announced it would host and maintain the tz database via IANA, restoring distribution at data.iana.org and agreeing to handle legal matters. Prompted by the Astrolabe lawsuit/takedown and Olson's planned retirement.
technicalImpact: Re-established an authoritative, institutionally backed distribution point (data.iana.org/time-zones) that vendors continue to fetch from. Gave the database an organization able to absorb legal defense, de-risking the single-volunteer dependency.

validation.supportingQuote: ICANN stated it is "willing to deal with any legal matters as they arise," referring to the Astrolabe copyright lawsuit that prompted the withdrawal of the original FTP server.
validation.corrections: Minor wording nuance (not an error): RFC 6557 cites the canonical homepage as http://www.iana.org/time-zones; data.iana.org/time-zones is the data-download path. Both are real and current, so the event's "data.iana.org/time-zones" reference is accurate.

headline: A new home for the time-zone database
blurb: A larger organization stepped in to host and maintain the world's time-zone data.
story: After the lawsuit and the shutdown, the organization ICANN agreed to host and maintain the time-zone database through its IANA group. It restored the official download site and took on the legal side of the work. This gave the database a stable home and a backer able to handle legal trouble, instead of depending on a single volunteer.

title: Europe/Tiraspol demoted to backward-compat link after Transnistria reverses DST split
body: IANA tz database maintainers, following the Pridnestrovian Moldavian Republic (Transnistria)
whatChanged: Europe/Tiraspol was briefly added as a distinct zone in tzdb 2011m when Transnistria appeared to be abolishing DST independently of Moldova; that distinct zone was set to UTC+3 with no DST (FET, Further-eastern European Time) from 2011 March onward, which is why a separate zone was needed. Transnistria reversed that decision, so a later 2011 release (2011n) removed the zone entry and reinstated Europe/Tiraspol as a backward-compat link to Europe/Chisinau.
technicalImpact: Applications using Europe/Tiraspol get silently routed to Europe/Chisinau. This is accurate for current timestamps but encodes a political assumption: if Transnistria ever diverges from Moldova on DST or offset, the link will silently produce wrong results for Transnistrian users with no warning.
headline: The zone that came and went in a week
blurb: Transnistria got its own IANA time zone for a few days in 2011, then lost it when the breakaway state changed its mind about splitting from Moldova's clocks.
story: In October 2011 Transnistria, a disputed strip of land between Moldova and Ukraine, announced it would stop observing daylight saving time, unlike Moldova. The IANA tz database added a separate Europe/Tiraspol zone to reflect the split. Within days Transnistria reversed course and rejoined Moldova's schedule, so the zone was pulled and moved back to the backward-compat file as a plain link to Europe/Chisinau. The episode shows how political instability in unrecognized states can ripple into software: any future unannounced DST change in Transnistria would silently affect all users mapped to the shared zone.

title: Samoa (and Tokelau) skip Friday 30 December 2011, jumping from UTC-11 to UTC+13
body: Government of Samoa (and Tokelau), encoded by IANA tz database
whatChanged: At end of Thursday 2011-12-29 Samoa moved its standard offset from UTC-11 to UTC+13 (DST effectively UTC+14), placing it west of the International Date Line to align trading days with Australia/New Zealand. Friday 2011-12-30 was skipped entirely and never existed locally. Tokelau (Pacific/Fakaofo) made the same jump simultaneously.
technicalImpact: A 24-hour discontinuity with no local midnight on 2011-12-30 broke any system iterating calendar days, computing durations, or storing timestamps assuming every date exists. Pacific/Apia gained a +24h discontinuity, the largest single-day local-time jump ever encoded. The textbook case for converting through UTC and never assuming every calendar date exists locally.

validation.supportingQuote: In 2011, Samoa changed its time zone from UTC-11 to UTC+13 by shifting the date line to the west and removing December 30, 2011 from the calendar. The change occurred at midnight on December 29, 2011, taking the Pacific island nation straight into December 31, 2011.

headline: Samoa skipped a day
blurb: Samoa jumped across the date line, so Friday 30 December 2011 never happened there.
story: At the end of one Thursday in December 2011, Samoa moved to the other side of the international date line to line up its working week with Australia and New Zealand. As a result the following Friday simply did not exist there and went straight to Saturday. The nearby islands of Tokelau made the same jump at the same time. Any software that assumed every calendar date exists could break on that missing day.

title: Samoa and Tokelau cross the International Date Line, skipping an entire calendar day
body: Government of Samoa (International Date Line Act 2011) and Government of Tokelau
whatChanged: Samoa moved from UTC-11 to UTC+13 (UTC+14 during DST) by skipping December 30, 2011: at midnight ending Thursday 29 December, clocks jumped directly to Saturday 31 December. Tokelau made the same change at the same moment, also skipping December 30, 2011, when it moved from UTC-11 to UTC+13. Both territories crossed from the east side of the International Date Line to the west side.
technicalImpact: December 30, 2011 is a valid date globally but does not exist in Samoa or Tokelau local time. Any application storing or computing local dates for these zones around that period may produce invalid or ambiguous results. UNIX timestamps were unaffected but local date arithmetic and calendar logic breaks across the gap. tzdata 2011n captured the change for Pacific/Apia and Pacific/Fakaofo.
headline: The day that never existed in Samoa
blurb: Samoa erased an entire calendar day in 2011 by jumping across the International Date Line, meaning December 30 never happened there.
story: For over a century, Samoa sat on the American side of the date line, sharing a calendar day with Hawaii and the US West Coast. But its biggest trading partners, Australia and New Zealand, were almost a full day ahead, costing Samoa two working days of overlap every week. To fix that, the government passed a law skipping the country straight across the line: at midnight on Thursday 29 December 2011, Samoa jumped forward to Saturday 31 December. Tokelau, which relies on Samoa for many services, made the same hop the following night. For software, the consequence is stark: December 30, 2011 is a real date everywhere on Earth except Samoa, so any code that stores or computes local Samoan dates around that period must account for the gap or it will produce wrong results.

title: Tokelau crosses the International Date Line, skipping December 30, 2011
body: Government of Tokelau (New Zealand administered territory)
whatChanged: Tokelau (Pacific/Fakaofo) moved from UTC-11 to UTC+13 at the start of local December 30, 2011, causing that calendar date to never exist in Tokelau. The territory jumped directly from December 29 to December 31, mirroring the simultaneous move by neighbouring Samoa.
technicalImpact: Any timestamp recorded as 2011-12-30 in Tokelau local time is ambiguous or invalid. Software parsing historical Pacific/Fakaofo timestamps around this transition must account for the missing calendar day; libraries relying on pre-tzdata-2012a data will compute incorrect offsets for dates after the transition.
headline: The day Tokelau erased from the calendar
blurb: Tokelau skipped an entire calendar day in 2011 to jump to the other side of the International Date Line, aligning itself with New Zealand and Samoa.
story: Tokelau, a tiny New Zealand territory in the South Pacific, decided to move from UTC-11 to UTC+13 to put its business hours in sync with New Zealand and nearby Samoa, which made the same jump on the same day. When local clocks reached midnight on December 29, they advanced straight to December 31. December 30 simply never happened there. For software, any timestamp or log entry stamped with a Tokelau local date of December 30, 2011 is effectively invalid and requires special-case handling.

title: Tokelau skips December 30, 2011, crossing to UTC+13
body: Government of Tokelau (administered by New Zealand)
whatChanged: Tokelau (Pacific/Fakaofo) moved from UTC-11 to UTC+13 on 2011-12-30, skipping that calendar date entirely. The transition mirrored Samoa's dateline jump the same weekend. The tzdb maintainers note that Shanks recorded the pre-1901 offset as UTC-10 but believe the change was more likely to UTC-11; tzdb uses UTC-11 from 1901.
technicalImpact: Code iterating over Tokelau timestamps will find December 30, 2011 missing. The UTC-10 versus UTC-11 historical uncertainty makes pre-1901 Tokelau timestamps unreliable. tz releases from 2012 onward reflect UTC+13 and the corrected historical offset.
headline: The day Tokelau erased from the calendar
blurb: Tokelau skipped an entire day in December 2011, jumping across the International Date Line to align with New Zealand.
story: Tokelau is a tiny territory in the Pacific, long sitting on the opposite side of the date line from New Zealand, which administers it. In late 2011, it crossed the line the same weekend Samoa did, wiping December 30 off the local calendar. Anyone on the islands went from December 29 to December 31 without a Friday in between. For software, this means any loop walking through Tokelau dates hits a one-day gap, and older historical data for the territory carries an offset error too.

title: Morocco observes negative DST during Ramadan, breaking the two-transitions-per-year rule
body: Government of Morocco
whatChanged: Morocco falls back one hour just before Ramadan each year and springs forward just after, a practice in place since 2012. This creates a period of negative DST (clocks behind standard time) during Ramadan in the main and vanguard tzdb formats. In years where Ramadan straddles the boundary with the rest of the year, the result can be three DST transitions in a single Gregorian year. The rearguard tzdb format avoids negative DST by keeping clocks at +00 and treating the non-Ramadan months as the DST period instead. Encoded into tzdb release 2018h with predictions extended into future years.
technicalImpact: Breaks the common assumption of at most two DST transitions per year. Scheduling libraries, calendar parsers, and date pickers that hardcode a two-transition model miscalculate Morocco times during Ramadan. The negative DST offset representation is only supported in the tzdb main and vanguard formats; parsers built for the rearguard format see a different but compatible inverted model. Three-transition years require dynamic rules rather than a fixed annual pattern.
headline: Three clock changes in one year
blurb: Morocco turns its clocks back for Ramadan each year, which sometimes causes three separate time changes in a single calendar year.
story: Morocco has adjusted its clocks around the Muslim holy month of Ramadan since 2012, falling back one hour just before it starts and springing forward again once it ends. Because the Islamic calendar is shorter than the Gregorian one, Ramadan drifts earlier by about eleven days each year. In some years this drift positions the usual spring-forward and fall-back so that a single Gregorian year contains three clock changes instead of the usual two. Timezone databases record this as a negative DST offset, meaning local time runs behind the nominal standard time during Ramadan. Software that assumes clocks change at most twice a year will silently produce wrong answers for Morocco during these periods.

title: tzdb documents that future timezone predictions can be invalidated by government action at any time
body: IANA tz database (tzdb), Paul Eggert and contributors
whatChanged: The tzdb theory.html document explicitly states that future timestamp predictions are based on current law and will be incorrect after a government changes its rules. Morocco is the cited example: a meeting scheduled for 13:00 on October 1 in Casablanca could be thrown off if Morocco changes its DST rules before that date.
technicalImpact: Applications must never cache far-future local-time conversions. Future events stored as local time must be re-evaluated against the current tzdb release whenever an update ships. Systems that store only a UTC timestamp lose the user's intent when the governing rules change after the event is scheduled.
headline: Future times are guesses until the law says otherwise
blurb: The tzdb explicitly warns that any future clock prediction can be made wrong overnight by a single government decision.
story: The timezone database has to predict future DST transitions based on laws that exist today. But governments can and do change those laws with little notice. Morocco is the textbook case: its DST rules shift around Ramadan, so a meeting booked months ahead in Casablanca might land at the wrong time after a rule change. The tzdb cannot fix this, only software can, by re-checking stored future times every time tzdb releases an update.

title: RFC 6557 (BCP 175): Procedures for Maintaining the Time Zone Database
body: IETF; authors Eliot Lear (Cisco) and Paul Eggert (UCLA); published as BCP 175
whatChanged: Published February 2012, RFC 6557 formalized tz governance after the lawsuit and ICANN takeover: it defines the TZ Coordinator role, the tz@iana.org mailing list, update/appeal procedures, IANA's duty to host the repository and hold the signing key, and the public-domain status.
technicalImpact: Turned an ad-hoc single-volunteer process into a documented, succession-proof procedure, assuring vendors updates would continue through a defined channel with cryptographically signed releases.

validation.supportingQuote: RFC 6557, "Procedures for Maintaining the Time Zone Database," BCP 175, by Eliot Lear (Cisco Systems GmbH) and Paul Eggert (UCLA), February 2012. Defines the TZ Coordinator role, the tz@iana.org mailing list, IANA maintenance of a repository with cryptographic signing, and that the TZ database "is in the public domain, and is intended to remain in the public domain."

headline: Clear rules for running the time-zone database
blurb: A published standard set out how the world's time-zone database should be maintained.
story: After the lawsuit and the move to a new host, a formal document laid out how the time-zone database would be run. It defined who coordinates it, how updates are made and appealed, where it is stored, and that it stays free for anyone to use. This turned a loose, one-person process into a documented system that could survive changes in the people involved.

title: Astrolabe v. Olson lawsuit voluntarily dismissed
body: Astrolabe, Inc.; defendants represented by the Electronic Frontier Foundation (EFF)
whatChanged: On 2012-02-22 Astrolabe voluntarily moved to dismiss the suit without having served the defendants, after EFF intervened and sought Rule 11 sanctions. Astrolabe apologized and entered a covenant not to sue.
technicalImpact: Removed the legal cloud over redistributing tzdata, confirming the project could continue using historical sources and ship freely under public-domain terms. Stabilized the legal footing downstream OS/language vendors rely on.

validation.supportingQuote: The astrology software company that filed the lawsuit, Astrolabe, has also apologized and agreed to a 'covenant not to sue' going forward.

headline: The time-zone lawsuit was dropped
blurb: The company dropped its lawsuit against the time-zone maintainers and promised not to sue again.
story: In early 2012 the company that had sued the time-zone maintainers withdrew its case, after a digital rights group stepped in to defend them. The company apologized and agreed not to bring the claim again. This lifted the legal cloud and confirmed the project could keep using historical sources and share its data freely.

title: Windows Azure 2012 leap-day certificate date bug outage
body: Microsoft (Windows Azure / Azure Fabric Guest Agent)
whatChanged: The Guest Agent computed a transfer certificate's valid-to date by adding 1 to the current year. On 2012-02-29 that produced the non-existent 2013-02-29, so certificate creation failed.
technicalImpact: From 00:00 UTC 2012-02-29 new VM provisioning failed; hosts retried, declared hardware faults and migrated workloads, cascading into a multi-cluster outage. Service management was restored to most clusters by 05:23 PST (~13h 23m after onset); a botched rollback on seven clusters extended the disruption to ~08:00 PST. Microsoft issued a 33% service credit to all Compute, Access Control, Service Bus and Caching customers for the affected billing month(s), regardless of whether their service was impacted.

validation.supportingQuote: The Guest Agent (GA) calculated the valid-to date by simply taking the current date and adding one to its year, meaning that any GA that tried to create a transfer certificate on leap day set a valid-to date of February 29, 2013, an invalid date that caused the certificate creation to fail.

headline: A leap day broke a cloud service
blurb: On 29 February 2012, a date mistake took down part of Microsoft's cloud.
story: Microsoft's cloud service worked out a security date by simply adding one to the current year. On 29 February 2012 that produced 29 February 2013, a date that does not exist, and the step failed. New machines could not be set up, and the trouble spread into a wider outage that took many hours to fix. Microsoft gave affected customers service credits.

title: TomTom sat-navs lose GPS signal due to leap-year firmware bug
body: TomTom (third-party GPS receiver chipset firmware)
whatChanged: A leap-year handling bug in third-party GPS receiver firmware caused TomTom Start 20/25, Via 1xx, Go Live 8xx/1xxx/2xxx and Blue&Me 2 devices worldwide to fail to acquire a GPS lock starting 2012-03-31. Devices showed a grey screen with no position fix. A 20-second power hold offered a temporary workaround; TomTom shipped a permanent firmware update via the MyTomTom desktop tool.
technicalImpact: GPS receivers embed their own clocks and firmware; a leap-year miscalculation in the chipset firmware caused the device to reject valid satellite signals. Any application or vehicle system relying on those sat-navs for position lost navigation on the affected date. The incident showed that GPS firmware carries its own calendar logic, independent of the host OS or app layer, and that leap-year edge cases can quietly propagate through embedded systems.
headline: The sat-nav that forgot leap years
blurb: Millions of TomTom GPS devices stopped finding their position on 31 March 2012 because firmware inside the GPS chip did not handle leap years correctly.
story: On 31 March 2012, TomTom owners across the world switched on their sat-navs and saw a grey screen instead of a map. The GPS chip inside the devices had a bug in how it counted time across a leap year, causing it to reject the signal from satellites even though the satellites themselves were fine. Pressing and holding the power button for 20 seconds temporarily got the device working again, but the fix did not last. TomTom released a firmware update through its MyTomTom desktop software within days. The episode showed that GPS hardware carries its own calendar logic buried deep in firmware, entirely separate from the phone or app on top, and that leap-year edge cases can bring navigation to a halt for anyone who relies on an embedded clock getting the date right.

title: Morocco introduces Ramadan DST suspension, creating a lunar-calendar-linked offset cycle
body: Government of Morocco (Decree No. 2-12-126, issued by the President of the Government)
whatChanged: Morocco formalised a policy in April 2012 whereby Daylight Saving Time (clocks forward one hour, UTC+1) is suspended each year during Ramadan, reverting to standard time (UTC+0) for roughly four to five weeks, then resuming DST. Since Morocco moved to permanent UTC+1 in October 2018, the pattern inverted: the country now stays on UTC+1 year-round except during Ramadan, when it drops back to UTC+0. The exact suspension dates shift every year because Ramadan follows the Islamic lunar calendar. The tzdb Africa/Casablanca zone carries individually specified Rule entries for every year through 2087 to encode this.
technicalImpact: Up to four UTC-offset transitions can occur in a single calendar year for Africa/Casablanca, compared to the usual two for a standard DST zone. Because Ramadan dates are announced weeks or even days in advance, future transitions cannot be hard-coded reliably; apps and systems must pull fresh tzdb data close to each Ramadan. Every year requires its own Rule entries in tzdata, making Morocco one of the most maintenance-intensive zones in the database. Systems that cache or compile timezone data statically may display incorrect local times during Ramadan until updated.
headline: Morocco's Ramadan clock
blurb: Morocco suspends Daylight Saving Time every year during Ramadan, meaning the clocks shift up to four times in a single year instead of the usual two.
story: In 2012, Morocco's government passed a decree making DST pause for Ramadan each year, so the country reverts to standard time for the holy month and then jumps forward again when it ends. Because Ramadan is set by the Islamic lunar calendar, the exact dates move earlier by about 11 days every year. After Morocco switched to permanent summer time in 2018, the effect flipped: now the country drops back an hour for Ramadan rather than skipping ahead. The tzdb encodes a separate set of rules for every single year stretching decades into the future, and software that does not refresh its timezone data before each Ramadan will show the wrong local time.

title: Leap second #25 (2012-06-30) triggers Linux kernel hrtimer livelock, crashing major sites
body: IERS (Bulletin C 43) inserted the leap second; root-cause bug in the Linux kernel timekeeping/hrtimer subsystem
whatChanged: A positive leap second was inserted at 2012-06-30 23:59:60 UTC (TAI-UTC 34s -> 35s). The kernel applied the leap second but failed to call clock_was_set() to notify the hrtimer subsystem, so CLOCK_REALTIME hrtimers were not reprogrammed: timers fired early/immediately, producing a livelock. Bug present since ~2.6.26.
technicalImpact: futex-heavy and timer-heavy processes (Java/JVM, MySQL, Firefox) spun at 100% CPU. Reddit was down ~30-40 minutes; Mozilla (Hadoop/ElasticSearch), LinkedIn, Yelp, Foursquare, StumbleUpon were hit; the Amadeus Altea airline reservation system (Qantas, Virgin Australia) failed, forcing manual check-in. Workaround: 'date -s now'. Fixed by John Stultz's patch adding clock_was_set_delayed() (commit bb88e92477def647976cd3d6964af98beceba900).

validation.supportingQuote: "It is believed the leap second causes the Linux kernel to livelock when the system attempts to adjust the time and date of the computer." (headline: "Leap second bug cripples Linux servers at airlines, Reddit, LinkedIn"; affected: Qantas and Virgin Australia, plus servers run by Mozilla, StumbleUpon, Yelp, FourSquare, Reddit and LinkedIn; leap second inserted "midnight on Saturday, 30 June").

headline: An extra second crashed major websites
blurb: A one-second adjustment in 2012 set off a Linux bug that froze big sites.
story: An extra second was added to world time in mid-2012, but a long-standing Linux bug failed to tell part of the system about it. Timers then fired too early and locked up, so many programs spun at full processor load. Reddit went down for about an hour and a half, several other well-known sites were hit, and an airline reservation system failed, forcing manual check-in. A quick fix was to reset the clock, and a proper patch followed.

sidenotes.label: Linux kernel fix commit (hrtimer leap-second livelock)
ServerFault: mass Linux server crashes on leap-second day
Stack Overflow: 'Java is using 100% of CPU on Linux'

academicRefs.authors: Kuhn, Markus G.
academicRefs.title: Leap-second considerations in distributed computer systems
academicRefs.venue: ITU-R SRG 7A Colloquium on the UTC timescale, Torino (University of Cambridge Computer Laboratory)
academicRefs.keyFinding: Enumerates the failure modes an OS get_time() faces at a leap second (jump back, stop clock, block/suspend processing, abort with error, struct minor-component overflow where 2x10^9 > nsec >= 10^9) and shows the classic POSIX 'seconds since the Epoch' definition is self-contradictory: it both counts SI seconds (86401 on a leap day) and encodes a broken-down time in which 23:59:60 and the following 00:00:00 map to the same integer. Proposes UTS, a smoothed UTC that stretches the last 1000 seconds of a positive-leap-second day to 1001 ms each (a 0.1% rate reduction) to preserve monotonicity and bound interval error to 0.1%.

academicRefs.authors: Finkleman, D., Allen, S., Seago, J. H., Seaman, R. and Seidelmann, P. K.
academicRefs.title: The Future of Time: UTC and the Leap Second
academicRefs.venue: American Scientist, Vol. 99, No. 4, pp. 312-319
academicRefs.keyFinding: States that 'Leap seconds can create problems in systems that require precise synchronization but are not designed to handle minutes with an extra second,' that those sharing data across distributed computer systems are especially exposed, and that even software designers and hardware manufacturers 'are not always aware of UTC's prescriptions for leap seconds.' Notes the leap-second notification path is largely manual (announcements must be converted by a human into machine-readable form), with GPS providing ~6 months electronic notice but GPS time itself carrying no leap seconds.

title: Red Hat Enterprise Linux leap-second guidance: futex CPU spike (RHEL 6) and early-hrtimer (RHEL 7)
body: Red Hat (vendor advisory RHBA-2012:1199 and KB)
whatChanged: Red Hat documented two distinct leap-second kernel defects: on RHEL 6 (and other contemporary Linux kernels around the 2.6.32 to 3.4 series) the leap second caused futex-heavy applications to consume large amounts of CPU; on NOHZ kernels absolute timers expiring at midnight UTC could fire early. Separately, a spurious pending-leap-second flag was erroneously advertised by some NTP servers, threatening a bogus leap-second insertion at midnight UTC on August 31, 2012.
technicalImpact: Production guidance: disable kernel leap-second insertion and slew instead (stop ntpd, step the clock, restart) or apply patched kernels. Java/MySQL/Firefox-class workloads were the canonical victims of the futex CPU spike.

validation.supportingQuote: After the leap second has been inserted futex heavy applications began consuming a large amount of CPU... Hrtimers may fire early when the leap second is inserted... An additional leap second notification was erroneously issued by some NTP servers within the North America region for midnight UTC on August 31, 2012.

headline: Red Hat warned about an extra second
blurb: Red Hat told customers how to avoid software problems caused by an added second.
story: Red Hat found that adding an extra second to world time could cause problems on its Linux systems, with some software using far too much processor power and some timers firing too early. It advised customers to turn off the automatic second insertion and adjust the clock gently instead, or to apply fixed software. Common programs like Java and MySQL were the usual victims.

title: Palestine DST prediction rule revised repeatedly from 2013 to 2024
body: Palestinian Authority (Gaza and West Bank)
whatChanged: Palestine's DST schedule for Asia/Gaza and Asia/Hebron was corrected or revised in nearly every tzdb release from 2013c through 2024a. Transitions were predicted, then shifted by days to weeks. The 2013c release corrected historical timestamps (Gaza/Hebron transitions from 2008-2011) and set an early predicted rule. From around 2020 the maintainers began adjusting future transitions to track observed behavior, and from 2023a the spring-forward delays were attributed directly to Ramadan: in 2023a the spring-forward was moved from March 25 to April 29 due to Ramadan. In 2024a the prediction rule was changed from 'first Saturday after Ramadan' to 'second Saturday after Ramadan', affecting 2024, 2025, and predictions from 2039 onward.
technicalImpact: Asia/Gaza and Asia/Hebron carry among the most frequent future-timestamp corrections of any zone in the tzdb. Far-future timestamps in these zones are unreliable; applications must not cache DST offsets beyond a few weeks and must update to each new tzdb release to track corrections. The zone is the canonical example of why software should treat predicted DST transitions as provisional.
headline: Palestine's moving clock
blurb: Palestine's clocks have been rescheduled in almost every timezone database release for over a decade, making it the hardest zone in the world to predict.
story: Every spring, Palestine sets its clocks forward for summer, but the exact date has changed repeatedly because the Islamic holy month of Ramadan shifts it. When Ramadan falls near the usual transition date, authorities delay the clock change until after it ends. Timezone database maintainers have had to issue corrections in nearly every release from 2013 through 2024, sometimes moving the date by weeks. In 2024 they changed the underlying prediction rule from one Saturday after Ramadan to two, acknowledging that one week was never quite enough. For software, this means any far-future timestamp stored for Gaza or Hebron should be treated as a best guess, not a fact.

title: Systematic replacement of Shanks & Pottenger pre-1970 timezone data in tzdb (2013-2024)
body: IANA Time Zone Database maintainers (Paul Eggert and contributors)
whatChanged: tzdb systematically replaced pre-1970 historical timezone data sourced from Shanks & Pottenger's 'The International Atlas' and related works with data from primary legal and archival sources. Corrections span Australia, the Bahamas, Bermuda, Belize, Ghana, Israel, Palestine, Kenya, Nigeria, Seychelles, Vanuatu, Uruguay, Mexico, Portugal, Barbados, Cook Islands, Guyana, Tonga, and others. Offsets were wrong by 30 to 90 minutes over two dozen or more distinct periods in some regions (Uruguay alone had adjustments across 1920 to 1990). Corrections shipped across multiple tzdb releases including 2018d, 2020e, 2021b, and 2024b.
technicalImpact: Any historical timestamp before 1970 computed from tzdb data prior to the relevant correction release may be wrong by 30 to 90 minutes. Applications doing historical date arithmetic (genealogy software, legal records, financial history, astronomical calculation) are most exposed. The tzdb NEWS file documents which releases corrected which regions, allowing targeted audit.
headline: The corrupted atlas
blurb: A widely trusted reference book had thousands of pre-1970 timezone records that were wrong or simply invented, and tzdb carried those errors for decades.
story: For years, tzdb used 'The International Atlas' by Shanks and Pottenger as its main source for pre-1970 historical timezone data. Researchers eventually found that many entries in that book were incorrect or fabricated outright. Over time, tzdb maintainers began replacing Shanks data country by country with official decrees and archival records, shipping corrections across many releases through 2024. The practical lesson for software: treat any historical timestamp before 1970 as suspect unless your tzdb version postdates the correction for that region.

title: tzdb collapses duplicate zones into Links, ending one-zone-per-country-entry practice
body: IANA tz database maintainers (Paul Eggert, editor/maintainer)
whatChanged: Beginning in 2013, tzdb stopped maintaining a separate Zone entry for every line in zone.tab. Locations that share identical UTC offset history since 1970 were merged: one location keeps the canonical Zone name, and the others become Link entries pointing to it. Dozens of zones across Africa, Asia, the Pacific, Europe, and the Americas were collapsed this way, most actively in the period from 2013 through 2022. The merged names moved to the 'backward' file and, where pre-1970 data differed, to 'backzone'.
technicalImpact: Zone names that were canonical before 2013 but are now Links still resolve correctly, but their reported canonical name changes. Tools that cache or compare canonical zone identifiers, such as Java's ZoneId.of() with normalization or Python's pytz key iteration, may behave differently depending on whether they follow Link indirection. Code that hard-codes or enumerates canonical names against a pre-2013 snapshot can silently diverge.
headline: The great zone collapse
blurb: The tz database stopped giving every country its own unique zone entry and began merging identical zones into shared aliases.
story: For years the tz database kept a separate named zone for almost every inhabited territory, even when two places had used the exact same clock offsets since 1970. Starting in 2013, maintainers began collapsing those duplicates: one location becomes the canonical name and the others become Link aliases pointing to it. The 'backward' compatibility file preserves all the old names so existing software does not break. The catch for developers is that a name that was canonical yesterday can become an alias today, and code that compares or enumerates canonical names without following Links can silently return wrong results.

title: The 1970/POSIX-epoch accuracy cutoff codified as governing design principle; pre-1970 LMT not reliable
body: tz coordinators (Arthur David Olson, Paul Eggert), documented in IANA tz theory.html
whatChanged: The project formalized in the tzdb 'Theory' file (a plain-text document in 2013, converted to theory.html in October 2017) the principle that zones are partitioned so all clocks within a zone agree about timestamps after 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC; pre-1970 data is best-effort, often from 'unreliable sources, often astrology books', and LMT offsets 'should not be considered meaningful'. This principle drove the 2021 zone-merging controversy.
technicalImpact: Sets the correctness contract downstream software relies on: timestamps before 1970 may be inaccurate or coalesced when zones merge, so apps must not depend on pre-1970 historical offsets or on distinct zone IDs sharing post-1970 history. Genealogy, historical-finance, and astronomy apps must not trust zoneinfo for pre-1970 accuracy.

validation.supportingQuote: "The tz database attempts to record the history and predicted future of civil time scales... whose clocks all agree about timestamps that occur after the POSIX Epoch (1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC)." / "Most of the pre-1970 data entries come from unreliable sources, often astrology books..." / "the tz database's LMT offsets should not be considered meaningful..." / "This web page is in the public domain, so clarified as of 2009-05-17 by Arthur David Olson."

headline: Why old dates are not fully trusted
blurb: The time zone project promises accuracy only for times after the start of 1970.
story: The time zone project set a clear rule about how far back its data can be trusted. Clocks within a zone are guaranteed to agree only for times after the start of 1970. Information from before then is a best effort and sometimes comes from unreliable sources. Apps that deal with history, old finance records, or astronomy should not rely on these older offsets.
reviewNote: Re-dated from 2009-05-17 (the public-domain license clarification date) to 2013-09, when the 1970-cutoff principle was documented in tzdb's theory.html 'Accuracy of the tz database' section. The principle itself dates to Olson's original 1980s design.

title: Morocco extends DST to the last Sunday of October and codifies the Ramadan suspension (decree 2.13.781)
body: Government of Morocco (decree 2.13.781)
whatChanged: Morocco issued decree 2.13.781 setting DST to run from the last Sunday of March at 02:00 to the last Sunday of October at 03:00, similar to EU rules, extending it past its previous late-September end. Separately, since 2012 Morocco has suspended DST during Ramadan, reverting clocks toward standard time for the fasting month under ad-hoc annual announcements rather than the decree. For 2013 the Ramadan suspension ran from 2013-07-07 to 2013-08-10.
technicalImpact: Systems running tzdata from before the fix showed Morocco one hour fast from late September through late October 2013. The Ramadan suspension also drove repeated mid-year tzdata patches in 2013, each requiring an update for any system where correct Morocco local time mattered.
headline: Late notice, one more month of summer time
blurb: Morocco extended summer time by a full month while its separate Ramadan clock-back rule kept changing, catching software with stale timezone data off by an hour.
story: In 2013 Morocco issued a decree extending DST to the last Sunday of October, pushing the end well past its old late-September date. Systems still running tzdata from before the fix immediately showed the wrong local time for Casablanca. Separately, the country's annual Ramadan clock-back, a practice begun in 2012 and announced ad hoc each year, had already forced emergency tzdata patches earlier that year, making Morocco one of the most update-intensive zones of the decade.

title: Libya cancels a DST fall-back the day before it happens, switching to permanent UTC+2
body: Government of Libya
whatChanged: Libya had resumed DST in 2013. On 2013-10-24, the day before the scheduled 02:00 fall-back to UTC+1, the government cancelled the transition. Clocks stayed at UTC+2 (EET) and DST was abandoned entirely. tzdata2013h (released 2013-10-25) encodes Africa/Tripoli switching from UTC+1 with DST to UTC+2 permanently.
technicalImpact: Any system running tzdata older than 2013h fell back an hour when it should not have, putting Libyan clocks one hour behind the actual local time. The change illustrates how a last-minute government decision issued less than 24 hours before a transition can leave stale tz databases deployed across software, servers, and devices, causing real scheduling errors.
headline: Libya's last-minute clock reprieve
blurb: Libya cancelled a scheduled daylight saving rollback the day before it was due, leaving outdated software clocks an hour wrong.
story: In 2013 Libya had brought back daylight saving time after years without it. The government scheduled a fall-back on 25 October 2013, but on the afternoon of the 24th it reversed course and kept clocks at UTC+2 year-round. The tzdb maintainers rushed out release 2013h the same day the transition was supposed to happen. Any device or server that had not updated its timezone data fell back an hour automatically, disagreeing with every clock in the country.

title: Acre and western Amazonas revert from UTC-4 to UTC-5
body: Brazilian Federal Government (Federal Law 12.876/2013, championed by Senator Jorge Viana of Acre)
whatChanged: Acre state and the western Amazonas municipalities covered by America/Eirunepe moved back from UTC-4 to UTC-5 (Acre Time) on 2013-11-10, reversing the 2008 change that had merged them into UTC-4. Encoded in tzdata2013h; affects America/Rio_Branco and America/Eirunepe.
technicalImpact: Any system still running pre-2013h tzdata shows America/Rio_Branco and America/Eirunepe one hour ahead of actual local time from 2013-11-10 onward. Both zones needed updating; codebases that hardcoded UTC-4 for Acre broke silently.
headline: Acre turns back the clock
blurb: Brazil's Acre state reversed a 2008 timezone change and moved back one hour to UTC-5.
story: In 2008, Brazil merged Acre and part of western Amazonas into UTC-4 to cut the number of time zones in the country. Residents complained the change pushed sunsets and prime-time TV into uncomfortable early hours. After a 2010 referendum in which 56.87% of Acre voters rejected the UTC-4 change, Federal Law 12.876/2013 restored UTC-5 for the region on 2013-11-10. Systems running tzdata older than 2013h kept reporting local time in Acre as one hour fast.

title: tz database removes the Saudi Arabia solar-time zones (solar87, solar88, solar89)
body: IANA / tz database project (Paul Eggert et al.)
whatChanged: The tz distribution had shipped solar87/88/89, experimental zones encoding apparent solar time at Riyadh, where each day had a different offset (~UTC+3:07 nominal) driven by the equation of time, with hundreds of daily Rule lines. Release 2013i (2013-12-17) stopped distributing them as 'a negative experiment'.
technicalImpact: Each day needed a distinct UTC offset driven by the equation of time, encoded at 5-second resolution.
