The release called the zones a 'negative experiment', a demonstration that tz data can represent solar time only with difficulty and error, and noted their removal shrinks runtime table size. Software referencing 'Asia/Riyadh87' had to fall back to Asia/Riyadh.

validation.supportingQuote: The files solar87, solar88, and solar89 are no longer distributed. They were a negative experiment, that is, a demonstration that tz data can represent solar time only with some difficulty and error.
validation.corrections: No core correction needed. Minor nuance: release/commit date = 2013-12-17 (matches event); tz-announce email date = 2013-12-19. The removal RATIONALE in the event's technicalImpact (256-type limit exhaustion forcing 5-second rounding) is NOT the official stated reason, official reason was "negative experiment" causing confusion. The 256-type unsigned-char limit and apparent-solar/equation-of-time nature are independently true but were not cited as the cause of removal.

headline: Saudi solar-time zones were removed
blurb: An experiment that tracked the sun's exact time over Riyadh was dropped from the time-zone data.
story: The time-zone data once included experimental entries that followed the sun's actual position over Riyadh, giving every single day a slightly different time. They needed hundreds of rules a day. In late 2013 the project removed them, calling the attempt a 'negative experiment' that caused confusion because Riyadh's civil time was generally not solar time in those years. Anything still pointing to those entries had to fall back to the normal Saudi zone.

title: Jordan abandons year-round DST and reverts to standard time in December 2013
body: Government of Jordan
whatChanged: Jordan switched back to standard time at 00:00 on December 20, 2013, ending a period of permanent DST. The 2006-2011 seasonal transition schedule was planned to resume in 2014.
technicalImpact: Required an off-cycle tzdata release (2013i) in December; any system that had not applied the patch before midnight local time on 2013-12-20 ran one hour ahead of the correct Jordan civil time.
headline: Jordan's mid-winter clock reversal
blurb: Jordan quietly dropped out of permanent daylight saving time on a Friday night in December, catching many systems off guard.
story: Jordan had been holding its clocks in a permanent summer-time offset since October 2012, when it first skipped the usual autumn fallback. In December 2013 the government reversed course, ordering clocks back at midnight on the 20th. The change landed in an emergency tzdata release (2013i) just days before it took effect. Any server or device running outdated timezone data reported Jordan time an hour fast until it was patched.

title: Australia's timezone abbreviation dispute: AEDT vs EDT vs EST vs ESuT
body: IANA tz database maintainers (Paul Eggert); Australian federal agencies including the Bureau of Meteorology, the Civil Aviation Safety Authority, and the Australian Transport Safety Bureau
whatChanged: After surveying Australian newspapers and government sources in 2014, the IANA tz database standardised on AEDT and ACDT for Australian daylight saving abbreviations. Earlier the database used EST for both Eastern Standard Time and Eastern Summer Time, because Australian legislation calls DST 'Summer Time' rather than 'Daylight Time', and Summer and Standard both start with S. Federal agencies stayed split: the Bureau of Meteorology used EST, CST, WST, EDT, and CDT (dropping the A prefix from every form), the Civil Aviation Safety Authority used EST and CST, the Australian Transport Safety Bureau mostly used ESuT and CSuT (alongside some EDT/CDT and AEDT), and the Australian Government used AEDT and ACDT.
technicalImpact: Parsing user-supplied Australian timezone abbreviations is unreliable. 'EST' in an Australian context collides with US Eastern Standard Time. Applications should use IANA zone identifiers (Australia/Sydney, Australia/Adelaide) rather than abbreviations to identify Australian times.
headline: When 'S' means two things at once
blurb: Australia calls its daylight saving period 'Summer Time', so the S in AEST could stand for Standard or Summer, and for years nobody agreed on the abbreviation.
story: Australian law uses the term 'Summer Time' rather than 'Daylight Time', which meant the clocks shifted but the abbreviation arguably did not have to. For years the tz database kept EST for both Eastern Standard Time and Eastern Summer Time in Australia. The maintainers then surveyed major newspapers and several government agencies and found no consensus: the weather bureau wrote EDT, the aviation regulator wrote EST, and the transport safety board invented ESuT. The database settled on AEDT, but the disagreement still lives on in legacy systems. Any code that parses an Australian timezone abbreviation as a string is on shaky ground.

title: Egypt's four-transition DST year: Ramadan suspension and resumption in 2014
body: Egyptian Cabinet (Council of Ministers)
whatChanged: Egypt reintroduced DST in 2014 after a four-year absence, but suspended it for Ramadan, producing four clock transitions in a single year: clocks sprang forward on 15 May 2014 (UTC+2 to UTC+3), fell back on 26 June 2014 (UTC+3 to UTC+2) for Ramadan, sprang forward again on 31 July 2014 (UTC+2 to UTC+3) after Ramadan ended, and fell back on the last Thursday of September 2014 (UTC+3 to UTC+2).
technicalImpact: Software, OS patches, and calendar libraries that assume at most two DST transitions per year (one spring-forward, one fall-back) computed wrong UTC offsets for Africa/Cairo during summer 2014. Microsoft issued an emergency fix for Windows (KB2967990, "2014 - Egypt Ramadan DST changes"). tzdb releases 2014c and 2014e (Africa/Cairo) encode the four Rule lines; any application bundling an earlier tzdata version returned incorrect local times for Egypt between May and September 2014.
headline: Four clock changes in one Egyptian summer
blurb: Egypt suspended daylight saving time for the fasting month of Ramadan in 2014, then resumed it, forcing clocks to change four times in a single year instead of the usual two.
story: Egypt brought back daylight saving time in 2014 after abandoning it in 2010. But because Ramadan fell in summer that year, the government paused DST for the whole fasting month so that sunset, and therefore the end of the daily fast, came an hour earlier. That meant Egyptian clocks changed four times: forward in May, back in late June, forward again in August, and back in late September. Most software and operating systems are written to expect exactly two clock changes per year, so the extra pair of transitions scrambled scheduling apps, airline systems, and calendar software worldwide. Microsoft had to ship an emergency fix for Windows.

title: Troll station's UTC+1 shoulder window commented out for zic compatibility
body: The accurate three-step transition for Antarctica/Troll (UTC+0 to UTC+1 to UTC+2 in spring, and back in autumn) was written into tzdb Rule lines, then commented out. Older zic compilers, before tzdb 2014b, could not handle the rules, so the live encoding was simplified to a direct UTC+0 to UTC+2 jump, dropping the short UTC+1 shoulder windows. The source comment reads that the CET-switching Troll rules require zic from tz 2014b or later, so they are commented out for now and approximated with only UTC and CEST.
whatChanged: The correct three-step transition for Antarctica/Troll (UTC+0 to UTC+1 to UTC+2 in spring, and UTC+2 to UTC+1 to UTC+0 in autumn) was encoded in tzdb Rule lines but then commented out. Older zic compilers (pre-tzdb 2014b) could not handle the rules correctly, so the live encoding was simplified to a direct UTC+0 to UTC+2 jump, dropping the short UTC+1 shoulder windows in March and October-November.
technicalImpact: The accurate rules require zic from tzdb 2014b or later to compile correctly; 2014b shipped that zic fix alongside the Troll zone itself. Because many deployed systems were still running older zic toolchains that could not process the four-transitions-per-year rules, the data shipped as a documented approximation: Antarctica/Troll skips UTC+1 entirely in the compiled output, making the zone appear to observe only UTC+0 and UTC+2. Software consuming tz data inherited this inaccuracy silently.
headline: The timezone rule that had to stay commented out
blurb: The tz database deliberately shipped an inaccurate rule for Troll station because the correct version would have broken older compilers.
story: Troll, Norway's Antarctic research station, actually observes UTC+1 for a short window each spring and autumn before jumping to or from summer time at UTC+2. The tzdb maintainers wrote the correct rules, then had to comment them out: older versions of the zic compiler could not process the three-step transition without errors. The live rules shipped instead skip UTC+1 entirely, jumping straight between UTC+0 and UTC+2. It is a rare case where the authoritative timezone database knowingly contains an approximation, and the correct code sits in commented-out lines waiting for the toolchain to catch up.

title: Antarctica/Troll added: the only zone that swings UTC+0 <-> UTC+2 with a two-hour DST jump
body: IANA / tz database project (data from Norwegian Polar Institute contributors)
whatChanged: Release 2014b (2014-03-24) added Antarctica/Troll for Norway's Troll research station. It uses UTC+0 from October to March and UTC+2 (matching CEST) from March to October, a two-hour shift, transitioning at CET/CEST dates to stay aligned with mainland Norway's logistics flights.
technicalImpact: The only zone where SAVE = 2:00, so code assuming DST adds at most one hour mis-handles Troll's spring-forward (clocks jump 01:00->03:00, a two-hour gap) and fall-back (two-hour ambiguous window). The definitive test case for libraries to validate they read the actual SAVE value and correctly size skipped/repeated intervals.

validation.supportingQuote: Rule Troll 2005 max - Mar lastSun 1:00u 2:00 +02 ... From Paul Eggert (2014-03-21): The CET-switching Troll rules require zic from tz 2014b or later
validation.corrections: Replace "It uses UTC+0 in the austral winter and UTC+2 (matching CEST) in the austral summer" with "It uses UTC+0 in the austral summer (Oct-March) and UTC+2 (matching CEST) in the austral winter (the dark March-October season with no flights, following mainland Norway's summer time)." The two-hour spring-forward occurs on the last Sunday of March (austral autumn/winter onset), fall-back on the last Sunday of October.

headline: A station that shifts its clock two hours
blurb: An Antarctic research station was added with a rare two-hour seasonal clock change.
story: In 2014 a new time zone was added for Norway's Troll research station in Antarctica. To stay in step with flights to and from mainland Norway, it shifts its clock by a full two hours between seasons rather than the usual one. That makes it the only zone with a two-hour seasonal jump, which trips up software that assumes the change is always one hour.

title: Crimea abandons Ukrainian time for Moscow Time (UTC+2 to UTC+4) after annexation
body: Russian Federation (following annexation of Crimea from Ukraine)
whatChanged: At 02:00 local time on 2014-03-30, the Republic of Crimea and Sevastopol moved clocks two hours forward, leaving Ukrainian EET (UTC+2, with EU DST rules) for Moscow Time, then permanently at UTC+4. In October 2014, when Russia moved to permanent UTC+3, Crimea followed. Encoded in tzdata2014b as Europe/Simferopol.
technicalImpact: tzdata2014b updated Europe/Simferopol; any software or database using cached Ukrainian offsets for Crimean locations immediately returned wrong times. The contested jurisdiction created ambiguity: Ukrainian systems kept UTC+2/EEST for the same territory, so the correct offset depended on whose political position the software adopted.
headline: Crimea's two-hour clock jump
blurb: After Russia annexed Crimea in 2014, the peninsula's clocks jumped two hours forward overnight to match Moscow Time.
story: In March 2014, days after Russia formally annexed Crimea, authorities ordered clocks moved from Ukrainian time (UTC+2) to Moscow Time (then UTC+4). Ordinary residents made the switch at 2am on March 30, a two-hour leap in a single night. Ukraine never recognised the change and kept its own offset on record for the same territory. The split created a rare case where the correct UTC offset for a location genuinely depended on which country's database you used.

title: Europe/Simferopol: Crimea switches to Moscow time after annexation
body: Russian Federation (de facto authority over Crimea from March 2014)
whatChanged: Crimea moved from EET (UTC+2, DST to UTC+3) to Moscow time (UTC+4 initially, then UTC+3 permanent from 2014-10-26 when Russia abolished DST). The IANA zone Europe/Simferopol is listed with both RU and UA country codes in zone1970.tab, with an explicit comment that the listing does not imply endorsement of any territorial claim.
technicalImpact: Apps must not infer DST behaviour from Crimea's current UTC+3 offset: it is permanent Moscow time, not EEST. The dual RU,UA country-code listing means country-based zone lookups may return unexpected results. tzdb releases from 2014 onward carry a disclaimer that disputed-territory listings are geographical, not political.
headline: Crimea's contested clock
blurb: After Russia annexed Crimea in 2014, the peninsula switched from Ukrainian time to Moscow time, leaving its timezone listed under two countries in the IANA database.
story: When Russia annexed Crimea in March 2014, the peninsula moved its clocks to Moscow time, skipping the usual DST cycle Ukraine observes. IANA had to list the Europe/Simferopol zone under both RU and UA country codes, alongside a header disclaimer that the table takes no position on legal or territorial claims. Then in October 2014, Russia abolished DST entirely, locking Crimea at UTC+3 permanently. Software that assumes the current UTC+3 offset behaves like EEST (summertime) will mishandle timestamps from the region.

title: Turkey delays 2014 spring-forward by one day for local elections
body: Turkish government (Prime Minister's office)
whatChanged: Turkey moved its 2014 spring-forward from Sunday March 30 to Monday March 31 at 03:00, delaying the clocks-ahead change by one day to avoid disrupting local elections held on March 30. The change was announced with roughly five weeks' notice.
technicalImpact: Systems that could not be updated in time (airline baggage handling, cell phones) kept the old March 30 rule, causing widespread clock skew on March 31. tzdb release 2014a (2014-03-07) captured the corrected rule for Europe/Istanbul.
headline: The election that moved Turkey's clocks
blurb: Turkey pushed its spring clock change back one day to avoid confusing voters on election morning, and the short notice left airlines and phones running on the wrong time.
story: In early 2014 the Turkish government announced it was shifting the spring-forward from March 30 to March 31, giving just five weeks' notice so the local elections on March 30 would not be disrupted. Cell phones and airline baggage systems never got the update in time and kept springing forward on the 30th. Travelers arriving in Istanbul that day found that not even the airlines respected the new rule. The episode is a textbook example of why late timezone changes, even small single-day shifts, can break real infrastructure.

title: Egypt re-introduces DST on short notice in 2014 with mid-period Ramadan suspensions
body: Arab Republic of Egypt (interim government / Cabinet decision May 2014), encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: After abolishing DST in 2011, Egypt's interim government reinstated it with ~a week's notice: DST started 2014-05-15, then clocks fell back for Ramadan (2014-06-26 to 2014-07-31) and sprang forward again afterward, producing four offset transitions in a single summer.
technicalImpact: The week-of notice outran normal tzdata/OS update cycles; Microsoft shipped emergency 'Easy fix' hotfixes, IBM issued APAR IV61426, and moment-timezone was re-patched. The four-transition Ramadan pattern broke code assuming at most two DST transitions per year, and the religiously-timed dates defeated future-date guessing. Devices disagreed by an hour around each transition until patched (tzdata 2014c, refined 2014e).

validation.supportingQuote: Egypt's 2014 Ramadan-based transitions are June 26 and July 31 at 24:00. Egypt temporarily switches to standard time at midnight the last Thursday before Ramadan, then returns to DST at midnight the first Friday after Ramadan.

headline: Egypt changed clocks four times in one summer
blurb: Egypt brought back daylight saving with about a week of notice, then paused it for Ramadan, so clocks shifted four times that summer.
story: In 2014 Egypt decided to use daylight saving time again after dropping it in 2011. The notice was very short, only about a week. Clocks then moved back for the month of Ramadan and forward again afterward, so the time changed four times in one summer. Phones and computers that had not been updated showed the wrong time by an hour around each change.

title: tzdb 2014d: zic stops generating timestamps before the Big Bang
body: IANA tz project (zic compiler)
whatChanged: In tzdb release 2014d, zic was changed so it no longer generates TZif files containing transition timestamps well before the Big Bang. The previous dummy initial transition (about 2**63 seconds before the epoch) was reduced to time -2**59 seconds before the epoch (still roughly 18 billion years before 1970, before the Big Bang). This worked around GNOME Bugzilla bug 730332, where glib computed the wrong UTC offset (an integer overflow in interval end-time calculation) when parsing such far-past timestamps. (Such transitions were not removed entirely until release 2018f.)
technicalImpact: GNOME and GTK applications using glib for timezone parsing reported wrong UTC offsets (e.g. a clock several minutes off, or a zone shown as 'LMT') before this fix, caused by an integer overflow when handling minimum-value timestamps. The fix landed in zic (the compiler), not in glib itself, so any other TZif reader consuming files with extreme far-past timestamps could still be affected. Consumers of TZif files should treat arbitrarily early timestamps as valid input.
headline: Timestamps older than the universe
blurb: A bug in GNOME's glib library caused wrong timezone offsets when timezone files contained transition dates billions of years before the Big Bang, so the tz compiler was patched to reduce how far back it emitted them.
story: TZif files can encode timezone transitions all the way back to the earliest representable timestamp. The GNOME glib library hit a crash when it encountered entries predating the Big Bang, roughly 13.8 billion years ago. Rather than fix glib, the tzdb team patched the zic compiler to stop emitting those extreme timestamps entirely. The episode shows that any library reading TZif data needs to handle arbitrarily ancient timestamps, because the format permits them.

title: Australian timezone abbreviations changed from EST/CST/WST to AEST/ACST/AWST
body: IANA tz database maintainers (tzdb project)
whatChanged: tzdb release 2014f renamed Australian timezone abbreviations: eastern zones changed from EST to AEST/AEDT, central zones from CST to ACST/ACDT (and ACWST/ACWDT for central-west), and western zones from WST to AWST/AWDT. UTC offsets were not changed, only the short abbreviation strings.
technicalImpact: Any code that parsed the abbreviation string 'EST' to infer an Australian eastern offset would silently break, since EST also identifies US Eastern Standard Time (UTC-5). The fix highlighted a broader rule: timezone abbreviations are not unique identifiers and must never be used alone to determine UTC offset.
headline: Australia drops the ambiguous EST
blurb: The tz database renamed Australian timezone abbreviations to include a country prefix, ending a long-standing collision with US Eastern Standard Time.
story: For years, Australian eastern time shared the abbreviation EST with US Eastern Standard Time, two zones with opposite signs on the clock. In 2014 the tz database fixed this by prepending A to all Australian abbreviations: EST became AEST, CST became ACST, WST became AWST. No clocks moved, only the labels changed. Software that keyed on those abbreviation strings to guess an offset had to be corrected, and the episode became a textbook example of why abbreviations alone cannot identify a timezone.
sourceTitle: [tz-announce] 2014f release of tz code and data available

title: China's five time zones collapsed to two; three zones removed as imaginary
body: IANA tz database maintainers (based on research by Luther Ma and Alois Treindl, translating papers by Guo Qingsheng)
whatChanged: Asia/Harbin, Asia/Chongqing, and Asia/Kashgar were removed from the tz database; post-1970 differences in those zones were found to have been imaginary. Backward-compatibility links remain (Harbin and Chongqing link to Asia/Shanghai, Kashgar links to Asia/Urumqi) but return different pre-May-1980 timestamps. Asia/Urumqi's 1980 transition to UTC+08 was also removed, leaving it at UTC+06.
technicalImpact: Code referencing Asia/Harbin or Asia/Chongqing silently falls back to Asia/Shanghai data for post-1970 timestamps; Asia/Kashgar instead falls back to Asia/Urumqi. Asia/Urumqi at UTC+06 (not UTC+08) is a common pitfall for western China; any app that assumed +08 for all of China gets wrong results.
headline: China's phantom time zones
blurb: Three of China's five named time zones were deleted after researchers found their post-1970 differences never actually existed.
story: China officially uses one standard time across the whole country, but the tz database had historically carried five named zones. When researchers dug into the historical records, the post-1970 differences in three of those zones turned out to be invented, not real policy. The 2014f tz release dropped Asia/Harbin, Asia/Chongqing, and Asia/Kashgar, and also corrected Asia/Urumqi to sit at UTC+06 rather than UTC+08. Software that hard-coded any of those removed zone names now silently gets Shanghai data, and any app that assumed all of China runs on UTC+08 will mishandle western Xinjiang.

title: tzdb introduces zone1970.tab: 1970 as the zone-grouping cutoff
body: IANA tz database maintainers (led by Paul Eggert)
whatChanged: tzdb release 2014f introduced zone1970.tab, a new timezone table that supersedes the older zone.tab. The file groups all locations into a single zone entry if and only if their civil timestamps have agreed since 1970-01-01. Disagreement before 1970 is tolerated and does not force a split; any disagreement from 1970 onward requires a separate zone entry. Multiple country codes per zone are permitted, unlike zone.tab.
technicalImpact: Software querying only current or post-1970 offsets can safely use zone1970.tab groupings and get a smaller, simpler table. Any application making pre-1970 historical timestamp lookups must use the fuller primary .zi zone data; using zone1970.tab for pre-1970 queries will silently return wrong offsets. The 1970 cutoff also explains why some locations share a zone name despite having different pre-1970 histories, which surprises developers who expect each distinct historical offset to map to its own zone.
headline: The 1970 line in the timezone database
blurb: The tz database draws a firm line at 1970: if two places have kept the same clock since then, they share one zone entry, no matter what they did before.
story: The timezone database has to decide when two cities are different enough to get separate entries. The answer, formalized in a file called zone1970.tab introduced in 2014, is the Unix epoch: January 1, 1970. If two places have always agreed on their local time from that date onward, they share a single zone, even if they disagreed for decades before. This keeps the database from ballooning with historical curiosities. The rule has a sharp edge for software: any lookup for a timestamp before 1970 using zone1970.tab can silently produce the wrong offset, because the file was never designed to cover that era.
sourceTitle: [tz-announce] 2014f release of tz code and data available

title: Belarus keeps UTC+3 but drops the FET abbreviation for MSK
body: IANA tz database maintainers (reflecting Belarus's decision not to follow Russia's clock change)
whatChanged: When Russia rolled its clocks back from UTC+4 to UTC+3 on 2014-10-26, Belarus had already been on UTC+3 since 2011 and stayed put. Because both countries now shared UTC+3, the Europe/Minsk tzdb entry dropped the FET (Further-Eastern European Time) abbreviation and reverted to the more traditional MSK label, effective 2014-10-26 at 01:00. No offset changed; only the abbreviation metadata changed.
technicalImpact: Code keyed on timezone abbreviation strings rather than numeric offsets could break silently: FET and MSK both mean UTC+3, but any hardcoded string comparison or switch referencing FET stopped matching. Packaged in 2014i.
headline: The abbreviation that outlived its reason
blurb: Belarus quietly lost its unique FET timezone label when Russia rejoined it at UTC+3, even though no clock actually moved.
story: In 2011 Belarus abolished daylight saving and locked its clocks to UTC+3, taking the FET label to distinguish itself from Moscow, which was then at UTC+4. In 2014 Russia reversed course and dropped back to UTC+3, so Minsk and Moscow shared the same offset again. The tzdb maintainers retired FET and restored the traditional MSK abbreviation for Europe/Minsk. No clock changed by a single second, yet any software treating timezone abbreviations as stable identifiers had to be updated.

title: Russia abolishes permanent summer time, subtracting an hour from most zones
body: Government of the Russian Federation
whatChanged: On 2014-10-26 at 02:00 local time, Russia subtracted one hour from most of its time zones, reversing the 2011 move to permanent summer time. Europe/Moscow dropped from UTC+4 to UTC+3. Magadan Oblast and Zabaykalsky Krai subtracted two hours. Chukotka, Kamchatka, Kemerovo, Samara, and the Udmurt Republic made no change. Two new zones were added: Asia/Chita (split from Asia/Yakutsk, minus two hours) and Asia/Srednekolymsk (split from Asia/Magadan, minus one hour).
technicalImpact: Russia's reversal meant applications encoding Russian time offsets needed emergency updates twice in three years (2011 and 2014). The Europe/Moscow offset history now has a UTC+4 segment spanning 2011 to 2014. The addition of Asia/Chita and Asia/Srednekolymsk required new zone entries in all tz databases. Captured in tzdb release 2014f.
headline: Russia's decade of clock experiments
blurb: Russia reversed a 2011 decision to stay on summer time year-round, subtracting an hour from most of the country in 2014.
story: In 2011, Russia eliminated the twice-yearly clock change by staying permanently on summer time. The experiment proved unpopular: winter mornings became very dark. So in 2014 the government reversed course and moved most of the country back, subtracting an hour on October 26. Not every region moved the same way: some dropped two hours, and a handful did not change at all. Two brand-new time zones had to be created to capture regions that ended up on different offsets than their neighbors. Any software that hardcoded Russian UTC offsets had to be updated urgently, twice in three years.

title: Russia reverts to permanent standard time, cutting most zones by an hour and re-splitting its zone map
body: Russian Federation (Federal Law No. 248-FZ, signed by President Putin), encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: On 2014-10-26 at 02:00 local most Russian zones subtracted one hour and froze on permanent standard time (no DST). Moscow moved from UTC+4 to year-round UTC+3. The law also re-drew the zone map: some oblasts subtracted two hours while others kept their offset, adding new fixed zones.
technicalImpact: A one-time backward offset change with no recurring rule meant every Russian timestamp after 2014-10-26 from an un-updated system was an hour ahead. Because regions changed by different amounts (0, 1, or 2 hours) and some split into new zones (Asia/Chita, Asia/Srednekolymsk), simple country-wide patches were wrong; libraries needed the full tzdata 2014f reshaping across Russia's ~16 IANA zone identifiers (the law defined 11 legal zones).

validation.supportingQuote: "Most Russians moved to permanent Standard Time, or 'winter time', by setting their clocks 1 hour back on October 26, 2014... The bill also introduced 2 new time zones... increasing the number of time zones in Russia from 9 to 11." President Vladimir Putin signed the law on July 22, 2014.
validation.corrections: In whatChanged/technicalImpact, replace the new-zone example 'Europe/Samara' with 'Asia/Chita'. Correct phrasing: "some split into new zones (Asia/Chita, Asia/Srednekolymsk)". Europe/Samara was not new in 2014f and did not shift on 2014-10-26.

headline: Russia moved most clocks back and stopped daylight saving
blurb: Russia set most of its regions to a permanent winter time, with some areas changing more than others.
story: On a single day in 2014 most of Russia moved its clocks back and stopped changing them for the seasons. Moscow settled on a fixed year-round time. Different regions changed by different amounts, and a few were placed into new time zones. Computers that were not updated showed Russian times an hour off afterward.

title: IANA tzdb backzone file flags Shanks and Pottenger atlas as error-ridden and unsourced
body: IANA tz database maintainers (Paul Eggert and contributors)
whatChanged: Paul Eggert added a formal header to the tzdb backzone file explicitly stating that the primary source for much pre-1970 data, Thomas G. Shanks and Rique Pottenger's 'The International Atlas', contains many errors and cites no sources. Zones moved to backzone are those whose pre-1970 entries are considered questionable or unverifiable. The file is not compiled by default and requires a special build flag (PACKRATDATA=backzone) to include.
technicalImpact: Software or data pipelines that consume tzdb backzone data for pre-1970 timestamps should treat all results as best-guess approximations. Offsets in this file must not be used in legal, forensic, or auditing contexts. Libraries built on tzdb (Java, Python, Go, ICU, etc.) exclude backzone by default, but applications that opt in face silent inaccuracy for historical timestamps.
headline: The atlas that guessed
blurb: The main historical source for pre-1970 time zone data in the global tz database openly admits it contains many errors and cites no sources.
story: When the tz database needed historical time zone offsets before 1970, maintainers relied heavily on a printed atlas by Thomas Shanks and Rique Pottenger. Researchers later found the book full of errors, with offsets that appeared to be guesses rather than documented facts. Maintainers moved this shaky data into a separate file called backzone, and on 2014-10-31 Paul Eggert added a header warning that the source is unreliable. Today that file is excluded from most builds of the tz database by default. Any software that opts in and uses it for old timestamps may quietly return wrong results.

title: Turks and Caicos Islands aborts planned switch to year-round UTC-4
body: Government of the Turks and Caicos Islands
whatChanged: Turks and Caicos had been scheduled to stop observing US Eastern time and switch to permanent UTC-4 on 2014-11-02 at 02:00. The switch did not occur. tzdata2014j (released 2014-11-10) reverted the rule and rescheduled the change to 2015-11-01 at 02:00.
technicalImpact: Any system or application that had already consumed the predicted 2014j rule set the wrong offset for Turks and Caicos from November 2014 onward. Developers updating tzdata at different times could see inconsistent local times for America/Grand_Turk between tzdata releases. Highlighted the hazard of deploying tz rules before a government formally enacts them.
headline: The island that blinked on its own time change
blurb: Turks and Caicos Islands canceled a scheduled break from US Eastern time at the last minute, forcing a tzdata rollback just days after the planned switch.
story: Turks and Caicos had planned to stop clocking alongside the US East Coast and stay on UTC-4 year-round starting November 2, 2014. The switch never happened. Eight days later, the IANA tzdb released version 2014j to undo the rule and push the target date to 2015. Any device or server that had already loaded the earlier tzdata was now showing the wrong local time for the islands. It is a textbook example of why software should not trust scheduled government time changes until they actually take effect.

title: Pacific/Bougainville created as PNG's autonomous region shifts to UTC+11
body: Autonomous Region of Bougainville, Papua New Guinea
whatChanged: The Autonomous Region of Bougainville switched from UTC+10 to UTC+11 (Bougainville Standard Time) on 2014-12-28 at 02:00 local time. tzdata2014i (released 2014-10-21) added the new IANA zone identifier Pacific/Bougainville to cover this region separately from the rest of Papua New Guinea, which remained on UTC+10.
technicalImpact: Any system that did not ship tzdata2014i before 2014-12-28 would have placed Bougainville clocks one hour behind. Applications and OSes must ship updated tz data to resolve new IANA zone identifiers; hardcoded UTC+10 offsets for all of PNG broke as soon as the split took effect.
headline: Papua New Guinea splits its clock
blurb: Bougainville broke away from the rest of Papua New Guinea's time zone to run one hour ahead, forcing a new IANA zone identifier into existence.
story: The Autonomous Region of Bougainville, a group of islands in the far east of Papua New Guinea, decided it wanted its clocks to match its geography better. At 2 AM on 28 December 2014 the region jumped from UTC+10 to UTC+11, splitting off from the mainland. IANA had to create a brand-new zone, Pacific/Bougainville, to track it separately. Any software or OS that had not updated its tz database before that morning silently placed Bougainville residents an hour behind.

title: Metlakatla Indian Community cycles through four timezone changes in four years
body: Metlakatla Indian Community (Alaska)
whatChanged: America/Metlakatla changed zone three times: PST (no DST) until 2015-11-01, then AKST/AKDT until 2018-11-04, then PST (no DST) for 77 days, then AKST/AKDT from 2019-01-20 onward. A December 2018 special election triggered the final return, forcing two clock changes within eleven weeks.
technicalImpact: America/Metlakatla gained one of the most turbulent recent histories in tzdb. Systems caching zone data without regular updates silently computed wrong local times during the 77-day PST window (2018-11-04 to 2019-01-20).
headline: The town that changed its clocks three times in four years
blurb: A small Alaska community voted to switch time zones so many times that software had to be updated repeatedly just to track what hour it was there.
story: Metlakatla, a Native community on Annette Island in Alaska, had stayed on Pacific Time while the rest of Alaska used Alaska Time. In 2015 it switched to Alaska Time and daylight saving. Then in November 2018 it quietly stopped observing the fall-back, putting itself back on Pacific Time. A special community vote in December 2018 reversed that, and in January 2019 the clocks shifted again, meaning residents changed their clocks twice in eleven weeks. Every flip required a tzdb update, and any system running stale timezone data gave the wrong local time during each gap.

title: Chile suspends DST and stays on permanent summer time (UTC-3)
body: Ministry of Energy, Government of Chile
whatChanged: On 28 January 2015 Chile's Ministry of Energy announced the country would stop its biannual clock changes and remain on summer time (UTC-3) year-round for mainland Chile. The plan was reversed in March 2016: clocks returned to standard time (UTC-4) on 15 May 2016, with DST resuming on 14 August 2016.
technicalImpact: tzdata 2015a (released 29 January 2015) carried the Chile mainland update; 2015b (released 19 March 2015) fixed a related regression in Pacific/Easter. Microsoft shipped a Windows hotfix for the change. The 2016 reversal required a second round of OS and tzdata patches. Cached offset assumptions broke twice in 14 months, a textbook policy-whipsaw scenario.
headline: The year Chile tried to abolish the clock change
blurb: Chile announced it would stay on summer time all year, then reversed the decision just 14 months later.
story: In January 2015, Chile's government decided to stop switching clocks twice a year and stay permanently on summer time, three hours behind UTC. The official reason was energy efficiency. Within a year, parents and schools were complaining that winter mornings had become dangerously dark, and in March 2016 the government reversed course. Two policy changes in 14 months meant every platform that handles Chilean time had to be patched twice in quick succession, a clear example of how short-lived political decisions can cascade into real software pain.

title: Quintana Roo (Cancun) switches to permanent Eastern Standard Time
body: Government of Mexico (Diario Oficial de la Federacion decree, 2015-01-31)
whatChanged: Quintana Roo (America/Cancun) moved from UTC-6 with Mexico DST to UTC-5 (Eastern Standard Time) with no DST, effective 2015-02-01 at 02:00 local. Mexico's Hour System Law was amended to define a Southeast Zone covering Quintana Roo alone.
technicalImpact: Systems that assumed all of mainland Mexico follows Central Time broke immediately. Travel booking engines, flight schedulers, and hotel systems serving Cancun needed a tz-database update (2015a or later) to reflect America/Cancun as a fixed UTC-5 zone. Any code hard-coding Mexico to CST/CDT was silently wrong from this date.
headline: Cancun ditches DST for good
blurb: Mexico's Yucatan Peninsula tourist hub permanently moved one hour ahead of the rest of Mexico and stopped observing daylight saving time.
story: Quintana Roo had been out of step with local sunrise and sunset times for years, and a municipality called Felipe Carrillo Puerto had simply refused to follow Mexico's clock changes for over a decade. In February 2015 the federal government formalized a new Southeast Zone, fixing Cancun at UTC-5 year-round with no DST. The practical effect was that Cancun now matches Miami's winter clock (UTC-5), though the two diverge in summer when Miami shifts to EDT (UTC-4) while Cancun stays fixed at UTC-5. Software serving Mexico had to stop treating the country as a single timezone and handle America/Cancun as a permanently fixed UTC-5 offset.

title: Cancun locks to permanent EST, ending decades of zone changes
body: Government of Mexico / State of Quintana Roo
whatChanged: On 2015-02-01 at 02:00 local time, Quintana Roo advanced clocks one hour from CST (UTC-6) to EST (UTC-5) permanently, with no DST. America/Cancun is now fixed at UTC-5 year-round. This was the final step in a long history: LMT (-5:47, until 1922-01-01) -> CST (-6, 1922-01-01 to 1981-12-26) -> EST (-5, 1981-12-26 to 1983-01-04) -> CST with Mexico DST (1983-01-04 to 1997-10-26) -> EST with Mexico DST (1997-10-26 to 1998-08-02) -> CST with Mexico DST (1998-08-02 to 2015-02-01) -> permanent EST (-5).
technicalImpact: America/Cancun is UTC-5 with no DST, not CDT in summer. Systems that label it CST or apply Mexican DST rules show wrong times. The multi-transition history in tzdb means old snapshots or hand-rolled offset tables are likely stale. Any code caching the offset without a tz database lookup will be wrong by one hour for about half the year.
headline: Cancun's permanent beach time
blurb: Quintana Roo locked its clocks to UTC-5 year-round so Cancun visitors get longer lit evenings every day of the year.
story: Hotel owners and the Quintana Roo state government lobbied for years to keep the sun up later into the evening, arguing it drove tourism spending. On 1 February 2015, Mexico granted the exception: the state moved to UTC-5 and stopped observing daylight saving time permanently. The rest of Mexico still springs forward and falls back, so for half the year Cancun is one hour ahead of Mexico City. Software that treats all of Mexico as a single time zone, or caches the old CST offset, will display wrong times for Cancun.

title: Turks and Caicos flips to year-round AST in 2015, then back to US Eastern time in 2018
body: Government of Turks and Caicos Islands (Standard Time Declaration Orders L.N. 15/2015 and L.N. 31/2017)
whatChanged: On 2015-03-08 at 02:00 local time, Turks and Caicos switched from US Eastern time (EST/EDT, UTC-5/UTC-4) to permanent AST (UTC-4) year-round, eliminating DST. On 2018-03-11 the islands reversed course and rejoined the US Eastern time zone. The 2018 legal order stated 02:00 as the cutover, but since EDT in the US did not start until an hour later, the tzdb assumes the actual switch took effect at 03:00 -04 when US clocks sprang forward. The offset stayed at -04 throughout, so only the abbreviation and DST flag for that hour are affected.
technicalImpact: Any scheduling system that cached 'Grand Turk = AST' before 2018 would break after the reversal. The ambiguous 2018 legal text (which of the two 02:00 moments?) is documented in the tzdb comments; the zone entry resolves it by using 03:00 to avoid recording a second clock change within one hour. America/Grand_Turk must be used rather than a static Caribbean-AST assumption.
headline: The island that changed its mind twice
blurb: Turks and Caicos ditched daylight saving in 2015 for permanent summer time, then quietly reversed the decision three years later.
story: In 2015 the Turks and Caicos Islands decided to stop changing their clocks and stay on Atlantic Standard Time all year, matching their Caribbean neighbours. By 2017 the government changed course again, agreeing to rejoin US Eastern time with its seasonal DST shifts starting in March 2018. The 2018 law was itself ambiguous about the exact moment of the switch, and the timezone database had to pick a reasonable interpretation to avoid recording two clock changes within a single hour. Software that assumed the islands were permanently on AST broke the moment they sprang forward in 2018.

title: Boeing 787 GCU 248-day software counter overflow
body: U.S. FAA (Airworthiness Directive 2015-09-07); Boeing
whatChanged: A software counter inside the 787's Generator Control Units increments at a fixed rate and overflows after 248 days of continuous power (consistent with a signed 32-bit counter ticking ~every 10 ms). On overflow each affected GCU enters fail-safe mode.
technicalImpact: If all four main GCUs are powered up simultaneously, after 248 continuous days they all enter fail-safe at once, causing loss of all AC electrical power regardless of flight phase. The FAA's AD (effective 2015-05-01) mandated electrical power deactivation at intervals not to exceed 120 days, pending a Boeing software fix.

validation.supportingQuote: A Model 787 airplane that has been powered continuously for 248 days can lose all alternating current (AC) electrical power due to the generator control units (GCUs) simultaneously going into failsafe mode... caused by a software counter internal to the GCUs that will overflow after 248 days of continuous power. (FAA AD 2015-09-07, effective May 1, 2015)

headline: Boeing 787 power units failed after 248 days on
blurb: A counter inside the 787's power control units overflowed after 248 days of nonstop power and put them into a safe shutdown mode.
story: The Boeing 787 has units that control its electrical generators. A counter inside them runs up while the power stays on and overflows after 248 days without a break. When that happens the units switch into a protective shutdown. If all four ran nonstop at once, they could shut down together and cut the plane's main electrical power, so regulators required crews to power the system off and on at regular intervals until a software fix was ready.

title: AWS adopts a 24-hour leap smear for the 2015 leap second
body: Amazon Web Services (vendor mitigation)
whatChanged: AWS announced it would not insert a literal leap second; instead each AWS clock-second would be lengthened by 1/86400 across a 24-hour window (12:00 UTC 2015-06-30 to 12:00 UTC 2015-07-01), so AWS clocks fall behind, never show :60, and re-converge with UTC.
technicalImpact: AWS smeared the leap second only across its own infrastructure and timestamps: the Management Console and backend systems, resource-creation timestamps, metering and billing records, Amazon CloudFront logs, and AWS CloudTrail logs, so those avoid the :60 / backward-step faults and stay smooth. Customer compute was NOT smeared: EC2 instances running the Amazon Linux AMI, plus ECS, EMR, RDS, Redshift, and CloudSearch, implemented the one-second backward jump and saw 23:59:59 twice. During the 24-hour smear window AWS infrastructure time could differ from external UTC by up to ~0.5s, so cross-checking against non-smeared sources shows drift.

validation.supportingQuote: Starting at 12:00:00 PM on June 30th, 2015, we will slow down AWS clocks by 1/86400. Every second on AWS clocks will take 1+1/86400 seconds of 'real' time, until 12:00:00 PM on July 1st, 2015 ... You will not see a ':60' second in these places.

headline: Amazon stretched a day to absorb a leap second
blurb: Amazon spread one extra second across a full day so its clocks never had to show an unusual sixty-first second.
story: In 2015 the world's clocks were due to add one extra second. Instead of adding it all at once, Amazon made each second of a 24-hour window very slightly longer. That let its clocks take in the extra second gradually and stay smooth, never showing an odd time. During that window Amazon's time could differ from official time by up to about half a second.

title: Uruguay permanently abolishes DST after restaurant industry complaint
body: Government of Uruguay (Consejo de Ministros)
whatChanged: Uruguay abolished DST via Decreto 178/015 of 2015-06-29, repealing Decreto 311/006 (2006), which had set the annual DST schedule first introduced by Decreto 328/004 in 2004. No clock change occurred from October 2015 onward, and Uruguay has stayed permanently at UTC-3 since. The repeal landed about three months before the next scheduled spring-forward on 2015-10-04.
technicalImpact: Software and tz databases with stale rules expecting Uruguay to enter DST each October from 2015 onward would compute times one hour off all summer. tzdata releases from mid-2015 onward (2015e and later) carry the fix.
headline: The beach that killed the clock change
blurb: Uruguay scrapped daylight saving time in 2015 partly because restaurateurs complained it sent diners to the beach instead of their tables.
story: Each southern-hemisphere summer, Uruguay pushed its clocks forward an hour, putting sunset later in the evening. Tourism and restaurant groups argued the extra evening light drew people outdoors rather than into their dining rooms, hurting trade. On 29 June 2015 the government signed Decreto 178/015 and simply stopped changing the clocks, about three months before the next switch was due. Uruguay has been on permanent UTC-3 ever since. Systems that still expected the annual October change compute Uruguayan times an hour wrong every summer.

title: Azure and Windows Time Service mishandled the 2015 leap second
body: Microsoft (Azure platform and Windows Time Service)
whatChanged: On 30 June 2015, a leap second was inserted at 23:59:60 UTC. Google and AWS used clock smearing to absorb it gradually (AWS over a 24-hour window, 12 hours either side of the leap; Google over roughly 20 hours). Microsoft's Windows Time Service applied neither a smear nor the leap at the leap moment, so Azure VMs and Windows clients ran approximately 1 second fast after the leap and silently reconciled the offset at the next scheduled synchronization rather than at the leap instant.
technicalImpact: Cross-cloud distributed systems saw up to 1 second of inter-provider clock skew around the leap: Google and AWS smeared while Azure stepped and resynced on its own schedule. This divergence created ordering hazards for distributed logs, timestamp comparisons, and TLS certificate validity windows in mixed-provider deployments.
headline: The cloud that missed its second
blurb: When a leap second was added in 2015, Azure and Windows clocks ran roughly one second out of sync with other providers until their next sync.
story: Every few years, timekeepers add a leap second to keep clocks aligned with Earth's rotation. In June 2015, Google and Amazon gradually blended the extra second into their clocks over many hours (AWS across a full day, Google across most of one). Microsoft's Azure platform and Windows Time Service did neither: their clocks ran one second ahead after the leap and quietly corrected themselves only at the next scheduled sync. For systems that spanned multiple cloud providers, this meant Azure machines and Google or AWS machines briefly disagreed about what time it was by about one second. A one-second gap sounds trivial, but it is enough to reorder log entries, break distributed locking, or fall outside a certificate validity window in tightly timed systems.

title: Leap second #26 (2015-06-30): GNSS receivers apply it early, causing -1s offsets
body: IERS (Bulletin C 49); GNSS receiver vendors (faulty firmware)
whatChanged: A positive leap second was inserted at 2015-06-30 23:59:60 UTC (TAI-UTC 35s -> 36s), first since 2012; encoded in tzdb 2015a. Several GNSS receiver models (TrueTime XL-AK, unpatched Trimble Lassen SQ/iQ) applied the announced leap second as soon as it appeared in the GPS almanac rather than at the scheduled epoch, retarding their time by one second early.
technicalImpact: Faulty receivers fed a -1s offset into NTP servers, radios, computer clocks and seismic/data-logging equipment (USGS NCSN observed it in Jan 2015; US Coast Guard NAVCEN issued an alert). Downstream NTP clients inherited the wrong time. Reinforced interest in leap smearing (already used by Google since 2008 and AWS) and post-2012 kernel hardening validation.

validation.supportingQuote: 3644697600	36	# 1 Jul 2015, preceded by the 2012 entry at DTAI 35, confirming TAI-UTC 35s -> 36s. File header: "Updated through IERS Bulletin C (https://hpiers.obspm.fr/iers/bul/bulc/bulletinc.dat)".

headline: A leap second tripped up some GPS receivers
blurb: An extra second was added to world time in 2015, and some GPS receivers applied it too early and ran a second slow.
story: In mid 2015 one extra second was added to official world time, the first such addition in three years. Some GPS receiver models applied the change before the scheduled moment and ended up a second behind. Those faulty receivers passed the wrong time to other equipment that relied on them. The trouble pushed more companies to smooth the change over time instead of adding the second all at once.

title: The 2015 leap second trips up Twitter, Instagram, Pinterest, and Netflix
body: IERS (leap second authority); reported outages at Twitter, Instagram, Pinterest, Netflix, Amazon, and Apple's Beats 1
whatChanged: Leap second number 26 was inserted at 2015-06-30 23:59:60 UTC, moving TAI-UTC from 35 to 36 seconds. The extra second triggered hrtimer and futex misbehavior of the kind seen in the 2012 leap second, the class of bug that had pegged CPU cores in Java and Cassandra stacks. In 2015, Twitter, Instagram, Pinterest, Netflix, Amazon, and Apple's Beats 1 music service reported errors or brief outages during the same window.
technicalImpact: Exposed that the kernel and JVM leap-second hazards (hrtimer livelock, futex spin) first seen in 2012 were not fully mitigated at the application layer by 2015. Any service relying on a Linux kernel older than the 2012 HRTIMER patch, or a JVM without leap-smearing NTP, was at risk. The incident reinforced adoption of leap smearing (used by Google and Amazon) as the practical mitigation for distributed stacks.
headline: The second that tripped the web
blurb: On June 30, 2015 the world clock gained an extra leap second. Despite fears of a repeat of 2012's server crashes, the insertion itself passed largely without incident; a coincident AWS routing fault, not the leap second, briefly knocked out Netflix, Instagram, and Pinterest.
story: Every few years, timekeepers add a single extra second to the world clock to keep atomic time in sync with the earth's rotation. On June 30, 2015, that second arrived at 23:59:60 UTC. Several large services stumbled in the same window: Twitter, Instagram, Pinterest, Netflix, Amazon, and Apple's new Beats 1 music service all reported errors or brief downtime. The trouble traced back to the same kind of bug that had hit servers during the 2012 leap second, where older Linux kernels and Java stacks would spin in a tight loop and peg their CPUs. The 2015 event showed that fixes from 2012 had not reached every production stack, and pushed the industry toward leap smearing, a technique where the extra second is spread invisibly across many hours so no single timestamp ever reads :60.

title: North Korea adopts Pyongyang Time (UTC+8:30), splitting from the Korean peninsula
body: Government of the DPRK; encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: On 7 Aug 2015 the DPRK announced that on 15 Aug 2015 (70th anniversary of liberation) it would move standard time back 30 minutes from UTC+9 to UTC+8:30, naming it 'Pyongyang Time' (PYT, keeping the abbreviation 'KST'), framed as erasing a legacy of Japanese imperialism. First 30 minutes behind South Korea/Japan since 1912.
technicalImpact: Created a new half-hour offset for Asia/Pyongyang diverging from Asia/Seoul. Systems not patched to tz 2015f computed North Korean time half an hour wrong; with only 10 days notice, devices showed +9 until updated. Reusing 'KST' for +8:30 confused parsers expecting KST=+9.

validation.supportingQuote: North Korea switches to +0830 on 2015-08-15. (Thanks to Steffen Thorsen.)

headline: North Korea set its own clock half an hour back
blurb: North Korea moved its clocks back thirty minutes, putting the country half an hour behind its neighbors.
story: In 2015 North Korea announced that it would move its clocks back by thirty minutes, taking effect on an anniversary date about ten days later. It called the result Pyongyang Time. This left the country half an hour behind South Korea and Japan. Systems that had not been updated kept showing the old time until they caught up.

title: North Korea creates Pyongyang Time at UTC+8:30, then abandons it three years later
body: Government of North Korea (Democratic People's Republic of Korea)
whatChanged: North Korea moved from UTC+9 to UTC+8:30 (Pyongyang Time) at midnight on 2015-08-15, citing a symbolic break from Japanese colonial-era time. On 2018-05-05, just after an inter-Korean summit, it switched back to UTC+9 to align with South Korea. The reverse transition occurred at 23:30 local time on 2018-05-04 rather than at 00:00 on 2018-05-05.
technicalImpact: Apps and libraries that hardcoded UTC+9 for Pyongyang were silently wrong for nearly three years. The non-midnight transition time (23:30 rather than 00:00) is an additional edge case that catches naive implementations. tzdb releases 2015f and 2018e carried the forward and reverse changes; a later release corrected the exact transition instant.
headline: Pyongyang's short-lived clock
blurb: North Korea invented its own half-hour timezone in 2015 as a political gesture, then quietly scrapped it three years later to sync with the South.
story: On the 70th anniversary of Korea's liberation from Japan, North Korea moved its clocks back 30 minutes, calling the new time Pyongyang Time and framing it as a rejection of Japanese colonial influence. The half-hour offset put the country out of step with both South Korea and China. Three years later, around a historic inter-Korean summit, North Korea moved its clocks forward again to match Seoul, erasing the offset it had created. The switch back happened at 23:30, not midnight, a detail that broke software relying on a clean day boundary for the transition.

title: Turkey postpones the 2015 DST end, but auto-updating clocks fell back anyway
body: Republic of Turkey (government decree for the Nov 1 general election), encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: To keep polling stations in daylight for the 2015-11-01 election, Turkey moved its DST-end from the normal last Sunday of October (2015-10-25) to 2015-11-08, extending the summer offset (+03) by two weeks.
technicalImpact: tzdata 2015g (2015-10-01) carried the fix, but devices that hadn't updated fell back at 04:00 local (01:00 UTC) on 2015-10-25, dropping to 03:00 regardless, so phones, PCs, and servers across Turkey disagreed by an hour for two weeks. The hashtag #saatkac trended; a canonical example of why late, politically-driven tz changes break automatic clocks.

validation.supportingQuote: Due to general elections, Turkey delayed the end of DST 2015 by two weeks. Clocks were turned back one hour from 4:00 to 3:00 local time on Sunday, November 8, 2015... The change of the DST date was due to Turkey's general election on November 1, and the delay ensured daylight during the opening hours of the polling stations.
validation.linkNote: valid page, blocks automated fetch (HTTP 403 to bots)

headline: Turkey delayed a clock change but some clocks switched anyway
blurb: Turkey put off the end of daylight saving by two weeks, yet many devices fell back on the old date and showed the wrong time.
story: In 2015 Turkey delayed the end of daylight saving by two weeks so polling places would stay in daylight for an election. Updated systems handled the new date, but many phones, computers, and servers that had not been updated changed their clocks on the original date anyway. For about two weeks clocks across Turkey disagreed by an hour. It became a well known example of how late changes confuse automatic clocks.

title: Turkey delays DST end by two weeks for 2015 general election
body: Turkish Council of Ministers (cabinet decree, on notice from the Ministry of Energy)
whatChanged: Turkey's 2015 DST end was moved from the EU-standard date of 2015-10-25 to 2015-11-08 04:00 local time, keeping Turkey on EEST (UTC+3) for an extra two weeks. The change was made to extend daylight hours during the November 1 snap general election.
technicalImpact: Devices running auto-updating OSes (phones, laptops) fell back on 2015-10-25 following the EU rule, while devices on the official Turkish rule stayed on EEST. For two weeks there was no single correct local time across Turkish devices. tzdb release 2015g (2015-10-01) patched the Europe/Istanbul zone to reflect the Nov 8 transition.
headline: Turkey's two-week clock split
blurb: Turkey postponed its 2015 clock change by two weeks for an election, leaving phones and computers disagreeing with official time.
story: Ahead of a snap general election on November 1, Turkey's government pushed its autumn clock change from the usual EU date of October 25 to November 8. Automatic devices that sync with global time rules fell back on October 25 as normal, while official Turkish time stayed one hour ahead. For two weeks, a phone next to a wall clock could show a different hour depending on whose rule it followed. The episode is a clean example of how late government decisions, announced with little notice, can outpace software update cycles.

title: ITU WRC-15 defers leap-second decision, retains leap seconds
body: ITU-R, World Radiocommunication Conference 2015 (WRC-15), Geneva
whatChanged: After years of debate over suppressing the leap second and adopting a continuous reference time scale, WRC-15 adopted Resolution 655, declining to make the change: it retained leap seconds under Recommendation ITU-R TF.460-6 and deferred further study to WRC-23. This impasse pushed the question to the BIPM/CGPM track.
technicalImpact: Prolonged a decade of uncertainty for timekeeping vendors, GNSS, and OS/NTP maintainers, who kept supporting leap-second insertion indefinitely, leaving the 2012/2017-class bugs live and pushing vendors toward unilateral smearing.

validation.supportingQuote: "UTC shall continue to be applied as described in Recommendation ITU-R TF.460-6 and as maintained by the International Bureau of Weights and Measures (BIPM)." The conference deferred a decision on suppressing the leap second, calling for further studies before a 2023 conference.

headline: A 2015 meeting kept leap seconds and put off deciding
blurb: An international conference chose not to decide on ending the leap second, so the practice continued.
story: For years officials had debated whether to stop adding leap seconds to world time. At a 2015 conference they declined to make a decision and kept the leap second in place, pushing the question to later study. This left timekeeping vendors and software maintainers facing continued uncertainty. They had to keep supporting leap seconds, and some companies smoothed the changes on their own.

title: iOS 'January 1, 1970' epoch underflow bricking bug
body: Apple (64-bit iOS devices, iOS 8/9; fixed in iOS 9.3)
whatChanged: Setting a 64-bit iOS device's clock to 1970-01-01 set Unix time to 0; subsequent attempts to render an earlier timestamp is widely believed to have produced an underflowed time value that crashed time-zone/locale handling during boot, though Apple never officially disclosed the root cause.
technicalImpact: Affected iPhones/iPads/iPods entered a permanent reboot loop (effectively bricked) and could not be recovered normally; the date could also be pushed remotely via malicious NTP over open Wi-Fi. Apple mitigated the manual-set bug in iOS 9.3 by preventing the date from being set early enough to trigger it; a related remote variant (malicious NTP over open Wi-Fi, demonstrated by researchers in April 2016) was patched separately in iOS 9.3.1.

validation.supportingQuote: Manually changing the date to May 1970 or earlier can prevent your iOS device from turning on after a restart. An upcoming software update will prevent this issue from affecting iOS devices.

headline: Setting an iPhone to 1970 could break it
blurb: Setting a 64-bit Apple device's date to January 1, 1970 could send it into an endless restart loop.
story: On 64-bit iPhones and iPads, setting the date to the very start of 1970 confused the software during startup. The device could fall into a permanent restart loop and become very hard to recover. The bad date could even be pushed to a device over an open Wi-Fi network. Apple fixed it in a later software update by rejecting these out-of-range dates.

title: GPS receiver firmware rollover bugs: staggered early failures (2016 onward)
body: Various GPS receiver vendors (firmware)
whatChanged: The GPS week number is a 10-bit field that rolls over every 1024 weeks (about 19.7 years), at the well-known 1999 and 2019 dates. But some receivers fail BEFORE those dates because their firmware anchors the week count to a hardcoded build or manufacture date, shifting the rollover threshold and causing staggered early failures on otherwise arbitrary weekends. A noted wave hit around February 2016, with more expected later that year.
technicalImpact: Affected units report a wrong date, and therefore wrong UTC, since GPS time converts to UTC using the week number, breaking time-stamped logs and any system trusting GPS for wall-clock time. Because each vendor's anchor date differs, the failures do not line up with the standard 1999 and 2019 rollovers.

reverify.note: newly added this pass; vendor-specific, editorial prose new

headline: Every GPS receiver with its own doomsday
blurb: Some GPS receivers roll over on a private 9.8-year cycle, failing on dates the public rollover never warned about.
story: GPS time is famous for resetting its week counter every so often. Some receivers used their own private counter that reset on a different, vendor-specific schedule. Those units showed the wrong date and time without warning. Because each maker's reset date differed, the failures were scattered and hard to predict.
reviewNote: Corrected: there is no 512-week GPS cycle; the standard GPS rollover is 1024 weeks (~19.7 years). Early failures come from build-date-anchored firmware, not a shorter hardware cycle.

title: RFC 7808: Time Zone Data Distribution Service (TZDIST)
body: IETF TZDIST working group (originating in CalConnect); authors M. Douglass and C. Daboo
whatChanged: Published March 2016, RFC 7808 defined an HTTP/JSON service for reliable, secure, fast delivery of time-zone data and leap-second rules to clients, with conditional/incremental requests so clients sync only changed zones. Companion RFC 7809 let CalDAV clients reference time zones by ID.
technicalImpact: Let calendaring/scheduling apps and OSes pull tz updates over the network independently of OS/vendor release cycles, closing the gap where a client couldn't get a fresh DST rule until its platform shipped a tzdata package. Reduced wrong-time bugs from stale embedded VTIMEZONE definitions.

validation.supportingQuote: Title: "Time Zone Data Distribution Service"; Publication Date: March 2016; Authors: M. Douglass and C. Daboo. The service uses HTTP with a JSON-based API to enable efficient delivery of time zone updates.

headline: A new standard for sending time-zone updates online
blurb: A 2016 standard let apps and systems download fresh time-zone rules over the internet on their own.
story: In 2016 a new technical standard described a way to deliver time-zone and leap-second rules to devices over the internet. Apps could request only the parts that had changed, keeping updates small. This let calendar apps and operating systems get fresh rules without waiting for a full software release. It helped reduce wrong-time problems caused by outdated time-zone data.

title: Six Russian regions shift UTC offsets on 2016-03-27
body: Russian Federation (Federal Laws signed by President Putin, February 2016)
whatChanged: At 02:00 local time on 2016-03-27, six Russian federal subjects changed their UTC offsets: Astrakhan Oblast moved from UTC+3 to UTC+4 (Europe/Astrakhan, new zone); Ulyanovsk Oblast moved from UTC+3 to UTC+4 (Europe/Ulyanovsk, new zone); Altai Krai and Altai Republic moved from UTC+6 to UTC+7 (Asia/Barnaul, new zone); Sakhalin Oblast moved from UTC+10 to UTC+11 (Asia/Sakhalin). Zabaykalsky Krai (Asia/Chita) also moved UTC+8 to UTC+9 the same day under a separate law signed December 2015. Magadan Oblast followed on 2016-04-24 (UTC+10 to UTC+11).
technicalImpact: IANA tzdb 2016b (released 2016-03-12) created three new zones (Europe/Astrakhan, Europe/Ulyanovsk, Asia/Barnaul) and updated Asia/Sakhalin; Asia/Chita's same-day change had already shipped in 2016a (2016-01-26). Systems that had not yet applied 2016b displayed wrong times for millions of users in these regions from 2016-03-27 onward. Russia's frequent offset churn since 2014 means any hardcoded or stale-cached Russian zone data is a recurring source of bugs.
headline: Russia reshuffles six clocks at once
blurb: Six Russian regions changed their UTC offsets simultaneously in March 2016, requiring three brand-new IANA time zones to be created.
story: Russia has repeatedly reshuffled its time zones since abolishing daylight saving in 2014, and March 2016 brought another batch: six regions moved their clocks in one night. Three of the zones were so new that the IANA database had to create entirely fresh entries for them just under two weeks before the change took effect. Software that had not updated to tzdb 2016b showed the wrong time for residents of Astrakhan, Ulyanovsk, Altai, and Sakhalin from that Sunday onward. It is a sharp reminder that Russian time zone data has been a moving target and that stale tz databases carry real risk.

title: Venezuela reverts the half-hour offset, UTC-4:30 back to UTC-4:00
body: Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela (President Nicolas Maduro), encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: At 02:30 on 2016-05-01 Venezuela advanced clocks 30 minutes, returning America/Caracas from UTC-4:30 to UTC-4:00 to save electricity during an El Nino hydropower crisis, reversing the 2007 change.
technicalImpact: Announced with roughly two weeks' notice (announced 2016-04-16, effective 2016-05-01), forcing out-of-cycle tzdata release 2016d (2016-04-17) and vendor hotfixes. Systems still on -4:30 ran 30 minutes ahead; embedded devices, POS terminals, and un-patched servers stayed wrong for weeks. Stored future timestamps cached under -4:30 became wrong, a classic 'tz update invalidates persisted local times' hazard.

validation.supportingQuote: Venezuela's government announced they would advance their clocks forward by 30 minutes on May 1st, 2016 at 02:30 AM... changed Venezuela's standard time zone offset from UTC -04:30 to UTC -04:00. President Nicolas Maduro announced the half-hour change... to reduce electricity demand in the early evenings.

headline: Venezuela moved its clocks forward half an hour
blurb: Venezuela shifted its clocks ahead by thirty minutes to save power, undoing an earlier change.
story: In 2016 Venezuela moved its clocks forward by thirty minutes to help save electricity during a power shortage. This undid a change it had made years earlier. The announcement came with only about two weeks of notice, so software had to be patched quickly. Devices that were not updated stayed half an hour off for weeks, and some saved future appointments ended up wrong.

title: Egypt cancels reinstated DST three days before it was to begin
body: Government of Egypt (Cabinet/Parliament); encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: Egypt announced in April 2016 it would reintroduce DST starting 7 Jul 2016 (after Ramadan). On 4 Jul 2016, three days before the switch, the Cabinet reversed course and cancelled DST entirely, keeping Africa/Cairo on UTC+2.
technicalImpact: A textbook last-minute timezone failure. IANA had shipped tz 2016e with the 7 Jul start and Microsoft a June update, so within 96 hours billions of devices couldn't be re-patched. iPhones/Samsung phones auto-advanced on 7-8 Jul, Google Calendar/Search showed wrong times, and Emirates flight 926 departed Cairo an hour early. IANA published 2016f on 5 Jul; Windows fix KB3177723 only arrived 18 Aug 2016.

validation.supportingQuote: Release 2016f - 2016-07-05 ... The Egyptian government changed its mind on short notice, and Africa/Cairo will not introduce DST starting 2016-07-07 after all.

headline: Egypt canceled a clock change three days before it
blurb: Egypt called off a planned return to daylight saving just three days before it was due to start.
story: In 2016 Egypt said it would bring back daylight saving in early July. Three days before the switch, the government reversed the plan and canceled it. By then the change had already been built into software worldwide, and there was no time to undo it everywhere. Many phones advanced an hour anyway, some calendars showed wrong times, and one flight even left Cairo an hour early.

title: Turkey cancels the autumn 2016 fall-back and adopts permanent UTC+3
body: Republic of Turkey (Council of Ministers decree, Resmi Gazete 2016-09-08), encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: Turkey announced it would skip the scheduled 2016-10-30 fall-back and stay permanently on the summer offset, switching Europe/Istanbul from EET/EEST (+02/+03) to a permanent +03 effective 2016-09-07. Turkey Time became year-round UTC+3 with no further DST.
technicalImpact: Weeks before the planned transition, forcing an out-of-cycle tzdata release (2016g) and emergency vendor patches (Windows hotfix, Joda-Time issue #403 fixed in 2.9.5 with tzdata2016i, Debian #838781). Unpatched systems performed the cancelled fall-back, leaving Turkey an hour behind; mixed-version fleets showed two different Turkish times simultaneously.

validation.supportingQuote: Turkey switched from EET/EEST (+02/+03) to permanent +03, effective 2016-09-07.

headline: Turkey kept summer time all year
blurb: Turkey skipped its autumn clock change and stayed on summer time permanently.
story: In 2016 Turkey announced it would not turn its clocks back that autumn and would keep summer time all year. The change took effect weeks before the planned switch. Software makers had to release emergency updates to keep up. Systems that were not updated still turned their clocks back, leaving some devices an hour behind and showing two different Turkish times at once.

title: Northern Cyprus breaks from the rest of Cyprus with a year-round +03 offset
body: Government of Northern Cyprus (following Turkey's lead)
whatChanged: On 2016-10-30, Northern Cyprus stopped observing winter time and stayed at UTC+03 year-round, matching Turkey. The rest of Cyprus remained on UTC+02/+03 (EET/EEST). The split lasted about one year. On 2017-10-29, Northern Cyprus reinstated winter time and rejoined the rest of the island on EU rules. tzdb added Asia/Famagusta in release 2016i to capture the split, and updated it in 2017c when the split ended.
technicalImpact: Software had to distinguish Asia/Famagusta (UTC+03 year-round during the split) from Asia/Nicosia (UTC+02/+03 with DST). Any app serving both sides of the island needed separate zone handling. The split is a textbook case of a city-level tzdb zone created for a temporary political arrangement.
headline: The island that had two time zones for one year
blurb: Northern Cyprus broke from the rest of the island for about a year by staying on summer time year-round, forcing tzdb to create a brand-new city zone.
story: Turkey abolished daylight saving in 2016 and Northern Cyprus followed. That meant the north stayed at UTC+03 through winter while the south fell back to UTC+02, splitting one small island into two time zones. The split lasted about a year, ending when Northern Cyprus reinstated winter time in October 2017. For software, even a one-year split is enough to require a permanent zone entry in tzdb, and Asia/Famagusta now stands as a record of the episode.

title: Google standardizes the public 24-hour noon-to-noon linear leap smear (2016)
body: Google (Public NTP); subsequently adopted by AWS and others as a de-facto standard
whatChanged: Google launched the public time.google.com NTP service ahead of the 2016-12-31 leap second, smearing the extra second linearly over 20 hours (10 hours before and 10 hours after the event) so no 23:59:60 ever appears on client clocks. Google later (from leap second #38, ~2018) switched to a 24-hour noon-to-noon linear smear as its recommended standard, since adopted by AWS and others. Google previously used 20-hour linear smears in 2012/2015 and a 20-hour cosine smear in 2008.
technicalImpact: Clocks deviate from UTC during the smear but never step or repeat a second, sidestepping the 2012/2017-class bugs. For the 24-hour noon-to-noon standard the max deviation is ~0.5s and the frequency change ~11.6 ppm, within quartz tolerance and well under NTP's 500 ppm slew cap. The shared, reproducible definition lets multiple providers (Google, AWS, Meta) smear in a compatible window, though implementation shapes differ (Meta uses a quadratic, not linear, smear).

validation.supportingQuote: Google's official time service page documents a "24-hour linear smear from noon to noon UTC" as the recommended standard, notes it "previously used a 20-hour smear duration" (and a 2008 "20-hour cosine smear"), cites the ~11.6 ppm frequency change, and confirms AWS adopted the same noon-to-noon approach.
validation.corrections: date: change "2017-08-01" to "2016-11-30" (public launch of Google Public NTP, time.google.com) or, if dating the 24-hour standard specifically, to "2016-12" (proposed on public-ntp-discuss, Dec 2016) with first intended use at leap second #38 (~2018). The body's claim that "the documented standard launched 2017" is unsupported and should be removed/corrected; nothing leap-related launched in 2017 (no 2017 leap second). Everything else (body, responsible party, technical impact) is accurate.

headline: Google set a standard way to absorb leap seconds
blurb: Google defined a method that spreads a leap second smoothly over a full day so clocks never show an odd extra second.
story: Google created a clear, recommended way to handle the extra leap second added to world time. The method spreads the second evenly across a full day, centered on the event, so clocks never display an unusual time. Clocks drift up to about half a second during this window but never jump or repeat. Because the method is precise and shared, different companies can use it and stay in step with each other.

title: Leap second #27 (most recent, 2016-12-31): Cloudflare RRDNS Go service panics on negative time delta
body: IERS (Bulletin C 52) inserted the leap second; bug in Cloudflare's Go-based RRDNS
whatChanged: A positive leap second was inserted at 2016-12-31 23:59:60 UTC (TAI-UTC 36s -> 37s; encoded in tzdb 2016g). The 27th and most recent leap second. In Cloudflare's RRDNS, an elapsed-time value computed from two time.Now() readings went negative across the leap second; that value was passed to rand.Int63n(), which panics on a negative argument.
technicalImpact: RRDNS instances panicked servicing DNS for CNAME records, returning SERVFAIL: ~0.2% of DNS queries and <1% of HTTP requests errored across Cloudflare's PoPs. The most affected machines were patched in ~90 minutes; full impact ended ~06:45 UTC. Fix was a one-character change that treats a negative elapsed time the same as zero so it never reaches server selection. A classic monotonic-vs-wall-clock confusion (Go's time.Now() was not monotonic before Go 1.9). DTAI has remained 37s ever since.

validation.supportingQuote: "rand.Int63n promptly panics if its argument is negative. That's where the RRDNS panics were coming from." ... "The problem only affected customers who use CNAME DNS records with Cloudflare" ... "at peak approximately 0.2% of DNS queries to Cloudflare were affected."

headline: An extra second crashed Cloudflare
blurb: A leap second at the end of 2016 made part of Cloudflare's system fail.
story: On the last day of 2016, an extra second was added to the world clock. A piece of Cloudflare's software measured time across that moment and got a negative result, which it could not handle, so it crashed. A small share of web requests failed; the most affected machines were patched within about 90 minutes, with full recovery around 06:45 UTC (roughly 6 hours 45 minutes after the impact began).

title: Mongolia abolishes DST after two-year revival (2015-2017)
body: Cabinet of Mongolia
whatChanged: Mongolia reinstated DST by cabinet resolution No. 82 (9 March 2015), applying spring-forward/fall-back in 2015 and 2016 for Asia/Ulaanbaatar (UTC+8) and Asia/Hovd (UTC+7). On 8 February 2017, the cabinet nullified that resolution and ended DST permanently, citing negative effects on public health, lifestyle, and disruption to international flight and train schedules.
technicalImpact: Both Asia/Ulaanbaatar and Asia/Hovd required multiple tzdata updates within a short window: once to add DST rules in 2015 and again to remove them in 2017 (tzdata2017a, released 28 Feb 2017).
