The rapid on-off cycle is Mongolia's third failed DST experiment since 1983 and is a reminder that DST rules can flip on short political notice, breaking cached tz data in long-running applications.
headline: Mongolia's third DST experiment ends
blurb: Mongolia reintroduced daylight saving time in 2015, ran it for two years, then scrapped it again in early 2017 after complaints about health and travel disruption.
story: Mongolia switched its clocks forward each spring and back each autumn starting in 2015, the third time it had tried daylight saving since 1983. The experiment lasted just two years. In February 2017, the cabinet voted to drop it again, pointing to sleep disruption, public health concerns, and knock-on problems with international flight and train timetables. Both Ulaanbaatar and the western city of Hovd returned to fixed standard time. For software, the episode meant two rounds of tzdata updates in under three years for the same zones, and it shows how quickly a government can reverse a time policy with almost no lead time.

title: Mongolia cancels daylight saving time (second abolition)
body: Government of Mongolia
whatChanged: On 2017-02-08 the Mongolian government decided to cancel DST, which had been reintroduced in 2015 after a prior abolition in 1998. Asia/Ulaanbaatar (UTC+8) and Asia/Hovd (UTC+7) revert to year-round fixed offsets. Encoded in tzdata 2017a (released 2017-02-28).
technicalImpact: Systems using tzdata prior to 2017a miscalculated Ulaanbaatar and Hovd times from March 2017 onward. Any cached DST transition tables for Asia/Ulaanbaatar or Asia/Hovd needed updating.
headline: Mongolia's on-again, off-again daylight saving
blurb: Mongolia tried daylight saving time again in 2015, then scrapped it for good two years later.
story: Mongolia first dropped DST in 1998, then revived it in 2015 after a government push. By February 2017 the government changed course again and decided to cancel it entirely. Ulaanbaatar and Hovd went back to fixed UTC+8 and UTC+7 offsets year-round. Software that skipped the 2017a tz update kept applying phantom clock shifts to Mongolian times every spring.

title: Haiti resumes daylight saving time after 2016 gap
body: Government of Haiti (Secretary General of the Presidency)
whatChanged: Haiti reinstated DST in 2017 after skipping it entirely in 2016. Clocks moved forward one hour on 2017-03-12 (second Sunday in March, aligning with US/Canada rules). tzdata 2017b encoded the resumed schedule.
technicalImpact: Any system relying on the 2016 Haiti offset for future dates would have been wrong. tzdata 2017b (released 2017-03-17) encoded the change; apps using older tzdata continued to show Haiti as UTC-5 year-round through the affected period.
headline: Haiti's on-again, off-again clocks
blurb: Haiti skipped daylight saving entirely in 2016, then brought it back in 2017.
story: Haiti has a long history of inconsistent DST observance, toggling the practice on and off across different years. After following US and Canadian DST rules from 2012 through 2015, the government quietly dropped it for 2016 with little official notice. In March 2017 it came back, and tzdata 2017b re-encoded the schedule. Software that cached Haiti's offset without a current tzdata update would have shown the wrong time for months.

title: Chile's Magallanes region stops observing DST, going permanent UTC-3 (new zone created)
body: Government of Chile (President Michelle Bachelet); encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: Chile cancelled the scheduled May 2017 fall-back for its southernmost Magallanes (and Chilean Antarctic) region, keeping it on permanent UTC-3 while the rest of the country continued switching between -04 and -03. The change was set by executive decree (Decreto 1820 of 2016), published in the Diario Oficial on 17 Jan 2017; IANA modeled it as effective 4 Dec 2016, with Magallanes clocks diverging from the rest of Chile from 23:00 on 13 May 2017. The Chilean government initially said the change would expire in May 2019, but it was kept in place permanently.
technicalImpact: Forced the IANA tz database to split Chile into a new zone, America/Punta_Arenas, separate from America/Santiago. Systems treating all mainland Chile as one zone computed Magallanes time wrong by an hour for part of the year. Chile is a recurring tz headache, repeatedly changing DST dates on short notice.

validation.supportingQuote: Magallanes is currently observing DST, and the transition back to standard time, scheduled for 14 May, 2017, has been cancelled. ... Magallanes is 3 hours behind UTC.
validation.corrections: tzRelease should be "2017a" (released 2017-02-28), not "2016k". Quote from IANA tz-announce 2017a: "Chile's Region of Magallanes moves from -04/-03 to -03 year-round. Its clocks diverge from America/Santiago starting 2017-05-13 at 23:00, hiving off a new zone America/Punta_Arenas."

headline: Chile's far south stopped changing clocks
blurb: Chile's southernmost region stopped switching its clocks for summer and winter.
story: In 2017 Chile decided that its far southern Magallanes region would stop changing its clocks twice a year. The rest of the country kept switching, so the two areas no longer matched for part of the year. To track this, the main time database created a separate zone for the region.
reviewNote: Original journalistic source (timeanddate summer_time_all_year_in_magallanes) is dead; replaced with the IANA tzdb southamerica zone file, which documents the America/Punta_Arenas split. Underlying facts corrected per Decreto 1820/2016 (published 17 Jan 2017).

title: Namibia abolishes DST and fixes clocks at UTC+2 year-round
body: Government of Namibia (Namibian Time Bill, National Council)
whatChanged: Namibia ended its practice of observing negative DST. Previously, clocks fell back from +02 (summer) to +01 (winter), making DST a subtraction rather than the usual addition. On 2017-09-03 at 02:00, clocks stayed at +02 permanently, eliminating the seasonal clock change. Encoded in tzdata2017c (released 2017-10-23): Africa/Windhoek moved from +01/+02 to year-round +02 (Central African Time).
technicalImpact: Removes one of the rare negative-DST zones where tm_isdst=1 coincided with a smaller UTC offset. Software that assumed DST always means add an hour broke for Namibia during winter. After tzdata2017c, Africa/Windhoek is fixed at +02 with no DST transitions from 2018-04-01 onward.
headline: The zone where winter DST meant losing an hour
blurb: Namibia was one of the only countries where daylight saving time ran backwards, subtracting an hour in winter rather than adding one.
story: Most countries spring forward in summer, but Namibia did the opposite: clocks fell back from +02 to +01 in the southern-hemisphere winter, so DST meant you lost an hour. In 2017, parliament passed the Namibian Time Bill to end the confusion and stay on +02 all year. Clocks stopped changing on 3 September 2017. Software that assumed DST always adds an hour had quietly mishandled Namibian winter time for years, and tzdata2017c finally closed that edge case.

title: Northern Cyprus reverses permanent +03 and rejoins DST as Asia/Famagusta
body: Government of Northern Cyprus
whatChanged: On 2017-10-29 at 01:00 UTC, Northern Cyprus abandoned its 2016 permanent UTC+3 and resumed EU DST rules (UTC+2 in winter, UTC+3 in summer). The Asia/Famagusta zone, created in tzdata2016i to split from Asia/Nicosia, was updated in tzdata2017c to reflect the reversal.
technicalImpact: Systems that hardcoded UTC+3 for Asia/Famagusta after the 2016 change needed another update in tzdata2017c. Any app that skipped the 2017c release would show Northern Cyprus clocks one hour ahead in winter.
headline: Northern Cyprus changes its mind on time
blurb: A year after going permanently to UTC+3, Northern Cyprus reversed course and brought back seasonal clock changes.
story: In 2016 Northern Cyprus broke from the rest of Cyprus and stopped changing its clocks, sitting permanently at UTC+3. Just over a year later the government reversed the decision and rejoined EU DST rules starting 29 October 2017. The about-face forced a second update to the Asia/Famagusta zone within 13 months. Software that picked up tzdata2016i but skipped 2017c would show Northern Cyprus time an hour fast every winter.

title: Tonga abandons DST, settles permanently at UTC+13
body: Government of Tonga (Kingdom of Tonga)
whatChanged: Tonga discontinued daylight saving time effective 2017-11-05, ending a brief one-season revival of DST (November 2016 to January 2017) that had pushed clocks to UTC+14. The country reverted to permanent UTC+13. Encoded in tzdata2017c as a likely (unconfirmed) change based on IATA data.
technicalImpact: Systems using the Pacific/Tongatapu zone needed updating to tzdata2017c or later. Any software or calendar data that scheduled events during the projected 2017-11-05 DST start would have been wrong by one hour. The 'likely' flag in the release notes meant some downstream databases carried provisional data until confirmation.
headline: Tonga's one-season DST experiment
blurb: Tonga tried daylight saving time for one season in 2016-2017, then quietly dropped it and never went back.
story: Tonga had abandoned DST back in 2002 after a short experiment it began in 1999, when it pushed clocks to UTC+14 specifically to be among the first nations to enter the year 2000. In late 2016 the government revived DST, pushing clocks one hour forward to UTC+14 from November through January. After that single season ended, no renewal came, and the country settled permanently at UTC+13. Tz database maintainers flagged the change as 'likely' based on IATA signals because no official government announcement was found, which is exactly the kind of ambiguity that causes scheduling software to carry wrong data for months.

title: Ethiopia's traditional solar timekeeping cannot be represented in tzdb
body: IANA tz database maintainers (Paul Eggert, 2018)
whatChanged: In 2018, the tzdb comment for Africa/Addis_Ababa was updated to explicitly state that tzdb records only Western clock time for Ethiopia, because the format cannot record the country's solar-based timekeeping practice. The traditional Ethiopian system anchors the clock to sunrise (roughly 06:00 Western time), making Ethiopian 1:00 equal to Western 07:00. Africa/Addis_Ababa is a Link to Africa/Nairobi (EAT, UTC+3); the tzdb format has no mechanism to represent a solar-anchored 6-hour offset.
technicalImpact: Any app displaying local time to Ethiopian users who follow traditional timekeeping must manually apply a 6-hour shift on top of EAT (UTC+3). The tzdb entry for Africa/Addis_Ababa gives no hint this is needed.
headline: The clock tzdb cannot read
blurb: Ethiopia uses a traditional clock anchored to sunrise that runs 6 hours behind the Western clock, and tzdb simply has no way to represent it.
story: In Ethiopia, the day starts at sunrise, not midnight. So Ethiopian 1:00 in the morning is Western 7:00 AM, and the clocks run 6 hours apart all day. The tzdb maintainers acknowledged in 2018 that the database format has no way to encode this, so Africa/Addis_Ababa only covers the Western (UTC+3) offset used in official contexts. Software that trusts tzdb for local Ethiopian time will quietly show the wrong hour to users who follow the traditional system.

title: Ireland modeled with negative DST in the tz database (2018a, 2018c, 2018e)
body: IANA tz database maintainers (Paul Eggert and others), with problems reported by Deborah Goldsmith (Apple/ICU) and Stephen Colebourne (Joda-Time / Java ecosystem)
whatChanged: 2018a changed Europe/Dublin to model Irish Standard Time (UTC+1) as standard time and GMT (UTC+0) as negative daylight saving in winter, using negative SAVE values in the Eire rules. 2018c reverted this because ICU and OpenJDK do not support negative SAVE values. 2018e reintroduced negative DST in the main data format and added a separate rearguard tarball for parsers that cannot handle negative DST. The negative-DST approach also covers other zones such as Africa/Windhoek and Europe/Prague in their historical data.
technicalImpact: Negative DST inverts the usual assumption: tm_isdst=1 no longer means clocks are ahead of standard time. ICU and OpenJDK rejected the 2018a data, forcing the 2018c rollback. The rearguard tarball (tzdataVERSION-rearguard.tar.gz) was created specifically for parsers that cannot handle negative SAVE. Systems consuming tzdata from ICU or OpenJDK may report tm_isdst differently from systems using the main tzdata format for Europe/Dublin timestamps.
headline: The winter that broke the DST assumption
blurb: Ireland's clocks legally run on summer time year-round, with winter treated as negative daylight saving, and getting that right in software took three tzdata releases in 2018.
story: Ireland passed the Standard Time Act in 1968, which defined UTC+1 as the statutory standard time, but the Standard Time (Amendment) Act 1971 reintroduced a winter reversion to GMT. From 1971 onward, Ireland's clocks have observed UTC+1 in summer and GMT in winter, while UTC+1 remains the legal standard, making winter GMT a form of negative daylight saving. In January 2018 the tz database tried to model this correctly using negative SAVE values, but two major consumers, ICU (used by many platforms) and OpenJDK (Java's time library), rejected the data outright. The change was rolled back within weeks. Over the following months the maintainers built a compatibility layer called the rearguard format, a separate data tarball with the old representation, and reintroduced the correct negative-DST model in May 2018. The episode shows that even a technically accurate change to time zone data can break the global software ecosystem if downstream parsers have not kept pace.

title: Ireland's inverted DST encoding breaks ICU and OpenJDK
body: IANA tz database maintainers, Unicode Consortium (ICU), Oracle (OpenJDK)
whatChanged: tzdb 2018a (released 2018-01-12) modeled Europe/Dublin using negative SAVE values in the Eire rules, encoding Irish Standard Time (UTC+1) as the base offset and winter GMT as negative daylight saving (-1:00). ICU rejected any negative DST offset with U_ILLEGAL_ARGUMENT_ERROR at runtime, and OpenJDK broke similarly (JDK-8195595). tzdb 2018c (released 2018-01-22) reverted the change as a temporary workaround. tzdb 2018d introduced a vanguard/rearguard build split, and 2018e (released 2018-05-01) reintroduced negative DST in the vanguard form, while the rearguard form reverses tm_isdst but keeps UTC offsets correct.
technicalImpact: Runtimes consuming a rearguard tzdb build report tm_isdst=1 during Irish winter and tm_isdst=0 during Irish summer, which is backwards from convention. ICU versions shipping before the fix crash with an illegal-argument error on any timezone rule with a negative DST offset. Applications that test for Irish DST by checking tm_isdst must verify behavior against the specific tzdb form (vanguard or rearguard) their platform ships.
headline: The sign that broke two runtimes
blurb: A single minus sign in Ireland's timezone rules made ICU and OpenJDK crash or return backwards results, forcing a rollback within ten days.
story: Ireland's law says the country runs on GMT in winter and moves forward an hour in summer, but it also declares the summer time to be the standard. In January 2018 the tzdb maintainers tried to encode that legal reality exactly, using a negative daylight-saving offset to say winter was the deviation. ICU, used by Android and many platforms, treated any negative DST value as an illegal argument and threw an error. OpenJDK broke the same day. The maintainers rolled back the change within ten days, then spent the next three and a half months building a dual-format system so strict parsers could get a safe approximation while correct parsers got the exact model. Even a technically valid encoding can shatter real software if the spec was never tested against it.

title: São Tomé and Príncipe: one-year oscillation from UTC+0 to UTC+1 and back (2018-2019)
body: Government of São Tomé and Príncipe (Decree No. 25/2017 and its repeal)
whatChanged: São Tomé and Príncipe moved from GMT (UTC+0) to WAT (UTC+1) on 2018-01-01 at 01:00 local time, then reversed back to GMT on 2019-01-01 at 02:00 local time after the change proved unpopular and a new government took office. The forward move was enacted by Decree No. 25/2017; the reversal repealed it. Encoded in Africa/Sao_Tome in tzdb 2018a (forward) and 2018i (reversal).
technicalImpact: Any application or database that cached Africa/Sao_Tome as permanently UTC+0 was wrong for exactly one year (2018). Systems that did not apply tzdb 2018i before 2019-01-01 showed clocks one hour fast. The zone also carries two historical LMT values (+0:26:56 pre-1884, then -0:36:45 Lisbon Mean Time 1884-1912), one of the few African zones with a historically negative LMT, which can surprise parsers of pre-modern timestamps.
headline: The island that changed its clock twice in a year
blurb: São Tomé and Príncipe jumped one hour forward on New Year's Day 2018, then quietly reversed the change exactly one year later after public backlash.
story: At the start of 2018, São Tomé and Príncipe moved its clocks one hour ahead to join the West Africa Time zone, hoping for economic and health benefits from more evening daylight. The change was unpopular, and after a change of government the country reversed it on 2019-01-01, returning to GMT. Software that treated the island's offset as a static value was wrong for an entire calendar year. Developers who missed the tzdb 2018i update before the reversal found systems showing the wrong time again.

title: Sao Tome and Principe switches to UTC+1, then back to UTC in one year
body: Government of Sao Tome and Principe
whatChanged: On 2018-01-01 at 01:00 local time, Sao Tome and Principe moved from UTC+00:00 (GMT) to UTC+01:00 (WAT, West Africa Time). Exactly one year later, on 2019-01-01 at 02:00 local time, the country reverted to UTC+00:00 (GMT). The 2018 change was tracked in tzdb release 2018a (released 2018-01-12, after the transition had already occurred); the 2019 revert was tracked in tzdb release 2018i (released 2018-12-30).
technicalImpact: Any system or application that did not apply tzdb 2018a before 2018-01-01 showed times one hour off for the country. Systems then needed a second update for the 2019 revert. The short-lived change is a reminder that tzdb updates must be applied promptly and that reversions are not unprecedented.
headline: The island that tried UTC+1 for exactly one year
blurb: Sao Tome and Principe moved its clocks forward by one hour in 2018, then quietly reversed the decision a year later.
story: In January 2018, Sao Tome and Principe shifted from GMT to West Africa Time, joining its continental neighbors at UTC+1. The change lasted precisely one year: on New Year's Day 2019, the island nation moved its clocks back again and returned to GMT. The back-and-forth caught many software systems off guard, since both the switch and the revert required separate tzdb updates applied on tight deadlines. It stands as a sharp example of how even small countries can force two timezone database releases within twelve months.

title: tz database adopts negative DST and splits into vanguard and rearguard data forms
body: IANA tz database (Paul Eggert)
whatChanged: The tz project introduced parallel 'vanguard' (modern) and 'rearguard' (legacy-compatible) data forms in 2018d so older parsers that could not handle negative SAVE values would still build, then in 2018e (2018-05-01) moved the main format to use negative daylight saving (winter as the deviation) for cases like Ireland (Europe/Dublin from 1971), Prague (1946/7), and Namibia (Africa/Windhoek 1994/2017), shipping a standalone rearguard tarball for trailing-edge parsers.
technicalImpact: ICU and OpenJDK did not support negative SAVE values, where a DST flag no longer meant clocks-ahead. The vanguard and rearguard split let consumers pick data their parser could handle, but added a build-time decision packagers had to make consciously.

reverify.source: IANA tzdb theory.html (vanguard/rearguard + negative DST); relates to [[ireland-negative-dst-1971]]
reverify.note: newly added this pass; primary announcement, editorial prose new

headline: Two versions of time, for old and new readers
blurb: To model places like Ireland correctly, the tz project added negative daylight saving and shipped two data formats.
story: Some places, like Ireland, are easier to describe by treating winter as the change from summer time. The time database started doing this, but older software could not handle the idea. So the project began publishing two versions of its data, a modern one and a backward-compatible one. Packagers now choose which version fits the software that will read it.

title: North Korea reverts to UTC+9 (KST) to realign with South Korea
body: Government of the DPRK (Kim Jong-un); encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: Following the April 2018 inter-Korean summit at Panmunjom, Kim Jong-un ordered clocks moved forward 30 minutes, abolishing Pyongyang Time and returning Asia/Pyongyang to UTC+9. The transition actually occurred at 23:30 on 2018-05-04 (clocks jumped to 00:00), not at 00:00 on 2018-05-05.
technicalImpact: Reversed the 2015 half-hour offset after only ~3 years. tz 2018e modeled the change at 00:00; the corrected exact instant (23:30 the prior day) required a follow-up fix in tz 2018f, so systems on 2018e had a 30-minute window of incorrect time on the transition night.

validation.supportingQuote: North Korea switches back from +0830 to +09 on 2018-05-05. ... The 2018-05-05 North Korea 30-minute time zone change took place at 23:30 the previous day, not at 00:00 that day.
validation.corrections: technicalImpact: the corrected exact instant (23:30 the prior day) was fixed in tz 2018f (2018-10-18), not 2018h. sourceUrl should be https://data.iana.org/time-zones/tzdb-2018f/NEWS

headline: North Korea matched South Korea's clock
blurb: North Korea moved its clocks forward 30 minutes to line up with South Korea.
story: Before a 2018 summit, North Korea moved its clocks forward by 30 minutes so it would match South Korea again. This undid a change it had made about three years earlier. The exact moment of the switch was recorded wrong at first, so a follow-up correction was needed.

title: Japan floats summer DST for the Tokyo 2020 Olympics, then drops it
body: Government of Japan / Tokyo 2020 organisers (proposal, never enacted)
whatChanged: In 2018, with heat a concern for the Tokyo 2020 Games, officials floated introducing daylight saving time (a two-hour summer shift was discussed) for the first time since the occupation era. The proposal was dropped after pushback, so Japan kept UTC+9 year-round.
technicalImpact: Had it passed on the short timeline discussed, every system serving Asia/Tokyo would have needed an emergency tz update for a brand-new Japanese DST rule. The episode is a reminder that even floated, near-term DST changes create scramble risk for tz maintainers and software vendors.

reverify.note: newly added this pass; proposal-only, well-documented

headline: The Olympic clock change that wasn't
blurb: Japan briefly considered summer time for the 2020 Olympics, which would have been its first DST since 1951.
story: Worried about summer heat at the 2020 Olympics, Japanese officials discussed moving clocks forward for the season. It would have been Japan's first daylight saving time since the postwar years. The idea met resistance and was dropped. Had it gone ahead on short notice, time software worldwide would have needed a rushed update.

title: EU proposes abolishing biannual DST clock changes (never enacted)
body: European Commission and European Parliament
whatChanged: After a public consultation drawing ~4.6 million responses, the European Commission proposed a directive on 12 Sep 2018 to end seasonal clock changes EU-wide, letting each member state choose permanent summer or winter time. The European Parliament adopted its first-reading position 26 Mar 2019 targeting 2021. The file stalled in the Council and has seen no progress since January 2020; never became law.
technicalImpact: A live, unresolved overhang for every timezone-aware system in the EU. Had it passed, dozens of Europe/* zones would have stopped DST on divergent dates per country, requiring large coordinated tz updates and breaking long-range scheduling. Because it remains pending, tz maintainers must keep the existing last-Sunday-March/October rules indefinitely.

validation.supportingQuote: On 12 September 2018, the European Commission published a proposal for a directive on discontinuing seasonal changes of time. ... While in January 2020, the Commission included the proposal in Annex III of the 2020 Commission Work Programme relating to 'Priority pending proposals', there have been no further progress in the Council on the file.

headline: Europe proposed ending clock changes
blurb: The EU proposed ending twice-a-year clock changes, but it never became law.
story: In 2018, after millions of people responded to a survey, the European Commission proposed ending seasonal clock changes across the EU. Each country would pick whether to stay on summer or winter time. The plan stalled and was never passed, so the old clock-change rules still stand.

title: Morocco adopts permanent UTC+1 with a recurring Ramadan exception (announced 2 days early)
body: Government of Morocco (Decree No. 2.18.855); encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: By Decree 2.18.855 issued 26 Oct 2018, two days before the scheduled 28 Oct fall-back, Morocco abolished biannual clock changes and made UTC+1 permanent, EXCEPT that it drops to UTC+0 for the month of Ramadan and returns afterward. Because Ramadan moves ~11 days earlier each Gregorian year, the two transition dates shift annually.
technicalImpact: Africa/Casablanca now has two DST-like transitions every year on dates pinned to the Islamic lunar calendar, requiring a fresh tz release nearly every year with hand-computed Ramadan dates. The 2-day notice in 2018 plus astronomical uncertainty repeatedly forces last-minute patches; stale rules show Moroccan time an hour off during/after Ramadan.

validation.supportingQuote: Morocco switches from +00/+01 to permanent +01 effective 2018-10-28, so its clocks will not fall back as previously scheduled. (Thanks to Mohamed Essedik Najd and Brian Inglis.) [release 2018g, 2018-10-26]; and "Guess that Morocco will continue to fall back just before and spring forward just after Ramadan, the practice since 2012." [release 2018h]

headline: Morocco keeps one clock except in Ramadan
blurb: Morocco stopped seasonal clock changes but still shifts its clock during Ramadan.
story: In 2018 Morocco announced, just two days before a planned change, that it would stop switching its clocks twice a year. It kept one steady time except during the month of Ramadan, when the clock drops back by an hour and then returns. Because Ramadan falls about eleven days earlier each year, those two switch dates move every year.

title: Qyzylorda, Kazakhstan drops from UTC+6 to UTC+5
body: Government of Kazakhstan
whatChanged: Qyzylorda (Kyzylorda) oblast moved its standard offset from UTC+06:00 to UTC+05:00 at midnight on 21 December 2018. The neighboring Qostanay oblast did not switch, forcing a zone split and the creation of a new IANA zone, Asia/Qostanay. The change was encoded in tzdb 2018h and required an out-of-band Windows update for affected systems.
technicalImpact: The split forced creation of Asia/Qostanay in tzdb 2018h; systems using a single Kazakhstan-east zone needed patching. Windows required an out-of-band KB update because the change fell between normal cumulative update cycles. Any app caching the old UTC+6 offset for Qyzylorda produced wrong timestamps immediately after the switch.
headline: Kazakhstan splits its clocks mid-winter
blurb: One Kazakhstani region quietly moved its clocks back an hour days before the new year, splitting a previously unified time zone.
story: In December 2018, Qyzylorda oblast switched from UTC+6 to UTC+5, while neighboring Qostanay stayed put. That disagreement meant a single IANA zone could no longer cover both regions, so tzdb 2018h coined Asia/Qostanay to keep them separate. The change landed between normal Windows update cycles, forcing Microsoft to ship an emergency patch. Software that had hard-coded UTC+6 for the region or cached the old zone data started producing wrong timestamps the moment the clocks changed.

title: RFC 8536: The Time Zone Information Format (TZif) standardized
body: IETF; authors Arthur David Olson, Paul Eggert (UCLA), Kenneth Murchison (FastMail)
whatChanged: Published February 2019, RFC 8536 gave the long-undocumented binary zoneinfo/TZif file format an official specification, defining versions 1, 2 and 3: transition times, UTC offsets, DST flags, zone abbreviations and optional leap-second records. Version 2+ uses 64-bit transition timestamps (vs 32-bit, 1901-2038-limited v1) plus a trailing POSIX TZ string. RFC 8536 was later obsoleted by RFC 9636 (October 2024), which added the version 4 format.
technicalImpact: Provided an interoperability contract for the compiled files produced by zic and consumed by libc, the JVM and others. The 64-bit v2/v3 format is the key mitigation for the 2038 problem in time-zone resolution, allowing correct local-time computation far beyond 2038.

validation.supportingQuote: RFC 8536, "The Time Zone Information Format (TZif)", February 2019; authors A. Olson, P. Eggert (UCLA), and K. Murchison (FastMail). Defines TZif versions 1 (32-bit, 1901-2038), 2 (64-bit transition times + POSIX TZ footer), and 3.

headline: Time zone files got a written standard
blurb: The format of the world's time zone files finally got an official specification.
story: Computers store time zone rules in a special file format that had long gone undocumented. In 2019 an official specification was published to describe exactly how those files work. The newer version uses bigger numbers for dates, which lets software handle times correctly far into the future.

title: TZif binary format formalized in RFC 8536 (2019), superseded by RFC 9636 (2024)
body: IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force)
whatChanged: RFC 8536 (February 2019) formally documented the TZif binary file format for the first time, including rules that time type 0 specifies local time for pre-first-transition timestamps, UT offsets must not equal -2^31, and leap-second entries must be at least 2419199 seconds (28 days) apart. The tzdb code was updated in 2024b to conform to RFC 8536 section 3.2 for these early timestamps. RFC 9636 (October 2024, cited starting in tzdb 2025a) obsoleted RFC 8536, retaining those rules and adding a version 4 format with optional leap-second table truncation and file expiration times.
technicalImpact: TZif parsers should validate against RFC 9636. Files generated by zic 2018e or earlier could deviate from the time-type-0 rule, so parsers reading those older files must handle the deviation. Libraries that hard-code TZif parsing logic may reject or misread files that conform to the newer rules.
headline: The spec that time zones forgot to have
blurb: For decades, every Unix system stored time zone data in a binary format that had no official written standard until 2019.
story: The TZif file format has shipped on virtually every Unix and Linux system since the 1980s, but no one wrote down the rules for it until the IETF published RFC 8536 in February 2019. A follow-up standard, RFC 9636, tightened several edge cases and obsoleted the first one by 2024. The gap mattered because implementers had to reverse-engineer the format from C source code, leading to subtle incompatibilities. Software that parses TZif files directly now has a testable specification to conform to, and non-conforming parsers may silently misread timestamps before a zone file's first recorded transition.

title: GPS week number rollover: second epoch
body: U.S. Air Force (GPS program office), with advance warnings from the U.S. Department of Homeland Security and the International Civil Aviation Organization
whatChanged: At 23:59:42 UTC on 6 April 2019, the legacy GPS NAV message 10-bit week counter rolled from 1023 back to 0, ending the second GPS Time Epoch (which began 21 August 1999). Receivers and embedded systems that did not account for this rollover jumped approximately 19.7 years backward in their reported date, producing timestamps in late 1999 or 1980.
technicalImpact: Devices and software that decoded GPS week numbers without rollover compensation reported wrong dates. Real-world failures included KLM flight delays (Honeywell flight management software), crash of the New York City Wireless Network (NYCWiN), NOAA weather buoy malfunctions, Vaisala radiosonde ground station suspensions of up to two weeks, and consumer GPS units and phones manufactured before 2013 showing incorrect dates. A Prague public-transport ticket terminal printed 23 August 1999 instead of 8 April 2019 (exactly 1024 weeks earlier), a 20-year regression. Honda and Acura vehicles manifested the same epoch-counting bug on 1 January 2022 (the 'Y2K22' issue), resetting clocks to 2002. GoPro Karma drones manifested the bug in January 2020. DHS and ICAO issued advance warnings; vendors shipped firmware patches before and after the event. No tzdb release was involved as this was a GPS receiver firmware issue, not a civil timezone policy change.
headline: The clock that forgot it had been running for 20 years
blurb: GPS satellites count weeks in a 10-bit number that wraps back to zero every 19.7 years, and devices that ignored this in 2019 suddenly thought it was 1999 again.
story: GPS satellites broadcast the current week as a 10-bit counter, which can only reach 1023 before wrapping to zero. On 6 April 2019 that counter hit its limit for the second time since GPS was launched. Devices with old firmware did not handle the wrap, so they subtracted roughly 20 years from their internal clock without warning. Planes were delayed, weather stations went offline, and a bus ticket machine in Prague sold tickets stamped with a date in August. The episode is a textbook reminder that any counter with a fixed bit width will eventually overflow, and timekeeping software must be designed to handle each rollover explicitly.

title: Brazil abolishes daylight saving time (horario de verao) indefinitely
body: Federative Republic of Brazil (Decree No. 9.772, signed by President Bolsonaro), encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: Decree 9.772 of 2019-04-25 ended Brazil's southern-region DST, which had run since 1985. No spring-forward in November 2019 and none since; affected zones (America/Sao_Paulo) stay on standard time year-round (Sao Paulo permanent UTC-3).
technicalImpact: DST rules for Brazil's southern zones had to be removed. Un-updated systems performed the cancelled 2019-11 spring-forward, putting Brazil an hour ahead, hitting Carnival/holiday scheduling and banking. Because Brazil's DST start had historically floated by yearly decree, libraries had long guessed future dates, making stale guesses likely to fire after abolition until tzdata 2019b propagated.

validation.supportingQuote: Daylight Saving Time has been permanently abolished in Brazil, according to the Senate bulletin of April 25, 2019. ... São Paulo observes Brasília Time all year with no Daylight Saving Time clock changes.

headline: Brazil ended daylight saving time
blurb: Brazil stopped changing its clocks for summer, ending the modern era of DST it had observed since 1985.
story: In 2019 Brazil ended daylight saving time, which parts of the country had used since 1985. There was no clock change that November, and the affected areas have stayed on one time ever since. Systems that were not updated kept making the old change and showed Brazil an hour ahead.

title: Brazil abolishes daylight saving time by presidential decree
body: President Jair Bolsonaro / Government of Brazil
whatChanged: Decree 9772, signed 2019-04-25, revoked all prior DST decrees and ended daylight saving time permanently across Brazil. DST had previously applied to Distrito Federal and ten states (ES, GO, MG, MS, MT, PR, RJ, RS, SC, SP). No DST transitions occur after the 2018-2019 season. The tzdb Rule Brazil entry ends at 2019 with no forward start rule.
technicalImpact: Any timezone database older than 2019b will schedule phantom DST transitions for Brazilian zones in 2019-2020 and beyond. Systems must run tzdb 2019b or later for correct Brazil wall-clock times from November 2019 onward. America/Sao_Paulo and related zones are affected.
headline: Brazil's last clock change
blurb: Brazil scrapped daylight saving time entirely in 2019, ending a decades-old practice with a single presidential decree.
story: For decades, Brazil shifted clocks forward each November across much of the country to save energy during summer evenings. President Bolsonaro signed Decree 9772 on 25 April 2019, citing studies showing the energy savings had become negligible and that the disruption harmed health and productivity. The 2018-2019 season was the last. Software using timezone data older than tzdb 2019b will incorrectly show Brazil observing DST in subsequent years, causing hour-long errors in scheduling and logging.

title: Japan's Reiwa era begins, forcing a new calendar era into global software
body: Government of Japan (Cabinet); encoded by Unicode CLDR, ICU, the JDK and Windows
whatChanged: Emperor Akihito's abdication ended the Heisei era and began the Reiwa era on 1 May 2019. Japanese civil dates use the wareki (era-name) calendar, and the new gengo name was only revealed on 1 April 2019, one month before it took effect, so every system that formats or parses Japanese dates needed a new era added on short notice.
technicalImpact: Unicode CLDR, ICU, the JDK (java.time.chrono.JapaneseEra and java.util.Calendar) and Windows all shipped updates; unpatched software rendered Reiwa dates as 'Heisei 31' or with a placeholder era character. A representation change, not an offset change, that still broke date handling worldwide.

reverify.note: newly added this pass; primary sources solid, editorial prose new, awaiting your review

headline: A new emperor, a new era, a scramble to patch
blurb: Japan's switch to the Reiwa era added a new calendar era that software worldwide had to support, with only a month's notice.
story: Japan counts years by the reign of its emperor, so when a new emperor took the throne in 2019 a new era called Reiwa began. The name was only announced one month before it started. Software that shows Japanese dates had to be updated quickly to know about the new era. Programs that missed the update showed the wrong era name.

title: Asia/Harbin (and Chongqing/Kashgar) pre-1970 offsets documented as incorrect in tzdb backzone
body: IANA tz database maintainers (note filed by Phake Nick, acknowledged by Paul Eggert)
whatChanged: A 2020-04-15 comment in the tzdb backzone file documents that Asia/Harbin's historical entries are wrong: per the official Manchukuo announcement, 1932 should adopt UTC+8 (the file lists +08:30), and 1937 should adopt UTC+9 on January 1. The 1940 entry is also flagged as incorrect. The same comment notes that Asia/Chongqing and Asia/Kashgar pre-1949 data is unreliable, since most of these cities should have adopted Beijing Time around 1949 to 1950. A separate contradiction is noted: the backzone file states these zones must not diverge from Asia/Shanghai after 1970, yet entries list a 1980 transition, which implies divergence.
technicalImpact: Any software resolving pre-1970 Chinese timestamps using Asia/Harbin, Asia/Chongqing, or Asia/Kashgar may produce wrong results. These zones live in backzone (not compiled by default), but installations built with PACKRATDATA=backzone will silently serve incorrect historical offsets until the data is corrected.
headline: China's wartime clocks, recorded wrong
blurb: The tz database's historical offsets for Harbin and other Chinese cities during the Manchukuo era are documented as incorrect but remain uncorrected.
story: When Manchukuo was established in the 1930s, Harbin adopted a series of time changes that the tz database recorded inaccurately. A contributor named Phake Nick traced official Manchukuo announcements in 2020 and found two errors: the 1932 entry was off by 30 minutes (the file lists UTC+8:30 where it should be UTC+8), and the 1937 switch to UTC+9 is missing entirely, with the data jumping straight to the 1940 entry. The errors sit in the backzone file, which holds pre-1970 data that most systems never compile. Software that does compile backzone and tries to resolve timestamps from wartime northeastern China will quietly return the wrong local time.

title: IANA tz database adds an Expires line to the leapseconds file (release 2020a)
body: IANA / tz database maintainers
whatChanged: tzdb 2020a: zic -L gained support for an Expires line in the leapseconds file and truncates TZif output accordingly, so a TZif file built with 'right' (leap-aware) zones records when its leap-second data becomes stale instead of being silently trusted forever.
technicalImpact: For systems using 'right/' zoneinfo or feeding the leapseconds file to NTP, an expired file now signals newer leap-second info may be missing rather than continuing with possibly-wrong UTC-TAI offsets. An expired leapseconds file is a known operational footgun.

validation.supportingQuote: zic -L now supports an Expires line in the leapseconds file, and truncates the TZif output accordingly.

headline: Leap second files got an expiry date
blurb: The time database added an expiry marker to its leap second data.
story: Some systems rely on a file that lists added seconds in the world clock. In 2020 the time database added a way to mark when that file's data goes out of date. Now an expired file warns that newer information may be missing, instead of being trusted forever.

title: tzdb leapseconds Expires line kept as a comment for old zic compatibility
body: IANA Time Zone Database maintainers (tzdb project)
whatChanged: Starting with tzdb 2020a, zic gained the ability to parse a formal Expires directive in the leapseconds file and embed expiry data in TZif output; this upgraded the older #expires comment mechanism that had shipped in leapseconds files since 2018f (and which zic 2018f+ already used as a fallback to truncate TZif). The distributed leapseconds file keeps the directive commented out (#Expires 2026 Dec 28 00:00:00) so that pre-2020a zic binaries do not reject the file. The expiry is also exposed via the #expires POSIX-timestamp comment line (1798416000).
technicalImpact: Build pipelines using zic older than 2018f silently get no expiry enforcement in compiled TZif files. zic from 2018f to 2019x parses the #expires comment and truncates TZif output, but only zic 2020a+ supports the formal Expires directive. Any tool that validates leap-second table freshness can parse the #expires comment timestamp rather than relying on an active Expires directive. Pipelines on zic 2020a+ can enable directive-based enforcement by uncommenting the line.
headline: The expiry date hidden in a comment
blurb: The leap-second table's expiry date is buried in a comment so old timezone compilers don't choke on it.
story: In 2020 the tzdb team added support for an Expires line that tells timezone compilers when the leap-second list runs out. But millions of systems still ran older compilers that would reject any file containing that directive, so the line was commented out. The real expiry sits in a separate comment as a raw Unix timestamp, readable by scripts but invisible to the compiler. Until old zic binaries are retired, any pipeline that needs to know when the leap-second data expires has to parse that hidden comment rather than a proper directive.

title: Negative leap second risk as Earth's rotation accelerates
body: IERS / scientific community; risk popularized by Meta, Google, NIST and geophysicists
whatChanged: Since 2020 Earth has repeatedly set records for shortest day (2020-07-19 was 1.46 ms short; further records in 2022, 2024, 2025, with 2024-07-05 the shortest yet at 1.66 ms). For the first time, length-of-day trends point toward UTC needing a NEGATIVE leap second, removing a second so the minute jumps from 23:59:58 to 00:00:00, skipping 23:59:59, rather than the historically-only-positive insertion. A 2022 CGPM resolution will retire leap seconds by 2035, so any negative leap second would have to fall in a narrow window (roughly 2026-2035, possibly as early as ~2029) or never happen at all.
technicalImpact: A negative leap second has never occurred and essentially no production NTP servers, OS kernels, time libraries, or smear schemes have been exercised against it. Smear implementations, ntpd/chrony state machines, and code assuming time never moves backward (the Cloudflare 2017 bug class) are all untested for the negative case, raising Y2K-style failure concerns.

validation.supportingQuote: Earth is spinning so fast that global timekeepers are considering something that's never been done before: adding a negative leap second. ... This follows a trend that has been observed since 2020.

headline: Earth is spinning slightly faster
blurb: The Earth has been spinning a little faster, which may force a never-tried clock fix.
story: Since 2020 the Earth has set records for its shortest days. For the first time, this points toward removing a second from the world clock rather than adding one. A removal like this has never happened, so the software and systems that keep time have never been tested against it.

academicRefs.authors: Agnew, Duncan Carr
academicRefs.title: A global timekeeping problem postponed by global warming
academicRefs.venue: Nature
academicRefs.keyFinding: Using satellite-gravity measurements of Greenland/Antarctic ice melt and Earth-orientation data, Agnew shows that since 1972 the liquid core's angular velocity has decreased at a steady rate, steadily speeding up the rest of the solid Earth (mantle/crust). Extrapolating the core trend plus other effects, UTC as now defined will require a negative discontinuity (negative leap second) by 2029. Critically, the accelerated polar ice melt slows Earth's rotation and counteracts the spin-up, postponing the problem by 3 years: without recent melt the negative leap second would have been needed around 2026. (Nature 628(8007):333-336.)

academicRefs.authors: Malkin, Zinovy
academicRefs.title: Should We Expect Further Acceleration of the Earth's Rotation in the Coming Years?
academicRefs.venue: arXiv preprint (astro-ph / Earth rotation; IERS data analysis)
academicRefs.keyFinding: Responding directly to Agnew (2024), Malkin analyses the latest IERS Earth-rotation and UT1 data plus their predictions and argues the acceleration observed over the past ~four years is likely to return to slowing down soon. He concludes that fears about needing a negative leap second in UTC in the next few years appear unfounded on current observational data, i.e. the recent spin-up is treated as a transient oscillation rather than a sustained trend.

title: Moment.js declared a legacy project in maintenance mode
body: Moment.js maintainers (ichernev, maggiepint, phailure, timrwood)
whatChanged: On 15 September 2020 the Moment.js maintainers published an official Project Status notice declaring Moment a legacy project in maintenance mode. No new features, no immutability or tree-shaking work, no v3. The library will only receive security fixes and IANA timezone data updates. Developers are directed to Luxon, Day.js, date-fns, js-Joda, or the native Temporal proposal instead.
technicalImpact: Projects still importing moment or moment-timezone were advised to migrate. The announcement accelerated mass ecosystem migration away from Moment's mutable API and large bundle size. Libraries and frameworks that had bundled Moment as a peer dependency began dropping it. Temporal (TC39 proposal) gained traction as the long-term native replacement for JavaScript date and time handling.
headline: The end of Moment.js
blurb: The team behind the most-used JavaScript date library told developers to stop using it and switch to something else.
story: Moment.js had been the default way to handle dates in JavaScript for nearly a decade, downloaded millions of times a week. In September 2020 its own maintainers published a notice saying the project was done growing: no new features, no fixes to its known problems with bundle size or mutability. They listed four newer libraries and the browser's own built-in date tools as better choices for new projects. The announcement triggered a wave of migrations across the JavaScript ecosystem and pushed the TC39 Temporal proposal, a modern date API built into the language itself, much closer to mainstream attention. Any application still relying on Moment for timezone handling now carries a dependency the authors themselves say should be replaced.

title: Antarctica's Casey Station changes time zone repeatedly
body: Australian Antarctic Division; encoded by IANA
whatChanged: Antarctica/Casey has flipped between UTC+8 and UTC+11 several times since 2018 (encoded across multiple tz releases) for logistics and flights, with no fixed seasonal rule, since research stations pick whatever offset suits operations.
technicalImpact: A zone whose offset changes on irregular, announced-late dates, exercising the part of tz data that has no algorithmic rule. Systems serving Antarctic zones must rely entirely on timely tz updates, since nothing can be predicted from a formula.

reverify.note: newly added this pass; niche, multiple releases

headline: A research station that keeps changing its clock
blurb: Antarctica's Casey Station has hopped between UTC+8 and UTC+11 several times since 2018.
story: Antarctic research stations set their clocks to suit flights and supply runs, not the sun. Casey Station has switched between two very different time zones several times since 2018. The changes follow no fixed rule and are often announced late. Software can only keep up by taking frequent time-data updates.

title: Python adds the zoneinfo module (PEP 615), starting the move away from pytz
body: Python core (PEP 615); Paul Ganssle
whatChanged: Python 3.9 added a standard-library zoneinfo module that reads the system IANA tz data directly, giving correct arithmetic across DST without the long-standing pytz pitfalls. It began a multi-year transition off pytz, whose localize()/normalize() dance had tripped up developers for a decade.
technicalImpact: pytz attached a fixed historical LMT offset when a timezone was passed directly to the datetime() constructor via tzinfo=, producing the famous off-by-a-few-minutes bug; localize() was the prescribed workaround, but the requirement itself was a footgun. zoneinfo follows the modern fold/gap model instead. Projects migrating from pytz to zoneinfo must change idioms, and a fallback (the tzdata PyPI package) is needed where the OS ships no zone data, such as Windows.

reverify.note: newly added this pass; primary source, editorial prose new

headline: Python finally bakes in time zones
blurb: Python 3.9 added a built-in zoneinfo module, beginning the long goodbye to pytz and its famous gotchas.
story: For years Python developers leaned on a separate library called pytz, which was easy to misuse and could be a few minutes off for old dates. In 2020 Python added its own time-zone support that reads the system's time data directly. It handles the spring-forward and fall-back edge cases more safely. Moving old code to it takes some care but removes a long-standing source of bugs.

title: IANA's zic switches to slim TZif output by default, exposing non-conformant parsers
body: IANA tz database (Paul Eggert)
whatChanged: tzdb 2020b made the zic compiler emit 'slim' TZif files by default, describing far-future transitions with the POSIX TZ rule rather than pre-expanding them. The data got smaller but readers now had to handle the TZif footer correctly; distributions could still build 'fat' files with zic -b fat.
technicalImpact: Parsers that ignored the TZif footer, or assumed every future transition was pre-expanded, produced wrong times after about 2037 once distros shipped slim data. It forced fixes in several language runtimes and zone readers and surfaced long-latent TZif non-conformance.

reverify.note: newly added this pass; primary announcement, editorial prose new

headline: Smaller time files, broken parsers
blurb: The tz compiler began shipping slimmer data files by default, breaking readers that cut corners.
story: The program that builds the world's time files started making them smaller by default. Instead of listing every future change, the slim files describe the repeating rule. Readers that did not follow the rules correctly started giving wrong times far in the future. Several tools had to be fixed to read the new, leaner files.

title: Go 1.15 misreads slim TZif files, producing wrong DST offsets
body: Go team (Google), triggered by the tzdb 2020b slim-default change from the IANA tz maintainers
whatChanged: tzdb 2020b (released 2020-10-06) changed zic's default output from 'fat' to 'slim' TZif format. Go 1.15's time package misread the POSIX footer in slim files, caching the wrong DST zone. Binaries running on Alpine Linux (which updated its tzdata package to 2020b/2020c) returned incorrect DST offsets for affected zones such as Europe/Berlin. The bug was filed 2020-10-19 and became widely visible after DST ended across Europe on 2020-10-25. It was fixed in Go 1.15.4 and Go 1.14.11, both released 2020-11-05.
technicalImpact: Any Go 1.15 or 1.14 binary that loaded system tzdata (not the embedded time/tzdata package) on a host with tzdb 2020b or later could silently return wrong DST abbreviations and offsets. Alpine-based Docker containers were the most common environment hit. The fix landed in Go 1.15.4 / 1.14.11 (2020-11-05); pinning an older Alpine image or embedding tzdata via the time/tzdata import were the available workarounds.
headline: Go's silent DST lie
blurb: A format change in the tz database caused Go 1.15 to silently return wrong daylight-saving offsets for any binary using system timezone files.
story: In October 2020 the tz database switched its default file format from 'fat' to 'slim', producing smaller timezone files. Go 1.15 did not know how to read the new footer in slim files, so it cached the wrong daylight-saving zone. Developers noticed in late October 2020, when clocks fell back across Europe and their Go services kept reporting summer time instead of winter time. The bug was silent: no error, just a wrong answer. Google patched Go 1.15.4 and 1.14.11 within two weeks, but it highlighted how a toolchain can quietly break when the underlying timezone data format changes beneath it.

title: Yukon abolishes clock changes, adopts permanent UTC-7 (MST)
body: Government of Yukon, Canada
whatChanged: America/Whitehorse and America/Dawson moved from -08/-07 (Pacific with DST) to permanent -07 on 2020-11-01. The Yukon government initially described this as 'permanent Pacific Daylight Saving Time' and now labels the year-round zone 'Yukon Standard Time' on yukon.ca; tzdb records it as MST for consistency with nearby Dawson Creek, Creston, and Fort Nelson. tzdb 2020a initially recorded the effective date as 2020-03-08; 2020b corrected it to 2020-11-01, changing only the abbreviation and tm_isdst flag, not the UTC offset.
technicalImpact: Code checking tm_isdst or the DST flag would get wrong results for the March-November 2020 window until updated to 2020b. The government-vs-tzdb naming conflict (PDT vs MST at the same offset) illustrates how political labeling can diverge from tzdb conventions.
headline: Yukon's permanent noon that never falls back
blurb: Yukon stopped changing its clocks and settled on UTC-7 year-round, but the government and the tz database disagreed on what to call it.
story: In 2020 Yukon decided to stop observing daylight saving time and stay on one offset all year. The government branded it 'permanent Pacific Daylight Saving Time', but the tz database recorded the same offset as Mountain Standard Time to match neighboring communities that had always used it. The initial tzdb release (2020a) got the effective date wrong, logging March 8; the follow-up release (2020b) corrected it to November 1. Software relying on the DST flag rather than the raw UTC offset would have reported incorrect daylight-saving status for that roughly eight-month window (March 8 to November 1) until the fix was applied.

title: Yukon abandons clock changes and stays on permanent UTC-7
body: Government of Yukon
whatChanged: Yukon stopped observing DST after 2020-11-01. Both America/Whitehorse and America/Dawson moved to a fixed UTC-7 (encoded as MST in tzdb) with no further DST rules. This is effectively permanent summer time: the territory stays at what used to be its summer offset year-round.
technicalImpact: Code that hard-codes Yukon as UTC-9 (old YST) or UTC-8 plus DST (PST/PDT) is wrong. America/Whitehorse and America/Dawson are now UTC-7 all year. The tzdb captured the change in release 2020b (2020-10-06); systems using stale tz data will misplace Yukon timestamps by one hour in winter.
headline: Yukon's permanent summer clock
blurb: Yukon stopped changing its clocks in 2020 and now sits at UTC-7 all year, making Canadian winters an hour brighter in the territory.
story: Yukon surveyed its residents and found a strong majority wanted to end the twice-yearly clock shuffle. From November 1, 2020, the territory locked its clocks at UTC-7, the offset it had only used in summer before. That offset is now labelled MST in the tz database even though Yukon is geographically in the Pacific time belt. Software that built in assumptions about Yukon being UTC-8 in winter will place timestamps an hour off for anyone in Whitehorse or Dawson.

title: Volgograd drops from UTC+4 to UTC+3 (Moscow time)
body: Russian federal government
whatChanged: Volgograd Oblast moved from UTC+4 (Samara time) to UTC+3 (Moscow time) on 2020-12-27 at 02:00 local time. The change was captured in tzdb release 2020e.
technicalImpact: Systems using the Europe/Volgograd zone picked up the change via tzdata 2020e; anything caching the old UTC+4 offset for that zone produced timestamps one hour ahead after the switch.
headline: Volgograd rejoins Moscow time
blurb: Russia's Volgograd region wound its clocks back an hour to align with Moscow, dropping from UTC+4 to UTC+3.
story: Volgograd had sat at UTC+4 since October 2018, one hour ahead of Moscow, which caused friction for residents dealing with the capital. At 02:00 on 27 December 2020 the clocks rolled back to match Moscow time. The change was modest in scope but required a tzdata update for any software that tracked the Europe/Volgograd zone, since the stored offset was wrong from that moment on.

title: IANA tzdb declares pre-standard LMT offsets 'should not be considered meaningful'
body: IANA tz database maintainers (Paul Eggert et al.)
whatChanged: The tzdb theory document states that the database's LMT (Local Mean Time) offsets should not be considered meaningful and should not prompt the creation of new timezones. These offsets are pre-standard placeholders derived from longitude, not from any recorded clock practice.
technicalImpact: Applications that query historical offsets before a region adopted standard time receive LMT values computed from longitude arithmetic, not from any real clock practice. Displaying or processing these offsets as historical fact (e.g., in a timezone picker scrolled back to 1880) gives users misleading precision. Libraries built on tzdata inherit this ambiguity silently.
headline: The offsets that never meant anything
blurb: Before countries adopted standard time, the tz database fills in offsets calculated from city longitude, which its own authors say should not be treated as meaningful history.
story: When you look up the UTC offset for a city in 1870, tzdb gives you a number derived from that city's longitude on a map. No government set that time, no clock was adjusted to it; it is just arithmetic. The tzdb theory document says plainly that these LMT offsets should not be considered meaningful and should not prompt the creation of timezones. Software that surfaces them to users as historical fact is building on a foundation the database itself warns against.

title: South Sudan moves Africa/Juba from UTC+3 to UTC+2
body: Government of South Sudan
whatChanged: Africa/Juba switched from East Africa Time (UTC+3) to Central Africa Time (UTC+2) at 00:00 on 2021-02-01, setting clocks back one hour. The change was the sole data update in tzdata 2021a, released 2021-01-24.
technicalImpact: Any system or application that cached Africa/Juba as UTC+3 gave wrong timestamps from 2021-02-01 onward. Updating to tzdata 2021a before the transition was essential; systems that did not would be one hour fast for all Juba-based timestamps.
headline: South Sudan's quiet hour-back
blurb: South Sudan moved its clocks back one hour in early 2021, shifting from East Africa Time to Central Africa Time.
story: South Sudan had been keeping East Africa Time (UTC+3) since 2000, the offset it inherited from Sudan and shared with neighbors Uganda and Kenya. In early 2021 the government decided to shift west by one hour to UTC+2, matching the Central Africa Time zone. The change took effect at midnight on 1 February 2021. It was significant enough to be the only update in tzdata 2021a, released just days before, meaning any software that did not update in time would report Juba timestamps an hour ahead for every event after that date.

title: South Sudan switches from EAT (UTC+3) to CAT (UTC+2)
body: Government of South Sudan
whatChanged: Africa/Juba moved from EAT (UTC+3) to CAT (UTC+2) on 2021-02-01 00:00, setting clocks back one hour. Africa/Juba had been a distinct zone since South Sudan's independence in 2011; it stayed at UTC+3 when Sudan (Africa/Khartoum) moved to UTC+2 in November 2017, then independently made the same shift four years later.
technicalImpact: Any system using Africa/Khartoum as a proxy for South Sudan produced correct offsets before 2017 but was wrong from 2017 to 2021 (Khartoum was UTC+2, Juba was UTC+3), then converged again. tzdb release 2021a (released 2021-01-24) carried the fix; systems running older tzdata showed the wrong offset until updated.
headline: South Sudan's four-year detour
blurb: South Sudan quietly set its clocks back an hour in February 2021, rejoining its northern neighbor on Central Africa Time after four years apart.
story: When Sudan moved from UTC+3 to UTC+2 in 2017, South Sudan stayed put, splitting what had been one shared offset into two. That divergence lasted four years and caught many systems off guard because Africa/Juba, created as a distinct zone at South Sudan's 2011 independence, had tracked the same UTC+3 offset as Africa/Khartoum until then. On 1 February 2021 South Sudan finally made the same shift, closing the gap. Software that had assumed the two countries shared an offset was wrong for exactly that four-year window.

title: Samoa cancels daylight saving time, Pacific/Apia stays on UTC+13 year-round
body: Samoa Ministry of Commerce, Industry and Labour
whatChanged: Samoa announced in mid-September 2021, less than two weeks before the scheduled 26 Sep DST start, that it would no longer observe daylight saving time. Pacific/Apia remains permanently on UTC+13. The tzdb WS rules reflect DST ending after 2020; the change was codified in tzdb release 2021b (2021-09-24).
technicalImpact: Short-notice cancellation required an emergency tzdb 2021b release and interim guidance from vendors including Microsoft. Any system that had pre-cached the 2021 DST transition for Pacific/Apia would have applied a +1 hour shift that never happened, corrupting timestamps for Samoan users around late September 2021.
headline: Samoa's last-minute DST cancellation
blurb: Samoa scrapped daylight saving time with less than two weeks' notice, forcing an emergency update to the global time zone database.
story: Samoa had observed daylight saving time since 2010, shifting clocks one hour forward each spring. In September 2021, the government announced it was done, eleven days before the clocks were due to spring forward. The tzdb maintainers shipped an emergency release to remove the transition. Systems that had already loaded the old schedule showed the wrong time for anyone in Samoa for weeks afterward.

title: Pacific/Enderbury renamed to Pacific/Kanton in tzdb
body: IANA tz database maintainers (Paul Eggert et al.), with the underlying zone change decided by the Government of Kiribati in 1994
whatChanged: tzdb release 2021b renamed Pacific/Enderbury to Pacific/Kanton, making Kanton canonical and demoting Enderbury to a backward-compatibility link. The rename reflects that Enderbury is uninhabited while Kanton (population roughly two dozen) is the only inhabited island in that timezone. The underlying offset history shows Kanton jumped from UTC-11 to UTC+13 on 1994-12-31, skipping New Year's Eve 1994 entirely, when Kiribati moved its Phoenix and Line Islands to the west side of the international date line.
technicalImpact: Code referencing Pacific/Enderbury still resolves via the backward link, but canonical zone lookups should use Pacific/Kanton. The 1994 date-line crossing (UTC-11 to UTC+13, a 24-hour jump) means duration calculations spanning that boundary for this zone will encounter a missing day. Any system that hardcoded Pacific/Enderbury as a canonical name needs updating.
headline: The island that skipped New Year's Eve
blurb: Kiribati's Kanton Island jumped 24 hours across the date line in 1994, and the timezone database only caught up with the correct island name in 2021.
story: In 1994 Kiribati moved its outer islands from UTC-11 to UTC+13 so all its territory would share the same calendar day. Kanton Island, with about two dozen rotating caretakers, skipped New Year's Eve 1994 entirely. When tzdb first added the zone in 1993 it used the name Pacific/Enderbury, not realising Enderbury is uninhabited. The rename to Pacific/Kanton arrived in 2021b, nearly three decades late. Software that calculates durations across the 1994 boundary for this zone will hit a silent 24-hour gap.

title: 2021 zone-merging controversy and fork threat; tzdb 2021b release
body: TZ Coordinator Paul Eggert vs. tz community (notably Stephen Colebourne, Joda-Time/JSR-310 author); IANA tz project
whatChanged: Eggert proposed merging zones whose timestamps agree since 1970 into aliases of one canonical zone (e.g. Europe/Oslo, Stockholm, Amsterdam becoming links to Europe/Berlin), dropping distinct pre-1970 history. The plan sparked threats to fork tzdb. The 2021b release (2021-09-25), driven by urgent DST changes in Samoa and Jordan, reverted the controversial European merges and shipped only nine less-contested ones (Africa/Accra, America/Atikokan, America/Blanc-Sablon, America/Creston, America/Curacao, America/Nassau, America/Port_of_Spain, Antarctica/DumontDUrville, Antarctica/Syowa), omitting most 1970-agreement merges after pushback.
technicalImpact: Aliasing previously independent zone IDs breaks software storing or enumerating those IDs as canonical: Colebourne warned Joda-Time users could no longer hold a zone ID like Europe/Oslo as a first-class object, and ICU/CLDR display-name and round-tripping behavior changes. Forced downstream libraries and packagers to decide whether to follow upstream merges or carry patches.

validation.supportingQuote: "Merge more location-based Zones whose timestamps agree since 1970, as pre-1970 timestamps are out of scope." ... "Jordan now starts DST on February's last Thursday. Samoa no longer observes DST."

headline: A plan to merge time zones caused a fight
blurb: A proposal to combine similar time zones sparked a dispute among maintainers.
story: In 2021 the time database's editor proposed merging zones that have kept the same time since 1970, treating several as one. The plan dropped some older history and upset many users, with some threatening to split off their own version. A release that year applied only part of the plan after the pushback.

title: TZif version 4 and the 'Expires' leap-second handling change (tzdb 2021b)
body: IANA tz database (Paul Eggert); TZif format
whatChanged: tzdb 2021b introduced TZif version 4 to express leap-second tables that carry an explicit expiration. To do so, zic now appends a no-change entry to the leap-second table, and the TZif reader now allows the table to begin with a correction other than -1 or +1. It refined how compiled zone files signal how long their leap data is valid.
technicalImpact: Readers that only understood TZif v1 to v3 had to be updated to parse v4 correctly; mishandling the leap-expiration semantics risks using stale or out-of-range leap data. Because v4 does not conform to RFC 8536 and some readers reject it, the 'Expires' feature is disabled by default in 2021b and must be opted into via the EXPIRES_LINE Makefile variable, limiting immediate real-world exposure. Of interest mainly to runtime and library authors that consume compiled TZif.

reverify.note: newly added this pass; release detail, may overlap existing entries

headline: A new time-file format for leap seconds
blurb: tz release 2021b added TZif version 4, refining how compiled files describe leap seconds and their expiry.
story: The compiled files that hold time-zone data got a new version in 2021. It improved how those files describe leap seconds and when that data stops being valid. Programs that read the files had to learn the new version. This mostly mattered to the people who build time libraries.

title: zic -L truncates DST transitions at leap-second table expiry (2020a-2021a bug, fixed in 2021b)
body: IANA tz coordinator (Paul Eggert and the tz mailing list community)
whatChanged: Starting with release 2020a, zic -L truncated TZif output at the leap-second table expiry date (from the 'Expires' directive in the leapseconds file), causing DST transitions after that date to be omitted. Systems using 'right' timezone files would show timestamps off by a full hour instead of the expected few seconds. Fixed in 2021b (released 2021-09-24): zic -L no longer truncates DST transitions at expiry, and instead appends a no-change entry to the leap-second table.
technicalImpact: Systems using 'right' TZif files in tzdb releases 2020a through 2021a had DST transitions silently missing after the leap-second table expiry date, producing up to hour-scale errors in near-future timestamps. Upgrading to 2021b restores correct DST handling. The fix's new 'Expires' output uses TZif version 4, which can be rejected by pickier readers (including tzdb 2017c through 2021a); because of this, 'Expires' directives are disabled by default in 2021b and must be opted into via the EXPIRES_LINE Makefile variable, so a vanilla upgrade does not trigger the compatibility risk.
headline: The zic bug that hid an hour inside a leap-second file
blurb: A zic compiler bug caused 'right' timezone files to silently drop future DST transitions, producing hour-scale timestamp errors.
story: When the IANA tz team added an expiry date to the leap-second table, the zic compiler started using that date to cut off TZif output early. Any DST transition after the expiry was simply gone from the compiled file. The error was much worse than a missed leap second: clocks could be a full hour off for near-future timestamps. The 2021b release fixed it by keeping DST transitions intact and handling the expiry in a separate table entry.

title: tzdata 2021c emergency revert: 2021b's backward-file restructure broke Joda-Time
body: IANA tz database maintainers (Paul Eggert et al.)
whatChanged: tzdata 2021b (released 2021-09-24) moved almost all Link directives out of the 'backward' file into other source files. Within seven days, 2021c (2021-10-01) reverted those moves back to where they were in 2021a, after Stephen Colebourne reported that Joda-Time broke because it relied on the previous file locations. 2021c also fixed two Link line typos (Atlantic/Jan_Mayen and a 'backzone' typo for America/Virgin) and a 'zic -b fat' bug in pre-1970 32-bit data.
technicalImpact: Libraries and tooling that parsed tzdb source files by location (not by 'zic' output) broke silently when Link directives moved. Joda-Time was the reported case; any similar parser faced the same risk. The episode established that the backward file's structure is a de-facto public interface, not just an internal layout.
headline: The backward-file revert
blurb: A tzdb restructure broke Joda-Time within a week and was rolled back in an emergency release.
story: In September 2021 the tz database moved a batch of compatibility Link entries to different source files, a housekeeping change that 'zic' handles fine. Joda-Time, a widely used Java date library, parsed those source files directly and assumed the links lived in 'backward'. It broke immediately. Seven days after 2021b shipped, 2021c reverted almost every moved link. The incident showed that the layout of tzdb source files is effectively a public API, and tools that read raw tzdb text rather than compiled output are fragile to internal restructuring.

title: Fiji suspends then permanently abolishes daylight saving time
body: Government of Fiji (Prime Minister Josaia Voreqe Bainimarama, then Cabinet under the successor government)
whatChanged: On 2021-10-10 PM Bainimarama announced no DST for the 2021/22 season, so clocks did not spring forward on the previously expected date. Fiji skipped DST again in 2022/23. On 2023-10-03 the cabinet approved a permanent suspension and agreed to consider repealing the Daylight Saving Act 1998; the formal repeal was confirmed in January 2024, leaving Pacific/Fiji a fixed UTC+12 zone. The tzdb defines no Fiji DST rules after the 2020/21 season.
technicalImpact: tzdb tracked this across releases: 2021d recorded the seasonal suspension and 2022f assumed DST was suspended indefinitely. Systems that forward-projected Fiji DST from its historical November spring-forward pattern computed wrong future transitions until updated, and any code hardcoding a November clock change for Pacific/Fiji broke.
headline: Fiji's quiet farewell to daylight saving
blurb: Fiji dropped daylight saving in 2021 as a pandemic-era convenience and never brought it back, eventually scrapping the law entirely.
story: In October 2021, on Fiji's national day, Prime Minister Bainimarama folded a time-policy change into a Covid-19 announcement: clocks would not spring forward that season, partly to simplify international flight schedules during the reopening. Fiji skipped DST the following year too, and in October 2023 the cabinet permanently suspended daylight saving and agreed to consider repealing the Daylight Saving Act 1998, with the formal repeal confirmed in January 2024, locking Pacific/Fiji at UTC+12. Software that projected Fiji's future DST transitions from its old November-to-January pattern suddenly held stale data, which is why the change rippled across more than one tzdb release.

title: Fiji suspends DST for 2021/2022 season with four days notice
body: Government of Fiji
whatChanged: Fiji announced suspension of daylight saving time for the 2021/2022 season on 2021-10-10, about five weeks before the scheduled 2021-11-14 start. tzdata 2021d (released 2021-10-15) encoded the suspension, assuming DST would return the following year. A later release (2022f, 2022-10-28) updated the assumption to indefinite suspension after Fiji skipped 2022/2023 as well.
technicalImpact: Forced an out-of-cycle tzdata point release (2021d). Systems that had not applied the update by the DST start date began computing Fiji times an hour off. Any service with cached or compiled tz data (JVM, embedded devices, OS images) required a patch cycle on short notice.
headline: Fiji's last-minute DST cancellation
blurb: Fiji cancelled its upcoming daylight saving time shift weeks before it was due, forcing an out-of-cycle update to the global timezone database.
story: Every year Fiji had been springing clocks forward for summer, but in October 2021 the government called it off with barely four days to spare. The IANA timezone database team rushed out a point release (2021d) to encode the change before the clocks were supposed to move. Software that missed the patch started computing Fiji times an hour wrong. Fiji never brought DST back, and a later database release quietly changed the assumption from 'temporary suspension' to 'suspended indefinitely'.

title: .NET 6 reworks TimeZoneInfo for cross-platform IANA and Windows id interop
body: Microsoft (.NET 6)
whatChanged: .NET 6 let TimeZoneInfo accept both Windows and IANA zone ids on any platform and added conversion between them, ending the long split where Windows used names like 'Pacific Standard Time' and the rest of the world used 'America/Los_Angeles'.
technicalImpact: Code that had hard-coded one naming scheme, or relied on platform-specific lookups, behaves differently after upgrading; the change smooths cross-platform time handling but is a migration hazard for apps that assumed Windows-only ids.

reverify.note: newly added this pass; tooling evolution rather than a time change

headline: One set of zone names for .NET
blurb: .NET 6 taught TimeZoneInfo to speak both Windows and IANA zone names on every platform.
story: Windows and the rest of the computing world long used different names for the same time zones. In 2021 .NET 6 let programs use either naming style anywhere and convert between them. This made cross-platform apps easier to write. Older code that assumed only Windows names had to be checked when upgrading.

title: Microsoft Exchange FIP-FS 'Y2K22' int32 date overflow
body: Microsoft (Exchange Server FIP-FS / scanning engine)
whatChanged: The FIP-FS anti-malware scanning engine version/signature date was stored as a yyMMddHHmm value in a signed 32-bit integer. At 2022-01-01 the value became 2201010001, exceeding the signed int32 max of 2,147,483,647, so the conversion to a long failed.
technicalImpact: On-premises Exchange 2016/2019 mailbox-role servers stopped delivering mail at local midnight on 2022-01-01; messages queued and Event Log error 1106 reported "Can't convert '2201010001' to long". Microsoft shipped an emergency reset script and a permanent build using a larger version number.

validation.supportingQuote: Error Code: 0x80004005. Error Description: Can't convert "2201010001" to long. ... dates in 2022 have a minimum value of 2,201,010,001, which is greater than the maximum value that can be stored in the signed int32 variable.

headline: A date number broke Microsoft email
blurb: On New Year's Day 2022 a date stored as a number grew too big for Microsoft Exchange.
story: Microsoft Exchange stored a date as a single number for its malware scanner. When 2022 began, that number grew larger than the software could hold, so the check failed. On-site Exchange servers stopped delivering mail until Microsoft released a fix.

title: Honda and Acura navigation clocks stick 20 years in the past (GPS epoch issue)
body: Honda / Acura (in-car navigation; GPS time decoding)
whatChanged: From 1 January 2022, clocks in many Honda and Acura vehicles jumped to January 2002 and could not be corrected.
