The most credible analysis attributes it to a signed 32-bit integer overflow in the navigation firmware's YYMMDDhhmm date encoding (2022 dates exceed the int32 maximum of 2,147,483,647), and the manufacturer initially said it would resolve only later in the year.
technicalImpact: A consumer-visible date-arithmetic bug: the firmware stored the date as a YYMMDDhhmm value in a signed 32-bit integer, which overflowed once 2022 dates exceeded 2,147,483,647, rolling the calendar back roughly 20 years to 2002. Owners could not fix it manually.

reverify.source: Wikipedia (GPS week number rollover) + press coverage
reverify.note: newly added this pass; vendor anecdote, secondary sourcing

headline: Cars stuck in 2002
blurb: In 2022 many Honda and Acura dashboards jumped to 2002 and couldn't be reset, an integer-overflow date bug.
story: At the start of 2022 the clocks in many Honda and Acura cars suddenly showed the year 2002. Owners could not set them back by hand. The cause was a mistake in how the car read the date from GPS satellites. It showed that GPS time bugs reach everyday devices, not just specialized gear.

title: CGPM votes to end leap seconds by 2035
body: Bureau International des Poids et Mesures (BIPM) / General Conference on Weights and Measures (CGPM)
whatChanged: Leap seconds were introduced in 1972 to keep UTC aligned with Earth's irregular rotation. In November 2022 the CGPM passed Resolution 4, deciding that the maximum allowed value of the difference UT1-UTC will be increased in, or before, 2035, effectively ending the practice of inserting leap seconds. After that point UTC and atomic time (TAI) will be allowed to drift apart by more than one second.
technicalImpact: POSIX time_t excludes leap seconds by definition, so most systems are already diverged from true UTC. After 2035 the accumulated gap between UTC and TAI (currently 37 seconds total) will grow without correction. Tzdb leapseconds files and leap-second-aware timescales (TAI, GPS) will still be required to resolve timestamps from 1972 onward. Systems depending on UTC accuracy must audit how they handle the transition.
headline: The end of the leap second
blurb: The world's timekeeping authority voted in 2022 to let UTC drift freely from astronomical time, ending leap-second insertions by 2035 at the latest.
story: Since 1972, timekeepers have occasionally added a single second to UTC to keep clocks in step with Earth's slowing rotation. These insertions caused enough trouble in computer systems, from crashes to duplicate timestamps, that the CGPM decided to stop them. By 2035 at the latest, UTC will be allowed to drift freely from astronomical time for the first time. Software will still need to handle the 27 leap seconds inserted since 1972, and any library or system that assumes UTC equals atomic time will eventually break as the gap widens.

title: Palestine DST transitions corrected twice in 2022 (tzdata 2022a and 2022d)
body: Palestinian Authority
whatChanged: tzdata 2022a (released 2022-03-15) corrected Palestine's spring-forward from 2022-03-26 to 2022-03-27 and revised future fall-back predictions to the first Friday on or after October 23. tzdata 2022d (released 2022-09-23) further revised Palestine's transitions to occur on Saturdays at 02:00, moving the 2022 fall-back from 2022-10-28 at 01:00 to 2022-10-29 at 02:00.
technicalImpact: Any application or OS that cached tz data before 2022a or 2022d showed incorrect Palestine local time around both transitions. Systems requiring two rapid tzdata updates in a single year to stay correct; Asia/Gaza and Asia/Hebron both affected.
headline: Palestine's double correction year
blurb: Palestine's DST dates were wrong twice in 2022, forcing back-to-back tzdata releases to fix both the spring-forward and the fall-back.
story: Palestine sets its DST dates close to Ramadan, which shifts each year, making advance predictions hard. In March 2022, tzdata had to push a correction just days before the spring-forward after the actual date changed. By September, a second correction changed the day-of-week and time-of-day for all future transitions to Saturdays at 02:00. Two emergency updates in one calendar year is unusual, and any software that did not apply both patches showed the wrong clock in Palestinian territories at two separate points in 2022. It is a reminder that politically linked DST schedules can change faster than release cycles allow.

title: tzdb backward file workaround for link-to-link parser bug (tzcode through 2022e)
body: IANA tz database maintainers (Paul Eggert et al.)
whatChanged: tzcode through 2022e and several .zi parsers cannot follow a Link whose target is itself another Link. To work around this, every entry in the backward file that would naturally chain through an intermediate Link instead points directly to the ultimate Zone. A '#= TARGET1' comment on each affected line records what the target would be if parsers handled link chains correctly. Examples: Australia/ACT links to Australia/Sydney (not Australia/Canberra), America/Virgin links to America/Puerto_Rico (not America/St_Thomas), Africa/Asmera links to Africa/Nairobi (not Africa/Asmara).
technicalImpact: Custom tzdb parsers, .zi file readers, and any tool that traverses Link entries must handle flattened link chains in the backward file rather than natural chaining. Tools built on tzcode 2022e or older silently get wrong intermediate targets. tzcode 2022f (released 2022-10-28) fixed this: zic now supports links to links and vanguard form uses them, but the backward file keeps the flattened layout for compatibility with older parsers, so code relying on that flattened structure may need updating when consuming newer releases that use vanguard form.
headline: The link that could not point to a link
blurb: A long-standing parser bug forced the tzdb backward file to flatten all link chains, hiding the true alias relationships behind a workaround comment.
story: In tzdb, a Link entry is an alias: it says one timezone name is equivalent to another. But some parsers, including the reference tzcode through 2022e, mishandle a Link that points to another Link instead of a real Zone. To keep things working, maintainers wired every backward-compatibility alias directly to the ultimate Zone and left a '#= TARGET1' comment showing what the natural chain would be. The workaround has been in place quietly for years. Any code that reads .zi files and tries to follow Link chains will encounter this flattened structure rather than the logical one.

title: tzdb backward file is optional: omitting it breaks legacy timezone names
body: IANA tz database maintainers (Paul Eggert et al.)
whatChanged: The tzdb backward file, which holds all Link entries mapping legacy and merged zone names (such as US/Pacific, Brazil/East, Europe/Kiev) to current canonical names, is build-time optional. Building with 'make BACKWARD=' drops it entirely, causing any lookup of a non-canonical name to return an unknown timezone error instead of silently redirecting.
technicalImpact: Although tzdb distributions today typically include the backward file, embedded and stripped-down builds (some IoT firmware and minimal containers) can still ship without it by building with 'make BACKWARD='. On such a build, any application using old timezone names like US/Pacific or Europe/Kiev fails with an unknown-timezone error rather than falling back gracefully. Developers targeting minimal platforms should validate timezone names against the actual tzdb build on the target, not just a full desktop install.
headline: The optional file that breaks everything
blurb: Leaving out a single optional tzdb file silently removes all legacy timezone name support, turning quiet redirects into hard failures.
story: The tzdb backward file holds hundreds of Link entries that map old or renamed timezone identifiers to their current canonical equivalents. It is optional at build time, meaning any build that passes 'make BACKWARD=' to the compiler simply omits it. On a stripped-down system, code asking for US/Pacific or Europe/Kiev gets an unknown timezone error with no fallback. This trips up IoT firmware and minimal containers where disk space pressure tempts builders to drop the file.

title: Sunshine Protection Act passes US Senate (permanent DST proposal)
body: US Senate (117th Congress), S.623, sponsored by Sen. Marco Rubio
whatChanged: The Senate passed S.623 by unanimous consent on 2022-03-15, which would make daylight saving time the permanent, year-round standard time across the US (states already exempt would stay on standard time), eliminating the biannual clock change. The bill stalled in the House and was never enacted; reintroduced in subsequent Congresses.
technicalImpact: Not encoded in any tz release because it never became law, the engineering lesson is the danger of premature timezone changes: had it passed near its effective date it would have required removing all US DST transitions, echoing the short-lead-time scramble of 2007. A standing reminder to track legislative status, not assume DST rules are stable.

validation.supportingQuote: "The Sunshine Protection Act S.623 passed the Senate with an amendment by Unanimous Consent on March 15, 2022 ... is 'An Act to make daylight saving time permanent' and was sponsored by Marco Rubio in the 117th Congress ... while the bill passed the Senate on March 15, 2022, it was never passed by the House." (per congress.gov actions / GovTrack)

headline: US Senate voted for permanent daylight time
blurb: The US Senate passed a bill to make daylight saving time year-round, but it stalled.
story: In 2022 the US Senate passed a bill to make daylight saving time permanent across the country and end the twice-a-year clock change. The bill stalled in the House and never became law. It has been brought back in later sessions of Congress.

title: Meta calls to retire the leap second (industry coalition with Google, Microsoft, Amazon, NIST, BIPM)
body: Meta (Facebook) Engineering, with stated support from Google, Microsoft, Amazon, US NIST and BIPM
whatChanged: Meta published 'It's time to leave the leap second in the past', publicly advocating to stop introducing new leap seconds and standardize on continuous time, coalescing major cloud/tech vendors behind UTC reform months before the CGPM 2022 vote.
technicalImpact: Documents the operational case: leap seconds are rare, poorly tested events that 'devastate the community every time'. Promotes smearing as the de-facto mitigation and frames the negative leap second as an unacceptable, untested risk, directly motivating vendor smear standards and the push for abolition.

validation.supportingQuote: we're supporting an industry effort to stop future introductions of leap seconds and stay at a current level of 27

headline: Tech giants called to drop leap seconds
blurb: Meta and other major companies urged the world to stop adding leap seconds.
story: In 2022 Meta published a call to stop adding leap seconds to the world clock, backed by other large technology companies and standards bodies. They argued that these rare changes are hard to test and cause repeated trouble. They pointed to smoothing the change over time as a better approach and warned against ever removing a second.

academicRefs.authors: Levine, J., Tavella, P. and Milton, M.
academicRefs.title: Towards a consensus on a continuous coordinated universal time
academicRefs.venue: Metrologia (IOP Publishing / BIPM)
academicRefs.keyFinding: Argues UTC's leap-second discontinuities break digital systems (cites the 2012 leap second crashing Reddit, Foursquare and other Linux hosts) and that leap smearing is an inconsistent mitigation: vendors smear at different rates/times, losing exact UTC alignment and causing sync chaos in mixed environments. Flags the untested negative leap second as potentially catastrophic and proposes a framework for a continuous, monotonic UTC by greatly increasing the UTC-UT1 tolerance.

academicRefs.authors: Agnew, D. C.
academicRefs.title: A global timekeeping problem postponed by global warming
academicRefs.venue: Nature
academicRefs.keyFinding: Using satellite-gravity measurements of Greenland and Antarctic ice melt, shows polar ice loss has slowed Earth's angular velocity, delaying the first-ever negative leap second from ~2026 to ~2029. UTC as currently defined will require a negative discontinuity by about 2029, which complicates computer-network timekeeping because no negative leap second has ever been applied or tested.

title: IANA renames Europe/Kiev to Europe/Kyiv
body: IANA tz database (Paul Eggert)
whatChanged: tzdb release 2022b renamed the primary Ukrainian zone from Europe/Kiev to Europe/Kyiv, matching the spelling Ukraine and most of the world now use, and turned the old name into a backward-compatibility link.
technicalImpact: Code or config that hard-coded 'Europe/Kiev' keeps working through the compatibility link, but display logic that shows the canonical zone id, and any allowlist or validation of zone names, had to learn the new spelling. A reminder that tz ids are identifiers, not guaranteed-stable labels.

reverify.note: newly added this pass; primary announcement, editorial prose new

headline: Kiev becomes Kyiv in the time database
blurb: The world's time database renamed Ukraine's main zone to Europe/Kyiv, keeping the old name as an alias.
story: The time database had long used the spelling Europe/Kiev for Ukraine's main zone. In 2022 it switched to Europe/Kyiv to match how Ukraine spells its capital. The old name still works so existing settings do not break. Programs that show the official zone name had to update to the new spelling.

title: Iceland timezone alias resolves to Africa/Abidjan, not Atlantic/Reykjavik
body: IANA tz database maintainers
whatChanged: In tzdb 2022b, Atlantic/Reykjavik was retired as a primary zone and moved to backzone, because Iceland's timestamps since 1970 are identical to Africa/Abidjan (UTC+0, no DST). Both the legacy bare name 'Iceland' and 'Atlantic/Reykjavik' now link to Africa/Abidjan. The backward file documents this: Link Africa/Abidjan Iceland #= Atlantic/Reykjavik.
technicalImpact: Code or timezone pickers that resolve the legacy name 'Iceland' will receive the canonical zone 'Africa/Abidjan', not 'Atlantic/Reykjavik'. Audit logs, display names, and CLDR lookups may show an African zone for Icelandic timestamps. Applications that hardcode 'Atlantic/Reykjavik' as the expected canonical still work but now point to a backzone entry.
headline: Iceland's clock, filed under Abidjan
blurb: The legacy timezone name 'Iceland' now resolves to an African timezone, because Iceland and Ivory Coast have kept identical clocks since 1970.
story: Iceland's clocks last sprang for daylight saving on October 29, 1967, and the country has sat at UTC+0 ever since, exactly matching Ivory Coast's Africa/Abidjan zone. The tzdb maintainers noticed the two zones were byte-for-byte identical from 1970 onward, so in 2022 they retired Atlantic/Reykjavik as a primary zone and made it a link to Africa/Abidjan. The old backward-compatibility name 'Iceland' now chains through to that same African entry. Software that looks up 'Iceland' and expects to see 'Atlantic/Reykjavik' as the canonical name will instead get 'Africa/Abidjan', which can produce confusing labels in timezone pickers and audit trails.

title: Atlantic/Reykjavik demoted to a link of Africa/Abidjan
body: IANA tz database maintainers
whatChanged: Atlantic/Reykjavik was moved to the 'backzone' file and replaced with a backward-compatibility link whose canonical target is Africa/Abidjan. Iceland shifted from UTC-1 to UTC+0 year-round and abolished DST on 7 April 1968, and Cote d'Ivoire shares the same post-1970 data. The change shipped in tzdb release 2022b (2022-08-10) as part of a multi-year consolidation of zones that are duplicates since 1970.
technicalImpact: Systems using ICU, java.time, or other libraries that resolve the canonical zone name rather than the link will display 'Africa/Abidjan' for Icelandic users. Looking up 'Atlantic/Reykjavik' still works through the backward-compatibility link, but the canonical identifier is now an African city. Any tooling that renders zone names to users needs to map the link name back to a human-friendly label.
headline: Iceland's timezone became an African city
blurb: Iceland's timezone identifier was merged into Africa/Abidjan because the two have been identical since 1968.
story: Iceland was at UTC-1 until 7 April 1968, when it permanently shifted to UTC+0 and abolished daylight saving at the same time, settling on the same offset Cote d'Ivoire uses year-round. The tzdb maintainers spent years consolidating zones that carry identical data after 1970, and in 2022 they moved Atlantic/Reykjavik into the backzone file, leaving Africa/Abidjan as the canonical representative. The backward-compatibility link means old code keeps working, but any software that renders the canonical name shows an Ivorian city to anyone in Reykjavik.

title: Chile delays 2022 DST start by one week for constitutional referendum
body: Government of Chile (presidential decree, 2022-08-13)
whatChanged: Chile postponed its 2022 spring DST start from September 4 to September 11. The government issued a decree on August 13, 2022 to avoid a clock change on the same day as the national constitutional referendum. The tzdb 2022 Chile rule changed from Sun>=2 to Sun>=9 for that year only, shortening the 2022-23 DST period by one week.
technicalImpact: Required tzdata 2022b (released 2022-08-10), announced less than four weeks before the transition. Systems running tzdata 2022a or older showed Chile clocks one hour behind reality from September 4 to 11. Microsoft issued a separate advisory warning Windows users of bugs caused by the late update.
headline: The referendum that moved Chile's clock
blurb: Chile pushed its clocks-forward day back by a week so the change would not fall on a national vote.
story: In August 2022, Chile announced it was delaying its spring DST start from September 4 to September 11 because citizens were voting on a new constitution that day. The decree came less than four weeks before the original transition, leaving little time for systems to update. Microsoft issued a specific advisory warning that Windows machines still on the old tzdata would show Chilean times incorrectly for that week. Any server or app that did not apply tzdata 2022b in time would have been an hour off for Chilean users during the gap.

title: Iran permanently abolishes daylight saving time
body: Islamic Consultative Assembly (Majlis) and Guardian Council of Iran; encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: Iran's parliament approved a bill abolishing DST on 15 Mar 2022; promulgated by President Raisi on 22 May 2022. Iran observed its final fall-back on 21 Sep 2022 and stays on permanent standard time UTC+3:30 (IRST) year-round, ending decades of one-hour seasonal shifts to/from UTC+4:30 (IRDT).
technicalImpact: tz 2022b removed all future DST transitions for Asia/Tehran. Unpatched systems wrongly apply DST each March/September, putting Iranian time 60 minutes off for months. Combined with Iran's unusual +3:30 base offset, a frequent source of scheduling errors.

validation.supportingQuote: However, on 21 September 2022, Iran abolished daylight saving time effective immediately and now observes standard time year-round. The plan was approved by the Parliament on 15 March 2022.
validation.corrections: tzRelease should be "2022b" (released 2022-08-10), not "2022e". Correspondingly, technicalImpact should read "tz 2022b removed all future DST transitions for Asia/Tehran" instead of "tz 2022e".

headline: Iran ended daylight saving time
blurb: Iran stopped its twice-a-year clock changes and settled on one time.
story: Iran's parliament approved ending daylight saving time in 2022, and the country made its final clock change that September. Iran now stays on one time all year, ending decades of seasonal one-hour shifts. Systems that were not updated keep making the old changes and show Iranian time an hour off for months at a time.

title: Iran permanently abolishes daylight saving time after 2022
body: Government of Iran
whatChanged: Iran stopped observing DST permanently after the final fallback on 2022-09-21. The country now stays on Iran Standard Time (IRST, UTC+3:30) year-round, with no spring-forward or fall-back transitions. The tzdb entry also corrected historical errors: Iran adopted standard time in 1935 (not 1946), and the 1977-1979 DST transition dates were wrong.
technicalImpact: Any application caching Iran's DST rules needed an update via tzdb 2022b or later. Iran's non-Gregorian (Solar Hijri) calendar interaction with DST rules had previously limited automated predictions to 2038; those predictions became moot after the abolition. Systems using stale tz data show Tehran an hour ahead of reality during the months that used to be summer DST.
headline: Iran's last clock change
blurb: Iran permanently ended daylight saving time in September 2022, settling on a fixed UTC+3:30 all year.
story: For decades Iran shifted its clocks each spring and autumn, but the government decided the disruption was not worth it. After the final fallback on 21 September 2022, the clocks stopped moving. Iran now sits at a fixed UTC+3:30 (IRST) all year round. Software that had not updated to tzdb 2022b or later would silently show Tehran one hour wrong during what used to be summer DST months.

title: Fiji permanently suspends DST after 2022/2023 season skipped
body: Government of Fiji
whatChanged: Fiji did not observe DST for the 2022/2023 season and the suspension was treated as indefinite. The tzdb rule set was updated in tzdata 2022f to remove future DST transitions for Pacific/Fiji, leaving the zone at UTC+12 year-round.
technicalImpact: Systems relying on pre-2022f tzdata would have scheduled a DST clock change in October/November 2022 that never happened, causing a 1-hour offset error for Pacific/Fiji until updated. Requires tzdata 2022f or later.
headline: Fiji drops the clock change for good
blurb: Fiji skipped its 2022/2023 daylight saving season and declared the suspension indefinite, leaving the islands on UTC+12 all year.
story: Fiji had observed daylight saving time each October since 1998, shifting clocks forward by one hour into the southern summer. In 2021 the government paused DST to ease aviation scheduling for the reopening of commercial air service. Then in late October 2022 officials confirmed there would be no DST for 2022/2023 either, and the tzdb maintainers treated the policy as an indefinite end. Any software using tzdata older than 2022f would have expected Fiji to spring forward in late 2022 and would have been wrong by one hour for the entire season.

title: Jordan abolishes seasonal clock changes, fixing Asia/Amman at UTC+3 year-round
body: Jordan Council of Ministers
whatChanged: On 5 October 2022, Jordan's Cabinet decided clocks would not fall back at the end of October 2022. Asia/Amman stayed at UTC+3 (summer time) permanently, ending decades of annual DST transitions between UTC+2 and UTC+3.
technicalImpact: Asia/Amman changed from a DST zone (+02/+03) to a fixed +03 offset. tzdb 2022e (released 2022-10-11) captured the change before it took effect on 2022-10-28. Any system or app that did not apply 2022e in time showed Jordan one hour behind for the winter period.
headline: Jordan's permanent summer time
blurb: Jordan stopped turning its clocks back in October 2022, locking the country at UTC+3 all year.
story: Every autumn Jordan had wound its clocks back an hour, as most of Europe and the Middle East do. In early October 2022 the government decided that would not happen again. The last scheduled fall-back, on 28 October 2022, simply did not occur. Software that had not picked up tzdb 2022e before that date showed Jordan an hour behind its actual time all winter.

title: Jordan and Syria permanently adopt year-round DST (UTC+3)
body: Jordan Council of Ministers (decision 2022-10-05); Syrian Cabinet (decision 2022-10-05)
whatChanged: Both Jordan (Asia/Amman) and Syria (Asia/Damascus) stopped observing seasonal clock changes and remain permanently at UTC+3. The transition took effect on 2022-10-28 at the moment clocks would have otherwise fallen back from EEST (UTC+3 DST) to EET (UTC+2). The tzdb models both zones as plain +03 from that point forward, with no ongoing DST offset.
technicalImpact: Any system still applying pre-2022 Jordan or Syria DST rules will calculate winter times one hour ahead of reality. The tzdb change is a zone redefinition, not a rule tweak: Asia/Amman and Asia/Damascus moved from EE%sT with seasonal rules to a fixed %z at +03. Software relying on stale tz data packages will produce wrong UTC offsets for all dates after 2022-10-28.
headline: Two countries stop the clock-swap forever
blurb: Jordan and Syria both decided in October 2022 to stay on summer time all year, eliminating their annual clock change.
story: Every autumn Jordan and Syria had set clocks back an hour, returning to standard time for the winter. In October 2022, both governments announced the same week they would keep clocks forward permanently. Because the switch happened at the exact moment clocks would have fallen back, there was no visible gap for residents. For software, the change is subtle but serious: systems running old timezone data still apply a winter offset that no longer exists, silently placing Jordan and Syria one hour behind where they actually are.

title: Jordan and Syria abolish DST and adopt permanent UTC+3
body: Government of Jordan; Government of Syria
whatChanged: Jordan (announced October 5, 2022) and Syria (announced October 5, 2022) both cancelled the DST fall-back that had been scheduled for October 28, 2022, moving from a +02/+03 seasonal regime to permanent UTC+3 year-round. IANA tzdata 2022e (released October 11, 2022) encoded the change ahead of the effective date.
technicalImpact: Systems running tzdata older than 2022e continued to schedule a fall-back to +02 on October 28 that never happened, producing a one-hour error for Jordan and Syria timestamps from that date onward. Microsoft published interim guidance for Windows users. Any application caching the previous EET/EEST rules needed a tz database update to reflect the permanent +03 offset.
headline: The fall-back that never came
blurb: Jordan and Syria quietly abolished seasonal clock changes in October 2022, locking both countries onto UTC+3 year-round with only days of notice.
story: Every autumn Jordan and Syria had turned their clocks back one hour as summer ended. In early October 2022, both governments announced they would skip that change permanently. On October 28, when clocks across the region were supposed to fall back, they stayed put. The IANA time zone database had to rush out a corrective release, tzdata 2022e, just days after the announcements. Any device or server that had not applied the update kept scheduling a clock change that would never happen, putting Jordan and Syria timestamps one hour off for the rest of the year.

title: Mexico abolishes DST nationally, border zones excepted
body: Mexican federal government (law published in Diario Oficial de la Federacion, 2022-10-28)
whatChanged: Mexico abolished DST for almost the entire country effective 2022-10-30. Border municipalities that align with the US (America/Matamoros, America/Ciudad_Juarez, America/Ojinaga, etc.) retained US DST rules, while inland Chihuahua and the rest of the country settled on fixed offsets. In tzdb 2022g the northern border strip of Chihuahua was split into America/Ciudad_Juarez (-07/-06, like El Paso) from America/Ojinaga, both following US DST. Rule Mexico, the tzdb DST rule that ran 2002-2022, ended in 2022. Major interior zones (America/Mexico_City, America/Monterrey, America/Merida) now use fixed UTC offsets.
technicalImpact: Any system or tz database older than 2022f will apply phantom DST shifts to Mexican zones after October 2022. Border zones still follow US rules, creating an asymmetry within the country that can surprise code treating all Mexico zones the same. Chihuahua's mid-year split between US and fixed rules required careful handling in tzdb releases 2022f and 2022g.
headline: Mexico drops the clock change
blurb: Mexico stopped observing daylight saving time across almost the whole country in October 2022, leaving only US-border towns still changing their clocks.
story: For decades Mexico ran its clocks forward each spring and back each autumn, in step with a pattern set in 1996. In October 2022 the federal government passed a law ending that practice for the vast majority of the country. Border towns kept changing clocks to stay in sync with the US cities they trade and commute with, while the rest of Mexico settled on a fixed offset. Software relying on pre-2022f tz data will add a phantom hour to Mexican timestamps after the cut-off date, a silent error that hits payroll, scheduling, and log correlation.

title: Syria abolishes DST and locks Asia/Damascus to permanent UTC+3
body: Syrian Arab Republic government
whatChanged: Syria stopped observing daylight saving time. Instead of falling back from UTC+3 to UTC+2 on 2022-10-28 as usual, clocks stayed permanently at UTC+3 (Arabia Standard Time). Asia/Damascus was updated to reflect a year-round +03 offset with no DST transitions. Some northern territories not under central-government control had already been on EEST year-round, complicating modeling.
technicalImpact: tzdata 2022e (released 2022-10-11) carried the change, merging Asia/Damascus to year-round UTC+3. Systems that had not applied 2022e before 2022-10-28 showed Syria falling back an hour that never happened. The fragmented territorial situation also caused ambiguity for tz maintainers about which rules applied to which regions.
headline: Syria's clock that never fell back
blurb: Syria kept its summer hours permanently in 2022, ending the biannual clock change for good.
story: Every October, Syria used to set clocks back an hour from UTC+3 to UTC+2. In 2022 the government decided the country would simply stay on UTC+3 all year, skipping the October switch entirely. The decision was announced about three weeks before the scheduled changeback, giving software vendors little time to react. Systems without the tzdata 2022e update showed Syrian clocks jumping backward an hour that never came.

title: Mexico abolishes nationwide daylight saving time
body: Mexican Congress (Senate approved 2022-10-26); signed into the Ley de los Husos Horarios by President Lopez Obrador on 2022-10-28 in La Paz
whatChanged: Most of Mexico ended DST, with the last clock change on 2022-10-30. Exceptions kept on a US-aligned DST schedule: all of Baja California and northern border municipalities in Chihuahua, Coahuila, Nuevo Leon, and Tamaulipas, to preserve alignment with adjacent US zones.
technicalImpact: Encoded in tz 2022f (2022-10-28), days before the effective date, a textbook short-lead-time emergency update. America/Mexico_City, /Monterrey, /Mazatlan etc. stopped observing DST; Chihuahua shifted to year-round -06. Systems not on 2022f computed Mexican time an hour wrong from 2022-10-30, and the border carve-outs broke single-zone assumptions.

validation.supportingQuote: Mexico will no longer observe DST after 2022, except for areas near the US border that continue to observe US DST rules. On 2022-10-30 at 02:00 the Mexican state of Chihuahua moves from -07 (-06 with DST) to year-round -06, thus not changing its clocks that day.
validation.corrections: Optional precision: the tz 2022f release is dated 2022-10-29 in the icann.org tz-announce header (event body says 2022-10-28); IANA NEWS uses -0700 timestamps so this is a one-day rounding nuance, not an error in the core event.

headline: Mexico stops changing its clocks
blurb: Most of Mexico made its last seasonal clock change and dropped daylight saving time.
story: In October 2022, most of Mexico ended daylight saving time after one final clock change. A few areas near the United States border kept changing their clocks to stay in step with their neighbors. The rule lists were updated just days before the change took effect, so any computer that missed the update showed the wrong time.

title: Mexico abolishes DST nationwide, splits Chihuahua border strip into new zone
body: Government of Mexico (Congress of the Union)
whatChanged: Mexico stopped observing DST after 2022, except for border areas that follow US DST rules. On 2022-10-30, Chihuahua state moved to year-round UTC-6, skipping the clocks-back that day. A correction in tzdb 2022g clarified that the northern Chihuahua border strip (Ciudad Juarez) instead switched to US DST rules on 2022-11-30. A new zone, America/Ciudad_Juarez, was split from America/Ojinaga to represent Ciudad Juarez under US DST (-07/-06), while most of Mexico moved to fixed offsets.
technicalImpact: Applications that assumed all of Mexico tracked US DST are now broken for most of the country. The new America/Ciudad_Juarez zone must be present in any zone database serving Mexican border users. Two tzdb releases (2022f and 2022g) were needed to capture the full change, meaning systems on 2022f had incorrect data for the Chihuahua border strip until they updated to 2022g.
headline: Mexico turns off the clock change
blurb: Mexico abolished daylight saving time for most of the country, leaving the entire state of Baja California and border municipalities in four northern states still changing clocks to stay in sync with the US.
story: For decades Mexico switched its clocks twice a year in step with the US. In 2022 the Mexican Congress voted to end that practice for nearly the whole country, leaving only the border towns that depend on being in sync with their US neighbors. The change needed two separate tzdb releases to get right: 2022f captured the main abolition, and 2022g split the Chihuahua border strip into its own zone and defined that it adopts US DST rules. Any software with hardcoded assumptions about Mexico following US DST broke quietly for most Mexican users overnight.

title: Amazon Time Sync goes public over the internet with 24-hour leap smear
body: Amazon Web Services (AWS)
whatChanged: AWS made Amazon Time Sync available as a public NTP service at time.aws.com, backed by satellite-connected atomic reference clocks, and documented that leap seconds are absorbed via a 24-hour linear smear from noon to noon UTC (the same noon-to-noon smear Google and others use).
technicalImpact: Extends smeared, leap-second-free time to IoT and on-prem clients beyond AWS, further entrenching smearing as the production answer to leap seconds. Mixing AWS/Google smeared sources with non-smeared (leaping) NTP risks sub-second clock disagreement during a leap event.

validation.supportingQuote: In the event of a leap second, the Amazon Time Sync service automatically handles this for you by smoothing out the addition, or removal, of the leap second with a 24-hour linear smear from noon to noon UTC.
validation.corrections: date: 2022-11-10 (cited aws.amazon.com What's New page posted Nov 10, 2022; not 2022-11-21)

headline: Amazon opens its clock service to everyone
blurb: Amazon let anyone use its time service, which spreads out extra seconds slowly.
story: In November 2022, Amazon made its time service available to anyone on the internet, not just its own customers. The service is backed by very accurate reference clocks. When an extra second needs to be added, it spreads that second out gradually over a full day instead of adding it all at once.

title: CGPM Resolution 4 (2022): phase out leap seconds by or before 2035
body: 27th General Conference on Weights and Measures (CGPM), Versailles
whatChanged: CGPM adopted Resolution 4 'On the use and future development of UTC', deciding that the maximum allowed value of |UT1-UTC| will be increased in or before 2035 (from the current ~0.9s), directing CIPM (with ITU) to propose a new tolerance ensuring UTC continuity for at least a century. In effect this ends the regular insertion of leap seconds. The US and France led; Russia voted against citing GLONASS impact.
technicalImpact: After 2035 no new leap seconds will be added for decades, removing the recurring 23:59:60 discontinuity that has caused outages. TAI-UTC will eventually become a fixed constant, leap-seconds.list will stop growing, and 'right'/POSIX zoneinfo divergence freezes. The resolution cites risks to GNSS, telecom, and energy infrastructure and flags the untested negative-leap-second case. Software hardcoding the 0.9s tolerance or expecting future leap seconds must be updated.

validation.supportingQuote: "On the use and future development of UTC", "the maximum value for the difference (UT1-UTC) will be increased in, or before, 2035" with CIPM directed to consult ITU and propose a new value ensuring "continuity of UTC for at least a century."

headline: World agrees to phase out leap seconds
blurb: An international body voted to stop adding occasional extra seconds by 2035.
story: In 2022, an international group that oversees measurement agreed to let clock time drift a little further from the sun's time. This decision effectively ends the practice of adding occasional extra seconds, with the change due by 2035. The group asked others to work out the exact details. Most members agreed, though Russia voted against it.

academicRefs.authors: Levine, J., Tavella, P. and Milton, M.
academicRefs.title: Towards a consensus on a continuous coordinated universal time
academicRefs.venue: Metrologia (IOP Publishing)
academicRefs.keyFinding: Authored by the BIPM Director (Milton), the BIPM Time Department head (Tavella) and NIST's Levine, the paper sets out the official metrological case behind CGPM Resolution 4. It explains that the 1972 leap-second procedure was adopted to keep UTC within ~0.9 s of UT1, but that the resulting discontinuities (the 23:59:60 step) break modern applications needing a continuous, monotonic time scale; it lays out the advantages/disadvantages and 'the general outline of a way forward' addressing the deficiencies of the leap second while preserving UTC as the international reference scale.

academicRefs.authors: De Angelis, A. and Moretti, B. G.
academicRefs.title: Economic and Operational Implications of the Leap Second and its Cancellation
academicRefs.venue: Ecological Economics and Management (EEM)
academicRefs.keyFinding: Estimates that a single leap second plausibly exposes the global economy to between the low tens of millions and about USD 100 million in direct disruptions, with risk concentrated in finance, aviation and internet platforms; cites documented incidents (2012 Qantas Linux leap-second bug, 2015 CME Group / LSE trading advisories and sporadic internet outages). Concludes that the CGPM 2022 plan to eliminate leap seconds by 2035 reduces coordination burden and removes a rare but brittle failure mode, especially given the unprecedented risk of a first-ever negative leap second.

title: Three tzdb releases in six days: Lebanon's DST reversal triggers 2023a/b/c churn
body: IANA (Paul Eggert, maintainer) responding to government policy changes in Lebanon, Egypt, Morocco, and Palestine
whatChanged: IANA shipped three tz releases within six days. 2023a (2023-03-22): Egypt resumes DST from April through October; Morocco springs forward April 23 not April 30; Palestine delays its spring-forward to April 29. 2023b (2023-03-23): Lebanon announced it would postpone its spring-forward from March 25/26 to April 20/21. 2023c (2023-03-28): Lebanon's policy collapsed, and 2023c reverted Lebanon's data back to 2023a modeling. The churn forced emergency patches across Linux distros, Microsoft, IBM AIX, Oracle, and other vendors.
technicalImpact: Three tzdata updates in six days forced emergency OS and runtime patches globally. Systems that applied 2023b but not 2023c showed Lebanon clocks one hour wrong during the confusion window. Vendors shipping tzdata bundles (Java, ICU, Windows, AIX) had to push back-to-back hotfixes, exposing the lag between upstream releases and downstream distribution.
headline: Three releases in six days
blurb: Lebanon's last-minute reversal of its DST decision forced IANA to ship three timezone database releases in under a week.
story: In late March 2023, Lebanon's government announced it would delay its clocks-forward, prompting IANA to rush out tzdb 2023b the very next day after 2023a. Within days the Lebanese policy collapsed and the original schedule was back on, so IANA issued 2023c to revert 2023b. Any system that picked up 2023b but not 2023c displayed Lebanese time an hour off. The episode exposed how fragile software stacks are when governments change DST policy at short notice and vendors must race to catch up.

title: Palestine delays 2023 DST start by five weeks for Ramadan
body: Palestinian Authority (Council of Ministers)
whatChanged: Palestine (Asia/Gaza and Asia/Hebron) postponed the 2023 spring-forward from March 25 to April 29, announced on short notice on 2023-03-22, to avoid advancing clocks during Ramadan. IANA shipped tzdata2023a the same day to capture the change.
technicalImpact: Devices and systems using pre-2023a tz data flipped clocks on March 25 as originally scheduled, showing the wrong time for over a month. Vendors shipped tzdata2023a updates to correct the change: Oracle Java picked it up via JDK-8304763, and Microsoft addressed Palestinian Authority DST through its regular monthly Windows update channel. Systems that did not apply tzdata2023a (or later) in time showed Palestine one hour ahead of the correct local time.
headline: The Ramadan clock freeze
blurb: Palestine shifted its clocks five weeks later than planned in 2023 to keep Ramadan observance free from a time change.
story: Every year Palestine advances its clocks by one hour in spring to observe daylight saving time. In 2023 the Palestinian Authority decided on short notice to delay that shift from March 25 to April 29 so the change would not fall inside Ramadan. IANA captured the change in tzdata2023a, released on March 22, but many devices had already been programmed with the old date and flipped an hour early. Software vendors shipped updated tz data to stop phones and computers from showing the wrong time for over a month.

title: Lebanon's last-minute DST delay triggers tzdb 2023b then 2023c reversal within 5 days
body: Lebanese caretaker government (Prime Minister Najib Mikati, acting on a request from Parliament Speaker Nabih Berri)
whatChanged: tzdb 2023b (released 2023-03-23) updated Lebanon's spring-forward from March 25/26 to April 20/21, based on a government announcement to delay DST past Ramadan. Five days later, tzdb 2023c (released 2023-03-28) reverted Lebanon's data entirely back to 2023a, describing the situation as DST chaos. Lebanon ultimately applied DST from midnight on March 30, 2023.
technicalImpact: Systems that auto-updated to tzdb 2023b and then 2023c within a 5-day window could have applied contradictory offsets. Any cache of tzdata releases that did not track the rapid reversion would produce wrong local times for Lebanon between March 25 and March 30. The episode is a leading example of why aggressive tzdata caching and lazy update strategies are hazardous.
headline: The week Lebanon had two clocks
blurb: Lebanon's government changed its mind about daylight saving time at the last minute in 2023, forcing the timezone database to issue two contradictory updates within five days.
story: In March 2023, Lebanon's caretaker government announced just days before the scheduled spring-forward that clocks would not change until after Ramadan ended. Half the country followed the new date, half kept the old one, and airlines, hospitals, and banks sharing systems with institutions abroad were caught in the middle. The timezone database published a corrective release within five days, reverting Lebanon's data and labelling the situation simply as DST chaos. The government then reversed itself again, applying DST on March 30. For software, the episode is a warning: two official tzdb releases inside one week carried contradictory rules for the same zone, so any app that cached the first release without quickly picking up the second would have shown the wrong time.

title: Lebanon splits into two simultaneous time zones over a last-minute DST delay
body: Government of Lebanon (caretaker PM Najib Mikati); encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: On 23 Mar 2023, two days before the scheduled spring-forward, caretaker PM Mikati decreed DST be postponed from 25/26 Mar to 20/21 Apr. Christian institutions and broadcasters rejected the decree and moved clocks forward anyway, so from 26 Mar the country ran on two parallel times an hour apart ('Muslim time' UTC+2 and 'Christian time' UTC+3). The government reversed itself, making DST effective from midnight 29/30 Mar.
technicalImpact: A real-world demonstration of timezone fragmentation. IANA shipped 2023b (23 Mar) encoding the Apr delay; on 28 Mar shipped 2023c which modeled the chaos by reverting to tzdb 2023a data. Phones, banks, airlines and hospital systems disagreed on the hour; carriers told users to disable NTP and set clocks manually, breaking calendars. Downstream Linux distros lagged a week behind tz releases, making manual intervention unavoidable.

validation.supportingQuote: 2023b: "This year Lebanon springs forward April 20/21 not March 25/26. (Thanks to Saadallah Itani.) [This was reverted in 2023c.]" | 2023c (2023-03-28): "Model Lebanon's DST chaos by reverting data to tzdb 2023a. (Thanks to Rany Hany for the heads-up.)"

headline: Lebanon ran on two times at once
blurb: A last-minute clock decision split Lebanon into two times an hour apart.
story: In March 2023, just days before clocks were due to move forward, the government delayed the change by several weeks. Many institutions rejected the decision and moved their clocks forward anyway. For a few days the country ran on two times that were an hour apart. The government soon reversed itself and let the clock change happen.

title: Greenland (America/Nuuk) re-dates its 2023 DST change
body: Government of Greenland; encoded by IANA
whatChanged: 2022g encoded America/Nuuk as permanently abolishing DST (a fixed -02 zone after March 2023). 2023a corrected this: Greenland continues to observe DST using EU rules, so America/Nuuk changes from -03/-02 to -02/-01 effective 2023-10-29, with seasonal DST resuming from 2024-03-30. Per IANA, the change does not affect timestamps until 2024-03-30 and does not affect tm_isdst until 2023-03-25, so systems pinned to 2022g mismodel 2024-onward timestamps.
technicalImpact: A case where the tz data itself changed shortly before the event, so systems that updated early and then did not refresh held the wrong rule. Late re-dating of an announced change is a classic way for caches and pinned tz versions to go wrong.

reverify.note: newly added this pass; niche, release-churn detail

headline: Greenland's DST rule re-modeled days before the change
blurb: Greenland's 2023 DST rule was re-modeled in tz data three days before the event, catching systems pinned to the earlier version.
story: Greenland changed how its clocks work in 2023, and the time database revised how it encoded the rule. The 2022g release (Nov 2022) modeled America/Nuuk as permanently abolishing DST; the 2023a release (Mar 2023, three days before the event) corrected this so Greenland keeps DST under EU rules, with seasonal changes resuming in 2024. Systems that grabbed the earlier 2022g version and never refreshed held the wrong rule. Last-minute encoding changes like this are a common cause of clock errors.

title: Egypt reintroduces DST in 2023 on short notice
body: Government of Egypt; encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: After abandoning DST entirely from 2015, Egypt reinstated it in 2023, observing DST from the last Friday of April through the last Thursday of October. The cabinet approved the restoration on 1 Mar 2023, about two months before the 28 Apr 2023 spring-forward, and Law 34/2023 was ratified on 17 Apr 2023.
technicalImpact: Required tz 2023a (2023-03-22) to encode the new rule (last Friday of April to last Thursday of October). Unlike pre-2015 Egypt or present-day Morocco, the 2023 rule is a fixed open-ended algorithmic rule (no Ramadan-based suspension) and needs no annual hand-maintenance. The short lead time still risked unpatched systems being an hour off for the Egyptian summer.

validation.supportingQuote: The Egyptian government decided on Wednesday to restore applying daylight-saving time annually, starting from April's last Friday and until October's last Thursday, with the aim of "rationalising energy use"; the change will be lifted during Ramadan.
validation.corrections: Remove the Ramadan carve-out entirely. Corrected whatChanged: "After abandoning DST entirely from 2015, Egypt reinstated it in 2023, observing DST from the last Friday of April through the last Thursday of October. Unlike pre-2015 practice (and unlike Morocco/Palestine), the 2023 policy did NOT suspend DST during Ramadan. Confirmed only weeks before the 28 Apr 2023 spring-forward." Corrected technicalImpact: "Required tz 2023a (2023-03-22) to encode the new fixed rule (last Friday of April to last Thursday of October). No Ramadan suspension is encoded for Africa/Cairo, the rule is algorithmic and needs no annual hand-maintenance (contrast Morocco/Palestine). The short lead time risked unpatched systems being an hour off for the Egyptian summer." Title should drop "with a Ramadan carve-out."

headline: Egypt brings back seasonal clock changes
blurb: Egypt started changing its clocks again after years without it.
story: Egypt had stopped changing its clocks back in 2015. In 2023 it decided to bring seasonal clock changes back, confirming the plan only weeks before it began. The country also pauses the change during Ramadan, and those dates shift each year, which makes the rules harder to keep up to date.

title: Greenland's Nuuk moves standard time from UTC-3 to UTC-2
body: Government of Greenland (Naalakkersuisut)
whatChanged: America/Nuuk shifted its permanent standard offset from UTC-3 to UTC-2 on 2023-10-29, coinciding with the end of the 2023 DST period. Nuuk still sprang forward in spring 2023 under EU rules (to UTC-2 as usual); what changed is that the October fall-back was skipped, so the clock simply stayed at UTC-2, making the shift invisible to residents. From 2024 onward, DST resumes as UTC-2/UTC-1. Ittoqqortoormiit (America/Scoresbysund) joined Nuuk's schedule on 2024-03-31.
technicalImpact: America/Nuuk's standard offset changed permanently. Apps or caches that hardcoded UTC-3 for Nuuk were wrong from 2023-10-29 onward. The invisible clock-change (no physical DST end that night) made the shift easy to miss. Covered in tzdb 2023a with a correction in 2023d for Ittoqqortoormiit.
headline: The clock change nobody noticed
blurb: Greenland's capital Nuuk permanently moved one hour closer to UTC, disguised behind a DST transition that left clocks unchanged.
story: Greenland's parliament voted to shift Nuuk's standard time from UTC-3 to UTC-2 to bring it closer to Denmark's clock. The change landed on 2023-10-29, the same night clocks would normally fall back for winter, so the two adjustments cancelled each other out and nobody needed to touch their clock. From 2024, Nuuk resumes daylight saving time but now swings between UTC-2 and UTC-1 instead of the old UTC-3 and UTC-2. Software that had UTC-3 baked in for Nuuk silently produced wrong results from that night onward.

title: Vostok station switches from UTC+7 to UTC+5 to match resupply partners
body: Antarctica/Vostok moved from UTC+7 to UTC+5 on 2023-12-18 at 02:00 local time. The change was ordered by the station director to align with Progress base, the incoming construction convoys, and aviation partners, all of which used UTC+5. Earlier, around 2001, a US camp at Vostok ran on New Zealand time while the Russian station nearby ran on a different offset, putting two neighbors hours apart while solar noon sat near UTC+7.
whatChanged: Antarctica/Vostok moved from UTC+7 to UTC+5 on 2023-12-18 at 02:00 local time. The change was ordered by the station director to align with Progress base, the incoming construction convoys, and aviation partners, all of which used UTC+5. Solar noon at Vostok sits near UTC+7, so the offset was a logistics choice, not a geographic one.
technicalImpact: Antarctica/Vostok offset changed from +07 to +05 in the IANA tzdb (2023d release). Software caching old zone data, or inferring offset from coordinates, would be off by two hours. The polar setting, where solar noon and the chosen offset diverge widely, illustrates why coordinate-based offset inference is unreliable for Antarctic stations.
headline: The station that picked its clock by shipping schedule
blurb: Vostok, Russia's deep-Antarctic base, changed its clocks in 2023 not because of geography but because its resupply chain ran on a different time.
story: Vostok sits near the geomagnetic south pole, where solar noon falls around UTC+7. But nobody set the clocks by the sun. The Russian station synced to UTC+7, while construction convoys, aircraft, and the nearby Progress base all ran UTC+5. In December 2023 the station director switched Vostok to UTC+5 so radio calls and convoy schedules would line up. It is a clean reminder that polar time zones are logistics decisions, not geography, and that inferring a UTC offset from coordinates will silently break at the poles.

title: tzdb introduces zonenow.tab: an experimental coarser zone grouping
body: Paul Eggert / IANA tzdb maintainers
whatChanged: zonenow.tab was added to tzdb (comment dated 2023-12-18) as a new experimental file grouping timezone zones that are predicted to agree from now on. It is coarser than zone1970.tab: column 1 (country code) is always 'XX' and is explicitly ignored; sorting is by UTC offset then DST offset. The file header warns the format is experimental and may change in future versions.
technicalImpact: Code or UI tooling that parses zonenow.tab by column position risks breakage if the schema changes. The 'XX' sentinel in column 1 is intentional, not a data error. Library and app authors should treat this file as unstable until the format is stabilised in a future tzdb release.
headline: The zone list that admits it might change
blurb: tzdb added a new experimental file that groups time zones by predicted future agreement, explicitly warning its format is not yet stable.
story: Most tzdb files track historical timezone agreements going back to 1970 or earlier. zonenow.tab takes the opposite approach: it only cares about what clocks will do from now on, grouping zones that are expected to stay in sync. The format is deliberately marked experimental, with the country-code column replaced by a meaningless 'XX' placeholder. Software that parses it by column position, or treats 'XX' as a real country code, will produce wrong output and may break without notice if the schema changes.

title: POSIX.1-2024 mandates geographical TZ support and tm_gmtoff/tm_zone
body: The Open Group (IEEE/ISO/IEC POSIX standard body)
whatChanged: POSIX.1-2024 became the first POSIX edition since 1988 to require platforms to support geographical TZ values (e.g. 'America/New_York') and to mandate that struct tm include tm_gmtoff (UTC offset in seconds) and tm_zone (timezone abbreviation string). Previous editions only required proleptic TZ string support. The older tzname, daylight, and timezone global variables no longer suffice and are no longer needed.
technicalImpact: Code can now portably use tm_gmtoff and tm_zone on POSIX.1-2024-compliant systems without platform-specific ifdefs. The tzname, daylight, and timezone globals should be replaced. Systems still on POSIX.1-2017 or earlier remain unaffected.
headline: POSIX finally demands real time zones
blurb: The 2024 POSIX standard, for the first time in 36 years, requires systems to support named time zones like 'America/New_York' instead of only cryptic rule strings.
story: For decades, POSIX only required platforms to understand hand-crafted timezone rule strings that encoded a single offset and a single DST rule. That was fine for 1988 but useless for a world where New York has changed its clock rules multiple times. POSIX.1-2024 finally mandates support for named geographical zones and requires C's struct tm to carry the UTC offset and zone abbreviation directly. For developers this means portable code can now read tm_gmtoff and tm_zone without wrapping them in platform guards, though most deployed systems still run older standards.

title: POSIX.1-2024 extends DST transition time range to plus or minus 167 hours
body: The Open Group (POSIX standards body)
whatChanged: POSIX.1-2024 extended the legal range of DST transition times in proleptic TZ strings from 00:00:00 through 24:59:59 to the range -167:59:59 through 167:59:59. Negative times like -1:00 mean 23:00 the previous day, enabling proleptic TZ strings for zones such as <-02>2<-01>,M3.5.0/-1,M10.5.0/0.
technicalImpact: POSIX TZ string parsers must handle negative transition times. Code that assumes DST transitions always fall between midnight and 24:59:59 will reject or misparse valid TZ strings built to the new standard.
headline: The TZ string goes negative
blurb: POSIX.1-2024 let DST transition times go negative, so a clock can switch at 11pm the night before instead of always on the same day.
story: Before 2024, POSIX only allowed DST transition times from midnight up to 24:59:59, so a rule like 'switch at 23:00 the previous day' could not be expressed. POSIX.1-2024 opened the range all the way to plus or minus 167 hours, which covers any offset a real zone might need. This unlocked proleptic TZ strings for zones where the legal transition moment falls before local midnight. Any parser that validates transition times against the old bounds will now wrongly reject legal strings.

title: POSIX TZ strings cannot express non-Gregorian DST rules
body: IANA Time Zone Database project (tzdb theory documentation)
whatChanged: POSIX proleptic TZ strings (e.g. 'CET-1CEST,M3.5.0,M10.5.0/3') encode DST start and end rules using only Gregorian calendar month/week/day notation. Countries such as Morocco tie DST boundaries to the Islamic (Hijri) calendar, specifically Ramadan. No proleptic TZ string can represent these rules; only a full IANA zone name (e.g. Africa/Casablanca) backed by the compiled tzdb can correctly handle them.
technicalImpact: Any system or embedded device using a hardcoded POSIX TZ string for Africa/Casablanca will compute wrong local times during and around Ramadan every year. The fix is to use geographical IANA zone identifiers and keep the compiled tzdb up to date rather than relying on proleptic TZ strings.
headline: The calendar POSIX forgot
blurb: POSIX TZ strings only understand the Gregorian calendar, so they cannot correctly express DST rules tied to Ramadan or other Islamic calendar boundaries.
story: POSIX defines a compact string format for encoding a time zone's DST rules, but the format only knows about Gregorian months and weeks. Morocco suspends DST during Ramadan, whose start date shifts roughly 11 days earlier each Gregorian year. No proleptic TZ string can capture that. The tzdb theory document cites Morocco as an example of DST rules not based on the Gregorian calendar, and the fix is always the same: use a full IANA zone name backed by a current compiled database, not a hardcoded string.

title: IANA tzdb: timezone boundaries are not a stable interface
body: IANA tz coordinator (Paul Eggert et al.), documented in tzdb theory.html
whatChanged: The tzdb theory document explicitly states that timezone boundaries are not part of the stable interface. Asia/Bangkok currently includes Chiang Mai, Hanoi, and Phnom Penh, but the theory document warns this grouping can split at any time. Applications must not treat a zone ID as a proxy for a geographic region.
technicalImpact: Calendar and scheduling apps that store Asia/Bangkok (or any zone ID) as a location identifier rather than a time-offset rule may silently mis-represent events after a future zone split. Best practice: store the actual location separately and resolve the IANA zone ID at display time.
headline: A zone ID is not a map boundary
blurb: The IANA tz database warns that which cities belong to a zone ID can change without notice.
story: Asia/Bangkok today covers Chiang Mai, Hanoi, and Phnom Penh as well as Bangkok, but that grouping is not a promise. If any of those cities ever adjusts its clocks differently, the tzdb will split the zone and the old ID will no longer represent the same geography. The database maintainers document this explicitly: boundaries are not part of the stable interface. Apps that store a zone ID as a stand-in for a physical location can silently break after such a split.

title: Palestine encodes 60+ individual DST rules to avoid Ramadan through 2086
body: Palestinian Authority
whatChanged: Palestine adjusts its DST transitions each year to avoid Ramadan: clocks revert to standard time before Ramadan begins and spring forward again after it ends. Because Ramadan follows the Islamic lunar calendar and shifts roughly 11 days earlier each Gregorian year, no simple recurring rule can describe this. The IANA tz database encodes the transitions as dozens of individual Rule Palestine lines, one pair per year, computed from the Islamic calendar using GNU Emacs and hand-integrated far into the future, past 2080.
technicalImpact: No POSIX TZ string can represent this rule set; systems that derive Palestinian DST from a simple expression will compute wrong transitions. The multi-decade horizon means future tzdb releases will be needed to extend coverage. Any timezone library that does not ship the full compiled tzdata will silently give wrong offsets for Gaza and Ramallah in affected years.
headline: The calendar inside the calendar
blurb: Palestine suspends daylight saving time every Ramadan, and because Ramadan drifts across the Gregorian calendar, the timezone database must list a different DST start and end date for every single year for decades into the future.
story: Palestine chose to keep standard time during Ramadan, the Islamic holy month of fasting, when the evening call to prayer marks the end of the daily fast and an extra hour of daylight would complicate that schedule. The Islamic calendar is lunar, so Ramadan moves about 11 days earlier every Gregorian year. That means Palestine's DST start and end dates shift by roughly that amount each year too. There is no simple mathematical formula that captures this drift across a multi-decade horizon, so the IANA timezone database maintainers computed each transition date individually using an Emacs calendar program and encoded the results as dozens of separate rules. For software, this is a worst case: no compact rule expression works, the full precomputed table must be distributed, and the table will need updating again before the horizon runs out.

title: tzdb 2024a fixes localtime mishandling Ciudad Juarez timestamps in year 2422
body: tzdb maintainers (Paul Eggert et al.), bug reported by Gilmore Davidson
whatChanged: localtime and related C library functions mishandled timestamps occurring roughly 400 years after a time zone switches to a DST schedule. In 2023d data the problem was visible for November 2422, November 2822, and so on in America/Ciudad_Juarez. Fixed in tzdb 2024a (released 2024-02-01).
technicalImpact: Any system or language runtime linking against tzcode (glibc, musl, macOS, BSDs) could return wrong wall-clock times for far-future timestamps in affected zones. Calendar and scheduling software computing dates centuries out, astronomical or long-range planning tools, and tz-aware databases using America/Ciudad_Juarez data from 2023d or earlier were silently incorrect. Upgrading to 2024a corrects the TZif handling.
headline: The bug 400 years in the future
blurb: A structural flaw in how tzcode handles 400-year Gregorian cycles quietly broke wall-clock conversion for Ciudad Juarez timestamps in the year 2422.
story: When Ciudad Juarez got its own time zone in 2022, it inherited a DST schedule. About 400 years out, a quirk in how localtime handles the Gregorian cycle made it miscalculate timestamps for November 2422, 2822, and beyond. The bug was spotted by Gilmore Davidson and traced to how TZif files are walked when a zone has a DST rule far past its last explicit transition. It was fixed in tzdb 2024a. Though the affected dates are centuries away, the flaw reveals a Y2K-style cycle-boundary assumption that any software doing long-range scheduling or astronomical time conversion could hit.

title: Kazakhstan unifies the country on a single time zone (UTC+5)
body: Government of Kazakhstan
whatChanged: On 1 March 2024 Kazakhstan abolished its eastern UTC+6 zone and moved the whole country to UTC+5. tzdb 2024a encoded the change, shifting Asia/Almaty and Asia/Qostanay (both previously UTC+6, covering the eastern regions) permanently back one hour.
technicalImpact: Systems with users in the former UTC+6 areas saw a permanent one-hour shift; cached offsets and scheduled jobs pinned to the old Asia/Almaty offset were wrong until tz data updated. Whole-country offset moves like this are exactly what stale tzdata gets wrong.

reverify.source: IANA tzdb NEWS (2024a) + Wikipedia (Time in Kazakhstan)
reverify.note: newly added this pass; tz-encoded, editorial prose new

headline: Kazakhstan drops to one clock
blurb: Kazakhstan abolished its eastern time zone and put the whole country on UTC+5.
story: Kazakhstan used to span two time zones. In March 2024 it moved the entire country to a single zone, one hour back for the eastern regions. Software relying on the old eastern offset was an hour off until its time data updated. Country-wide changes like this are a common source of clock bugs.

title: Kazakhstan unifies all regions to UTC+5
body: Government of the Republic of Kazakhstan
whatChanged: Kazakhstan ended its two-zone arrangement (UTC+5 in the west, UTC+6 across most of the country including Almaty, Astana, and Shymkent). From 2024-03-01 00:00 local time, all regions moved to UTC+5. Asia/Almaty and Asia/Qostanay shifted from UTC+6 to UTC+5; western zones already on UTC+5 stayed unchanged.
technicalImpact: Systems running stale tzdata (pre-2024a) still show UTC+6 for Almaty and Astana. Asia/Almaty and Asia/Qostanay are the affected IANA zones. The change shipped in tzdata 2024a (released 2024-02-01).
headline: One clock for all of Kazakhstan
blurb: Kazakhstan collapsed its two time zones into one, moving the whole country to UTC+5 on 1 March 2024.
story: For decades Kazakhstan ran on two clocks: the western regions sat at UTC+5 while Almaty, Astana, and most of the country used UTC+6. In early 2024 the government decided a single national time would simplify coordination in transport and business. At midnight on 1 March 2024, Almaty and Astana clocks turned back one hour to join the west. Software relying on tzdata older than 2024a still reports UTC+6 for Asia/Almaty, producing one-hour errors for any application scheduling or displaying times in Kazakhstan.

title: Agnew (Nature, 2024): negative leap second projected ~2029, postponed by ice melt
body: Duncan Agnew, Scripps Institution of Oceanography (UC San Diego); published in Nature
whatChanged: Peer-reviewed analysis of Earth's core/mantle angular-velocity trends concluded UTC as currently defined would require its first-ever negative leap second by about 2029, and that polar ice melt from global warming has delayed this by roughly three years (otherwise ~2026).
technicalImpact: Gives a concrete near-term date for the untested negative-leap-second scenario, potentially BEFORE the 2035 abolition takes effect. Pressures the timekeeping community to either implement a negative leap second on systems never designed for it or accelerate the UTC-tolerance change, and strengthens the case for retiring leap seconds entirely before 2029.

validation.supportingQuote: Agnew calculated that a "negative leap second" event would be needed by 2029, the first in history; without the effects of climate change, Earth would need that negative leap second in 2026 instead of 2029 (Duncan Agnew, Nature, published 2024-03-27).

headline: Study warns clocks may lose a second
blurb: A study said clocks may need to drop a second for the first time around 2029.
story: A 2024 study looked at how fast the Earth is spinning. It concluded that official time may need to remove a second for the first time ever, around 2029. The study also found that melting polar ice has slowed the Earth a little and pushed that date back by about three years. Removing a second has never been done before, so it is an untested situation.

academicRefs.authors: Agnew, D. C.
academicRefs.title: A global timekeeping problem postponed by global warming
academicRefs.venue: Nature (vol. 628, issue 8007)
academicRefs.keyFinding: Extrapolating trends in the angular velocity of Earth's liquid core (decreasing at a near-constant rate, which steadily accelerates the rest of Earth) and other phenomena, UTC as now defined would require its first-ever negative leap second by 2029; had polar ice melt in Greenland and Antarctica not recently accelerated (moving mass toward the Equator and slowing rotation), that negative discontinuity would arrive about 3 years earlier, ~2026. Global warming is thus already measurably affecting global timekeeping.

academicRefs.authors: Boehm, S. and Salstein, D.
academicRefs.title: Atmospheric Excitation of Length of Day Inferred From 21st Century Climate Projections
academicRefs.venue: Journal of Geophysical Research: Atmospheres
academicRefs.keyFinding: Using CMIP6 climate projections across emission scenarios, the authors find that higher-emission pathways produce gradual increases in atmospheric angular momentum dominated by strengthening subtropical westerly jets; under the most intense scenario this could slow Earth's rotation by ~0.43 ms/day (about one-fifth the magnitude of tidal friction), while sustainable-development scenarios show no significant long-term length-of-day trend.

academicRefs.authors: Comite International des Poids et Mesures / 27th CGPM
academicRefs.title: Resolution 4 of the 27th CGPM (2022): On the use and future development of UTC
academicRefs.venue: Bureau International des Poids et Mesures (BIPM)
academicRefs.keyFinding: The CGPM resolved that the maximum allowed value of the difference UT1-UTC will be increased in or before 2035, instructing the CIPM (with the ITU) to propose a new tolerance ensuring UTC continuity for at least a century and an implementation plan, with a draft resolution for the 28th CGPM in 2026, effectively ending routine leap-second insertions.

title: Greenland's Ittoqqortoormiit (America/Scoresbysund) shifts its offset
body: Government of Greenland / Denmark; encoded by IANA
whatChanged: tzdb 2023d moved America/Scoresbysund from UTC-01/UTC+00 to UTC-02/UTC-01 on 2024-03-31, aligning eastern Greenland's small Ittoqqortoormiit community with the rest of Greenland's timekeeping (America/Nuuk).
technicalImpact: A small but real offset change that any system serving that zone had to pick up via a tz update; another reminder that even tiny populations generate zone-data churn that stale tzdata gets wrong.

reverify.note: newly added this pass; tz-encoded, editorial prose new

headline: A tiny Greenland town shifts its clock
blurb: Eastern Greenland's Scoresbysund zone changed offset, encoded in tz release 2023d.
story: A small community in eastern Greenland changed which time it keeps. The world's time database recorded the new offset in a 2023 update. Any program serving that area needed the update to stay correct. Even places with very few people create changes that software must track.

title: Palestine delays 2024 spring-forward a week past Ramadan (Asia/Gaza, Asia/Hebron)
body: Palestinian Authority
whatChanged: Palestine moved its 2024 spring-forward from the first Saturday after Ramadan to the second, shifting the clocks-forward date to 2024-04-20 for Asia/Gaza and Asia/Hebron. The same rule was applied to 2025 (2025-04-12). tzdb 2024a (released 2024-02-01) updated the Palestine rules and changed the default prediction algorithm to 'second Saturday after Ramadan', affecting projected dates from 2039 onward as well.
technicalImpact: Systems running stale tzdata before 2024a predicted the wrong spring-forward date for Gaza and Hebron in 2024 and 2025. Any software that cached or hard-coded the prior predicted date was off by one week. The rule change also silently shifted computed DST transitions for years beyond 2039.
headline: Palestine's Ramadan clock shuffle
blurb: Palestine pushed its 2024 clocks-forward date back by a week to let Ramadan end before daylight saving began.
story: Each year Palestine waits for Ramadan to finish before springing clocks forward, and in 2024 it shifted that moment a full week later than software had predicted. The tzdb maintainers updated the rule in release 2024a, changing the formula from the first Saturday after Ramadan to the second. Any device or server running old timezone data would have applied the wrong offset for the Gaza Strip and West Bank for a week. The incident is a textbook case of why late political DST decisions break systems that pre-compute transition dates.

title: RFC 9636: Updated TZif specification, obsoletes RFC 8536
body: IETF; authors Arthur David Olson, Paul Eggert, and Kenneth Murchison (FastMail)
whatChanged: Published 2024-10-01, RFC 9636 replaced and obsoleted RFC 8536 as the authoritative TZif specification, refining and clarifying the version 1/2/3 binary format and its registered media types while remaining upward-compatible with deployed files. It also newly specifies TZif version 4, which RFC 8536 did not cover, allowing the leap-second table to be truncated at the start and to carry an expiration time.
technicalImpact: Supersedes RFC 8536 as the document implementations should cite when reading/writing zoneinfo files; the tz project updated its documentation to reference RFC 9636. Provides current, corrected guidance for parser authors, reducing ambiguity-driven bugs in TZif handling.

validation.supportingQuote: "This document replaces and obsoletes RFC 8536, providing editorial improvements, new details, and errata fixes while keeping full compatibility with the interchange format of RFC 8536." (header: "Request for Comments: 9636 / Obsoletes: 8536 / ... K. Murchison / Fastmail / October 2024")

headline: New standard for time zone files
blurb: A new technical document updated the rules for time zone data files.
story: In October 2024, a new technical document replaced an older one as the official guide for how time zone data files are written and read. It clarified and refined the existing format while staying compatible with files already in use. The time zone project updated its own notes to point to the new document, giving software authors clearer guidance.

title: Paraguay abolishes DST and locks to UTC-3 permanently
body: Government of Paraguay
whatChanged: Paraguay stopped changing its clocks after the spring-forward transition on 2024-10-06 and is now permanently at UTC-3, ending 49 years of seasonal clock changes. IANA tzdb captured this in release 2025a (2025-01-15): the change affects real timestamps starting 2025-03-22, the date the autumn fall-back would otherwise have happened, and the obsolescent tm_isdst flags starting 2024-10-15.
technicalImpact: America/Asuncion no longer falls back in March, so any system running a tzdata release older than 2025a will wind Paraguay's clocks back one hour on 2025-03-22 and be an hour off all winter. The tm_isdst flag also flips meaning from 2024-10-15 onward, which can confuse code that keys off the DST flag rather than the actual offset.
headline: Paraguay's last clock change
blurb: Paraguay decided to stay on UTC-3 year-round, ending 49 years of seasonal clock shifts.
story: For decades Paraguay advanced its clocks each October and wound them back each March, like most of its neighbors. After the October 2024 spring-forward, it simply stopped, choosing to stay at UTC-3 all year. The first clock change that would have been canceled is the fall-back in March 2025, so the tz database does not show any real effect until then, even though the policy took hold in late 2024. Systems still running old tzdata will dutifully roll Paraguay's clocks back in March 2025 and sit an hour off for the whole winter, the classic failure when a country quietly stops observing daylight saving.

title: Paraguay adopts permanent daylight saving time (year-round UTC-3)
body: Government of Paraguay (decree)
whatChanged: Paraguay stopped switching clocks and stayed on summer time (UTC-3) year-round, ending the seasonal change to UTC-4. The final spring-forward was on 2024-10-06, after which Paraguay remained permanently at UTC-3 (law published 2024-10-14); tzdb 2025a encoded the permanent offset.
technicalImpact: America/Asuncion no longer has a fall-back transition; logic that assumed Paraguay observes DST, or that expected an autumn 2025 transition, is wrong. Permanent-DST adoptions repeatedly catch out hard-coded DST rules.

reverify.source: IANA tzdb NEWS (2025a) + Wikipedia (Time in Paraguay)
reverify.note: newly added this pass; tz-encoded, editorial prose new

headline: Paraguay stops changing its clocks
blurb: Paraguay decided to stay on summer time all year, ending its twice-yearly clock change.
story: Paraguay used to move its clocks twice a year. In 2024 it decided to keep summer time permanently and never fall back again. Programs that still expect the old autumn change will get the time wrong. Choosing permanent summer time is a change that often trips up older calendars.

title: Etc/Unknown reserved in tzdb to avoid CLDR collision
body: IANA Time Zone Database maintainers (Paul Eggert et al.), with thanks to Justin Grant, Mark Davis, and Guy Harris
whatChanged: tzdb release 2025a formally reserved the name Etc/Unknown, guaranteeing it will never be assigned real zone data. The reservation exists because CLDR uses the string 'Etc/Unknown' to represent an unknown or invalid timezone, and a future tzdb zone with that name would silently collide with that sentinel.
technicalImpact: Code that receives 'Etc/Unknown' from a CLDR or ICU source can now safely treat it as an invalid or unknown zone rather than a valid tzdb entry. Any library or runtime that delegates zone lookup to tzdb will return an error for this name, which is the intended behavior. Runtimes that upgraded to tzdb 2025a got this guarantee automatically.
headline: The timezone name nobody can claim
blurb: tzdb blocked the name Etc/Unknown from ever becoming a real timezone so it stays safe to use as an error sentinel.
story: CLDR, the Unicode locale data project, uses the string Etc/Unknown as a placeholder whenever a timezone is missing or invalid. If tzdb ever created a real zone by that name, any code relying on CLDR's sentinel would silently receive real time data instead of an error. The 2025a release closed that gap by formally reserving the name. It is a rare case of two standards coordinating to keep a name permanently empty.

title: strftime %s silently returned wrong epoch values for out-of-range dates
body: IANA tzdb maintainers (Paul Eggert et al.)
