whatChanged: strftime %s now returns the correct numeric string even when the value does not fit in time_t. From release 96a (when %s was introduced) through 2020a, and again in 2022b through 2024b, it returned the numeric equivalent of (time_t)-1. In releases 2020b through 2022a it returned 0. The fix shipped in tzdb 2025a.
technicalImpact: Any code using strftime %s to convert large, future, or distant-past timestamps to epoch strings received a silent -1 or 0 across most tzdb releases from 1996 onward. Log pipelines, audit trails, and epoch-based comparisons were affected without error. The fix requires upgrading to tzdb 2025a or a libc built against it.
headline: The epoch formatter that lied for decades
blurb: A bug in strftime caused the %s format specifier to silently return wrong values whenever a timestamp didn't fit in the system's time_t type.
story: The %s specifier in strftime is supposed to print a Unix epoch number as a plain string. For nearly 30 years, across almost every tzdb release, it returned -1 cast to a string whenever the date was too large or too small for the system's time_t. A brief stint of releases between 2020 and 2022 swapped that for a silent 0. Code that logged timestamps for distant dates or far-future events got plausible-looking but completely wrong numbers, with no error raised. The fix finally landed in 2025a.

title: Chile's Aysen Region splits off as America/Coyhaique, stays on permanent -03
body: Government of Chile (Aysen Region authorities)
whatChanged: Chile's Aysen Region stopped observing DST and moved to permanent UTC-03, diverging from America/Santiago. The tzdb 2025b release modelled this as a standard-offset change and created a new zone America/Coyhaique. Clocks in the region did not fall back on 2025-04-05 as the rest of Chile did.
technicalImpact: Apps that treated all of continental Chile as America/Santiago will produce wrong timestamps for Aysen. Hardcoded zone lists need America/Coyhaique added; tzdb 2025b introduced the new zone.
headline: The region that stayed in summer time
blurb: A Chilean region stopped changing its clocks and got its own time zone entry.
story: Aysen, a remote region in southern Chile, stopped observing daylight saving time to stay on UTC-03 year-round, joining the Magallanes Region. When the rest of Chile set clocks back in April 2025, Aysen did not, permanently splitting from the national America/Santiago zone. The IANA tz database responded with a new zone, America/Coyhaique, in the 2025b release. Any software that assumed a single zone for all of continental Chile now mishandles Aysen timestamps.

title: Chile's Aysen region splits off as America/Coyhaique on permanent UTC-3
body: Government of Chile; encoded by IANA
whatChanged: tzdb 2025b created a new zone, America/Coyhaique, for Chile's Aysen region, which stays on permanent UTC-3 rather than following the rest of mainland Chile between UTC-4 and UTC-3.
technicalImpact: A new IANA zone id appears, so software with hard-coded Chilean zone lists, or that assumed one mainland Chile rule, must learn the split. New-zone creation is a recurring source of 'unknown time zone' errors in older builds.

reverify.note: newly added this pass; tz-encoded, editorial prose new

headline: Chile gains a new time zone
blurb: Chile's Aysen region became its own zone, America/Coyhaique, staying on UTC-3 year-round.
story: Part of southern Chile decided to keep one steady time instead of switching with the rest of the country. The time database created a new named zone for the region in 2025. Software that listed Chile's zones by hand had to add the new name. New zones often cause errors in programs that have not been updated.

title: Chile's Aysen Region splits from America/Santiago into America/Coyhaique
body: Ministry of the Interior and Public Security of Chile (Decreto 93 of 2025)
whatChanged: Chile's Aysen Region stopped observing DST and adopted UTC-3 year-round, diverging from America/Santiago (which still follows seasonal -04/-03). The region did not change its clocks on 2025-04-05 at 24:00. A new IANA zone, America/Coyhaique, was created to model this split. tzdb already carried America/Punta_Arenas for Magallanes, which made the same move in 2017.
technicalImpact: Apps that map all of mainland Chile to America/Santiago will compute wrong offsets for Aysen users. Correct handling requires America/Coyhaique, added in tzdb 2025b (released 2025-03-22). Three separate IANA zones now cover Chilean mainland time.
headline: Chile's shrinking DST territory
blurb: Chile's Aysen Region opted out of clock changes and became its own time zone.
story: After a public consultation in which roughly 94% of Aysen residents voted to keep permanent summer time (UTC-3 year-round), Chile's government issued a decree in March 2025 making the region's summer time permanent. That put Aysen permanently at UTC-3, out of step with most of Chile, which still springs forward and falls back. The IANA tz database responded by adding a new zone, America/Coyhaique, so the region could be tracked separately. Any software that treats all of mainland Chile as one time zone will silently compute wrong times for users in Aysen.

title: IERS Bulletin C: no leap second 2017 onward; TAI-UTC frozen at 37s through 2025
body: IERS, Earth Orientation Centre
whatChanged: Following the last positive leap second (2016-12-31), every subsequent semi-annual Bulletin C has announced NO leap second. Bulletin C 70 (July 2025) again confirmed no leap second at end-2025, keeping UTC-TAI = -37s unchanged since 2017-01-01.
technicalImpact: A nearly decade-long pause means leap-second handling code paths have not been exercised in production since 2016, so latent bugs sit untested, and any future leap second, especially the projected negative one, would land on under-rehearsed systems. The leap second count in leap-seconds.list has not changed since 2016, though the file itself is updated periodically to extend its expiry date.

validation.supportingQuote: "NO leap second will be introduced at the end of June 2026. ... UTC-TAI = -37 s", Bulletin C, Earth Orientation Center of IERS (Observatoire de Paris), signed by Christian Bizouard.
validation.corrections: date should be 2025-07-07 (Bulletin C 70 published 7 July 2025, not 4 July 2025)

headline: No extra second added for years
blurb: No extra second has been added to official time since the end of 2016.
story: The last extra second was added at the end of 2016. Every check since then has confirmed that no extra second was needed, including the one made in July 2025. Because the gap has lasted so long, the software that handles these extra seconds has not been used in real life for years, so any hidden problems remain untested.

sidenotes.label: IERS Bulletin C (latest issue): leap-second status

title: Debian 13 'trixie' completes the 64-bit time_t (Y2038) ABI transition
body: Debian Project
whatChanged: Debian 13 (trixie), released 2025-08-09, shipped all release architectures except i386 with a 64-bit time_t ABI, extending date support beyond 2038-01-19. On 32-bit ARM architectures (armel and armhf) the ABI of roughly 495 library packages changed without soname bumps, requiring third-party software to be recompiled and checked for silent data loss. Approximately 5,000 to 6,000 packages were rebuilt in total, one of the largest coordinated ABI transitions in Debian history. The i386 architecture was excluded to preserve legacy binary compatibility.
technicalImpact: Any 32-bit ARM software linked against affected Debian libraries must be recompiled; failing to do so risks silent data corruption when time_t values are read or written across the ABI boundary. The soname-silent change means the dynamic linker will not warn at load time, so breakage may not surface until runtime. Software that hardcodes assumptions about 32-bit time_t (overflow at 2038-01-19 03:14:07 UTC) needs auditing even on rebuilt packages.
headline: Linux distros race the 2038 clock
blurb: Debian 13 rebuilt thousands of packages so that Linux on 32-bit ARM hardware can handle dates after January 2038.
story: Every 32-bit system that stores time as a signed integer will overflow on 19 January 2038, much like Y2K but for clocks. Debian 13 tackled this by switching almost all of its supported architectures to a 64-bit time type, which pushes the overflow roughly 292 billion years into the future. Around 6,000 packages had to be rebuilt, one of the largest ABI migrations the project has run. On 32-bit ARM boards the library interfaces quietly changed without the usual version signal, so software that was not rebuilt alongside Debian can corrupt time data without any error at startup. Developers shipping binaries for Raspberry Pi-class hardware on Debian need to audit and recompile their code to stay safe past 2038.

title: 28th CGPM (2026): decision on the new UT1-UTC tolerance (draft proposes 1-hour limit, continuous UTC from 20 May 2027)
body: CGPM / CIPM, in consultation with ITU and other organizations
whatChanged: Resolution 4 (2022) directs CIPM to propose a specific new maximum UT1-UTC value and an implementation plan, and to draft a resolution for agreement at the 28th CGPM (2026). The meeting at which the concrete tolerance and the path to 2035 are expected to be formally set.
technicalImpact: Will fix the numeric tolerance determining how far civil UTC may diverge from solar time before any correction, defining whether/when systems must ever apply a UT1-UTC offset, and confirming the timeline after which leap-second code can be safely retired. Drives long-horizon planning for GNSS, astronomy, and OS/NTP maintainers.

validation.supportingQuote: The resolution requests CIPM to "propose a new maximum value for the difference (UT1-UTC) that will ensure the continuity of UTC for at least a century" and to "draft a resolution including these proposals for agreement at the 28th meeting of the CGPM (2026)"; it also "decides that the maximum value for the difference (UT1-UTC) will be increased in, or before, 2035."

headline: A 2026 meeting will set the new limit
blurb: A 2026 meeting is set to decide how far clock time may drift from the sun.
story: A 2022 decision asked experts to propose exactly how far official clock time may drift from the sun's time, along with a plan to carry it out. A meeting in 2026 is the place where that exact limit and the path toward 2035 are expected to be settled. The outcome will guide long-term planning for navigation, astronomy, and computer systems.

title: IANA tz code adds -DTZ_RUNTIME_LEAPS=0 and a never-expiring leapseconds file
body: IANA / tz database maintainers
whatChanged: tzdb 2026a added the compile option -DTZ_RUNTIME_LEAPS=0 to disable runtime leap-second support (NEWS cites POSIX conformance, a smaller attack surface, and efficiency, while noting it drops RFC 9636 leap-second support). Earlier, tzdb 2023a made leapseconds.awk able to emit a leapseconds file that never expires, with NEWS noting this 'might be useful if leap seconds are discontinued' and that 'there are plans to discontinue leap seconds by 2035.'
technicalImpact: Gives OS and library builders a supported path to compile out leap-second handling and ship a non-expiring leapseconds file once leap seconds stop, reducing the surface area of leap-second-specific bugs.

validation.supportingQuote: Release 2026a - 2026-03-01: "Compiling with the new option -DTZ_RUNTIME_LEAPS=0 disables runtime support for leap seconds. Although this conforms to POSIX, shrinks tzcode's attack surface, and is more efficient, it fails to support Internet RFC 9636's leap seconds." / Release 2021b - 2021-09-24: "leapseconds.awk can now process a leap seconds file that never expires; this might be useful if leap seconds are discontinued."
validation.corrections: date: 2026-03-01 (release 2026a), not 2025-12-10. tzRelease: 2026a, not 2025c. The -DTZ_RUNTIME_LEAPS=0 option was added in tzdb 2026a (2026-03-01). The leapseconds.awk never-expiring-file capability predates this, added in 2021b (2021-09-24). body/responsible body (IANA/tz maintainers) and technical impact are correct.

headline: Time data adds a switch to skip extra seconds
blurb: Time zone software gained an option to leave out support for extra seconds.
story: A 2026 update to the widely used time zone software added an option to turn off support for occasional extra seconds. An earlier update, three years prior, had already made it possible to produce a file of past extra seconds that never expires. The notes describe both changes as preparation for a future where extra seconds are no longer added.

title: tzdb stops installing "right" leap-second TZif files by default
body: IANA / tz database maintainers (Paul Eggert et al.)
whatChanged: In tzdb 2026a, the Makefile stopped installing the alternate "right/" TZif file set by default. Systems that count leap seconds in time_t must now explicitly build with 'make REDO=posix_right' or 'make REDO=right_only'. The leapseconds file itself continues to be installed.
technicalImpact: Any system or distribution that relied on the default zoneinfo install to provide leap-second-aware TZif files silently loses that support. Timekeeping daemons, PTP/GPS clocks, and software that calls posix2time/time2posix may behave incorrectly without an explicit opt-in rebuild. Downstream packagers must audit their Makefile flags.
headline: The quiet disappearance of leap-second zone files
blurb: The tz database stopped shipping its leap-second-aware time zone files by default, silently removing a feature that precision clocks depend on.
story: For decades, the tz database shipped two parallel sets of zone files: the standard POSIX set and a "right/" set that counts every leap second in raw time values. In 2026a the maintainers dropped the right/ set from the default install. Systems that need it must now opt in explicitly at build time. Any daemon or clock driver that assumed the files would just be there could start miscounting time with no visible warning.

title: IANA tzdb makes leap-second ('right') support optional and non-default
body: IANA Time Zone Database (tzdb) maintainers
whatChanged: tzdb's 2026a release stopped installing the leap-second-aware 'right' TZif files by default and added a compile option (-DTZ_RUNTIME_LEAPS=0) to disable runtime leap-second support entirely. The leapseconds file itself is still installed as before, and the old default behavior can be restored with 'make REDO=posix_right'. This anticipates the eventual discontinuation of leap seconds following the 2022 CGPM resolution.
technicalImpact: Downstream OS/distro packagers building from tzdb get POSIX (leap-ignoring) behavior by default and must opt in for TAI-counting 'right' zones. Cements the ecosystem assumption that no new leap seconds are coming and simplifies the path to fixed TAI-UTC, while preserving historical leap data for systems needing true TAI timestamps.

validation.supportingQuote: The 'right' TZif files are no longer installed by default. ... Compiling with the new option -DTZ_RUNTIME_LEAPS=0 disables runtime support for leap seconds. Although this conforms to POSIX, shrinks tzcode's attack surface, and is more efficient, it fails to support Internet RFC 9636's leap seconds.
validation.corrections: Date should be 2026-03-01 (release timestamp 2026-03-02 UTC), not 2026-04-22. The 2026-04-22 2026a re-roll introduced no leap-second/'right' changes; it covered British Columbia's permanent -07 switch and zic overflow fixes. Releaser: Paul Eggert (IANA tzdb).

headline: Time data drops extra seconds by default
blurb: Time zone software stopped including extra-second support unless you ask for it.
story: A 2026 update to the widely used time zone software stopped including its extra-second mode by default. It also added an option to turn that support off entirely. The older data is still available for anyone who needs it. This reflects the expectation that no new extra seconds are coming.

title: TZif abbreviation storage raised from 50 to 256 bytes in tzdb 2026a
body: IANA tz coordinator / tzcode maintainers (Paul Eggert et al.)
whatChanged: tzdb 2026a raised the TZif abbreviation-string limit from 50 bytes to 256 bytes (counting trailing NULs). zic now generates and localtime.c now reads files using the larger limit. Some tzdata files were already using up to 40 bytes, leaving little headroom under the old cap.
technicalImpact: TZif parsers and tz libraries that hard-code a 50-byte buffer for abbreviation storage will misread or truncate zone names on files produced by zic 2026a onward. Conforming readers should handle at least 256 bytes per RFC 9636. Java, Go, and C runtime libraries that ship their own TZif reader may need updates before the limit is actually reached in practice.
headline: The 50-byte wall in time zone files
blurb: The internal limit on how many characters a TZif binary file can use for time zone abbreviations jumped from 50 to 256 bytes.
story: Every compiled time zone file stores its abbreviations like EST or AEST in a small fixed block. That block was capped at 50 bytes by a hard-coded limit in the tzcode tools (zic and localtime.c), and some zone files were already brushing against it. The 2026a release of tzdb raised the ceiling to 256 bytes, well beyond the old hard-coded limit and leaving generous headroom for future data needs. RFC 9636, the current TZif format spec, places no fixed cap on the total abbreviation block (its charcnt field is a 32-bit count), so conforming readers should already tolerate sizes far above the old 50.

title: Moldova's DST transition times corrected to EU standard (spring-forward 03:00, fall-back 04:00)
body: IANA tz database maintainers (fix credited to Heitor David Pinto)
whatChanged: tzdb 2026a corrected Moldova (Europe/Chisinau) to reflect EU transition times that had been in use since 2022: spring-forward at 03:00 local, not 02:00, and fall-back at 04:00 local, not 03:00. The fix applied retroactively from 2022 onward and corrected the upcoming 2026 spring-forward. Europe/Chisinau was also removed from zonenow.tab because its future timestamps now match Europe/Athens.
technicalImpact: Any system relying on pre-2026a tzdata would schedule Moldova's 2026 spring-forward one hour early. Applications using cached or embedded timezone data (Java, ICU, Python pytz, older Linux images) needed a tzdata update to avoid a one-hour scheduling error for Chisinau.
headline: Moldova's silent clock shift
blurb: Moldova had been springing forward and falling back at different times than the tzdb thought, and it took four years for the database to catch up.
story: Moldova quietly aligned its DST transitions with EU rules in 2022, moving its spring-forward from 02:00 to 03:00 and its fall-back from 03:00 to 04:00. The tzdb kept the old times for four years until a contributor noticed the discrepancy. The 2026a release fixed the record, but any system running older tzdata would have fired Moldova's clock change an hour off. It is a reminder that even small transition-time shifts can go undetected until a deadline forces a look.

title: British Columbia ends clock changes in 2026, forcing a TZDB abbreviation hack
body: Government of British Columbia, Canada
whatChanged: British Columbia enacted permanent daylight saving time, with the last clock change on 2026-03-08. The province moved to a permanent UTC-7 offset. The obvious abbreviation 'PT' (Pacific Time) is only two characters and violates the POSIX and TZDB requirement of 3-6 alphanumeric characters. TZDB works around this by modeling the transition as if the new law takes effect on 2026-11-01 02:00 instead of 2026-03-09 00:00, and uses 'MST' as the ongoing abbreviation for America/Vancouver.
technicalImpact: America/Vancouver is modeled as MST (UTC-7) from 2026-11-01 onward. A temporary hack in the northamerica file (modeling the interim as PDT) prevents CLDR v48.2 from displaying 'Pacific Standard Time' for a UTC-7 zone. Code relying on zone abbreviations or CLDR locale display names for BC after 2026 must be audited; the tzdb comment explicitly flags the hack as temporary, pending a CLDR fix.
headline: The two-letter abbreviation that broke BC's clock
blurb: British Columbia stopped changing its clocks in 2026, but the obvious abbreviation for its new permanent time zone was one letter too short for the rules, forcing a workaround in the global tz database.
story: BC passed a law making daylight saving time permanent, with March 8, 2026 as the last clock change. The natural abbreviation for the new 'Pacific Time' zone is 'PT', but POSIX and TZDB require zone abbreviations to be at least three characters long. Rather than wait for a political solution, the tz database used a documented hack: it pretends the new law took effect months later and labels the zone 'MST' to avoid misleading CLDR output. Software that reads zone abbreviations or locale display names for Vancouver after 2026 may show unexpected results until the underlying standards catch up.

title: British Columbia adopts permanent daylight offset (UTC-07 year-round)
body: Government of British Columbia, Canada (encoded in IANA tz 2026b)
whatChanged: British Columbia abolished seasonal clock changes. After its final spring-forward on 2026-03-08, the province stays on UTC-07 (permanent Pacific daylight) permanently; the change legally took effect 2026-03-09, and tzdata temporarily models the permanent offset at a later transition.
technicalImpact: America/Vancouver loses the autumn 2026 fall-back. Systems without tzdata 2026b read one hour behind through the winter, diverging from updated systems.

validation.supportingQuote: British Columbia moved to permanent -07 on 2026-03-09.

headline: British Columbia stops changing its clocks
blurb: British Columbia ended seasonal clock changes and kept one time all year.
story: British Columbia decided to stop changing its clocks twice a year. After one final spring clock change in 2026, the province stays on the same time all year. Computers that did not get the updated time data would read an hour behind through the winter, putting them out of step with updated systems.

title: Alberta adopts permanent Mountain Daylight Time (UTC-6 year-round)
body: Government of Alberta (Premier Danielle Smith)
whatChanged: Alberta introduced Bill 31 (the Red Tape Reduction Statutes Amendment Act, 2026) on April 23, 2026 and received Royal Assent on May 14, 2026, fixing the province to Mountain Daylight Time (UTC-6) year-round once in force. The relevant provisions come into force on proclamation by Order in Council, which had not yet been issued as of mid-2026. The Daylight Saving Time Act is retired and replaced by a new Official Time Act. No clock change is to occur on 2026-11-01 once the Act is proclaimed.
technicalImpact: tzdata must be updated to reflect Alberta staying at UTC-6 permanently with no autumn fallback; any software or system that relies on the standard Mountain Time DST schedule (UTC-7 in winter) will compute Alberta times incorrectly without the update.
headline: Alberta locks the clock
blurb: Alberta passed legislation in 2026 to stay on summer time all year, ending twice-yearly clock changes.
story: Following British Columbia's lead, Alberta's government tabled Bill 31 in April 2026 and received Royal Assent on May 14. The Act sets the province to UTC-6 year-round, meaning clocks would not fall back in November once it takes effect, but the change is not yet in force: it comes into effect only on proclamation by Order in Council, which had not been issued as of mid-2026 (mirroring British Columbia, whose 2019 bill was only proclaimed in March 2026). Saskatchewan, Alberta's eastern neighbour, already sits permanently at UTC-6, though it does so as Central Standard Time while Alberta's law fixes permanent Mountain Daylight Time, so the two share an offset rather than a designation. Systems that hard-code the standard Mountain Time winter offset of UTC-7 will produce wrong timestamps for Alberta once the change is proclaimed and timezone databases are updated.

title: NTP Era 0 rollover (unsigned 32-bit seconds since 1900)
body: NTP Project / IETF (RFC 5905); originally David L. Mills
whatChanged: NTP timestamps are 64-bit fixed-point: a 32-bit seconds field counting from the NTP epoch (1900-01-01T00:00:00Z) plus a 32-bit fraction. The unsigned 32-bit seconds field overflows after 2^32 seconds (~136 years), rolling from Era 0 to Era 1 at 2036-02-07T06:28:16Z.
technicalImpact: Implementations ignoring the era number wrap to 1900 (and the wrap precedes the 2038 Unix limit, so it hits first). Clients booting with a wildly wrong clock can mis-infer the era, refuse to sync, or step by ~136 years. RFC 5905's era/era-offset arithmetic mitigates it when implemented correctly.

validation.supportingQuote: "This format can represent the 136 years from 1900 to 2036 with a precision of 232 ps." / "The concern now is a possible meltdown when the ... NTP timestamp rolls over in 2036." / Table 1 shows the transition from era 0 to era 1 between 7 Feb 2036 and 8 Feb 2036.

headline: A clock counter runs out in 2036
blurb: A common network clock format reaches the end of its count in 2036.
story: Many computers keep time using a counter that started in 1900. That counter has a limit it reaches in early 2036, after which it starts over. Software that does not track which cycle it is in can jump back to 1900, refuse to sync, or set the clock off by well over a century. Correctly written software handles the reset.

title: Unix Year 2038 problem (signed 32-bit time_t overflow)
body: Unix/POSIX ecosystem; ISO C / The Open Group (POSIX time_t); Linux kernel and glibc maintainers driving 64-bit time_t
whatChanged: POSIX time counts seconds since the Unix epoch (1970-01-01T00:00:00Z) in a signed 32-bit integer. The max value 2,147,483,647 s is reached at 2038-01-19T03:14:07Z; the next second overflows to -2^31, interpreted as 1901-12-13T20:45:52Z.
technicalImpact: Any 32-bit time_t (or 32-bit storage of Unix timestamps) wraps to 1901, breaking timers, certificate validity, filesystem timestamps, schedulers, and embedded controllers. Remediation is widening time_t to signed 64-bit; The Linux kernel gained 64-bit time on 32-bit architectures in 5.6 (2020), and glibc 2.34 (2021) added opt-in 64-bit time_t for 32-bit platforms via the _TIME_BITS=64 build flag (which must be paired with _FILE_OFFSET_BITS=64 or compilation fails); since it is not the default, embedded/legacy 32-bit devices remain at risk.

validation.supportingQuote: "03:14:07 UTC on 19 January 2038 ... Unix time is defined as the number of seconds elapsed since 00:00:00 UTC on 1 January 1970 ... stored as a signed 32-bit integer ... Systems will interpret as 20:45:52 UTC on 13 December 1901 ... Modern systems and software updates address this problem by using signed 64-bit integers."
validation.corrections: Optional precision fix: 64-bit time_t opt-in on 32-bit platforms (_TIME_BITS=64) became fully usable in glibc 2.34 (2021) with Linux kernel 5.1+, not 2.32. Underlying __time64_t plumbing landed in glibc 2.32.

headline: A clock counter runs out in 2038
blurb: A common computer time counter reaches its limit in early 2038.
story: Many computers count time as seconds since 1970, stored in a way that runs out in early 2038. When it overflows, the time wraps back to 1901. This can break timers, security certificates, file dates, and schedules. The fix is to store the count in a larger format, which newer systems already do, but many older and embedded devices remain at risk.

academicRefs.authors: Suzuki, K., Kubota, T. and Kono, K.
academicRefs.title: Detecting and Analyzing Year 2038 Problem Bugs in User-Level Applications
academicRefs.venue: 2019 IEEE 24th Pacific Rim International Symposium on Dependable Computing (PRDC)
academicRefs.keyFinding: In an empirical study of over 32,000 C-based GitHub projects (2012-2018), 7.35% of the compiled projects contained Year 2038 bugs; some trigger undefined behavior capable of crashing systems. The authors' patches were accepted upstream by major projects including AWS tooling and the Linux Test Project. Existing tools could not detect these bugs because doing so requires understanding the problem's unique encoding semantics (signed 32-bit time_t).

academicRefs.authors: Okabe, R., Yabuki, J. and Toyama, M.
academicRefs.title: Avoiding Year 2038 Problem on 32-bit Linux by Rewinding Time on Clock Synchronization
academicRefs.venue: 2020 IEEE 25th International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Factory Automation (ETFA)
academicRefs.keyFinding: Because embedded systems running 32-bit Linux still hit the 2038 system-clock overflow, the authors propose a mitigation that rewinds time inside the time-synchronization (NTP-style) software so the system clock is deliberately delayed from real time by a whole number of years, keeping the signed 32-bit counter below its overflow point without widening time_t.

title: GPS legacy week number third rollover (10-bit LNAV, November 2038)
body: U.S. Space Force (GPS Directorate) / GPS receiver and firmware vendors
whatChanged: The legacy GPS LNAV navigation message encodes the week number in 10 bits (range 0-1023, cycle length about 19.6 years). On the night of 20-21 November 2038 the counter wraps to zero for the third time (previous rollovers: 21-22 August 1999 and 6-7 April 2019). Receivers that have not been firmware-updated to disambiguate the 10-bit epoch, or that do not consume the modernised CNAV 13-bit week number (which will not roll until around 2137), will report dates roughly 19.6 years in the past.
technicalImpact: GPS-disciplined NTP servers, telecom stratum-1 clocks, embedded navigation gear, and any system relying on GPS for time-of-day can jump about 19.6 years backward on this date. The 2019 rollover caused flight delays (Honeywell avionics), network outages (New York City's wireless network), and failures in consumer devices, NOAA buoys, radiosondes, and automotive GPS units. The 2038 event is identical in mechanism; mitigation requires firmware that pivots the 10-bit week against a known reference epoch, or hardware capable of consuming CNAV's 13-bit week number.
headline: The GPS clock that forgets every 19 years
blurb: The original GPS signal stores its week number in only 10 bits, so after 1,024 weeks the counter resets to zero and unprepared receivers think the date has rolled back nearly two decades.
story: GPS satellites broadcast a week number alongside their timing signal, but the original format reserved only 10 bits for it, giving a maximum count of 1,023 before the number wraps back to zero. That cycle takes roughly 19.6 years. The counter rolled over in 1999, again in April 2019, and will do so a third time on the night of 20-21 November 2038. The 2019 event delayed flights, broke network clocks in New York City, and sent consumer dashboards back to the wrong year. Receivers that are not updated to handle the wrap, or that cannot read the newer 13-bit signal that lasts until around 2137, will suffer the same jump backward in time, corrupting any software that trusts GPS for its clock.

title: Apple ProDOS two-digit year window (1940-2039) expires
body: Apple Computer (via ProDOS 8 Technical Note #28)
whatChanged: ProDOS stores the year as a 7-bit field; only values 0-99 are valid (100-127 are invalid). Apple Technical Note #28 defined values 40-99 as 1940-1999 and values 0-39 as 2000-2039. From 2040 onward the window is exhausted: the value 0 wraps back to 1940, so ProDOS-era software reads 2040 as 1940, 2041 as 1941, and so on.
technicalImpact: Any code that retains the original 7-bit ProDOS date handling (including Apple II emulators, disk image tools, and vintage software running under emulation) computes wrong years from 2040 onward. The GS/OS layer is unaffected because it uses a separate byte offset from 1900, giving a range of 1900-2155. The community ProDOS 2.5 release also addresses this by expanding the year field from 7 bits to 10 bits, supporting dates through 2924, so the rollover affects only systems and tools still using the original 7-bit format.
headline: The Apple II clock that thinks 2040 is 1940
blurb: Apple's ProDOS operating system stores years in a 100-year window that ends in 2039, so dates in 2040 and beyond silently roll back a century.
story: ProDOS, the operating system for Apple II computers, stores the year as a small number between 0 and 99. Apple issued a technical note in the 1990s explaining that 40-99 meant 1940-1999 and 0-39 meant 2000-2039, a trick to sidestep the Y2K problem. That trick runs out on 1 January 2040. At that point the number 0 appears again, and the system interprets it as 1940. Software built on ProDOS date handling will silently compute ages, sort files, and display timestamps using dates a century in the past. Emulators and disk tools that faithfully replicate ProDOS date parsing inherit the same flaw.

title: Apple HFS/HFS+ 32-bit timestamp overflows, wrapping clocks back to 1904
body: Apple Inc.
whatChanged: Classic Mac OS and HFS/HFS+ store time as an unsigned 32-bit count of seconds since 00:00:00 on 1 January 1904. At 06:28:15 UTC on 6 February 2040, the counter reaches 2^32 - 1 seconds and wraps back to zero, making the clock read 1 January 1904. APFS (introduced with macOS 10.13 in 2017) uses a wider timestamp and is not affected; HFS and HFS+ volumes and any code using pre-APFS Mac date APIs are.
technicalImpact: File timestamps on HFS/HFS+ volumes wrap to 1904, corrupting sort order, backup incremental logic, certificate and expiry checks, and any modification-time comparisons. Software reading classic Mac date APIs gets a date 136 years in the past. Any system still mounting HFS-formatted media (external drives, disk images, legacy backups) is exposed.
headline: The Mac clock that rewinds to 1904
blurb: Apple's older HFS and HFS+ filesystems count seconds in a 32-bit bucket that runs dry on 6 February 2040, sending timestamps back to the year 1904.
story: Apple chose 1 January 1904 as the starting point for its classic Mac clock, a nod to the first year of the 20th century that made sense in the 1980s. The bucket used to hold that count is only 32 bits wide, which gives it just under 136 years before it overflows. On 6 February 2040 at 06:28:15 UTC, the counter wraps to zero and the clock jumps back to 1904. Apple fixed this in 2017 by shipping the Apple File System (APFS), which uses a much larger timestamp range. Any HFS or HFS+ volume still in service after that moment will report wrong file dates, breaking backups, sorting, and any logic that compares file ages.

title: Year 2042 problem: IBM mainframe TOD clock overflow
body: IBM (z/Architecture Time-Of-Day clock)
whatChanged: IBM's z/Architecture Time-Of-Day (TOD) clock counts from 1 January 1900. The original 64-bit TOD value overflows on 17 September 2042, after which it wraps. Software must use the extended TOD format (STCKE) or handle the carry.
technicalImpact: z/OS, z/VM and z/TPF systems that compute time from the 64-bit TOD value will miscalculate after the wrap; remediation has been a multi-year mainframe modernization effort. The mainframe equivalent of the Unix 2038 problem.

reverify.source: Wikipedia time-bugs overview; IBM primary doc to be pinned
reverify.note: newly added this pass; needs a primary IBM source pinned, hence the tag

headline: The mainframe's own 2038
blurb: IBM mainframe clocks count from 1900 and overflow in 2042 unless software moves to the extended format.
story: IBM mainframes keep time by counting from the year 1900. The original counter runs out of room in 2042. After that, software using the old counter would calculate the wrong time. Mainframe teams have spent years moving to a longer counter to avoid it.

title: SAP S/4HANA Maintenance Plan Scheduling caps at 19 January 2048
body: SAP SE
whatChanged: Maintenance Plan Scheduling in SAP S/4HANA cannot represent finish dates beyond 19 January 2048. That date is 24,855 days from 1 January 1980, which equals 2^31 seconds (2,147,483,648) rounded down to whole days. Attempts to schedule a maintenance call object past that ceiling fail, aborting the run.
technicalImpact: ERP maintenance plans, asset inspection schedules, and long-horizon capacity planning cap or fail at 2048-01-19. The root cause is a 32-bit signed integer counting elapsed time from a 1980 epoch, the same class of overflow behind the Unix Year 2038 problem but with a different epoch. Systems managing assets with multi-decade lifespans (infrastructure, heavy machinery) hit the ceiling before 2048 arrives. SAP addressed the limit in later S/4HANA releases by supporting dates through year 9999.
headline: The SAP clock that stops in 2048
blurb: SAP's maintenance scheduling software cannot book jobs past 19 January 2048, because an internal counter overflows on that date.
story: Inside SAP S/4HANA, dates for maintenance plans are limited by a count of seconds since 1 January 1980 held in a 32-bit signed integer. That integer maxes out at about 2.1 billion, which, converted to whole days, lands on 19 January 2048. Any schedule pushed past that date fails. Companies planning maintenance for long-lived assets like pipelines or turbines can hit the wall decades in advance if their schedules extend far enough. SAP later patched the limit in newer releases, extending support to the year 9999, but the bug is a reminder that epoch choices and integer widths made in one decade quietly decide what future dates software can handle.

title: Year 2106 problem: unsigned 32-bit time overflow
body: Systems using unsigned 32-bit seconds since 1970
whatChanged: Systems that store Unix time as an unsigned 32-bit integer do not overflow in 2038 but instead run out at 06:28:15 UTC on 7 February 2106, when the counter reaches its maximum. Several protocols and file formats use this representation.
technicalImpact: Unsigned 32-bit timestamps (seen in some embedded systems, file formats, and protocol fields such as DNSSEC RRSIG inception/expiration and NFS v3 timestamps) wrap in 2106 rather than 2038. The 2038 problem's longer-lived cousin, and easy to overlook because it looks safe for much longer.

reverify.note: newly added this pass; secondary source, editorial prose new

headline: Unsigned clocks last until 2106
blurb: Counting seconds with an unsigned 32-bit number pushes the overflow from 2038 out to 2106.
story: The well-known 2038 problem comes from counting seconds with a signed 32-bit number. If a system uses an unsigned number instead, it does not break in 2038 but in 2106. Some file formats and protocols use this style of counter. It is easy to miss because it looks safe for much longer.

title: Timezone abbreviations like CST and IST are multiply ambiguous
body: IANA tz database (theory.html)
whatChanged: The tzdb documents that common timezone abbreviations are not unique identifiers. CST means Central Standard Time in North America but China Standard Time in China. IST refers to India Standard Time, Irish Standard Time, and Israel Standard Time. The database recommends using numeric UTC offsets (e.g. -0600) or IANA zone IDs instead.
technicalImpact: Any code that stores or parses a timezone abbreviation like CST or IST cannot resolve it unambiguously without extra context. Use IANA timezone IDs (e.g. America/Chicago, Asia/Kolkata) or numeric offsets in all data storage and interchange.
headline: The abbreviation that means three different things
blurb: Popular timezone abbreviations like CST and IST each refer to multiple different time zones, making them unreliable as identifiers.
story: CST is used for Central Standard Time in North America and China Standard Time in Asia. IST covers India, Ireland, and Israel, three places on different offsets sharing the same three letters. The IANA tzdb calls this out explicitly in its theory document and tells developers to use numeric offsets or full IANA IDs instead. Software that stores an abbreviation without context can silently misinterpret timestamps.
